questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease receives 1 unit of packed RBCs for symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL). Twenty minutes after the transfusion starts, she reports mild shortness of breath. Vital signs: temperature 37.0°C (baseline 36.9°C), blood pressure 132/78 mmHg (baseline 134/80 mmHg), heart rate 94/min, respiratory rate 20/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air (baseline 97%). Physical examination shows clear breath sounds bilaterally, no jugular venous distension, no S3 gallop, and no urticaria or hives. Arterial blood gas shows a PaO2/FiO2 ratio of 340 mm Hg. Chest radiograph shows no infiltrates or pulmonary edema, and NT-proBNP is unchanged from the pretransfusion value. The transfusion is stopped, and her dyspnea resolves over the next few hours with supportive care alone, without diuretics. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Transfusion-associated dyspnea (TAD)
Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction (FNHTR)
Anaphylaxis

questions.explanation_correct: Transfusion-associated dyspnea (TAD) is defined as acute respiratory distress occurring within 24 hours of transfusion that does not meet the case criteria for TRALI, TACO, or an allergic/anaphylactic reaction; it is a diagnosis reached only after these more specific entities have been excluded, and management is supportive care while that workup proceeds. This patient's course lacks the hypoxemia (PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 mm Hg), fever, hypotension, and bilateral infiltrates required for TRALI; lacks the hypertension, jugular venous distension, S3 gallop, radiographic pulmonary edema, and BNP/NT-proBNP rise (≥1.5x baseline) required for TACO; and lacks urticaria or other signs of an allergic reaction. With all of the specific pulmonary and allergic categories excluded, TAD is the most likely diagnosis, and supportive care with resolution over hours is consistent with this label.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect. TRALI requires new acute lung injury with bilateral infiltrates and hypoxemia (PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 mm Hg), typically within 6 hours, often with fever and hypotension. This patient's PaO2/FiO2 is 340 mm Hg (not hypoxemic), lungs are clear, and there is no fever or hypotension.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect, though this is the classic board trap since many cases labeled TAD are actually mild or under-recognized TACO. However, TACO's surveillance features — hypertension, JVD, S3 gallop, radiographic pulmonary edema, and a BNP/NT-proBNP rise ≥1.5x the pretransfusion value — are all explicitly absent in this patient, so TACO's criteria are not met.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction with multi-organ (respiratory and cardiovascular) involvement, typically including cutaneous findings such as urticaria/hives, none of which this patient has.
option_explanations.A: Correct. TAD is diagnosed only after TRALI, TACO, and allergic reaction have been excluded, exactly as demonstrated by this patient's normal PaO2/FiO2, unremarkable cardiopulmonary exam and imaging, unchanged BNP, and absence of urticaria; management is supportive care.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. FNHTR is defined by a temperature rise of ≥1°C during or within 4 hours of transfusion and is not primarily a dyspnea-driven diagnosis; this patient's temperature is unchanged from baseline.

questions.teaching_point: Transfusion-associated dyspnea (TAD) is a diagnosis of exclusion: respiratory distress within 24 hours of transfusion that fails to meet the specific case criteria for TRALI (hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates, fever/hypotension), TACO (hypertension, JVD/S3, radiographic edema, BNP rise), or an allergic/anaphylactic reaction; once these are excluded, TAD is managed supportively, though many apparent TAD cases are actually mild or under-recognized TACO.

source_references.supports: Defines TAD as acute respiratory distress within 24 hours of transfusion not meeting TRALI/TACO/allergic criteria, diagnosed only by excluding those entities, managed with supportive care; also states hypoxemia is PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 mmHg and that many TAD cases are actually under-recognized TACO.

Provides TRALI and TACO clinical presentations used to build distractors: TRALI = hypoxemia, fever, hypotension, bilateral infiltrates within 6h; TACO = hypertension, JVD, S3 gallop, elevated BNP/NT-proBNP ≥1.5x baseline.

Defines FNHTR as a ≥1°C temperature rise during or within 4 hours of transfusion, and corroborates TRALI (hypoxemia/hypotension) versus TACO (hypertension/JVD/S3) clinical distinctions used for distractor construction.

review.notes:
Verified against all four approved sources (read in full). SRC-032 and SRC-060 contain no material relevant to TAD/TRALI/TACO/anaphylaxis/FNHTR and are correctly not cited for this item; the substantive grounding comes from SRC-050 and SRC-045, both of which strongly and almost verbatim support the core content:
- TAD definition (respiratory distress within 24h, not meeting TRALI/TACO/allergic criteria, diagnosis of exclusion, supportive management) — verbatim match to SRC-050 lines 17, 33, 49.
- TRALI criteria (bilateral infiltrates, hypoxemia PaO2/FiO2 ≤300, fever, hypotension, ~6h window) —

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is 2 days post-emergency cardiac surgery and is being treated in the ICU for sepsis. He receives a unit of apheresis platelets from a parous female donor whose HLA antibody status was not tested. Ninety minutes later he develops sudden dyspnea and fever, with oxygen saturation 84% on room air. Blood pressure is normal-to-low, there is no jugular venous distension or S3 gallop, and chest radiograph shows new bilateral infiltrates. B-type natriuretic peptide is not significantly elevated from baseline. The findings are consistent with transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI). According to the "two-hit" (threshold) model of TRALI pathophysiology, which mechanism best accounts for this reaction?

options.text: The recipient had preformed antibodies against donor leukocyte antigens, which agglutinated the transfused white cells and mechanically occluded the pulmonary capillary bed

The patient's sepsis and recent cardiac surgery served as a first hit that primed and sequestered neutrophils in the pulmonary microvasculature; anti-leukocyte antibodies (or bioactive lipids) in the transfused platelet unit then served as a second hit that activated these primed neutrophils, producing endothelial injury and capillary leak
The volume of the platelet transfusion exceeded the patient's cardiac reserve, producing hydrostatic pulmonary edema from circulatory overload
Immunocompetent donor lymphocytes in the platelet unit engrafted in the recipient's lung tissue and mounted a graft-versus-host response against pulmonary parenchyma
Preformed IgE on the patient's mast cells cross-linked with a donor plasma protein, triggering degranulation and a systemic anaphylactic reaction

questions.explanation_correct: TRALI pathophysiology is explained by a "two-hit" (threshold) model. The first hit comes from the recipient's underlying clinical state (e.g., sepsis, recent surgery, critical illness), which primes and sequesters neutrophils along the pulmonary microvascular endothelium. The second hit is delivered by the transfused component itself, typically donor anti-leukocyte (HLA or HNA) antibodies reacting against recipient antigens, or, in antibody-negative cases, biologically active lipids accumulated during storage. Activation of the already-primed neutrophils causes endothelial injury and increased capillary permeability, producing protein-rich (exudative) noncardiogenic pulmonary edema — the sudden hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates, fever, and hypotension/normal blood pressure seen in this patient, with a BNP that is not meaningfully elevated (distinguishing it from TACO). Because pregnancy is a recognized route of donor HLA alloimmunization, using an HLA-antibody-untested parous female donor increases the likelihood of delivering the antibody-mediated second hit in an already-primed (post-surgical, septic) recipient.

option_explanations.B: Correct. This is the accepted two-hit/threshold model for TRALI: a recipient-side first hit (critical illness such as sepsis or recent surgery) primes and sequesters neutrophils in the lung microvasculature, and a donor-side second hit (anti-HLA/anti-HNA antibodies or biologically active lipids in the component) activates those primed neutrophils, causing endothelial damage and a protein-rich (exudative) capillary leak that manifests as noncardiogenic pulmonary edema.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This describes the hydrostatic mechanism of TACO (volume/rate-related circulatory overload), not TRALI. The stem's normal-to-low blood pressure, absence of JVD/S3, and non-elevated BNP argue against a volume-overload (TACO) mechanism.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. This describes IgE-mediated anaphylaxis, a distinct hazard of transfusion with multi-organ (respiratory and cardiovascular) involvement driven by mast cell degranulation, not the neutrophil-mediated two-hit injury that defines TRALI.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. This reverses the antibody direction: in TRALI, donor antibodies (anti-HLA Class I/II or anti-HNA) react against recipient leukocyte antigens, not the reverse, and the injury mechanism is neutrophil activation and endothelial capillary leak rather than agglutination-driven vascular occlusion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. This describes transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD), caused by engraftment and proliferation of donor T lymphocytes, a delayed and largely fatal complication prevented by irradiation — an entirely different entity from the acute neutrophil-mediated lung injury of TRALI.

questions.teaching_point: TRALI pathogenesis follows a two-hit (threshold) model: recipient-side priming of pulmonary-sequestered neutrophils (first hit, from conditions like sepsis or surgery) followed by donor-derived anti-leukocyte antibodies or bioactive lipids that activate those primed neutrophils (second hit), causing endothelial injury, capillary leak, and exudative noncardiogenic pulmonary edema.

source_references.supports: Describes the Two-Hit Theory: a first hit from patient factors (sepsis, heart surgery) primes neutrophils, and a second hit from donor antibodies or lipids triggers lung injury; also supports TRALI clinical features (hypoxemia, fever, hypotension) and that TRALI does not respond to diuretics (unlike TACO).

Provides the fullest description of the Two-Hit Model for TRALI, donor workup for HLA(Class I/II)/HNA antibodies reacting with recipient antigens, TRALI mitigation via donor selection (male, never-pregnant, or HLA-antibody-negative parous female donors), and that TRALI edema fluid is protein-rich/exudative versus TACO's protein-poor/transudative fluid and normal BNP.
Confirms the general TRALI definition (new acute lung injury within 6 hours of transfusion) used to frame the vignette's diagnosis.

review.notes:
Verified against all four sources.

Core two-hit model claim (option A / explanation_correct) is directly and consistently grounded in two independent sources:
- SRC-045 line 85: "The 'Two-Hit' Theory: TRALI often requires a 'first hit' (patient factors like sepsis or heart surgery) to prime neutrophils and a 'second hit' (donor antibodies or lipids) to trigger lung injury."
- SRC-050 line 9: "The 'Two-Hit' Model (TRALI): Requires a 'first hit' (patient's clinical state like sepsis or surgery) that primes neutrophils, followed by a 'second hit' (donor antibodies or lipids) that triggers the in

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known Waldenström macroglobulinemia presents with blurred vision, retinal hemorrhages on fundoscopic exam, headache, and dizziness. His serum monoclonal IgM level is markedly elevated, consistent with hyperviscosity syndrome. He undergoes urgent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) using 5% albumin as the replacement fluid. A single 1.0-plasma-volume exchange produces a marked, sustained drop in his serum IgM level. In contrast, patients undergoing TPE for IgG-mediated autoimmune disorders typically require several exchange sessions over days to achieve a comparable reduction in pathogenic antibody level.

options.text: Waldenström macroglobulinemia is an ASFA Category I indication for TPE while IgG-mediated disorders are typically Category II, and this evidence-grading difference is what accounts for the difference in number of sessions required.
The albumin replacement fluid used during this patient's TPE selectively binds and removes circulating IgM more efficiently than it would remove IgG.

IgM is confined largely to the intravascular compartment (approximately 80%), so it is efficiently removed with each plasma volume exchanged; IgG is approximately 60% extravascular and re-equilibrates into the vascular space between exchanges, which is why IgG-mediated disorders typically require multiple TPE sessions.

Because increasing the exchanged volume from 1.0 to 2.0 plasma volumes doubles the amount of antibody removed, a single large-volume exchange for IgM achieves the same removal as two standard-volume exchanges for IgG.

A single plasma-volume exchange removes essentially all intravascular antibody regardless of isotype, and IgM's smaller molecular size relative to IgG allows it to pass through the apheresis separation membrane more readily.

questions.explanation_correct: The efficiency of TPE at removing a pathogenic immunoglobulin depends on how much of it resides in the intravascular space accessible to the apheresis circuit. IgM is predominantly intravascular (~80%), so a single plasma-volume exchange removes most of the circulating pool immediately, explaining why TPE is highly effective in a single session for IgM-mediated hyperviscosity syndrome from Waldenström macroglobulinemia. IgG, by contrast, is roughly 60% extravascular; only the intravascular fraction is removed per session, and extravascular IgG re-equilibrates into the plasma between treatments, which is why IgG-mediated conditions require repeated exchanges to achieve an equivalent reduction.

option_explanations.C: Correct. This is the compartmental-distribution explanation given for the IgM vs. IgG removal difference: IgM's predominantly intravascular location makes it efficiently cleared in one exchange, while IgG's larger extravascular pool requires multiple sessions for equilibration and removal.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. A 1.0-plasma-volume exchange removes only about 63%-70% of an intravascular substance, not essentially all of it, and the explanation for isotype differences given is compartmental distribution, not membrane filtration by molecular size — no such size-based removal claim is supported.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Albumin is chosen as TPE replacement fluid because it carries a low risk of allergic reaction and disease transmission, not because it has any antibody-binding or selective removal property; it is explicitly noted to lack immunoglobulins itself and functions only as a volume replacement, not an active clearance agent.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. This inverts a specific board-trap fact: increasing exchange volume from 1.0 to 2.0 plasma volumes yields only about an additional 20% removal (diminishing returns) while doubling time, cost, and risk — it does not double the antibody removed.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. ASFA category reflects the strength of clinical evidence supporting TPE as first-line versus second-line therapy for a given disease, not the pharmacokinetic reason fewer or more exchange sessions are needed to clear a pathogenic antibody; the true explanation is the intravascular/extravascular distribution difference between IgM and IgG.

questions.teaching_point: Immunoglobulin compartmental distribution (IgM ~80% intravascular vs. IgG ~60% extravascular) directly determines TPE efficiency: IgM-mediated disease (e.g., Waldenström hyperviscosity) responds robustly to a single exchange, while IgG-mediated disease requires repeated sessions as extravascular antibody re-equilibrates into plasma between treatments.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors - IgG vs. IgM Removal
source_references.supports: States IgM is primarily intravascular (~80%), making TPE highly effective in one session, whereas IgG is ~60% extravascular, requiring multiple sessions for equilibration.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations - Hyperviscosity Syndrome
source_references.supports: Hyperviscosity syndrome presents with visual disturbances (retinal hemorrhages), headache, and dizziness, and is common in Waldenström macroglobulinemia (IgM).

source_references.locator: Core Concepts - Replacement Fluid Kinetics; Common Board Traps - Diminishing Returns
source_references.supports: A 1.0-plasma-volume exchange removes approximately 63%-70% of an intravascular substance; increasing to 2.0 PV adds only about 20% more removal while doubling time/cost/risk.

source_references.locator: Management - Most Other TPE; High-Yield Comparison Points - Plasma vs. Albumin Replacement
source_references.supports: 5% human serum albumin is the standard TPE replacement fluid, chosen to minimize allergic reactions and disease transmission risk; it lacks coagulation factors and immunoglobulins.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts - Evidence-Based Application; High-Yield Comparison Points - ASFA Category I vs. II
source_references.supports: ASFA categories I-IV reflect the strength of clinical evidence for TPE as first-line vs. second-line therapy for a condition.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts - Plasma Composition
source_references.supports: Plasma composition includes immunoglobulins as the protein fraction responsible for immunity, alongside albumin and coagulation factors.

review.notes:
Both sources were read in full and independently cross-checked against every asserted fact.

SOURCE SUPPORT (pass): Every numeric/factual claim traces verbatim to SRC-047 "principles-and-clinical-management-of-therapeutic-apheresis.md": IgM ~80% intravascular / IgG ~60% extravascular ("Common Board Traps and Distractors - IgG vs. IgM Removal": "IgM is primarily intravascular (80%), making TPE highly effective in a single session. IgG is 60% extravascular, necessitating multiple sessions to allow for equilibration"); 1.0-PV exchange removes ~63-70% of an intravascular substance and 2.0 PV adds

questions.subdomain: Plasma Products – Indications and Clinical Use
questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman who is blood group A, D-negative presents for a routine visit at 28 weeks' gestation. Her indirect antiglobulin (antibody) screen is negative, and the father's D type is unknown. To reduce the risk that this patient will form anti-D — placing a current or future D-positive fetus at risk for hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn — which of the following plasma-derived products should be administered to her at this visit?

options.text: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP)
Intravenous immune globulin (IVIG)
Cryoprecipitate
Rh immune globulin (RhIG)
Albumin

questions.explanation_correct: Rh immune globulin (RhIG) is the plasma-derived product specifically indicated for D-negative, non-alloimmunized mothers to prevent alloimmunization to the D antigen. In this vignette the patient is D-negative with a negative antibody screen and an unknown-D-type partner/fetus — precisely the setting in which anti-D immunoprophylaxis is used to prevent maternal anti-D formation and the resulting risk of hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn in this or a future pregnancy.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The source material explicitly identifies RhIg as indicated for Rh-negative mothers to prevent alloimmunization to the D antigen, matching this patient's D-negative, non-sensitized status.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Albumin is a plasma-derived product, but the sources place it as a replacement-fluid option in therapeutic apheresis (contrasted with plasma) — it carries no anti-D antibody and has no role in preventing maternal D alloimmunization.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The sources describe plasma as an apheresis replacement fluid (e.g., in therapeutic plasma exchange for TTP, where the procedure works by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors), not as a source of anti-D antibody for immunoprophylaxis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Cryoprecipitate is not discussed in the source material as an antibody-containing or immunoprophylactic product; the sources single out RhIg — not other plasma derivatives — for prevention of D alloimmunization.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. IVIG is also a plasma-derived immune globulin product, which makes it a tempting distractor, but the sources specifically identify RhIg (targeted anti-D immune globulin), not generic/polyvalent IVIG, as indicated for preventing maternal anti-D alloimmunization.

questions.teaching_point: Among plasma derivatives, Rh immune globulin (RhIG) is the product specifically indicated to prevent maternal anti-D alloimmunization in D-negative, non-sensitized pregnant patients; other plasma-derived products (albumin, FFP, cryoprecipitate, IVIG) serve different roles (e.g., apheresis replacement fluid, generic immune globulin) and are not substitutes for this indication.

source_references.locator: Domain VII – Obstetric and Pediatric Patients, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that RhIg is indicated for Rh-negative mothers to prevent alloimmunization to the D antigen (Domain VII: Obstetric and Pediatric Patients).

source_references.locator: Domain IV – Apheresis, Major Concepts and High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Identifies albumin vs. plasma as replacement-fluid choices in therapeutic apheresis, and notes TPE treats TTP by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors, supporting why albumin/FFP are not immunoprophylactic agents.

review.notes: Core fact for the keyed answer is verbatim and correctly cited: SRC-010 line 101, "RhIg is indicated for Rh-negative mothers to prevent alloimmunization to the D antigen" (Domain VII, High-Yield Facts). The Apheresis citation (Domain IV, line 55/59: Albumin vs. Plasma replacement fluid; TPE removes ADAMTS13 inhibitors in TTP) is also accurately quoted. However: (1) Cryoprecipitate, FFP, and IVIG are not mentioned anywhere in any of the four approved sources (confirmed by exhaustive grep) — the distractor rationales for C/D/E assert specific facts ("IVIG is generic/polyvalent," "albumin carries

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man on chronic warfarin therapy for atrial fibrillation is brought to the emergency department after a fall. Noncontrast head CT reveals an acute intracranial hemorrhage, and his INR is markedly elevated. The team wants to reverse his anticoagulation as rapidly as possible while avoiding a large infusion volume in this actively bleeding patient. Which product is the treatment of choice for this urgent reversal?

options.text: Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC)
Recombinant factor VIIa
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP)
Cryoprecipitate
Fibrinogen concentrate

questions.explanation_correct: PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin in bleeding patients. It supplies concentrated vitamin K-dependent factors and, compared with FFP, corrects the INR faster while requiring a much smaller infusion volume — both important considerations in an actively bleeding patient with intracranial hemorrhage who should not receive a large fluid load.

option_explanations.A: Correct: sources identify PCC as the treatment of choice for urgent VKA/warfarin reversal, preferred over FFP because of lower volume and faster INR normalization.
option_explanations.C: FFP also contains vitamin K-dependent factors and was historically used for warfarin reversal, but the sources explicitly state PCC is preferred over FFP for urgent reversal due to FFP's larger volume and slower INR correction.
option_explanations.D: Cryoprecipitate is used to replace fibrinogen in consumptive coagulopathies such as DIC; the sources do not describe it as a treatment for vitamin K antagonist reversal.
option_explanations.B: Recombinant factor VIIa is described as a bypassing agent for high-titer factor VIII inhibitors, a different clinical scenario from vitamin K antagonist reversal.
option_explanations.E: Fibrinogen concentrate is described as a low-volume, pathogen-reduced alternative to cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen replacement, not as an agent for reversing vitamin K antagonist effect.

source_references.locator: Node 39, Key Products
source_references.supports: States PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists like warfarin in bleeding patients

source_references.locator: Node 47, Management Logic
source_references.supports: States PCC is preferred over FFP for urgent warfarin reversal because of lower volume and faster INR normalization

source_references.locator: Node 47, Management Logic
source_references.supports: Identifies recombinant Factor VIIa (with PCC) as a bypassing agent for high-titer Factor VIII inhibitors, a distinct indication from VKA reversal

source_references.locator: Node 47, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Describes DIC as requiring replacement with platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate, establishing cryoprecipitate's role in consumptive coagulopathy rather than VKA reversal

source_references.locator: Node 39, Key Products
source_references.supports: Describes fibrinogen concentrate as a low-volume alternative to cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen replacement

review.notes:
All asserted facts are directly and explicitly grounded in the cited sources, verified independently by reading the raw files.

SRC-051 (specialized-transfusion-medicine-and-clinical-quality-systems.md), Node 39 "Derivatives & Recombinant Proteins," subsection "Key Products" (line 93): "PCC (Prothrombin Complex Concentrate): Treatment of choice for the urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin in bleeding patients" — verbatim support for the keyed answer and explanation_correct.

Same node/subsection (line 95): "Fibrinogen Concentrate: A low-volume, pathogen-reduced alterna

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is scheduled for an elective bedside paracentesis. He is hemodynamically stable, has no history of a bleeding disorder, and is not on anticoagulant therapy. Preprocedure coagulation testing shows an INR of 1.4, with no active bleeding and no signs of ongoing hemorrhage or consumptive coagulopathy. The proceduralist orders 2 units of fresh frozen plasma (FFP) to be transfused immediately before the procedure to "correct" the INR. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation from the transfusion medicine service?

options.text: Approve the FFP order, dosed at 10-15 mL/kg to raise coagulation factor levels to at least 30% of normal before the procedure

Advise against plasma transfusion; a mildly elevated INR in a stable, non-bleeding patient before a low-risk procedure is not an appropriate indication and is unlikely to meaningfully improve hemostasis
Substitute cryoprecipitate for the FFP order, since it is a more concentrated coagulation factor product and will correct the INR more efficiently
Approve platelet transfusion in addition to the FFP, since the elevated INR suggests coexisting thrombocytopenia that must also be corrected before the procedure
Approve the FFP order, but administer it as a slow infusion to also provide volume expansion and nutritional support while further coagulation workup is pending

questions.explanation_correct: Transfusing plasma to "correct" a mildly elevated INR (roughly 1.3-1.6) in a patient who is not bleeding and is undergoing a low-risk procedure is a recognized board trap: this practice is usually ineffective at improving hemostasis and is not recommended. Plasma has a legitimate, source-supported dosing rule (10-15 mL/kg to reach ≥30% factor activity), but that rule applies when there is an actual indication for plasma, such as active bleeding or a substantially deranged coagulation profile (e.g., the higher INR/aPTT thresholds used to trigger plasma during massive transfusion) - not simply because a preprocedure INR is marginally abnormal in an otherwise stable, non-bleeding patient. The best course is to advise against transfusion in this scenario.

option_explanations.B: Correct: sources explicitly identify preprocedure plasma transfusion for a mildly elevated INR (about 1.3-1.6) in a non-bleeding patient as usually ineffective and not recommended.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the 10-15 mL/kg dosing target (aiming for ≥30% factor levels) is a real plasma-dosing principle, but it presumes a valid indication for plasma exists; applying it to a stable, non-bleeding patient with only mildly elevated INR is the classic distractor trap.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: cryoprecipitate is a concentrated source of fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor, not a general INR-correcting product, and has no indicated role in correcting an isolated mildly elevated INR.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: INR reflects prolongation of the PT-based coagulation cascade, not platelet count or function; nothing in the vignette suggests thrombocytopenia, so platelet transfusion is not indicated here.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: sources explicitly state plasma should never be used for simple volume expansion or as a nutritional supplement, making this an inappropriate use regardless of the INR question.

questions.teaching_point: Plasma transfusion before a low-risk procedure to correct a mildly elevated INR (roughly 1.3-1.6) in a stable, non-bleeding patient is a classic board trap - it is generally ineffective and not recommended; correct application of plasma dosing principles requires a genuine indication such as active bleeding or a substantially deranged coagulation profile.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors - INR Distractor
source_references.supports: States that transfusing plasma to correct a mildly elevated INR (about 1.3-1.6) in a non-bleeding patient before a procedure is usually ineffective and not recommended (the 'INR Distractor').

source_references.locator: Management - Plasma Dosing
source_references.supports: Gives the general plasma dosing principle: roughly 10-15 mL/kg, aimed at achieving factor levels ≥30% of normal.

source_references.locator: Definitions - Cryoprecipitate
source_references.supports: Describes cryoprecipitate as a concentrated source of fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors - Volume Expansion Distractor
source_references.supports: States plasma should never be used for simple volume expansion or as a nutritional supplement.

source_references.locator: Massive Transfusion Protocols - Diagnostic and Management Approach - Laboratory Triggers
source_references.supports: Shows that in an actively bleeding/massive transfusion context, plasma is triggered by a substantially elevated INR (>1.5) or aPTT (>60 sec), contrasting with a marginally elevated, non-bleeding preprocedure INR.

review.notes:
Verified against all four sources directly.

Stem's INR of 1.4 falls squarely inside the cited 1.3-1.6 range.
- "Plasma Dosing: Generally 10-15 mL/kg, aimed at achieving factor levels ≥30% of no

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man in the surgical ICU with septic shock and multi-organ dysfunction develops diffuse oozing from vascular access sites, petechiae, and hematuria. Coagulation studies show a prolonged PT/INR and aPTT with a low fibrinogen level and thrombocytopenia, a pattern consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) from ongoing pathologic activation of both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems. He continues to have clinically significant bleeding. Which of the following is most appropriate to specifically replace the broad range of coagulation factors being consumed in this process?

options.text: Cryoprecipitate
Vitamin K
5% Albumin
Fresh frozen plasma
Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC)

questions.explanation_correct: Fresh frozen plasma contains the full complement of coagulation factors and is the component that provides broad-spectrum factor replacement. In DIC, both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically activated, consuming multiple clotting factors; the sources specify that management of this consumption coagulopathy requires replacement with platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate. Among the listed options, plasma is the product that most directly supplies the range of consumed coagulation factors in a critically ill, actively bleeding ICU patient with this laboratory picture.

option_explanations.D: Correct: plasma is explicitly listed as a required replacement component (along with platelets and cryoprecipitate) for the consumption coagulopathy of DIC, and it supplies the broad range of coagulation factors being consumed.
option_explanations.E: Distractor/board trap: PCC is described as preferred over FFP specifically for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonist (e.g., warfarin) anticoagulation because of its lower volume and faster INR correction — it is not the source-described therapy for the multifactorial consumptive coagulopathy of DIC.
option_explanations.A: Distractor: cryoprecipitate is also part of DIC management per the source, but it is a concentrated source of fibrinogen (plus factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, and XIII) rather than the broad-spectrum coagulation factor replacement that plasma provides.
option_explanations.C: Distractor: the sources discuss albumin as an apheresis replacement fluid alternative to plasma (e.g., in therapeutic plasma exchange), not as a product used to replace consumed coagulation factors in DIC.
option_explanations.B: Distractor: vitamin K addresses the nonfunctional (PIVKA) factors II, VII, IX, and X seen in vitamin K deficiency, a distinct mechanism from the consumptive coagulopathy of DIC, and would not acutely replace factors already consumed.

questions.teaching_point: In DIC, the consumptive coagulopathy requires broad-spectrum coagulation factor replacement with plasma (alongside platelets and cryoprecipitate as needed), which is a distinct indication from targeted anticoagulant-reversal products like PCC (preferred for urgent warfarin reversal) or single-factor-focused products like cryoprecipitate.

source_references.locator: advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md, Subdomain Node 47 - Coagulation Defects & Management
source_references.supports: States that DIC is a consumption coagulopathy (procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems pathologically active) requiring replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class C) is scheduled for a bedside paracentesis. His INR is markedly prolonged, and the hepatology team consults the blood bank physician about whether fresh frozen plasma should be given to correct the coagulopathy before the procedure. Which of the following best characterizes the pathophysiology of the coagulation defect in chronic liver disease that should inform this discussion?

options.text: Factor VIII declines in parallel with the vitamin K–dependent factors because all clotting factors, including Factor VIII, are synthesized exclusively by hepatocytes
Hepatic synthesis of nearly all coagulation factors is decreased, Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor are relatively preserved, and clearance of activated clotting factors is also impaired
The defect is limited to impaired clearance of activated clotting factors; hepatic synthetic capacity for coagulation factors remains normal
Factors II, VII, IX, and X are preserved in liver disease because their synthesis does not depend on hepatic function
The defect is limited to decreased synthesis of coagulation factors; clearance of activated clotting factors remains normal

questions.explanation_correct: In chronic liver disease, the acquired coagulopathy reflects two documented mechanisms: decreased hepatic synthesis of nearly all clotting factors (with Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor characteristically spared) and impaired clearance of activated clotting factors. Recognizing that Factor VIII remains relatively normal despite a prolonged PT/INR is a key point when evaluating whether plasma transfusion is appropriate in liver disease.

option_explanations.B: Correct: liver disease decreases synthesis of nearly all clotting factors while sparing Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, and also impairs clearance of activated clotting factors — both mechanisms are described together for liver disease coagulopathy.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: Factor VIII is specifically excepted from the synthetic defect of liver disease and does not fall in parallel with the other factors; it is not produced exclusively by hepatocytes.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this captures only the clearance abnormality and wrongly claims synthesis is normal, but decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors is also part of the documented mechanism.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this captures only the synthetic defect and wrongly claims clearance is normal, but impaired clearance of activated clotting factors is described as an additional, distinct mechanism.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: Factors II, VII, IX, and X fall under the 'nearly all clotting factors' whose hepatic synthesis is decreased in liver disease; only Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor are described as relatively spared.

questions.teaching_point: Chronic liver disease coagulopathy results from decreased hepatic synthesis of nearly all clotting factors (Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor are characteristically spared) combined with impaired clearance of activated clotting factors — a two-mechanism picture that distinguishes it from simple factor deficiency and informs rational plasma transfusion decisions.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Hemostatic Disorders & Coagulation Management, Core Concepts, 'Liver Disease' bullet

source_references.supports: Describes Vitamin K deficiency (producing nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, X) as a distinct mechanism from liver disease's synthetic defect, supporting that these factors are among those affected by decreased hepatic synthesis in liver disease.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man receives multiple units of RBCs during emergency surgery. Four months later he develops jaundice, dark urine, and malaise, prompting an evaluation for transfusion-transmitted viral hepatitis. Which of the following statements regarding posttransfusion hepatitis and current donor infectious-disease screening is correct?

options.text: A repeatedly reactive HBsAg result always indicates true chronic HBV infection and mandates permanent donor deferral.
The NAT window period for HBV is shorter than the window periods for HIV and HCV.
HCV is the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis.
Federal law mandates recipient look-back and notification for HBV in the same way it does for HIV and HCV.
Nucleic acid testing (NAT) has completely eliminated the infectious window period for HBV, HCV, and HIV.

questions.explanation_correct: The approved source material explicitly states that among the causes of posttransfusion hepatitis (which presents with jaundice, dark urine, and malaise), HCV is the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis. This makes option A the only fully supported, correct statement among the choices.

option_explanations.C: Correct. The source directly states HCV is the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis, consistent with the clinical picture described.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. NAT significantly narrows but does not eliminate the window period — it reduces (not abolishes) the HIV window from about 22 to about 9 days and the HCV window from about 82 to about 7 days; an infectious window period still remains for all three viruses.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect and a classic board trap called out explicitly in the source: HBV has a significantly longer NAT window period (about 18-26 days) compared to HIV and HCV, not a shorter one.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Donors can test transiently HBsAg positive for up to 28 days after receiving a hepatitis B vaccination, a non-infectious cause of reactivity; repeatedly reactive screening results require supplemental/differentiating testing rather than being automatically treated as true chronic infection.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Per the source, only HIV and HCV look-back and recipient notification are legally mandated under federal regulation (21 CFR 610.46/47); look-back for other agents, including HBV, is governed by professional/AABB guidance rather than federal law.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations - Posttransfusion Hepatitis
source_references.supports: States HCV is the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis, presenting with jaundice, dark urine, and malaise.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings - NAT Sensitivity; Common Board Traps - Window Period Length
source_references.supports: NAT reduces (does not eliminate) HIV window to ~9 days and HCV window to ~7 days; HBV NAT window is significantly longer (~18-26 days) than HIV/HCV — an explicit board trap.

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls - HBV NAT Vaccination Interference
source_references.supports: Donors may test transiently HBsAg positive for up to 28 days after hepatitis B vaccination (false positive, not true infection).

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps - Mandatory vs. Recommended Look-back; Management - Mandatory Recipient Notification
source_references.supports: Only HIV and HCV look-back/notification is legally mandated (21 CFR 610.46/47); other agents including HBV are governed by professional guidelines, not federal law.

source_references.locator: Domain VI - High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Confirms NAT has significantly reduced (narrowed) the window period for HIV, HCV, and HBV, supporting that NAT narrows rather than eliminates the window.

- "NAT... significantly narrowing the preseroconversion window" and "Reduces the HIV window period from ~22 days to ~9 days and HCV from ~82 days to ~7 days" — supports B's falsity (narrows, does not eliminate) and the ~9/~7

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents to donate whole blood. He reports receiving a hepatitis B vaccine booster 10 days earlier and denies any other risk factors on the donor history questionnaire. Mandatory infectious disease screening (including HBsAg) is performed on the donation. The HBsAg screening test is reactive, and repeat testing of the original sample is also reactive ("repeatedly reactive"). The unit is quarantined pending further workup. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in determining this donor's eligibility and the disposition of the unit?

options.text: Initiate mandatory recipient look-back notification for hepatitis B, since federal regulations require look-back for HBV in the same manner as for HIV and HCV
Disregard the reactive screening result and release the unit for transfusion, since recent hepatitis B vaccination is a known cause of transient false-positive HBsAg results
Permanently defer the donor as a confirmed hepatitis B carrier based on the repeatedly reactive HBsAg screening result alone
Release the donor for reentry in 6 months under the standard HBV reentry protocol, without performing additional testing on the current sample
Perform supplemental (neutralization) testing for HBsAg to distinguish a true infection from a transient reactive result before finalizing donor eligibility

questions.explanation_correct: A repeatedly reactive HBsAg screen is not by itself diagnostic of true HBV infection. The sources state that supplemental testing (neutralization, for HBsAg) is required for repeatedly reactive serologic screens to differentiate true infections from false positives. This is especially relevant here because recent HBV vaccination is a recognized cause of a transient reactive HBsAg result for up to 28 days post-vaccination — this donor is only 10 days out. Neutralization testing resolves the ambiguity and should be performed before the donor is labeled infected (and permanently deferred) or the vaccination explanation is simply accepted.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Repeatedly reactive screening results are not equivalent to confirmed infection; the sources specify that supplemental (neutralization) testing is required before a final determination and permanent deferral for a confirmed HBsAg-positive donor.
option_explanations.E: Correct. Supplemental neutralization testing for HBsAg is the source-defined step for resolving a repeatedly reactive screen, and it specifically addresses the plausible vaccine-related false-positive explanation raised by this donor's recent HBV vaccination.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Mandatory recipient look-back notification is legally required only for HIV and HCV; for other transfusion-transmitted agents, including HBV, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance rather than federally mandated.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The 6-month reentry pathway described applies to donors with an HBV NAT-reactive result who subsequently have negative follow-up HBsAg, anti-HBc, and HBV NAT testing; it is a reentry algorithm for previously deferred donors, not a substitute for the initial supplemental testing needed to resolve this donor's repeatedly reactive HBsAg screen.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. While recent vaccination can transiently cause a reactive HBsAg result, this must be confirmed by supplemental testing rather than assumed, so releasing the unit without further workup bypasses the required confirmatory step.

questions.teaching_point: A repeatedly reactive HBsAg screen requires supplemental (neutralization) testing to distinguish true infection from a false positive — including the classic board trap of transient HBsAg reactivity for up to 28 days after HBV vaccination — before permanent deferral is assigned. Mandatory federal look-back applies only to HIV and HCV, and the 6-month NAT-reactive reentry algorithm is distinct from the initial confirmatory workup of a reactive HBsAg screen.

source_references.supports: Supplemental testing (e.g., neutralization for HBsAg) is required for repeatedly reactive serologic screens to differentiate true infection from false positives.
Donors may test transiently HBsAg positive for up to 28 days after HBV vaccination.
Mandatory recipient notification (look-back) is legally required only for HIV and HCV; other agents are covered by recommended, not mandatory, guidance.

HBV NAT-reactive donors are eligible for reentry after 6 months if follow-up HBsAg, anti-HBc, and HBV NAT testing is negative, establishing that this reentry pathway is specific to NAT-reactive status.
Background context that donor testing (screening) and NAT are core layers of infectious disease safety, including for HBV.

review.notes:

2. "HBV NAT Vaccination Interference: Donors may test transiently HBsAg positive for up to 28 days after an HBV vaccination" (SRC-013 line 85) — grounds the vaccine-related distractor logic in both the stem (10 days

questions.stem: A hospital-based blood center's donor testing laboratory finds that a repeat platelet apheresis donor's current donation is repeatedly reactive for anti-HCV and HCV RNA by nucleic acid testing (NAT), and confirmatory testing establishes a true hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection. Record review shows the same donor made an allogeneic whole blood donation several weeks earlier; that unit's anti-HCV and HCV RNA results were nonreactive at the time, and the red blood cells were transfused to a hospitalized patient. Per federal regulatory requirements for infectious disease look-back, what is the correct next step regarding the previously transfused recipient?

options.text: Because current HCV NAT dramatically narrows the window period compared with antibody testing alone, the earlier nonreactive donation could not have been infectious, so recipient notification is unnecessary.

The transfusion service must initiate look-back and notify the recipient's physician, because look-back investigation and recipient notification for HCV — like HIV — are legally mandated under federal regulation, unlike most other transfusion-transmitted infections.
Notification of the recipient is recommended but not legally required for HCV, in the same way that notification for West Nile virus or T. cruzi is guidance-based rather than mandatory.

No further action toward the recipient is required, because the earlier unit was nonreactive for both anti-HCV and HCV RNA at the time of donation, so the look-back requirement applies only to the donor's future donation eligibility.
The transfusion service should defer the donor and report the case, but recipient notification should be pursued only if the recipient later develops signs of hepatitis.

questions.explanation_correct: Among the relevant transfusion-transmitted infections, HIV and HCV are the two agents for which look-back investigation and recipient notification are legally mandated by federal regulation (21 CFR 610.46/47); for other agents, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance rather than required. Look-back exists precisely to capture prior donations that tested nonreactive at the time but came from a donor later confirmed infected, because NAT narrows but does not eliminate the window period, and residual risk from window-period donations persists despite screening. Therefore, once the donor's HCV infection is confirmed, the transfusion service is required to trace and notify the recipient of the earlier unit.

option_explanations.B: Correct: HCV and HIV are singled out as the two infections for which look-back and recipient notification are legally required under federal regulation, distinguishing them from agents such as WNV or T. cruzi where notification is only recommended guidance.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this misreads the purpose of look-back, which is specifically defined as identifying and notifying recipients of components from a donor subsequently found infected with HIV or HCV — it is not limited to managing the donor's future eligibility.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this reverses the source's mandatory-versus-recommended distinction — HIV and HCV notification is the legally mandated exception, while WNV/T. cruzi-type notification is the recommended, guidance-based category.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: NAT substantially shortens the HCV window period relative to antibody testing but does not eliminate it, and residual risk from window-period donations is the very reason look-back and notification remain necessary even after a nonreactive result.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: mandatory recipient notification for a confirmed HCV-positive donor is required regardless of whether the recipient shows any signs or symptoms of hepatitis; it is not contingent on clinical presentation.

questions.teaching_point: HIV and HCV are unique among transfusion-transmitted infections in that look-back investigation and recipient notification are federally mandated (21 CFR 610.46/47) rather than merely recommended, and this obligation applies even to units that tested nonreactive at the time of donation, because NAT narrows but does not eliminate the window period and its associated residual risk.

source_references.supports: Look-back is the mandatory process of notifying recipients of components from a donor later found infected, specifically for HIV or HCV; only HIV/HCV look-back is legally required (21 CFR 610.46/47) while other agents get recommended notification; NAT narrows the HCV window period (~82 to ~7 days) but residual risk from window-period donations persists.
General corroboration that NAT has significantly reduced (but not eliminated) the window period for HIV, HCV, and HBV.

Donor reentry logic notes that HCV/HIV NAT-reactive donors with a confirmed positive result are generally not eligible for reentry, supporting the framing of permanent donor deferral in the distractor about next steps.

review.notes: All load-bearing facts are explicitly grounded in SRC-013 (blood-safety-protocols-and-infectious-disease-look-back-standards-1.md): the look-back definition naming HIV/HCV specifically (line 15), mandatory vs. recommended notification split (line 53), the 21 CFR 610.46/47 citation for the mandatory HIV/HCV exception (line 67), and the ~82-day to ~7-day HCV NAT window-period narrowing (line 43) used to refute distractor D. SRC-010 gives thin, correctly-labeled 'moderate confidence' corroboration on window-period narrowing; SRC-002's reentry note is correctly labeled 'low confidence' and not loa

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman receives two units of RBCs during elective orthopedic surgery. Six weeks later, as part of a research surveillance protocol, she is found to have a newly reactive HCV NAT result. She reports feeling entirely well, and on examination there is no scleral icterus, no dark urine reported, and no hepatomegaly. Which of the following statements about the clinical presentation of acute transfusion-transmitted hepatitis, including HCV, is most accurate and best explains this patient's findings?

options.text: Acute HCV infection typically presents with headache, myalgia, and rash, with progression to meningitis or encephalitis.
Acute HCV infection produces malaria-like symptoms, including hemolytic anemia, after an incubation period of one to eight weeks.
Acute HCV infection is invariably symptomatic, with jaundice, dark urine, and hepatomegaly appearing within days of exposure.
Acute HCV infection characteristically produces a localized nodule ("chagoma") at the inoculation site followed by myocarditis.
Acute viral hepatitis can range from entirely asymptomatic to fulminant liver failure, so a normal examination does not exclude infection.

questions.explanation_correct: The source material's clinical presentation summary for transfusion-transmitted hepatitis states it "can range from asymptomatic to fulminant liver failure," with jaundice, dark urine, and hepatomegaly being the findings when disease is symptomatic — not a mandatory feature. This patient's positive NAT with an entirely unremarkable exam is consistent with the well-documented asymptomatic end of that spectrum, so a benign physical exam cannot be used to exclude acute HCV infection.

option_explanations.E: Correct: matches the source's explicit statement that hepatitis presentation spans a spectrum from asymptomatic to fulminant liver failure, accounting for this patient's lack of symptoms despite a reactive NAT.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this contradicts the source, which states hepatitis "can range from asymptomatic" — it is not invariably symptomatic, and this patient's normal exam illustrates that.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the chagoma-and-myocarditis pattern is the source's description of Chagas disease (T. cruzi), a distinct transfusion-transmitted pathogen, not HCV.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: headache, myalgia, rash, and progression to neuroinvasive disease (meningitis/encephalitis) is the source's description of West Nile Virus, not HCV.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: malaria-like symptoms with hemolytic anemia and a 1-8 week incubation is the source's description of Babesiosis, not HCV.

questions.teaching_point: Acute transfusion-transmitted hepatitis, including HCV, spans a spectrum from entirely asymptomatic infection to fulminant liver failure; jaundice, dark urine, and hepatomegaly are the findings only when disease is symptomatic. A normal physical exam therefore does not exclude acute HCV infection, and its presentation should be distinguished from the more organ/system-specific presentations of other transfusion-transmitted agents (WNV, Babesiosis, Chagas disease).

questions.stem:
A 61-year-old man received two units of RBCs three years ago during emergency abdominal surgery. He has no history of solid organ or stem cell transplantation, malignancy, HIV infection, or any other immunosuppressing condition, and he takes no immunosuppressive medications. Over the past several months he has had intermittent fatigue and episodes of dark urine. On examination he has mild scleral icterus, and laboratory testing shows persistently elevated liver transaminases. He denies recent travel, new medications, alcohol use, or insect bites.

Which of the following transfusion-transmitted agents is the most likely cause of this patient's chronic hepatitis?

options.text: Human T-lymphotropic virus type I (HTLV-I)
Hepatitis B virus (HBV)
Hepatitis C virus (HCV)
West Nile virus (WNV)
Hepatitis E virus (HEV)

questions.explanation_correct: Among transfusion-transmitted viruses, HCV is specifically identified as the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis, which presents with jaundice, dark urine, and malaise — consistent with the broader hepatitis presentation of jaundice, dark urine, and hepatomegaly ranging from asymptomatic infection to fulminant liver failure. This patient's persistent transaminase elevation, jaundice, and dark urine after a transfusion, occurring in an immunocompetent host (arguing against chronic HEV, which is described in immunosuppressed patients) and without features pointing to other transfusion-transmitted agents (neuroinvasive disease for WNV, leukemia association for HTLV-I), is most consistent with chronic HCV infection.

option_explanations.B: HBV is a mandatorily screened transfusion-transmitted hepatitis virus and can cause hepatitis, but the source material specifically names HCV — not HBV — as the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis, making HBV a less likely explanation here.
option_explanations.C: Correct. HCV is explicitly identified as the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis, presenting with jaundice, dark urine, and malaise, which matches this patient's persistent findings in the absence of immunosuppression or features suggesting another agent.
option_explanations.E: HEV can cause chronic infection, but the source specifies this occurs in immunosuppressed patients (who may require ribavirin therapy). This patient has no immunosuppressing condition or medication, making chronic HEV a less likely cause.
option_explanations.A: HTLV-I is a transfusion-transmitted agent, but it is associated with adult T-cell leukemia, not hepatitis, so it does not account for this patient's jaundice, dark urine, and transaminase elevation.
option_explanations.D: WNV is transfusion-transmitted but typically causes fever, headache, myalgia, or rash, and rarely neuroinvasive disease such as meningitis or encephalitis — not hepatitis or jaundice — so it does not fit this presentation.

questions.teaching_point: HCV is specifically identified as the most common cause of chronic posttransfusion hepatitis. Other transfusion-transmitted hepatotropic viruses (HBV, HEV) and non-hepatotropic agents (WNV, HTLV-I) have distinguishing features noted in the source material that make them less likely explanations for a chronic hepatitis presentation in an immunocompetent transfusion recipient.

questions.stem: A blood center's medical director is preparing a teaching session on infectious disease testing for allogeneic whole blood donors. A resident notes that donors are still tested for anti-HIV antibody even though nucleic acid testing (NAT) is also performed, and asks why NAT was added to the screening algorithm for retroviruses such as HIV rather than relying on antibody testing alone. Which of the following best explains the rationale for adding NAT to donor screening for HIV?

options.text: NAT reduces transfusion-transmitted HIV risk by inactivating any pathogens present in the collected unit
NAT was adopted because it is the only FDA-approved method available to screen donors for malaria
Antibody-based testing for HIV is no longer performed now that NAT is available
NAT detects the virus's genetic material directly, allowing detection earlier in infection than antibody-based assays and thereby narrowing the infectious window period
Gamma irradiation of the collected component achieves the same window-period reduction as NAT

questions.explanation_correct: The approved source states that nucleic acid testing (NAT) has significantly reduced the window period for HIV (along with HCV and HBV). NAT works by detecting viral genetic material, which becomes detectable earlier after infection than the host antibody response that antibody-based assays rely on. This earlier detection is precisely what narrows the "window period" — the interval during which an infected donor can still test negative — and is why NAT was layered onto, rather than substituted for, existing antibody testing.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The source directly credits NAT with a significant reduction in the window period for HIV, consistent with NAT's ability to detect viral genetic material before antibody seroconversion occurs.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The vignette itself notes antibody testing remains part of the panel alongside NAT; the source describes NAT as reducing the window period, not as a replacement for antibody screening.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect distractor drawing on a different source fact: malaria (along with vCJD) is explicitly described as relying solely on donor questioning because no FDA-approved screening test exists for it — this is the opposite of how HIV is screened.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The source lists pathogen inactivation as a distinct major concept from testing/NAT, not as NAT's mechanism; NAT is a detection method, not a decontamination method.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect distractor conflating domains: the source describes gamma irradiation as the method used to prevent TA-GVHD by inactivating donor T lymphocytes, an unrelated hazard-prevention measure, not a tool for narrowing the infectious window period for retroviruses.

questions.teaching_point: NAT shortens the infectious window period for retroviruses like HIV by detecting viral genetic material before antibody seroconversion; this is an addition to, not a replacement for, antibody-based testing, and is distinct from unrelated hazard-control measures like donor questioning-only screening (malaria/vCJD) or gamma irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention).

source_references.locator: Domain VI: Infectious Hazards of Transfusion — High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that NAT has significantly reduced the window period for HIV, HCV, and HBV, and lists donor screening (questioning vs. testing), window periods, and pathogen inactivation as distinct major concepts in this domain.
