It also states "Malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor questioning as no FDA-approved screening tests exist" (line 91) and "TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes" (line 75, Domain V). All of these are confirmed exactly as ci

questions.stem: A 34-year-old first-time volunteer whole blood donor passes the donor history questionnaire and physical examination without issue. Mandatory infectious disease testing on the donation shows the anti-HIV-1/2 screening immunoassay is repeatedly reactive, while HIV-1 RNA NAT performed on the same sample is nonreactive. All other mandatory infectious disease screening tests are nonreactive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this donation and donor?

options.text: Initiate mandatory look-back to identify and notify recipients of this donor's prior blood donations based on the repeatedly reactive screening result
Permanently defer the donor and discard all associated components as confirmed HIV-positive based on the repeatedly reactive screening result alone
Release the red cell and plasma components for transfusion because a nonreactive HIV-1 RNA NAT result overrides the reactive antibody screening result

Perform supplemental (confirmatory) testing, such as an HIV-1-specific Western blot or another FDA-licensed supplemental assay, to distinguish a true HIV infection from a false-positive screening result
Repeat the anti-HIV-1/2 screening immunoassay in duplicate before taking any further action, since the result needs to be reproduced

questions.explanation_correct: Per the mandatory allogeneic testing algorithm, every donation is tested with both an anti-HIV-1/2 screening immunoassay and HIV-1 RNA NAT. When a screening immunoassay is repeatedly reactive, the approved sources specify that supplemental (confirmatory) testing — such as an HIV-1-specific Western blot or another FDA-licensed supplemental assay — is required to differentiate a true HIV infection from a false-positive screening result. This applies regardless of the NAT result, because supplemental testing (not NAT status alone, and not simply repeating the screening assay) is the step the source designates for resolving a repeatedly reactive serologic screen. Only after supplemental testing clarifies the donor's true infection status can decisions about component disposition, donor deferral, and (if HIV infection is confirmed) mandatory look-back and recipient notification be made.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The source explicitly states supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot for HIV) is required for repeatedly reactive serologic screens to distinguish true infection from a false positive.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. A nonreactive NAT does not override a repeatedly reactive antibody screen; both anti-HIV-1/2 and HIV-1 RNA are mandatory tests, and a reactive result on any mandatory test triggers quarantine of the product (one of the FDA's five layers of blood safety), not release.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Concluding confirmed HIV infection and permanently deferring the donor based on the screening result alone skips the required supplemental/confirmatory testing step that the source specifies for resolving repeatedly reactive screens and distinguishing true infection from false positive.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Look-back is defined as the mandatory notification process for a donor 'subsequently found to have a transmissible infection' — i.e., after confirmation — not upon an unresolved repeatedly reactive screening test.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The result is already described as repeatedly reactive; the source directs that repeatedly reactive screens require supplemental (confirmatory) testing, not further repetition of the same screening immunoassay.

questions.teaching_point: A repeatedly reactive result on a mandatory infectious disease screening immunoassay (such as anti-HIV-1/2) must be resolved with supplemental/confirmatory testing to distinguish true infection from a false positive — regardless of a concurrently nonreactive NAT result — before the unit can be finally dispositioned, the donor deferred, or mandatory look-back/recipient notification (required specifically for confirmed HIV or HCV) is initiated.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach / Laboratory Findings - Supplemental Testing
source_references.supports: States that supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot for HIV) is required for repeatedly reactive serologic screens to differentiate true infection from a false positive result.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach - Mandatory Testing (Allogeneic)
source_references.supports: Lists anti-HIV-1/2 and HIV-1 RNA as both being part of mandatory allogeneic donation testing.

source_references.locator: Definitions - Look-back
source_references.supports: Defines look-back as the mandatory notification process for a donor 'subsequently found to have a transmissible infection,' specifically HIV or HCV.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts - Five Layers of Safety
source_references.supports: Describes quarantine of products as one of the FDA's five layers of blood safety, alongside donor testing.

source_references.locator: Domain VI - High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Notes that NAT has significantly reduced the window period for HIV, HCV, and HBV, providing general background on HIV donor testing in the infectious hazards domain.

review.notes: All load-bearing facts are grounded in SRC-013 (blood-safety-protocols-and-infectious-disease-look-back-standards-1.md): mandatory testing includes both anti-HIV-1/2 and HIV-1 RNA (line 33); supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot) is required to resolve repeatedly reactive serologic screens and differentiate true infection from false positive (line 45, near-verbatim match to option A/explanation); look-back is defined as requiring a donor "subsequently found to have a transmissible infection" i.e. confirmed status, not a raw repeatedly-reactive screen (line 15, directly refutes option D); qu

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with no significant medical history presents to a community blood drive to donate a unit of whole blood for the first time. During the pre-donation health history and physical assessment, the following values are recorded: weight 125 lb (57 kg), oral temperature 37.2°C, resting pulse 104 beats/min and regular, blood pressure 118/76 mmHg, and hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL by fingerstick. Based on standard donor physical examination criteria, which of the following findings should prompt further evaluation before this donor can be accepted for whole blood donation?

options.text: Hemoglobin of 12.8 g/dL
Weight of 125 lb
Oral temperature of 37.2°C
Resting pulse of 104 beats/min
Blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg

questions.explanation_correct: Standard donor physical examination criteria specify an acceptable resting pulse range of 50-100 beats per minute, with pathologic irregularities (or, by extension, rates outside this range) requiring further physician evaluation before donation. This donor's pulse of 104 beats/min falls above the upper limit of that range, so it is the finding that should trigger additional assessment. Her weight (minimum 110 lb), temperature (must be ≤37.5°C), hemoglobin (minimum 12.5 g/dL for females), and blood pressure (systolic 90-180 mmHg, diastolic 50-100 mmHg) all fall within the standard acceptable ranges for allogeneic whole blood donation.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the minimum weight requirement for allogeneic donation is 110 lb (50 kg); at 125 lb this donor comfortably exceeds that minimum and does not require deferral on this basis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: donor temperature must be at or below 37.5°C (99.5°F) to be acceptable; 37.2°C is within the acceptable range.
option_explanations.D: Correct: the acceptable donor pulse range is 50-100 beats/min. A rate of 104 beats/min is outside this range, which is the type of finding (along with pathologic irregularities) that requires further physician evaluation before donation.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: acceptable donor blood pressure is systolic 90-180 mmHg and diastolic 50-100 mmHg; 118/76 mmHg falls within both limits and is acceptable.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the minimum hemoglobin for a female allogeneic donor is 12.5 g/dL (hematocrit ≥38%); 12.8 g/dL exceeds this threshold and is acceptable. This value is a plausible distractor because it is close to the cutoff, but it does not fail the criterion.

questions.teaching_point: Donor evaluation requires applying a specific set of physical examination acceptability ranges (weight, temperature, hemoglobin/hematocrit, pulse, blood pressure) rather than relying on gestalt impressions; a candidate must recognize which single measured parameter falls outside its defined range even when the others are all normal or near a cutoff.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 30 - Donor Selection, Deferrals, and Physicals; 'Physical Examination Requirements' list
source_references.supports: Provides the specific donor physical exam acceptability ranges: weight ≥110 lb, temperature ≤37.5°C, Hb ≥13.0 g/dL male / ≥12.5 g/dL female, pulse 50-100 bpm (pathologic irregularities require physician evaluation), and BP systolic 90-180 / diastolic 50-100 mmHg.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 30 - 'Dual Responsibility' and 'Standardized Screening' core concepts
source_references.supports: Establishes that donor selection/physical evaluation protects both donor health and recipient safety and is standardized via the AABB Donor History Questionnaire process, framing why these physical criteria are applied.

review.notes: All numeric thresholds (weight ≥110 lb, temp ≤37.5°C, Hgb female ≥12.5 g/dL, pulse 50-100 bpm, BP systolic 90-180/diastolic 50-100) are verbatim-matched to SRC-003 lines 191-201 (Subdomain Node 30, Physical Examination Requirements). The other three approved sources (SRC-028, SRC-037, SRC-032) contain no donor physical-exam content and do not contradict SRC-003 — confirmed via targeted search and full read. Stem values were independently checked against the thresholds: pulse 104 bpm is the only out-of-range finding; weight 125 lb, temp 37.2°C, Hgb 12.8 g/dL, and BP 118/76 mmHg are all within r

questions.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents for her first whole blood donation. Before phlebotomy, a collection staff member reviews the donor history questionnaire with her and explains the venipuncture procedure. She asks two questions: whether she could be injured by the needle stick, and whether she will be told if any of her infectious disease screening tests come back reactive.

Which statement most accurately describes the donor's rights and the collection facility's obligations regarding consent in this scenario?

options.text: Because donation is voluntary, obtaining the donor's signature on a general consent form fully satisfies informed consent, so no further explanation of donation-related risks is required.

The donor has the right to be informed of her testing results, and the facility has an obligation to provide a standard of care that minimizes procedural injury, such as nerve damage during phlebotomy.
Infectious disease testing results belong solely to the collection facility and are never shared with the donor, since donor testing exists only to protect the transfusion recipient.

The facility is strictly liable for any needle-related injury during phlebotomy regardless of whether accepted procedural standards were followed, since blood collection is treated as a defective-product scenario.
If the facility later fails to notify the donor of a reactive test result, that omission is best classified as battery, because it violates the terms of the original consent.

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources describe donor rights as part of the medicolegal framework of consent: donors have the right to be informed of their testing results, and facilities owe a standard of care that avoids preventable procedural injury (the example given is nerve damage during phlebotomy). This is distinct from — but related to — the broader Doctrine of Informed Consent (explaining risks, benefits, and alternatives), the tort of battery (performing a procedure with no consent at all), negligence (breach of an owed duty causing harm), and strict liability (rarely applied to blood). Option A is the only choice consistent with all of these principles as stated in the sources.

option_explanations.B: Correct. The sources state that donors have the right to be informed of their testing results and to be treated with a standard of care that prevents injury (e.g., nerve damage during phlebotomy) — both are core components of the donor consent relationship.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The Doctrine of Informed Consent requires clinicians to actually explain risks, benefits, and alternatives to the patient/donor — a signature alone on a generic form does not substitute for that explanation.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This directly contradicts the stated donor right to be informed of her own testing results; donor testing protects the recipient but does not strip the donor of the right to learn her results.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The sources specifically note that strict liability (holding a provider liable for a defective product regardless of fault) is rarely applied to blood in most states; injury claims are instead evaluated under a negligence framework requiring breach of the standard of care.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Battery, per the sources, is performing a procedure without obtaining any consent at all (an unauthorized act). A properly consented collection followed by a later failure to disclose results is instead consistent with negligence — a breach of an owed duty — not battery.

questions.teaching_point: Donor consent encompasses more than a signed form: it includes the donor's right to be informed of her own test results and the facility's duty to minimize procedural injury. Distinguish this from the related but distinct medicolegal doctrines of battery (no consent at all), negligence (breach of an owed duty causing harm), and strict liability (rarely applied to blood).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 45 – Medicolegal & Ethical Aspects: 'Confidentiality & Rights' and 'Liability Framework' sections
source_references.supports: States donors have the right to be informed of testing results and to receive care preventing injury (e.g., nerve damage during phlebotomy); defines battery, negligence, strict liability, and the Doctrine of Informed Consent (risks, benefits, alternatives).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 63 – Advanced Medicolegal Scenarios: 'Liability Frameworks' and 'The Logic of Negligence' sections
source_references.supports: Reiterates that battery is performing a procedure without any consent, that strict liability is rarely applied to blood, that informed consent requires disclosure of risks/benefits/alternatives, and lists the five elements of negligence (duty, breach, harm, causation, damages).

review.notes: Both sources contain a nearly verbatim match for the keyed answer. SRC-004 (Node 45, 'Confidentiality & Rights'): 'Donor Rights: Donors have the right to be informed of their testing results and to be treated with a standard of care that prevents injury (e.g., nerve damage during phlebotomy)' — this maps almost word-for-word onto option A. SRC-004/SRC-006 also both state Battery = performing a procedure with no consent at all, Strict Liability is 'rarely applied to blood in most states,' and Informed Consent requires explaining risks/benefits/alternatives (not just a signature) — these ground

questions.stem:
A 34-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay in hemorrhagic shock after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. The massive transfusion protocol is activated, and over the next 15 minutes he rapidly receives multiple units of red blood cells, plasma, and platelets through a rapid infuser. Shortly afterward, he develops perioral tingling, numbness of his fingertips, and shivering. His temperature is normal, he has no hypertension or jugular venous distension, he is not in respiratory distress and remains normoxic, and there is no urticaria or wheezing.

Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings, and what is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload; place the patient upright and administer diuretics
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction; stop the transfusion and give vigorous fluid resuscitation to maintain urine output
Transfusion-related acute lung injury; begin aggressive respiratory support and avoid diuretics
Citrate toxicity from rapid infusion of citrate-anticoagulated blood components; slow the transfusion rate and give calcium supplementation
Anaphylaxis; stop the transfusion and treat as a severe, multi-organ allergic reaction

questions.explanation_correct: This patient's symptom pattern-perioral paresthesias, tingling fingertips, and shivering without fever, hemodynamic instability, hypoxemia, or urticaria-is the classic presentation of citrate toxicity, which occurs when large volumes of citrate-anticoagulated blood components are infused rapidly, as in a massive transfusion protocol for hemorrhagic shock. Citrate chelates ionized calcium, producing these neuromuscular symptoms and, if untreated, tetany or arrhythmias. Management is to slow the infusion rate and provide calcium supplementation, which allows the transfusion to safely continue rather than being stopped outright.

option_explanations.D: Correct: the symptom triad of perioral tingling, fingertip numbness, and shivering matches the defined presentation of citrate toxicity, and slowing the rate plus calcium supplementation is the specified management.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: TRALI presents with sudden respiratory distress, hypoxemia, fever, and hypotension-none of which this patient has; his oxygenation and breathing are normal, arguing strongly against TRALI.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: TACO presents with dyspnea, hypertension, tachycardia, JVD, and an S3 gallop; this patient has none of these volume-overload findings, and diuretics would not address his actual symptoms.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: AHTR presents with fever, chills, flank/back pain, and hypotension, and its management centers on maintaining urine output; this patient is afebrile and hemodynamically unchanged, and his symptoms (paresthesias) are not typical of AHTR.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening reaction with respiratory and cardiovascular involvement (and typically urticaria); this patient has no such multi-organ involvement or skin findings.

questions.teaching_point: During massive transfusion for hemorrhagic shock, rapid infusion of large volumes of citrate-anticoagulated blood components can cause citrate toxicity (perioral paresthesias, tingling, shivering, and, if severe, tetany/arrhythmias) via calcium chelation; this must be distinguished from AHTR, TRALI, TACO, and anaphylaxis by its distinct symptom pattern, and is managed by slowing the transfusion and giving calcium rather than stopping the transfusion outright.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Management sections
source_references.supports: Citrate toxicity presents with perioral paresthesias, tingling of fingertips, and shivering, and is managed by slowing the infusion rate and giving calcium supplementation.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Laboratory Findings sections
source_references.supports: Citrate chelates ionized calcium causing perioral paresthesias, tingling, and shivering (severe cases: tetany/arrhythmias); managed with slowed infusion and calcium replacement.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Management sections
source_references.supports: Defines the comparison presentations used as distractors: TRALI (respiratory distress, hypoxemia, fever, hypotension; treat with respiratory support, avoid diuretics), TACO (dyspnea, hypertension, JVD, S3 gallop; treat with diuretics/upright positioning), AHTR (fever, chills, back pain, hypotension; treat with fluids to maintain urine output), and anaphylaxis (severe multi-organ allergic reaction)

review.notes:
Findings from independent read of all four sources:

1. SYMPTOM PATTERN AND MANAGEMENT (well supported): SRC-047 and SRC-023 both state, nearly verbatim, that citrate toxicity "presents with perioral paresthesias, tingling in fingertips, shivering, and potentially tetany or arrhythmias" and is "managed by slowing the infusion rate and providing calcium supplementation (oral or IV)." This matches the stem's symptom triad and option A's management exactly. The distractor rationales for B (TRALI), C (TACO), D (AHTR), and E (anaphylaxis) are all accurately sourced to SRC-045's defined presentation

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay after a high-speed motor vehicle collision with a closed pelvic fracture and suspected intra-abdominal hemorrhage. He is hypotensive, tachycardic, and has cool, mottled extremities consistent with ongoing hemorrhagic shock. As resuscitation begins, an arterial blood gas shows a markedly decreased pH consistent with lactic (metabolic) acidosis from tissue hypoperfusion. Which of the following best describes the expected effect of this acidosis on hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and its physiologic consequence for this patient?

options.text: The decreased pH shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right, lowering hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and promoting oxygen release to the hypoperfused tissues
Acidosis does not meaningfully affect hemoglobin's oxygen affinity; curve position in this setting is governed only by body temperature
The decreased pH shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the left, raising hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and impairing oxygen release to the tissues
The physiologic effect described is attributable to the storage lesion of transfused RBC units, which depletes 2,3-DPG and shifts the curve left, impairing oxygen delivery
The acidosis acts primarily by depleting intracellular 2,3-DPG, producing a leftward shift that reduces oxygen unloading at the tissue level

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources list decreased pH (acidosis) alongside increased 2,3-DPG, increased CO2, and increased temperature as established causes of a rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve. A right shift lowers hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen and facilitates oxygen release to the tissues — an adaptive, compensatory response that helps deliver oxygen to hypoperfused tissue during hemorrhagic shock.

option_explanations.A: Correct: per the sources, decreased pH (acidosis) is a direct cause of a right shift, which lowers oxygen affinity and increases oxygen release to tissues, matching the compensatory need in hemorrhagic shock.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: reverses the direction. The sources associate a left shift with increased pH (alkalosis), not decreased pH, and a left shift raises oxygen affinity while impairing (not facilitating) tissue oxygen release — the opposite of the adaptive response needed here.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: conflates two mechanisms the sources list separately. Decreased pH causes a right shift directly; decreased 2,3-DPG is a distinct, separately listed cause of a left shift (and is specifically described as a feature of stored blood), not the mechanism driving this patient's acute lactic acidosis.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources explicitly list pH, along with 2,3-DPG, CO2, and temperature, as determinants of oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve position, so pH is not irrelevant to hemoglobin's oxygen affinity.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes the storage lesion of transfused stored RBC units (decreased 2,3-DPG causing a left shift, described in the sources as common in stored blood), which is a distinct process from the patient's own systemic lactic acidosis. The patient's endogenous acidosis causes a right shift, not a left shift, so attributing the described physiology to the storage lesion is a mismatch.

questions.teaching_point: In hemorrhagic shock, systemic lactic acidosis (decreased pH) is one of the recognized drivers of a rightward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve, an adaptive mechanism that lowers hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and enhances peripheral oxygen unloading. This is mechanistically distinct from the leftward shift caused by 2,3-DPG depletion in stored RBC units (the storage lesion) — a common board trap is conflating the two acid-base/shift phenomena.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 46 – Oxygen Transport & Adaptations, 'Mechanics of the Shift' section
source_references.supports: States that decreased pH (acidosis) causes a right shift facilitating oxygen release, while decreased 2,3-DPG (common in stored blood) and increased pH (alkalosis) cause a left shift that impairs oxygen delivery.

source_references.locator: 'Clinical Presentations' and 'High-Yield Comparison Points — Left Shift Causes' sections
source_references.supports: Confirms right shift occurs with low pH (hypoxia/acidosis) and releases oxygen more easily to tissues; lists decreased 2,3-DPG, decreased temperature, and increased pH (alkalosis) as left shift causes.

review.notes: The tested science is fully and almost verbatim grounded: SRC-002 (Node 46, 'Mechanics of the Shift') states decreased pH (acidosis) causes a right shift that 'facilitates oxygen release to the tissues,' while decreased 2,3-DPG (explicitly flagged as 'common in stored blood'), decreased CO2, decreased temperature, or increased pH (alkalosis) cause a left shift that 'potentially impair[s] tissue delivery.' SRC-005 corroborates this in 'Clinical Presentations' and 'High-Yield Comparison Points.' This cleanly validates option A as correct and B, C, D, E as each identifiably wrong for the reasons

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay in hemorrhagic shock after a high-speed motor vehicle collision and is placed on a massive transfusion protocol. Over the next 45 minutes he rapidly receives 8 units of RBCs, 8 units of plasma, and 1 apheresis platelet unit through peripheral IV lines without an inline blood warmer. His core temperature falls to 34.2°C, and the surgical team notes new, diffuse oozing from all wound edges and IV insertion sites. Repeat labs show a platelet count of 140,000/µL, fibrinogen 220 mg/dL, and a normal ionized calcium. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's new bleeding?

options.text: Citrate-induced hypocalcemia impairing the coagulation cascade
Dilutional coagulopathy from crystalloid-predominant resuscitation
Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction
Hyperkalemia-induced cardiac dysfunction
Hypothermia impairing platelet function and coagulation enzyme activity

questions.explanation_correct: The patient's core temperature of 34.2°C is below the 35°C threshold that defines transfusion-associated hypothermia, produced by rapid infusion of refrigerated blood components without a warmer. Hypothermia exacerbates coagulopathy because cold temperatures inhibit platelet function and slow the enzymatic reactions of the clotting cascade, causing diffuse, nonsurgical oozing even though the platelet count and fibrinogen are not critically depleted. This is the classic physical/metabolic complication of unwarmed rapid massive transfusion in trauma resuscitation.

option_explanations.B: Dilutional coagulopathy results from replacing lost whole blood mainly with RBCs and crystalloid; this patient instead received a balanced 1:1:1 RBC:plasma:platelet ratio, the strategy specifically used to prevent dilutional coagulopathy, and his platelet count/fibrinogen are not depleted, so this is less likely than hypothermia.
option_explanations.A: Citrate toxicity causes hypocalcemia (citrate chelates calcium) with perioral paresthesias, tetany, or arrhythmias, but the stem gives a normal ionized calcium — the correct assay for citrate toxicity — which excludes this mechanism.
option_explanations.E: Correct: rapid transfusion of unwarmed refrigerated components dropped the core temperature below 35°C, and cold temperature directly inhibits platelet function and the enzymatic activity of the coagulation cascade, producing the diffuse oozing described.
option_explanations.D: Hyperkalemia arises from potassium leakage from stored RBCs and presents with peaked T waves and cardiac arrest, not diffuse bleeding, so it does not explain this patient's oozing.
option_explanations.C: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction is an anamnestic antibody response occurring 2 to 14 days after transfusion with a hemoglobin drop, jaundice, and fever — not immediate intraoperative oozing during active massive transfusion.

questions.teaching_point: Transfusion-associated hypothermia (core temp <35°C) from rapid infusion of unwarmed refrigerated blood components impairs platelet function and slows coagulation-enzyme kinetics, producing a functional coagulopathy distinct from citrate-induced hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, dilutional coagulopathy, and delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction; blood warmers are indicated for rapid, large-volume transfusion to prevent this complication.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 34, Definitions/Clinical Presentations/Management
source_references.supports: Defines transfusion hypothermia as core temp <35°C from rapid refrigerated component infusion; states cold temperatures inhibit platelet function and clotting-cascade enzymatic activity, worsening coagulopathy; blood warmers mandatory for rapid large-volume infusion.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16, Core Concepts/Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Massive transfusion protocol context: hypothermia from refrigerated components causes arrhythmias and exacerbates coagulopathy via reduced enzymatic activity; defines dilutional coagulopathy and the 1:1:1 balanced ratio used to prevent it; describes citrate toxicity (hypocalcemia) and hyperkalemia presentations as distinct entities.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 33, Core Concepts/Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Defines delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction timing (2-14 days post-transfusion) and presentation (hemoglobin drop, jaundice, fever), used to exclude it as the cause of immediate intraoperative oozing.

review.notes: All stem premises, the keyed answer, and all five option rationales are fully and precisely grounded in the three approved sources. SRC-060 (Node 34) and SRC-032 (Node 16) both state verbatim that hypothermia (<35°C, from rapid unwarmed refrigerated-component infusion) "inhibits platelet function and the enzymatic activity of the clotting cascade" / "exacerbate[s] coagulopathy by reducing enzymatic activity" — an exact match to the correct-answer mechanism. Distractor eliminations are each independently sourced: dilutional coagulopathy (A) is defined in SRC-032 as arising from RBC+crystalloid-

questions.stem:
A hospital's transfusion-reaction investigation committee is completing its hemovigilance report to the NHSN Biovigilance Module for a case of new-onset dyspnea and radiographic pulmonary edema that developed shortly after a red cell transfusion in a patient with known underlying cardiac disease. On review, the case fulfills several, but not all, of the standardized case-definition criteria for transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO). Weighing the patient's cardiac history against the close temporal relationship to the transfusion, the committee concludes that transfusion is the most likely, though not certain, precipitant of the event.

Using the NHSN imputability scale applied to hemovigilance reporting, how should this reaction be classified?

options.text: Near-miss event
Possible
Definitive
Probable
Doubtful/Ruled Out

questions.explanation_correct: The NHSN Biovigilance Module imputability scale grades how likely it is that a reported reaction was actually caused by the transfusion. "Probable" is defined as a reaction that meets some, but not all, of the case-definition criteria, where transfusion is judged the most likely cause. That is exactly the committee's conclusion here: several (not all) TACO criteria are met, and after weighing the clinical picture, transfusion is deemed the most likely precipitant. This is distinct from "Definitive" (all criteria fulfilled), "Possible" (transfusion cannot be excluded but another cause is equally likely), and "Doubtful/Ruled Out" (evidence favors another cause) — and distinct from "near-miss event," which is a separate hemovigilance classification for occurrences that did not actually cause harm.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect. "Definitive" imputability requires that the reaction fulfill all case-definition criteria; this case meets only some of the standardized TACO criteria, so it cannot be called definitive.
option_explanations.D: Correct. "Probable" applies when a reaction meets some but not all case-definition criteria and transfusion is judged the most likely cause — precisely the committee's conclusion in this vignette.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. "Possible" is reserved for reactions where transfusion cannot be ruled out but another cause is considered equally likely; here the committee specifically judged transfusion to be the most likely cause, which is a stronger determination than "possible."
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. "Doubtful/Ruled Out" requires evidence that clearly favors or proves a cause other than transfusion. Nothing in the vignette points to a competing etiology outweighing transfusion as the cause.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. A near-miss event is a distinct hemovigilance classification for an unexpected occurrence that did not result in harm but could have. This patient had an actual adverse reaction (dyspnea, pulmonary edema), so the event is a completed reaction being graded on the imputability scale, not a near-miss.

questions.teaching_point: The NHSN Biovigilance Module's imputability scale (Definitive, Probable, Possible, Doubtful/Ruled Out) standardizes how likely a reported adverse reaction is attributable to transfusion, and is a separate axis from event-type classifications (incident, nonconformance, near-miss, unanticipated event) used elsewhere in hemovigilance surveillance. Board questions can test whether learners correctly apply imputability criteria to a scenario rather than confusing it with severity grading or event-type terminology.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 18 – Hemovigilance & Adverse Event Monitoring, 'NHSN Imputability Designations'
source_references.supports: Defines the NHSN imputability designations used in hemovigilance reporting: Definitive (all criteria met), Probable (some criteria met, transfusion most likely cause), Possible (transfusion not ruled out, other causes likely), Doubtful/Ruled Out (evidence favors another cause).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 18, 'Adverse Event Classifications'
source_references.supports: Defines Near-Miss Event as an unexpected occurrence that did not result in harm but could have, distinguishing it from a completed/graded adverse reaction.

source_references.locator: Prevention section, 'Hemovigilance'
source_references.supports: Confirms that the NHSN Biovigilance Module is the surveillance system used to standardize hemovigilance data collection for transfusion reactions.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 clinical section 'TRALI vs. TACO'; Appendix glossary
source_references.supports: Provides the TRALI (inflammatory, noncardiogenic) vs. TACO (hydrostatic, volume overload) distinction used to frame the clinical scenario, and defines near-miss event and biovigilance in its glossary.

review.notes:
All four sources were read in full. SRC-032 (essentials-of-transfusion-medicine...md, Subdomain Node 18 "Hemovigilance & Adverse Event Monitoring") contains a dedicated "NHSN Imputability Designations" subsection with wording that maps almost verbatim onto the stem and answer options:
- Definitive: "fulfills all case-definition criteria"
- Probable: "meets some criteria and transfusion is the most likely cause"
- Possible: "Transfusion cannot be ruled out, but other potential causes are likely"
- Doubtful/Ruled Out: "Evidence clearly favors or conclusively proves a cause other than transfusion

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man who takes clopidogrel after placement of a coronary stent is being evaluated for persistent oozing from a minor skin puncture site made during line placement. His platelet count is within the normal reference range. He has not received any platelet transfusions, and there is no clinical evidence of sepsis, splenomegaly, or disseminated intravascular coagulation. Which of the following best explains why his normal platelet count does not ensure adequate hemostasis?

options.text: His fibrinogen level has most likely fallen below the threshold associated with diffuse microvascular bleeding
Subclinical disseminated intravascular coagulation is nonimmune-mediated consumption of his platelets
Immune-mediated platelet refractoriness from HLA antibodies is impairing his platelet count increment
Platelet-mediated hemostasis depends on both platelet quantity and platelet quality (function); clopidogrel, a P2Y12 inhibitor, impairs platelet function even with a normal platelet count
Splenic sequestration is reducing his circulating platelet mass

questions.explanation_correct: Platelets form the initial physical plug at a site of vascular injury (primary hemostasis), and the source material specifies that platelet hemostatic efficacy depends on both quantity (count) and quality (function) — a normal count does not by itself guarantee adequate hemostasis. Clopidogrel is explicitly identified as a P2Y12 inhibitor that must be discontinued before elective surgery as part of "optimizing coagulation," reflecting its role in impairing platelet function. In this patient, a normal platelet count with ongoing oozing, no transfusion history, and no clinical picture of sepsis, DIC, or splenomegaly is best explained by drug-induced qualitative platelet dysfunction rather than a quantitative platelet or coagulation-factor problem.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The sources tie platelet hemostatic efficacy to both count and function, and separately identify P2Y12 inhibitors such as clopidogrel as agents that impair coagulation/hemostasis and must be stopped before elective surgery — together these explain bleeding despite a normal count.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Immune-mediated (HLA/HPA antibody) refractoriness describes an inadequate posttransfusion platelet count increment in previously transfused/alloimmunized patients; this patient has not received a platelet transfusion, so refractoriness does not apply.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Hypofibrinogenemia causes diffuse microvascular bleeding only once fibrinogen falls below the 100-150 mg/dL range; there is no fibrinogen data given, and this describes a distinct coagulation-factor mechanism rather than the antiplatelet-drug effect suggested by the vignette.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. DIC is listed as a nonimmune cause of poor platelet count increments (consumption), but it is typically accompanied by a low platelet count and a systemic clinical picture, neither of which is present here.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Splenomegaly/splenic sequestration is listed as a nonimmune cause of poor platelet increments and would be expected to lower the circulating platelet count, but this patient's count is normal.

questions.teaching_point: A normal platelet count reflects only the quantitative side of platelet-mediated (primary) hemostasis; adequate hemostasis also requires normal platelet quality/function, which can be impaired by antiplatelet agents such as P2Y12 inhibitors (e.g., clopidogrel) even when the count is normal.

source_references.supports: States that platelet efficacy in hemostasis/transfusion depends on both quantity (count) and quality (function), and that platelets form the initial plug at injury sites.

This supports the count-vs-function framing used for option A and the explanation, though note the source literally says "efficacy in transfusion," not general endogenous hemostatic efficacy — the

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is evaluated in the transfusion medicine clinic for a lifelong history of easy bruising, gingival bleeding, and heavy menstrual periods dating back to adolescence. Her brother has a similar lifelong bleeding history. She has no history of chronic kidney disease or uremia, has never undergone cardiac surgery or cardiopulmonary bypass, and denies use of aspirin, NSAIDs, or any other antiplatelet medication. Her complete blood count shows a normal platelet count. Based on this history, which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Uremia-associated platelet dysfunction
Cardiopulmonary bypass-associated platelet dysfunction
Drug-induced platelet dysfunction
Glanzmann thrombasthenia
Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)

questions.explanation_correct: The source material groups platelet function disorders into congenital (naming Bernard-Soulier syndrome and Glanzmann thrombasthenia as examples) and acquired (naming uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, and drugs as examples). This patient's presentation - lifelong mucocutaneous bleeding, an affected sibling, and the explicit absence of every named acquired trigger (no uremia, no cardiopulmonary bypass, no antiplatelet drug use) plus a normal platelet count (arguing against a destructive process like ITP) - fits the congenital category, making Glanzmann thrombasthenia the best answer among the choices given.

option_explanations.D: Correct. Glanzmann's is explicitly listed among the congenital platelet function disorders in the source material, in contrast to the acquired causes of platelet dysfunction. A lifelong bleeding history with a similarly affected sibling and no exposure to any of the recognized acquired triggers, together with a normal platelet count (arguing against a destructive process), is most consistent with an inherited platelet function disorder.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect (board trap). Uremia is named in the source material as an acquired cause of platelet dysfunction, not a congenital one. This patient has no history of chronic kidney disease or uremia, so this acquired trigger is not present.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect (board trap). Cardiopulmonary bypass is listed as an acquired cause of platelet dysfunction. The patient has never had cardiac surgery, eliminating this acquired etiology.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect (board trap). Drug exposure is listed as an acquired cause of platelet dysfunction. The patient specifically denies use of aspirin, NSAIDs, or other antiplatelet agents, so a drug effect cannot explain her lifelong symptoms.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. ITP is described as a disorder of platelet destruction for which HLA and HPA antibody assays are used in the workup, i.e., a quantitative/immune-mediated process rather than an inherited platelet function defect. Her normal platelet count argues against a destructive thrombocytopenic process, and ITP is not among the congenital disorders named in the source material.

questions.teaching_point: Genetic/congenital platelet function disorders (e.g., Glanzmann thrombasthenia, Bernard-Soulier syndrome) should be distinguished from acquired causes of platelet dysfunction (uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, drugs) and from destructive/immune platelet disorders such as ITP, based on history (lifelong onset, family history, absence of acquired triggers) and platelet count.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping
source_references.supports: States that platelet function disorders can be congenital (naming Bernard-Soulier syndrome and Glanzmann's as examples) or acquired (naming uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, and drugs as examples).

source_references.locator: Domain I: Clinical Practice – High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that ITP involves platelet destruction and that HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical to its investigation, framing ITP as a destructive/immune process rather than a congenital one.

WHAT IS SOLIDLY SOURCED:
- SRC-006, "Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping" (line 37): "Platelet Function: Disorders can be congenital (e.g., Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann's) or acquired, such as those caused by uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, or drugs" — this is the ONLY sentence in any of the four sources that mentions congenital platelet disorders. It directly and correctly supports: Glanzmann's = congenital; uremia, CPB, and drugs = the three named acquired causes (used verbatim as distractors B/C/D). T

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia and severe neutropenia (absolute neutrophil count 100/µL) develops progressive invasive pulmonary aspergillosis that is not responding to maximal antifungal therapy. The transfusion medicine service arranges for a granulocyte (neutrophil) concentrate to be collected by apheresis from a community donor and prepared for transfusion into this patient. Before this cellular blood component can be released for transfusion, which processing step is required, and what is its purpose?

options.text: T-cell depletion, the manipulation used for hematopoietic progenitor cell products
CD34+ selection, the manipulation used to enrich progenitor cells in hematopoietic progenitor cell products
Volume/plasma reduction, the manipulation used for hematopoietic progenitor cell products
Gamma irradiation, to inactivate donor T lymphocytes and prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease
CMV-reduced processing, the requirement described for neonatal recipients

questions.explanation_correct: Granulocyte (neutrophil) concentrates are cellular blood components rich in donor leukocytes, including immunocompetent T lymphocytes carried along with the therapeutic neutrophils. The approved source states that transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) is prevented specifically by gamma irradiation of cellular components, which inactivates donor T lymphocytes so they cannot engraft and attack the recipient. This is the required processing step for a granulocyte concentrate before release. It must be distinguished from manipulations the sources describe for a different product category — hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) grafts undergo volume/plasma reduction, CD34+ selection, or T-cell depletion — and from CMV-reduced component processing, which the sources tie specifically to neonatal recipients rather than to this adult patient.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The source states TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes; a granulocyte concentrate is exactly this type of lymphocyte-containing cellular product.
option_explanations.A: T-cell depletion is described in the source as a specialized manipulation applied to hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) products, not as a processing step for granulocyte concentrates; it is a board trap because it superficially sounds GVHD-related.
option_explanations.B: CD34+ selection enriches stem/progenitor cells and is described in the source as an HPC-specific manipulation; it is irrelevant to a mature neutrophil (granulocyte) product, which contains no progenitor cells to select.
option_explanations.C: Volume/plasma reduction is listed in the source as an HPC-specific specialized manipulation (along with CD34+ selection and T-cell depletion); it addresses volume, not the TA-GVHD risk posed by donor T lymphocytes in a granulocyte concentrate.
option_explanations.E: CMV-reduced components are described in the source as required for neonates (infants under 4 months), not for this adult AML patient; this option misapplies a real but population-specific requirement to the wrong clinical scenario.

questions.teaching_point: Granulocyte (neutrophil) concentrates are cellular components carrying donor T lymphocytes and therefore require gamma irradiation to prevent TA-GVHD; this must be distinguished from processing manipulations reserved for HPC products (T-cell depletion, CD34+ selection, volume reduction) and from CMV-reduced processing, which is a population-specific requirement for neonates rather than the TA-GVHD-prevention step needed here.

source_references.locator: Domain V: Hazards of Transfusion — High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes — the core grounding fact for the correct answer.

source_references.locator: Section 3, HPC Stages of Specialized Processing and Infusion — Specialized Manipulations
source_references.supports: Lists volume/plasma reduction, CD34+ selection, and T-cell depletion as specialized manipulations performed specifically on HPC products, distinguishing them from granulocyte concentrate processing.

source_references.locator: Domain VII: Obstetric and Pediatric Patients — High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that neonates (infants under 4 months) require specific practices such as CMV-reduced components, grounding why option E is population-mismatched for this adult patient.

review.notes: I independently read all four sources in full (not just grep hits) and confirm: the word "granulocyte" does not appear anywhere in SRC-028, SRC-010, SRC-050, or SRC-004. "Neutrophil" appears only in the contexts of (1) TRALI's "two-hit" neutrophil-priming pathophysiology, and (2) HNA (Human Neutrophil Antigen) discussions tied to NAN and TRALI — never in the context of granulocyte/neutrophil transfusion, collection, storage, dosing, or indications for invasive infection in neutropenic patients. The blueprint node targeted (S13.a.i, "Neutrophil Collection, Storage, and Transfusion") is therefor

questions.stem: A term neonate born to a healthy woman who is blood group O, Rh-positive develops jaundice at approximately 36 hours of life. This is the mother's first pregnancy, and her prenatal antibody screen (performed with reagent group O screening cells) was negative throughout gestation. The infant types as blood group A, Rh-positive. Hemoglobin and hematocrit at birth were within the normal range. A peripheral blood smear shows spherocytes without erythroblastosis, and the infant's direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive. Which of the following best explains how this neonate developed hemolytic disease despite a negative maternal antibody screen and no prior pregnancy?

options.text: A silent fetomaternal hemorrhage earlier in this pregnancy sensitized the mother to the infant's paternally inherited D antigen
The mother formed anti-D during this pregnancy, which crossed the placenta and caused hemolysis without any prior sensitizing exposure
Maternal IgM anti-A crossed the placenta and coated the infant's RBCs, producing the positive DAT
Anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring IgG antibodies that do not require a prior sensitizing pregnancy, and they are not detected by an antibody screen performed with group O reagent cells
The infant is Kell-antigen positive, and maternal anti-K suppressed fetal erythropoiesis independent of any sensitization history

questions.explanation_correct: This presentation (delayed mild jaundice at 12-48 hours, normal birth hemoglobin, spherocytes without erythroblastosis, positive DAT) matches the source-described profile of ABO HDFN, which is typically mild compared with Rh HDFN. Unlike Rh(D) HDFN, ABO HDFN can occur in a first pregnancy because anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring antibodies and do not require a prior sensitizing pregnancy or transfusion. Because routine prenatal antibody screens use reagent group O cells to detect "unexpected" (non-ABO) alloantibodies, naturally occurring anti-A/anti-B are not flagged by this screen, explaining why the mother's screen remained negative even though ABO-mediated hemolysis occurred in her group A infant.

option_explanations.D: Correct: anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring and need no prior sensitizing pregnancy, and group O reagent screening cells (used to detect unexpected, non-ABO antibodies) do not carry A or B antigens, so they cannot flag these isoagglutinins.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: Rh alloimmunization (anti-D) requires a prior sensitizing exposure to D-positive cells and would have been detected by the antibody screen; moreover, the mother is Rh-positive and shares the D antigen with the infant, so she cannot form anti-D against it.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: only IgG antibodies (IgG1 and IgG3) are actively transported across the placenta; IgM antibodies do not cross, so IgM anti-A cannot mediate this hemolytic process.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: anti-K-mediated disease is notable for suppression of erythropoiesis rather than the spherocytic hemolytic pattern seen here, and like Rh antibodies, anti-K formation requires a prior sensitizing exposure (transfusion or pregnancy), which this primigravida with a negative screen does not have.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: because the mother is Rh-positive, she and the infant share the D antigen, so no maternal anti-D sensitization to D is possible; any true Rh alloimmunization would also have produced a positive antibody screen.

questions.teaching_point: ABO HDFN differs fundamentally from Rh HDFN in that anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring IgG antibodies requiring no prior sensitizing pregnancy, so it can present in a first pregnancy with a negative antibody screen (which uses group O cells and only detects unexpected, non-ABO alloantibodies); it also tends to be clinically milder, with normal birth hemoglobin, delayed jaundice, and spherocytes rather than erythroblastosis.

source_references.supports: ABO HDFN can occur in a first pregnancy because anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring, unlike Rh HDFN which needs sensitization; only IgG crosses the placenta; ABO HDFN is typically mild with normal birth hemoglobin, delayed jaundice (12-48h), spherocytes (vs erythroblasts in Rh), positive DAT; anti-K suppresses erythropoiesis rather than causing simple hemolysis.
Antibody screens use reagent group O cells to detect unexpected (non-ABO) clinically significant alloantibodies such as anti-K, anti-Fya, anti-Jka.
General domain framing that pathogenesis of ABO and Rh HDFN is a major exam concept within Obstetric and Pediatric Patients.

review.notes: Core clinical/serologic facts in the stem and key rationale are explicitly grounded in SRC-017: (1) "ABO HDFN can occur in the first pregnancy because anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring" (Common Board Traps); (2) mild ABO HDFN phenotype — normal birth Hgb, delayed jaundice 12-48h, spherocytes rather than erythroblasts, positive DAT (Clinical Presentations / Laboratory Findings / High-Yield Comparison Points); (3) "Only IgG antibodies...are actively transported across the placenta, making them the primary cause of HDFN" (Core Concepts), which correctly kills option C (IgM doesn't cross);

questions.stem: A 15-year-old girl with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) is hospitalized after an acute ischemic stroke. To lower her percentage of hemoglobin S as rapidly as possible and reduce her risk of stroke extension or recurrence, the transfusion service plans an emergent RBC procedure. Routine serologic testing shows the patient types as D-positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate combination of transfusion procedure and pretransfusion RBC testing?

options.text: Extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP), selecting the first ABO/D-compatible units available without further antigen matching
Automated erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) to lower HbS below 30%, selecting the first ABO/D-compatible units available without further antigen matching

Automated erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) to lower HbS below 30%, selecting donor units only after molecular (genotype-based) testing is used to confirm the patient's true RhD status and support extended antigen matching
A simple (top-up) RBC transfusion, selecting donor units only after molecular (genotype-based) testing is used to confirm the patient's true RhD status and support extended antigen matching
Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) using fresh frozen plasma as replacement fluid, selecting donor units only after molecular (genotype-based) testing

questions.explanation_correct: Automated erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) is the apheresis procedure used in sickle cell disease to rapidly reduce the percentage of HbS below 30% for stroke prevention. Because SCD patients are recognized as a population at particular risk of harboring Rh variants that type as conventional D by serology but can still form allo-anti-D, molecular/genotype-based RBC testing is essential before selecting donor units, so that an underlying partial D can be identified and the patient managed as Rh-negative for transfusion, preventing sensitization to the missing D epitopes.

option_explanations.C: Correct: red cell exchange targeting HbS below 30% is the source-supported apheresis approach for sickle cell disease, and pairing it with molecular RhD testing addresses the SCD-specific risk that a serologically D-positive patient actually has an undetected partial D variant that could form allo-anti-D if exposed to conventional D-positive units.
option_explanations.B: Exchange transfusion is the correct procedure, but relying on serologic D-typing alone is a board trap: it can miss a partial D variant that appears as conventional D by serology yet still carries a risk of allo-anti-D formation, so extended/molecular matching should not be skipped in SCD patients.
option_explanations.D: A simple top-up transfusion does not accomplish the rapid, targeted reduction in %HbS that erythrocytapheresis provides; the sources specify red cell exchange, not simple transfusion, as the procedure used to lower HbS in sickle cell disease.
option_explanations.E: Therapeutic plasma exchange with FFP replacement is the apheresis therapy used for TTP to remove an ADAMTS13 inhibitor; it is not the modality used to reduce hemoglobin S in sickle cell disease.
option_explanations.A: Extracorporeal photopheresis is used for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and graft-versus-host disease, not for lowering HbS in sickle cell disease, and this option also omits the antigen matching needed in this population.

questions.teaching_point: In sickle cell disease, automated red cell exchange (erythrocytapheresis) is the apheresis modality used to rapidly lower %HbS (target <30%) for stroke prevention, and molecular RBC genotyping is essential in this population to detect Rh variants that mimic conventional D serologically yet carry allo-anti-D risk, guiding safe donor unit selection and RhIg/transfusion Rh-status decisions.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 56 - Therapeutic Apheresis, Specific Indications (Erythrocytapheresis)
source_references.supports: Erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) is used in sickle cell disease to rapidly reduce HbS levels below 30% for stroke prevention.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 41 - Molecular Testing Technologies, Clinical Applications (Sickle Cell Disease)
source_references.supports: Molecular/genotype testing is essential in SCD patients for extended antigen matching and for identifying Rh variants that appear as conventional D by serology but are at risk for allo-anti-D.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 58 - Rh System Variants, Clinical Management (Partial D in Patients)
source_references.supports: Recipients with partial D should be considered Rh-negative for transfusion to prevent sensitization to missing D epitopes.

source_references.locator: Domain IV: Apheresis, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Red cell exchange is frequently used for acute complications of sickle cell disease.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 56 - Therapeutic Apheresis, Specific Indications (TTP)
source_references.supports: TPE with FFP/cryo-poor plasma is the apheresis treatment for TTP via removal of an ADAMTS13 inhibitor, distinguishing it from SCD management.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 56 - Therapeutic Apheresis, Specific Indications (ECP)
source_references.supports: ECP treats leukocytes for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and GVHD, an indication distinct from sickle cell disease.

review.notes: Core facts are directly, near-verbatim grounded: SRC-059 Node 56 gives erythrocytapheresis lowering HbS "below 30%" for stroke prevention; SRC-004 Node 41 gives the exact SCD-specific rationale that molecular testing is "essential for providing extended antigen matching and identifying Rh variants that appear as conventional D by serology but are at risk for allo-anti-D"; SRC-059 Node 58 supports managing partial D as Rh-negative; SRC-059 Node 56 and SRC-010 Domain IV support the TTP/TPE and ECP/CTCL-GVHD distractor facts. All five options are clearly differentiable on source grounds and B/C/D

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child with a congenital hemoglobinopathy other than sickle cell disease has been receiving regular red blood cell transfusions since infancy to manage chronic anemia and now presents for a routine follow-up visit. Because this child's transfusion history reflects a large cumulative lifetime exposure to RBC units rather than a reaction from any single transfusion episode, which of the following complications should be the primary focus of long-term surveillance in this patient?

options.text: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from an ABO-identification (clerical) error
Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD)
Iron overload from cumulative transfusional iron loading

questions.explanation_correct: Iron overload is recognized as one of the major hazards of transfusion, and unlike the other listed complications it is driven by the cumulative burden of repeated RBC transfusions over time rather than by any single transfusion episode. A child with a non-sickle congenital hemoglobinopathy who has required regular transfusions since infancy has substantial cumulative RBC exposure, making progressive iron accumulation the dominant long-term surveillance concern, in contrast to the acute, per-episode hazards represented by the other options.

option_explanations.E: Correct: iron overload is explicitly named among the recognized hazards of transfusion and is the complication that specifically accumulates with a large lifetime burden of RBC transfusions, matching this child's long transfusion history since infancy.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are most commonly caused by a clerical/ABO-identification error occurring at the time of a single transfusion episode, not by the cumulative number of units received over a lifetime.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: TRALI is defined by new acute lung injury occurring within hours of a transfusion — an acute reaction tied to one episode, not a complication that builds with cumulative long-term exposure.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: TACO is a reaction to the volume or infusion rate of an individual transfusion episode, not a consequence of the total number of transfusions accumulated over time.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: TA-GVHD results from donor T lymphocytes in a transfused cellular product and is prevented by irradiation of components; it is an episode-related risk from a given unit, not the hallmark complication of a large cumulative transfusion burden.

questions.teaching_point: Distinguish cumulative, dose-dependent hazards of long-term transfusion support (iron overload) from acute, single-episode transfusion reactions (TRALI, TACO, TA-GVHD, acute hemolytic reactions from clerical error) — a distinction relevant to any pediatric patient, including those with non-sickle congenital hemoglobinopathies, who requires chronic RBC transfusion.

source_references.locator: Domain VII - Obstetric and Pediatric Patients (subdomain list)
source_references.supports: Names Congenital Hemoglobinopathies as a subdomain under Obstetric/Pediatric Patients, though no specific non-sickle disease facts (thalassemia, HbC, HbE, etc.) are detailed in this source.

source_references.locator: Domain V - Hazards of Transfusion (subdomains and high-yield facts)
source_references.supports: Lists Iron Overload as a recognized transfusion hazard category, and gives TRALI (acute lung injury within 6 hours), TACO (reaction to volume/infusion rate), and TA-GVHD (prevented by irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes) as distinct acute hazard categories.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20 - Antiglobulin Testing, Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States that clerical errors remain the primary, largely preventable cause of acute intravascular hemolytic transfusion reactions.

review.notes:
Independently read all four sources in full (SRC-010: 132 lines, SRC-032: 215 lines, SRC-011: 97 lines, SRC-004: 111 lines) and grepped for "iron," "thalassem/hemoglobinopathy," and "TRALI/TACO/GVHD/clerical."

questions.stem: A female infant is born at 26 weeks' gestation with a birth weight of 890 g; this is the mother's first pregnancy. On day of life 6, a routine complete blood count reveals thrombocytopenia. The infant has no petechiae, ecchymoses, or other evidence of bleeding, and there are no clinical signs of sepsis or necrotizing enterocolitis. The maternal platelet count is normal. The neonatology team requests a platelet transfusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate consideration when the blood bank selects and prepares this platelet unit?

options.text: The unit should be irradiated before issue, because the infant's birth weight is below the threshold at which irradiation is required to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease.
Irradiation may be omitted, because irradiation is required only for intrauterine and exchange transfusions, not for simple platelet transfusions in preterm infants.
Selecting a CMV-reduced-risk unit is sufficient on its own; irradiation can be omitted for platelet transfusions in premature infants.
HPA-1a–negative platelets should be selected, because this clinical picture is diagnostic of fetal and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia.
A standard-volume, non-aliquoted apheresis platelet unit is appropriate, since small-volume aliquots from dedicated donors apply only to red cell transfusions.

questions.explanation_correct: Irradiation of cellular blood components is mandatory for intrauterine transfusion, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates weighing less than 1200 g, to inactivate donor T lymphocytes and prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD). This infant, born at 890 g, meets that birth-weight criterion, so the platelet unit must be irradiated regardless of the underlying cause of the thrombocytopenia. Nothing in the vignette (no petechiae, ecchymoses, or intracranial hemorrhage; normal maternal platelet count) fits the classic presentation of fetal and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (FNAIT), so antigen-specific platelet selection is not supported here; the clearly source-supported action is birth-weight-based irradiation.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the approved source states irradiation is mandatory for premature neonates <1200 g (in addition to IUT and exchange transfusion) to prevent TA-GVHD, and this infant's 890 g birth weight meets that criterion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: FNAIT classically presents with petechiae, ecchymoses, or life-threatening intracranial hemorrhage. This infant has none of those findings, so there is no basis in the vignette to diagnose FNAIT and reflexively request HPA-1a-negative platelets; note that FNAIT can occur even in a first pregnancy, but the absence of hemorrhagic findings argues against invoking it here.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source specifies irradiation is mandatory not only for IUT and exchange transfusion but also for premature neonates <1200 g, which directly applies to this infant's simple platelet transfusion.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: source-based neonatal transfusion practice favors small-volume aliquots from dedicated units to minimize donor exposure as a general neonatal transfusion principle, not one limited to red cell components.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: CMV-reduced-risk product selection is a separate, additional neonatal blood-banking consideration and does not substitute for the birth-weight-based irradiation requirement, which specifically addresses TA-GVHD prevention rather than CMV transmission.

questions.teaching_point: Premature neonates weighing less than 1200 g require irradiated cellular blood products for any transfusion (not just IUT or exchange transfusion) to prevent TA-GVHD; incidental, non-hemorrhagic thrombocytopenia in a preterm infant should not be reflexively attributed to FNAIT, which classically causes petechiae, ecchymoses, or intracranial hemorrhage.

source_references.supports: Irradiation is mandatory for IUT, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates <1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD

source_references.supports: Neonates (<4 months) require specific blood banking practices such as dedicated units or CMV-reduced components

The infant's 890 g birth weight is below that threshold, so A is directly and unambiguously supported. Independen

questions.stem:
A 26-week-gestation infant, born weighing 900 g, is now 5 weeks old and weighs 980 g in the NICU. There is no evidence of hemolysis or blood group incompatibility (DAT negative, no jaundice out of proportion to expected), but over the past two weeks he has developed tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, and an increasing supplemental oxygen requirement. His hemoglobin has progressively fallen, and his serum erythropoietin level is inappropriately low for the degree of anemia. The neonatology team determines that RBC transfusion is indicated.

Which of the following represents the most appropriate transfusion approach for this infant?

options.text: Begin phototherapy at 460–490 nm instead of transfusion
Transfuse a small-volume aliquot (10–15 mL/kg) of irradiated RBCs from a dedicated donor unit
Administer IVIG to block reticuloendothelial Fc receptors
Perform a double-volume exchange transfusion to remove circulating bilirubin and antibody
Transfuse a full-unit volume of non-irradiated RBCs rather than a small-volume aliquot

questions.explanation_correct: Anemia of prematurity results from a developmentally blunted erythropoietin response to falling hemoglobin in the neonate, compounded by immature organ systems; it presents with tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, and increased oxygen requirement, as in this infant. The source-directed management for symptomatic anemia in this setting is a small-volume RBC aliquot (10–15 mL/kg) from a dedicated donor unit to limit donor exposure, and irradiation is mandatory because the infant weighs less than 1200 g, to prevent TA-GVHD. The remaining options are therapies specific to HDFN/hyperbilirubinemia or violate neonatal transfusion volume and irradiation requirements, not appropriate management of isolated anemia of prematurity.

option_explanations.B: Correct: small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg) from a dedicated unit minimize donor exposure, and irradiation is mandatory for premature neonates weighing less than 1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: double-volume exchange transfusion is used to remove bilirubin and antibody in severe HDFN/hyperbilirubinemia, not for isolated anemia of prematurity without immune hemolysis.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: phototherapy (460–490 nm) conjugates bilirubin to treat neonatal jaundice; it does not raise hemoglobin or address anemia of prematurity.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: IVIG blocks Fc receptors to reduce antibody-mediated hemolysis in HDFN; this infant's anemia is due to a blunted erythropoietin response rather than immune-mediated RBC destruction.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: neonatal transfusion should use small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg), and irradiation is required for infants under 1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD; a full, non-irradiated unit violates both principles.

questions.teaching_point: Anemia of prematurity is driven by a blunted erythropoietin response in the immature neonate (not immune hemolysis) and presents with tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, and increased oxygen need; management uses small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg) from a dedicated donor unit, irradiated if the infant weighs under 1200 g. This must be distinguished from HDFN-specific therapies (phototherapy, IVIG, exchange transfusion), which target bilirubin/antibody-mediated hemolysis rather than isolated anemia of prematurity.

source_references.supports: Neonates have a blunted EPO response to anemia; anemia of prematurity manifests as tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, or increased oxygen requirement.
Simple neonatal transfusions use small-volume aliquots (10–15 mL/kg) from dedicated units to minimize donor exposure.
Irradiation is mandatory for IUT, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates under 1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD.
Phototherapy, IVIG (to block Fc receptors), and double-volume exchange transfusion are listed as HDFN-directed neonatal therapies for bilirubin/antibody removal.
Neonatal transfusion logic corroborates small-volume aliquot dosing (10–15 mL/kg) and irradiation for TA-GVHD prevention.

review.notes:
Checked against SRC-017, SRC-010, SRC-061, SRC-043 directly.

- "Anemia of Prematurity: Manifests as tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, or increased oxygen requirements" — matches stem symptoms verbatim.
- "a blunted erythropoietin (EPO) response to anemia" — matches the low-EPO premise (this is stated as a general neonatal physiology fact adjacent to, not in a single sentence merged with, the "Anemia of Prematurity" clinical-presentation bullet; the ques

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with relapsed multiple myeloma is scheduled to receive high-dose melphalan chemotherapy followed by autologous hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation. His own peripheral blood stem cells were mobilized, collected by apheresis, and cryopreserved weeks earlier for reinfusion after conditioning. A resident asks the transfusion medicine attending why autologous HSCT is generally considered to carry the lowest risk of immune-mediated complications compared with allogeneic HSCT.

options.text: The recipient and the graft originate from the same individual, so there is no HLA disparity to trigger graft-versus-host disease or graft rejection
The apheresis product is treated with pathogen inactivation before cryopreservation, eliminating alloreactive donor T cells
The HPC product is irradiated before infusion, eliminating any immunocompetent lymphocytes capable of causing graft-versus-host disease
ABO compatibility between the graft and the recipient is confirmed before collection, preventing a hemolytic complication
Because the product is intended for autologous use only, it is exempt from infectious disease testing requirements

questions.explanation_correct: Autologous HSCT uses the patient's own previously collected and cryopreserved stem cells, so the "donor" and recipient are genetically identical. Because there is no HLA disparity between graft and host, the immunologic mechanisms that drive complications in allogeneic HSCT — graft-versus-host disease (donor T cells attacking recipient tissue) and graft rejection (host immune response against a foreign graft) — cannot occur. This is precisely why the source material identifies autologous HSCT as carrying the lowest risk of immunologic complications, in contrast to allogeneic HSCT, which carries GVHD, passenger lymphocyte syndrome, and rejection risk tied to HLA and donor-recipient antigenic differences.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Donor and recipient are the same person in autologous HSCT, eliminating the HLA disparity that underlies GVHD and graft rejection in the allogeneic setting; this is why autologous transplant carries the lowest immunologic risk.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. HPC grafts are not subjected to pathogen-reduction/inactivation processing as a routine step, and this is not the reason autologous transplants avoid immune complications — the protection comes from donor-recipient identity, not ex vivo product treatment.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect distractor. ABO matching is not essential for HPC donor selection at all (HPCs do not express ABO antigens and will not trigger hyperacute rejection), so 'confirming ABO compatibility' is neither the mechanism performed nor the reason autologous grafting is low-risk.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect and a classic board trap in the opposite direction: the HPC graft must NEVER be irradiated, because irradiation would destroy the stem cells' proliferative capacity. Irradiation is reserved for cellular blood components given to the patient, not the HPC product itself.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect distractor. Autologous HPC/blood products are not exempt from infectious disease testing; testing is still required, for example when the product is shipped to another facility.

questions.teaching_point: Autologous HSCT is immunologically the lowest-risk transplant modality because donor and recipient are the same person, eliminating the HLA disparity that drives GVHD and graft rejection in allogeneic HSCT — distinguish this from unrelated board traps: ABO matching is not required for any HPC graft, the HPC product itself must never be irradiated (only post-transplant blood components for the patient are irradiated), and autologous products are not automatically exempt from infectious disease testing.
