questions.stem:
A 34-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia in first complete remission has a healthy monozygotic (identical) twin sister who is willing to donate peripheral blood stem cells. The transplant team proceeds with a syngeneic HPC(A) transplant following myeloablative conditioning.

Which of the following is the most accurate statement about the biology of this transplant compared with a standard allogeneic HSCT from an HLA-disparate donor?

options.text: The graft should be leukocyte-reduced before infusion to decrease the number of immunocompetent donor cells transferred
The HPC graft should be irradiated before infusion to eliminate residual donor lymphocytes and further reduce GVHD risk
Donor and recipient must be ABO-identical for the transplant to proceed safely, since HPCs express the same ABO antigens found on mature red cells
Because there is no risk of graft-versus-host disease, this recipient will not need irradiated or pathogen-reduced blood components after transplant
Both GVHD and the graft-versus-neoplasm (GVN) effect are expected to be diminished, because the donor and recipient are genetically identical and lack the HLA disparity that drives each process

questions.explanation_correct: The source defines the major histocompatibility barrier (HLA disparity) as the primary driver of both graft-vs-host disease and graft rejection, and states that the graft-versus-neoplasm effect specifically arises when donor immune cells recognize and attack residual tumor cells in allogeneic transplants. A syngeneic graft, by definition, comes from a monozygotic twin who is genetically identical to the recipient. With no immunologic/HLA disparity between donor and recipient, the immune-mediated processes that underlie both GVHD and the GVN effect are expected to be diminished — this is the classic trade-off taught for syngeneic transplantation: lower GVHD risk but also loss of the graft-versus-tumor benefit that depends on donor-recipient immunologic difference.

option_explanations.E: Correct: syngeneic (identical twin) donor-recipient pairs lack the HLA disparity that the source identifies as the driver of both GVHD and the GVN effect, so both are diminished.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — this is the classic 'irradiation error' board trap: the HPC graft itself must never be irradiated, since irradiation destroys the stem cells' ability to proliferate and engraft.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — the source explicitly states HSCT does not require ABO identity because HPCs do not express ABO antigens, unlike solid organ transplants.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — the source explicitly states the HPC graft should never be leukocyte reduced, regardless of GVHD concerns.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — the source states HSCT recipients must receive irradiated or pathogen-reduced blood components for life to prevent transfusion-associated GVHD from residual blood product lymphocytes; this recommendation is tied to HSCT recipient status generally, not eliminated simply because the graft itself is syngeneic.

questions.teaching_point: In syngeneic (identical-twin) transplantation, the absence of HLA/genetic disparity between donor and recipient diminishes both GVHD and the graft-versus-neoplasm effect, since both depend on immunologic recognition of disparate tissue — this is distinct from other HSCT-wide safety requirements (never irradiate/leukoreduce the graft, no ABO identity needed, lifelong irradiated blood products for TA-GVHD prevention) that apply regardless of donor type.

- C (ABO identity required) is explicitly co

questions.stem:
A 52-year-old woman with high-risk AML in first complete remission lacks an HLA-identical sibling donor; her only full sibling was typed and found to be HLA-mismatched, which is consistent with the expected pattern of haplotype inheritance in siblings. The transplant program therefore initiates a search of the unrelated donor registry. Once a promising unrelated candidate is identified, the histocompatibility laboratory must perform typing on both the patient and the donor candidate before the donor can be finalized.

Which of the following best describes the required approach to HLA typing in this unrelated-donor evaluation?

options.text: High-resolution, DNA-based molecular typing at the allele (two-field) level should be performed at HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1.
A physical crossmatch mixing donor cells with recipient serum must be completed before the molecular HLA typing results can be interpreted.
Because HPCs do not express HLA antigens on their surface, HLA matching is no more essential to donor selection than ABO matching.
Serologic (antigen-level) typing of HLA-A and HLA-B is sufficient, since these are the only loci relevant to donor selection.
Typing only needs to cover HLA-DR and HLA-DQ, since these Class II loci are the ones expressed on antigen-presenting cells.

questions.explanation_correct: For unrelated donor selection, the sources specify that high-resolution, DNA-based typing at the allele (two-field) level is mandatory, and that the target loci for unrelated donors are HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 — this is the level of resolution needed to safely match an unrelated pair given the extreme polymorphism of the HLA system.

option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Antigen-level serologic typing is not sufficient for unrelated donor selection; the sources require high-resolution DNA-based (allele-level) typing. It is also incomplete, since HLA-C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 must also be typed, not just -A and -B.
option_explanations.A: Correct. The sources state that high-resolution DNA-based typing at the allele level (two-field) is mandatory for unrelated HSCT donor selection, with HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 as the target loci for unrelated donors.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This distractor misapplies the true statement that HPCs do not express ABO antigens (so ABO matching is not required for graft acceptance) to HLA, which is the opposite of what the sources teach: HLA compatibility is described as the paramount factor for engraftment and GVHD prevention in allogeneic HSCT, and Class I HLA molecules are expressed on most nucleated cells.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The sources describe a virtual crossmatch as comparing the recipient's known HLA antibody profile against the donor's documented HLA type specifically to predict compatibility without a physical mixture of cells and serum; DNA-based HLA typing is a molecular method (SSOP, SSP, or NGS) that does not require an antecedent physical crossmatch to be interpreted.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. While HLA-DR and -DQ (Class II) are correctly noted as restricted to antigen-presenting cells, limiting typing to these loci omits the Class I loci (HLA-A, -B, -C) that are also required targets for unrelated donor typing.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach - HLA Typing; Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls - The 25% Sibling Rule
source_references.supports: High-resolution DNA-based typing at the allele level (two-field) is mandatory for unrelated HSCT donor selection; also supports the 25% sibling HLA-match rule from linkage disequilibrium and that HLA compatibility is paramount for engraftment/GVHD prevention.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach - HLA Matching; Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls - Linkage Disequilibrium
source_references.supports: States that high-resolution DNA-based typing at HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 is the goal for unrelated donors, and reiterates the 25% sibling match rule from linkage disequilibrium.

source_references.locator: Section 1 MHC Organization table; Section 2 Critical Components of the HLA System - HLA Typing and Crossmatching/Virtual Crossmatch Expert Note
source_references.supports: Defines HLA Class I (HLA-A,-B,-C) as expressed on most nucleated cells and platelets and Class II (HLA-DR,-DQ,-DP) as restricted to antigen-presenting cells; describes HLA typing by molecular methods (SSOP, SSP, NGS) and defines the virtual crossmatch as comparison of antibody profile to documented donor HLA type without physical mixing.

review.notes:
Independently confirmed against all four sources.

CORE CONTENT (option B and its rationale): Fully and explicitly grounded, verbatim in two separate approved sources. SRC-035 ("Diagnostic Approach — HLA Typing"): "High-resolution DNA-based typing at the allele level (two-field) is mandatory for unrelated HSCT donor selection." SRC-038 ("Diagnostic Approach — HLA Matching"): "High-resolution DNA-based typing at HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1, and -DQB1 is the goal for unrelated donors." Option B is a legitimate synthesis of these two explicit statements (resolution/mandate from SRC-035 + loci list from

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem:
A hospital-based blood bank's quality manager investigates a deviation. A technologist performed antibody screening on a unit of red blood cells using a lot of antisera reagent before the lot's required day-of-use quality control had been performed and documented — a clear deviation from the facility's cGMP-based process control requirements. The lapse was discovered later the same shift during a routine review of QC logs. The affected unit had not yet been released from the blood bank; it was immediately quarantined, the reagent lot was pulled from use, and the unit was destroyed. The unit was never issued to any patient or shipped to another facility. The quality manager documents the event, performs a root cause analysis, and implements a corrective action.

Which of the following best explains why this event, as described, does not meet the FDA's reporting criteria for a Biological Product Deviation (BPD)?

options.text: The event does not require reporting because it was discovered and corrected within 45 days, the FDA's allowed window for resolving deviations without notification.
Since no patient received the unit, the event is a near-miss, and near-miss events are, by definition, exempt from any documentation or investigation requirements.
Because the quality manager's investigation classified the event as warranting a corrective action rather than labeling it an accident, external BPD reporting is not triggered.
The affected unit was never distributed — it remained within the facility's control and was destroyed before release — so the criterion requiring an already-distributed product was not met.
Reagent quality control lapses fall outside the scope of cGMP oversight, so they never qualify as manufacturing deviations.

questions.explanation_correct: FDA BPD reporting requires that an event meet all five criteria simultaneously: it is associated with manufacturing, represents a deviation from cGMP/specifications, may affect safety/purity/potency, occurred while the facility had control of the product, AND involves a product that has been distributed (left the facility's control). Here the reagent QC lapse is a genuine cGMP process-control deviation that occurred while the facility had control of the unit, but because the unit was quarantined and destroyed before release, the "distributed" criterion is never satisfied. Since all five criteria must be met for a BPD report to be required, this event does not trigger FDA BPD reporting — though it must still be documented, investigated, and corrected internally as a nonconforming event under the facility's QMS.

option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Process control, including performance of required reagent QC (e.g., day-of-use QC for antisera), is itself a core cGMP/QMS element; failing to complete it before use is precisely the kind of manufacturing deviation cGMP is meant to prevent.
option_explanations.D: Correct. BPD reporting requires all five criteria to be met, including that the product has been distributed (left the facility's control). Because the unit was quarantined and destroyed before release, this criterion fails, so no BPD report is required even though a real cGMP deviation occurred.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The 45-calendar-day window is the deadline for submitting a BPD report once an event that already meets all five reporting criteria has been discovered — it is not a grace period that excuses reporting if the deviation is corrected quickly.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Whether a BPD report is required depends on whether the event objectively meets the five defined criteria (manufacturing-associated, cGMP deviation, potential safety/purity/potency impact, facility control, and distribution), not on how the facility's internal corrective/remedial action is labeled.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A near-miss or nonconforming event is not exempt from documentation or investigation — it must still be captured and investigated under the QMS; the reason no BPD report is owed here is specifically the unmet distribution criterion, not a blanket exemption for near-misses.

questions.teaching_point: FDA Biological Product Deviation (BPD) reporting under cGMP requires that ALL FIVE criteria be met simultaneously — manufacturing association, cGMP/specification deviation, potential impact on safety/purity/potency, facility control at time of occurrence, and prior distribution of the product (it left the facility's control). A genuine cGMP process-control deviation caught and contained before release does not trigger mandatory FDA BPD reporting, because the "distributed" criterion is unmet — but it must still be documented, root-caused, and corrected internally as a nonconforming event under the facility's QMS.

source_references.locator: domain-2-clinical-practice-patient-management-study-guide.md, lines 105-118 (BPD reporting: all five criteria required, including 'Involves a product that has been distributed')
source_references.supports: Lists the five criteria required for FDA Biological Product Deviation reporting, including manufacturing association, cGMP deviation, potential safety/purity/potency impact, facility control, and prior distribution.

source_references.locator: domain-4-advanced-technologies-specialized-testing-study-guide.md, lines 185-197 (BPD Form FDA 3486 criteria)
source_references.supports: Confirms the same five BPD reporting criteria, including that the product must have been distributed/left the facility's control, using Form FDA 3486.

source_references.locator: blood-banking-regulatory-compliance-and-quality-management-systems.md, lines 17-19 and 57-58 (BPD definition and 45-day reporting requirement)
source_references.supports: Defines BPD as an event during manufacturing (collection to distribution) deviating from cGMP or affecting safety/purity/potency, and states BPDs must be reported to FDA within 45 calendar days of discovery.

source_references.locator: foundations-of-blood-bank-administration-and-laboratory-management.md, lines 15-18 and 55 (cGMP/BPD definitions and 45-day reporting)
source_references.supports: Defines cGMP as minimum standards ensuring safety, purity, and potency requiring investigation/reporting of deviations, and defines BPD with the same manufacturing/distribution scope and 45-day reporting timeline.

review.notes:
All load-bearing facts are directly grounded in the four approved sources.

(1) Five BPD criteria, conjunctive: SRC-027 lines 105-118 explicitly states "must report BPDs to the FDA when an event meets all five of these criteria" and lists: associated with manufacturing; deviation from cGMP/specifications; may affect safety/purity/potency; occurred while facility had control; "involves a product that has been distributed (left the facility's control)." SRC-028 lines 185-197 independently corroborates the identical five-item list (Form FDA 3486) including the same distribution criterion phrased

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem: A blood bank technologist is working the evening shift when she notices that a colleague from another hospital department was recently admitted as an inpatient. She has no role in that patient's care. Purely out of personal curiosity, she uses her own valid laboratory information system login to look up the colleague's ABO/Rh type and antibody screen results, and later mentions the results to a coworker in the break room. Which of the following legal/regulatory principles has this technologist most directly violated?

options.text: HIPAA Privacy Rule — unauthorized access to and disclosure of protected health information
Doctrine of Informed Consent — failure to disclose risks, benefits, and alternatives before transfusion
Battery — performing a procedure without obtaining patient consent
Strict liability — liability for providing a defective blood product regardless of fault
Negligence — deviation from the standard of care that causes patient injury

questions.explanation_correct: HIPAA governs the privacy and security of a patient's protected health information (PHI). Blood bank and hospital records (laboratory results such as ABO/Rh type and antibody screen findings) must be protected from unauthorized access as part of HIPAA compliance, and information management systems are required to protect the confidentiality of both electronic and paper patient data. Here, the technologist had no care-related reason to view the colleague's results, accessed them anyway, and then disclosed them to a third party — this is an unauthorized access/disclosure of PHI, i.e., a HIPAA Privacy Rule issue, not a treatment-related tort or consent failure.

option_explanations.E: Negligence is failure to follow established standards of care (e.g., a testing or labeling error) that leads to patient injury. No testing error or patient harm is described here — the issue is inappropriate access to and disclosure of results, not a deviation in technical care.
option_explanations.C: Battery is defined as performing a transfusion (or other procedure) without obtaining any consent. No transfusion or procedure was performed on anyone in this scenario, so battery does not apply.
option_explanations.A: Correct. HIPAA governs privacy and security of PHI, and records must be protected from unauthorized access as part of HIPAA compliance. The technologist accessed and disclosed a patient's laboratory results without any care-related justification, which is an unauthorized access/disclosure of PHI.
option_explanations.B: The Doctrine of Informed Consent requires clinicians to explain the risks, benefits, and alternatives of a transfusion to the patient before the procedure. No transfusion decision or consent discussion is involved in this scenario.
option_explanations.D: Strict liability is a doctrine (rarely applied to blood) holding a provider liable for a defective product regardless of fault. No blood product or product defect is at issue here.

questions.teaching_point: HIPAA Privacy Rule specifically addresses unauthorized access to and disclosure of protected health information, and must be distinguished on boards from other medicolegal doctrines governing transfusion practice — battery (no consent for a procedure), negligence (deviation from standard of care causing injury), informed consent (failure to disclose risks/benefits/alternatives before transfusion), and strict liability (defective product regardless of fault).

source_references.supports: HIPAA governs the privacy and security of a patient's protected health information (PHI), listed alongside and distinguished from battery, negligence, strict liability, and the doctrine of informed consent as separate medicolegal concepts.

source_references.locator: study-guide-domain-3-regulatory-quality-and-operations.md, lines 49 and 113
source_references.supports: Records must be protected from unauthorized access as a matter of HIPAA compliance; the Information Management quality system essential requires protecting the confidentiality of electronic and paper patient data.

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem: A hospital-based transfusion service performs ABO/Rh typing, antibody detection, and compatibility (crossmatch) testing, then issues red blood cell units to patients for transfusion. The service does not collect blood from donors and does not process, manufacture, or further modify components; all units are obtained from a separately FDA-licensed community blood center. During a regulatory affairs review, the laboratory director is asked to characterize this transfusion service's obligation to register as a blood establishment with the FDA. Which of the following best describes the facility's regulatory status?

options.text: It is exempt from FDA establishment registration, but it remains subject to CMS/CLIA regulation and may still be inspected by the FDA for cause.
It must obtain AABB accreditation as a legal prerequisite before it is permitted to perform compatibility testing.
It must register with the FDA and hold its own Biologics License Application (BLA), because it administers previously licensed blood products to patients.
It must register with the FDA as a blood establishment but does not need a BLA, because it never distributes components in interstate commerce.
It is exempt from all federal and regulatory oversight because it does not collect, process, or manufacture blood components.

questions.explanation_correct: Blood is regulated by the FDA as both a drug and a biological product, and FDA establishment registration is generally mandatory for blood establishments; a Biologics License (BLA) is required specifically for entities distributing blood in interstate commerce. However, the sources call out a specific, testable exception: transfusion services that are limited to crossmatching and transfusing (i.e., no collection or processing) are exempt from FDA establishment registration. This exemption does not remove all oversight — such facilities remain subject to CMS/CLIA regulation and can still be subject to FDA "for-cause" inspections. This scenario describes exactly that kind of facility (testing and issuing only, no collection/processing), so option C is the accurate description.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Registration and BLA obligations attach to blood establishments that collect, process, or distribute components; a transfusion service that only tests and administers already-licensed product does not itself need to register or hold a BLA — this is precisely the misconception the source flags as a board trap.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. This inverts the actual exemption: the source states transfusion services limited to crossmatch/transfuse are exempt from FDA registration itself, not merely exempt from the BLA while still needing to register.
option_explanations.A: Correct. Matches the source directly: crossmatch/transfuse-only services are exempt from FDA registration but remain subject to CMS/CLIA oversight and FDA for-cause inspection.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Overstates the exemption — the source explicitly notes the facility 'remain[s] subject to CMS/CLIA and for-cause FDA inspections,' so oversight is not eliminated entirely, only FDA establishment registration is waived.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The sources describe AABB as an accreditation agency that performs periodic inspections (like CAP), not as a body whose accreditation is a legal prerequisite substituting for FDA registration/licensure requirements.

questions.teaching_point: Blood establishment FDA registration is generally mandatory, and licensure (BLA) is required for interstate distribution — but transfusion services limited to crossmatching and transfusing (no collection/processing) are specifically exempt from FDA registration, while still remaining subject to CMS/CLIA regulation and FDA for-cause inspection authority.

source_references.locator: Regulatory/Compliance Approach section; Common Board Traps and Distractors section
source_references.supports: States the Registration vs. Licensure distinction (registration mandatory for blood establishments; BLA required for interstate distribution) and the specific board-trap exemption that crossmatch/transfuse-only transfusion services are exempt from FDA registration but remain subject to CMS/CLIA and for-cause FDA inspection.

source_references.locator: Domain IX (Administration and Laboratory Management) High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Confirms general background that FDA regulates blood as both a drug and biological product, and that accreditation agencies such as AABB perform periodic inspections as part of the oversight framework.

review.notes: Core content is well-grounded. SRC-009's "Common Board Traps and Distractors" section states, nearly verbatim to the stem/key: "Registration Exemption: Transfusion services that only crossmatch and transfuse (no collection/processing) are exempt from FDA registration but remain subject to CMS/CLIA and 'for-cause' FDA inspections." SRC-009's "Regulatory/Compliance Approach" section separately supports "Registration is mandatory for all blood establishments; Licensure (BLA) is required specifically for distributing blood in interstate commerce" — which grounds why A and B are wrong (registration

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman receiving unfractionated heparin develops a platelet count decline from 240 × 10⁹/L to 95 × 10⁹/L on treatment day 7. Peripheral smear shows no schistocytes, there is no evidence of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, renal function is normal, and she has no neurologic symptoms or fever. Her consultant states that this clinical and laboratory picture argues against a specific alternative diagnosis — one that would otherwise require urgent initiation of therapeutic plasma exchange while confirmatory testing (a clinical prediction score together with ADAMTS13 activity) is pending. Which alternative diagnosis is being excluded by this presentation?

options.text: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
Paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria (PCH)
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection (bacterial sepsis) from a platelet unit
Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (WAIHA)

questions.explanation_correct: TTP presents with the pentad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes), thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, renal abnormalities, and fever; the PLASMIC score and ADAMTS13 activity are used to differentiate TTP from other microangiopathies while confirmatory testing is pending, and TPE is emergent first-line therapy for TTP. The absence of schistocytes/MAHA, renal dysfunction, neurologic findings, and fever argues against TTP, so the isolated platelet drop is more consistent with an immune thrombocytopenia process (the classification domain HIT shares with ITP) than a microangiopathic consumptive process requiring emergent TPE.

option_explanations.C: Correct — the absence of MAHA/schistocytes, renal abnormality, neurologic signs, and fever is precisely what argues against the TTP pentad described in the sources, and TTP is the condition for which urgent TPE and ADAMTS13/PLASMIC-score-based workup are described.
option_explanations.E: WAIHA is an RBC autoantibody disease diagnosed by a positive polyspecific DAT with anti-IgG reactivity and a panreactive eluate — a red cell hemolysis workup, not the isolated platelet-count picture described.
option_explanations.A: TACO presents with dyspnea, hypertension, tachycardia, jugular venous distension, and S3 gallop from volume overload — unrelated to platelet count or microangiopathic findings.
option_explanations.D: Transfusion-transmitted bacterial sepsis presents with a high fever (≥2°C rise), rigors, and profound hypotension, particularly from room-temperature-stored platelets — a distinct clinical picture from the afebrile, isolated thrombocytopenia described.
option_explanations.B: PCH is diagnosed by a positive Donath-Landsteiner test (biphasic IgG hemolysin with anti-P specificity), typically in children after a viral illness with cold-induced hemoglobinuria — an RBC hemolytic process, not the isolated platelet drop described.

questions.teaching_point: In a heparin-exposed patient with isolated thrombocytopenia, absence of the TTP pentad (MAHA, renal, neurologic findings, fever) argues against TTP and its ADAMTS13/PLASMIC-based emergent TPE pathway, supporting an immune thrombocytopenia process instead.

source_references.supports: Describes use of the PLASMIC score and ADAMTS13 results to differentiate TTP from other microangiopathies while confirmatory testing is pending.

source_references.locator: Domain I, Major Concepts
source_references.supports: Groups HIT with ITP under Immune Thrombocytopenia, supporting the inference that excluding TTP points toward this classification.

review.notes: Core content is well grounded: TTP pentad (SRC-019, SRC-023 both state MAHA/thrombocytopenia/neuro/renal/fever), PLASMIC score + ADAMTS13 differentiation while pending (SRC-023 'TTP Differentiation'), and TPE as emergent TTP therapy (SRC-019, SRC-010, SRC-023 ASFA Category I) are all directly supported. Distractors B (WAIHA DAT/eluate), C (TACO presentation), D (bacterial sepsis/TTBI presentation), and E (PCH/Donath-Landsteiner) are each directly and accurately sourced from SRC-019/SRC-045. The one weak spot is decorative, not load-bearing: the explanation's closing claim that excluding TTP 's

questions.stem: A patient with confirmed vitamin K deficiency is found to have decreased activity of coagulation factors II, VII, IX, and X on factor assay testing. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiologic basis for these laboratory abnormalities?

options.text: Simultaneous pathologic activation of the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems consumes platelets, fibrinogen, and clotting factors
An autoantibody develops that neutralizes the biologic activity of a single clotting factor
Global hepatic synthetic failure reduces nearly all coagulation factors except Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor
Hepatocytes continue to synthesize factors II, VII, IX, and X, but the resulting proteins are biologically nonfunctional (PIVKA — proteins induced by vitamin K absence)
Hepatocytes synthesize a reduced total quantity of factors II, VII, IX, and X

questions.explanation_correct: The source states that vitamin K deficiency 'leads to the production of nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA—proteins induced by Vitamin K absence).' This is a qualitative/functional defect — the liver still makes the protein — rather than a failure to synthesize the protein at all, which is the key distinction tested here.

option_explanations.E: Incorrect and a classic board trap: the source distinguishes vitamin K deficiency (production of nonfunctional PIVKA proteins) from a true quantitative decrease in factor synthesis, which is instead how the source characterizes liver disease.
option_explanations.D: Correct: matches the source's exact description of vitamin K deficiency producing nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X, termed PIVKA.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this describes liver disease, which the source characterizes as decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and vWF, plus impaired clearance of activated factors — not vitamin K deficiency.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this describes DIC, a consumption coagulopathy with pathologic activation of both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems requiring platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement — a distinct entity from vitamin K deficiency.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: this describes a Factor VIII inhibitor (high-titer autoantibody neutralizing Factor VIII), a separate coagulation disorder managed with bypassing agents, not the mechanism of vitamin K deficiency.

questions.teaching_point: Vitamin K deficiency is a qualitative defect (nonfunctional PIVKA proteins), which must be distinguished from the quantitative synthetic failure of liver disease and the consumptive process of DIC.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency produces nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA) rather than a quantitative synthesis defect.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Core Concepts and Management Logic
source_references.supports: Liver disease causes decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII/vWF and impaired clearance of activated factors; DIC is a consumption coagulopathy requiring platelet/plasma/cryoprecipitate replacement; Factor VIII inhibitors are managed with bypassing agents — used to construct distractors.

review.notes: Core claim is verbatim from SRC-002 Node 47: 'Vitamin K Deficiency: Leads to the production of nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA...)' which directly supports keyed answer B, and clearly distinguishes it from A (liver-disease pattern: 'decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and vWF') and D (DIC: 'both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically active'), both accurately drawn from the same node. However, option E's rationale characterizes Factor VIII inhibitors as a 'high-titer autoantibody' — the source only says 'High-titer inhibitors

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient develops prolongation of both the PT and aPTT. The clinical team is trying to distinguish a vitamin K–dependent coagulation factor deficiency (or warfarin effect) from disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) as the cause. Which combination of coagulation factor assay and complete blood count findings would best support vitamin K deficiency (or warfarin effect) rather than DIC?

options.text: Reduced activity of Factors II, VII, IX, and X together with thrombocytopenia and hypofibrinogenemia requiring platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement
Reduced activity of Factors II, VII, IX, and X with a normal platelet count and normal fibrinogen
Isolated reduction of Factor VIII activity with a normal PT
Diffusely reduced activity of nearly all coagulation factors, including those not dependent on vitamin K, with sparing of Factor VIII

questions.explanation_correct: The source specifically limits the vitamin K deficiency factor pattern to nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA), with no mention of platelet or fibrinogen consumption. DIC, by contrast, is described as a consumption coagulopathy requiring replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate — implying concurrent thrombocytopenia and hypofibrinogenemia not expected in isolated vitamin K deficiency.

option_explanations.B: Correct: an isolated reduction in the vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) with preserved platelet count and fibrinogen matches the source's description of vitamin K deficiency, which does not involve consumption of platelets or fibrinogen.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this pattern, with thrombocytopenia and hypofibrinogenemia needing platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement, matches the source's description of DIC as a consumption coagulopathy, not vitamin K deficiency.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this pattern matches the source's description of liver disease (decreased synthesis of nearly all factors except Factor VIII/vWF), which affects a broader range of factors than the four vitamin K-dependent factors.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: isolated Factor VIII reduction with a normal PT is inconsistent with vitamin K deficiency, which the source describes as affecting Factors II, VII, IX, and X (extrinsic and intrinsic pathway factors reflected by both PT and aPTT), not Factor VIII.

questions.teaching_point: Coagulation factor assay patterns, combined with platelet count and fibrinogen, help distinguish the isolated vitamin K-dependent factor deficiency of vitamin K deficiency/warfarin effect from the broader consumptive process of DIC or the global synthetic failure of liver disease.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency's factor pattern is limited to nonfunctional II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA); used to build the correct option and contrast with broader patterns.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: DIC is a consumption coagulopathy requiring replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate, implying concurrent thrombocytopenia and hypofibrinogenemia; liver disease reduces nearly all factors except Factor VIII/vWF — used to build distractors.

review.notes: SRC-002 Node 47 explicitly supports the factor pattern for vitamin K deficiency (nonfunctional II, VII, IX, X) and for DIC (consumption coagulopathy 'requiring replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate') and for liver disease (near-global factor reduction sparing Factor VIII/vWF). However, the key differentiator that makes option A correct — that vitamin K deficiency/warfarin effect presents with a NORMAL platelet count and NORMAL fibrinogen — is never explicitly stated in the source. The source's DIC description also does not explicitly state 'thrombocytopenia' and 'hypofibrinogen

questions.stem: A patient on chronic warfarin therapy presents with active bleeding and a markedly elevated INR. The transfusion medicine service is consulted regarding the most appropriate product for urgent reversal of the anticoagulant effect. Which of the following is the best choice?

options.text: Recombinant Factor VIIa
Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC)
Platelet concentrate
Cryoprecipitate
Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP)

questions.explanation_correct: Both approved sources identify PCC as the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) such as warfarin in bleeding patients, noting that PCC is preferred over FFP because of its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the sources state PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent VKA (warfarin) reversal in bleeding patients, preferred over FFP due to lower volume and faster INR normalization.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: FFP can replace vitamin K-dependent factors but the source explicitly states PCC is preferred over FFP for urgent warfarin reversal because of PCC's lower volume and faster INR correction.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: cryoprecipitate is used to replace fibrinogen (e.g., in DIC), not the vitamin K-dependent factors depleted by warfarin, so it does not address the anticoagulant effect.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: recombinant Factor VIIa is described in the sources as a bypassing agent for high-titer Factor VIII inhibitors, not as the preferred agent for urgent VKA reversal.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: platelet concentrate treats thrombocytopenia or platelet dysfunction and does not replace the vitamin K-dependent clotting factors suppressed by warfarin.

questions.teaching_point: PCC, not FFP, is the preferred agent for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin in actively bleeding patients, owing to its smaller volume and faster INR correction.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Management Logic
source_references.supports: Urgent warfarin reversal: PCC is preferred over FFP due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 39 – Derivatives & Recombinant Proteins, Key Products
source_references.supports: PCC is the treatment of choice for the urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin in bleeding patients.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Management Logic
source_references.supports: Factor VIII inhibitors are managed with bypassing agents such as PCC or recombinant Factor VIIa — used to construct the recombinant FVIIa distractor as a different indication.

review.notes: Strongly and redundantly grounded: SRC-002 Node 47 states 'Urgent Warfarin Reversal: PCC is preferred over FFP due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR,' and SRC-051 Node 39 independently states 'PCC (Prothrombin Complex Concentrate): Treatment of choice for the urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin in bleeding patients.' Both locators verified verbatim in the source files. Distractors are well-constructed and consistent with the sources: cryoprecipitate is tied to fibrinogen/DIC replacement in SRC-002, recombinant FVIIa is explicitly tied to Factor V

questions.subdomain: Role of Vitamin K in Hemostasis
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man hospitalized for three weeks with poor oral intake and receiving broad-spectrum antibiotics develops new oozing from venipuncture sites. He has no history of liver disease, no signs of sepsis, and no schistocytes or microangiopathic hemolysis on smear. Which of the following best describes the mechanism underlying his coagulopathy?

options.text: Development of a high-titer autoantibody that neutralizes factor VIII activity
Pathologic, simultaneous activation of the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems producing a consumption coagulopathy
Decreased hepatic synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, combined with impaired clearance of activated factors
Deficiency of the ADAMTS13 protease leading to accumulation of ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers
The liver continues to produce factors II, VII, IX, and X, but without adequate vitamin K these are released as nonfunctional proteins (PIVKA — proteins induced by vitamin K absence)

questions.explanation_correct: In vitamin K deficiency (here from prolonged poor intake plus antibiotic suppression of gut flora), the liver keeps synthesizing factors II, VII, IX, and X, but without vitamin K these proteins are secreted in a nonfunctional form termed PIVKA. This is a selective post-synthetic defect of the vitamin K-dependent factors, distinct from the broader factor loss of liver disease, the consumptive process of DIC, an acquired factor VIII inhibitor, or ADAMTS13 deficiency in TTP.

option_explanations.E: Correct — matches the source's stated mechanism: vitamin K deficiency yields nonfunctional PIVKA forms of factors II, VII, IX, and X.
option_explanations.C: This is the liver disease mechanism (broad factor loss sparing VIII/vWF), explicitly excluded by this patient's lack of liver disease history.
option_explanations.B: This is the DIC mechanism (consumption plus fibrinolysis); nothing in the vignette suggests sepsis or a consumptive trigger.
option_explanations.A: This describes an acquired factor VIII inhibitor, an antibody-mediated process unrelated to vitamin K status.
option_explanations.D: This describes TTP, which the stem explicitly excludes (no microangiopathic hemolysis) and which is unrelated to vitamin K-dependent factor synthesis.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency causes production of nonfunctional (PIVKA) forms of factors II, VII, IX, and X.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Liver disease decreases synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and vWF, with impaired clearance of activated factors.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: DIC is a consumption coagulopathy with concurrent pathologic procoagulant and fibrinolytic activation.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Management Logic
source_references.supports: High-titer factor VIII inhibitors are an antibody-mediated coagulopathy managed with bypassing agents.

source_references.locator: Domain X, Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: TTP involves ADAMTS13 protease deficiency with profound thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolysis.

review.notes: Core mechanism claims are directly grounded in SRC-002 Node 47 (line 39: PIVKA definition for vit K deficiency; line 35: liver disease sparing FVIII/vWF; line 37: DIC as consumption coagulopathy; line 43: FVIII inhibitors managed with bypassing agents) and SRC-031 (line 27/77: TTP = profound thrombocytopenia + ADAMTS13 deficiency). Two minor unsourced-but-standard fill-ins: (1) the vignette's causal link between prolonged poor intake + broad-spectrum antibiotics and vitamin K deficiency is not explicitly stated in these four sources (it is standard external knowledge, non-contradictory); (2) c

questions.subdomain: Role of Vitamin K in Hemostasis
questions.stem: A patient who has been NPO for several weeks while receiving broad-spectrum antibiotics without nutritional supplementation develops mucosal bleeding. Coagulation testing shows a prolonged PT and a prolonged aPTT. Factor V activity is normal, and the platelet count and fibrinogen level are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this laboratory pattern?

options.text: TTP, characterized by profound thrombocytopenia and requiring plasma exchange to replace ADAMTS13
DIC, a consumption coagulopathy requiring replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate
Liver disease, causing decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and von Willebrand factor
An acquired factor VIII inhibitor managed with bypassing agents such as PCC or recombinant factor VIIa
Vitamin K deficiency, producing nonfunctional factors II, VII, IX, and X while sparing factor V and fibrinogen, which are not vitamin K-dependent

questions.explanation_correct: Isolated reduction of the vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) with a normal factor V, fibrinogen, and platelet count is most consistent with vitamin K deficiency producing nonfunctional PIVKA forms of those four factors. Liver disease would be expected to reduce nearly all clotting factors, which should include factor V — inconsistent with the normal factor V given here. DIC would also show thrombocytopenia and hypofibrinogenemia given its consumptive, plasma/cryoprecipitate-requiring nature, a factor VIII inhibitor would not lower II, VII, IX, or X, and TTP requires marked thrombocytopenia, none of which fit this profile.

option_explanations.E: Correct — normal factor V and fibrinogen with reduced II, VII, IX, X is the selective pattern expected from vitamin K-dependent factor dysfunction.
option_explanations.C: Liver disease decreases nearly all clotting factors, which would be expected to include factor V — contradicted by the normal factor V activity given.
option_explanations.B: DIC is a consumption coagulopathy needing platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement — contradicted by the normal platelet count and fibrinogen level.
option_explanations.D: A factor VIII inhibitor is an isolated antibody problem against factor VIII; it does not explain reduced II, VII, IX, and X.
option_explanations.A: TTP is characterized by profound thrombocytopenia — contradicted by the normal platelet count given.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency selectively yields nonfunctional (PIVKA) factors II, VII, IX, and X.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Liver disease decreases nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and vWF, implying broader factor loss including factor V.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: DIC requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate, implying consumption of platelets and fibrinogen.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Management Logic
source_references.supports: Factor VIII inhibitors are managed with bypassing agents as a distinct antibody-mediated process.

source_references.locator: Domain X, Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: TTP presents with profound thrombocytopenia and requires plasma exchange to replace ADAMTS13.

review.notes: The load-bearing lab fact used to clinch the diagnosis — 'Factor V activity is normal' as the differentiator between vitamin K deficiency and liver disease — is NOT explicitly stated in any of the four approved sources. SRC-002 Node 47 only says vitamin K deficiency produces nonfunctional II, VII, IX, X (silent on factor V/fibrinogen status) and that liver disease decreases 'nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and vWF' (factor V is never named, only inferable by omission that it's NOT excepted from the liver-disease list). The question's premise that factor V is spared in vit-K defi

questions.subdomain: Role of Vitamin K in Hemostasis
questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman on long-term warfarin therapy for atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with active hematemesis and hypotension. Her INR is markedly elevated. The transfusion medicine physician is consulted regarding the most appropriate product for urgent hemostatic correction. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold factor replacement and reassess the INR in 24 hours, as would be appropriate for a non-bleeding patient with a mildly elevated INR
Administer recombinant factor VIIa alone as first-line therapy for vitamin K antagonist reversal
Administer cryoprecipitate to replace fibrinogen and factor VIII
Administer fresh frozen plasma (FFP) as the first-line product
Administer prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC)

questions.explanation_correct: PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin in actively bleeding patients, and is preferred over FFP because of its lower infusion volume and faster normalization of the INR — both important considerations in a hypotensive patient with active gastrointestinal bleeding.

option_explanations.E: Correct — PCC is specifically identified as the treatment of choice for urgent VKA reversal in a bleeding patient, favored over FFP for its lower volume and faster INR correction.
option_explanations.D: FFP is not first-line here; the sources state PCC is preferred over FFP for urgent warfarin reversal due to lower volume and faster INR normalization.
option_explanations.A: Deferring correction is the recommended approach only for a non-bleeding patient with a mildly elevated INR before an elective procedure — not for an actively, hemodynamically significant bleeding patient.
option_explanations.C: Cryoprecipitate replaces fibrinogen, factor VIII, factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor, not the vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) responsible for this coagulopathy.
option_explanations.B: Recombinant factor VIIa is described as a bypassing agent for high-titer factor VIII inhibitors, not as first-line therapy for VKA/warfarin reversal, for which PCC is specified as the treatment of choice.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 39, Key Products
source_references.supports: PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists like warfarin in bleeding patients.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Management Logic
source_references.supports: PCC is preferred over FFP for urgent warfarin reversal due to lower volume and faster INR normalization.

source_references.locator: Domain X, Common Board Traps
source_references.supports: Transfusing plasma to correct a mildly elevated INR (about 1.3-1.6) in a non-bleeding patient before a procedure is usually ineffective and not recommended.

source_references.locator: Domain X, Definitions
source_references.supports: Cryoprecipitate is a concentrated source of fibrinogen, factor VIII, factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Management Logic
source_references.supports: Factor VIII inhibitors are managed with bypassing agents such as PCC or recombinant factor VIIa.

review.notes: Fully grounded. SRC-051 Node 39 (line 93) states PCC is 'Treatment of choice for the urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin in bleeding patients.' SRC-002 Node 47 (line 45) states PCC is preferred over FFP 'due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR,' directly supporting both the key and the rejection of option B. SRC-031 (line 65) verbatim supports the option C distractor rationale ('mildly elevated INR... non-bleeding patient... usually ineffective and not recommended'), and SRC-031 (line 19) defines cryoprecipitate as fibrinogen/FVIII/FXIII/vWF, corre

questions.subdomain: Other Causes of Vitamin K Deficiency
questions.stem: A 74-year-old man has been hospitalized for 4 weeks with minimal oral intake. He has not received warfarin, heparin, or any other anticoagulant. He develops new gingival bleeding and scattered ecchymoses, and coagulation studies show markedly reduced activity of factors II, VII, IX, and X. Which of the following best explains the mechanism responsible for this patient's coagulopathy?

options.text: High-titer autoantibodies form against factor VIII and neutralize its procoagulant activity
Impaired hepatic clearance of activated coagulation factors allows their ongoing consumption
Simultaneous pathologic activation of the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems consumes the clotting factors
The liver fails to synthesize nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and von Willebrand factor
Vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, and X are synthesized as nonfunctional (PIVKA) proteins because of vitamin K absence

questions.explanation_correct: Vitamin K deficiency, whatever the precipitating cause (here, prolonged inadequate intake rather than anticoagulant therapy), results in production of nonfunctional forms of the vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, and X, termed PIVKA (proteins induced by vitamin K absence). This selective defect in these four factors, rather than global factor loss or consumption, explains the bleeding diathesis in this non-anticoagulated, non-cirrhotic patient.

option_explanations.D: This describes the mechanism of liver disease, which depresses nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and von Willebrand factor - a broader pattern than the selective II/VII/IX/X defect described in this vignette.
option_explanations.E: Correct: vitamin K absence yields nonfunctional PIVKA forms of factors II, VII, IX, and X, matching the selective factor pattern and non-anticoagulated, non-hepatic clinical picture given.
option_explanations.C: This describes disseminated intravascular coagulation, a consumptive coagulopathy from simultaneous procoagulant and fibrinolytic activation, not the mechanism of isolated vitamin K deficiency.
option_explanations.B: This describes another feature attributed to liver disease (impaired clearance of activated factors), not the mechanism by which vitamin K absence produces coagulopathy.
option_explanations.A: This describes an acquired factor VIII inhibitor, an antibody-mediated process unrelated to vitamin K status and not consistent with reduced factors II, VII, IX, and X.

questions.teaching_point: Vitamin K deficiency of any cause (not only warfarin therapy) selectively renders factors II, VII, IX, and X nonfunctional (PIVKA), distinguishing it mechanistically from the broader factor loss of liver disease and from the consumptive process of DIC.

questions.subdomain: Other Causes of Vitamin K Deficiency
questions.stem: A woman with several weeks of poor nutritional intake, no history of chronic liver disease, and normal transaminases and albumin is not taking warfarin or any other anticoagulant. She has a prolonged PT and PTT with active mucosal bleeding, and the team wants to confirm that vitamin K deficiency rather than an occult hepatic synthetic defect explains her coagulopathy before starting therapy. Which additional coagulation result would best support vitamin K deficiency over liver disease as the underlying cause?

options.text: Factor V activity that is markedly reduced
Factor V activity within the normal reference range
Factor VIII activity that is markedly reduced
Platelet count that is markedly reduced with schistocytes on the peripheral smear

questions.explanation_correct: Liver disease depresses synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, which by extension includes factor V. Vitamin K deficiency, in contrast, selectively renders only factors II, VII, IX, and X nonfunctional, leaving factor V (hepatically synthesized but not vitamin K-dependent) intact. A normal factor V activity in the setting of a prolonged PT/PTT therefore favors vitamin K deficiency over hepatic synthetic failure.

option_explanations.B: Correct: factor V is not among the factors affected by vitamin K deficiency, so a normal value argues against a hepatic synthetic defect (which would depress nearly all factors) and supports isolated vitamin K deficiency.
option_explanations.A: A markedly reduced factor V activity would instead point toward liver disease, since factor V synthesis is liver-dependent but not vitamin K-dependent, and liver disease depresses nearly all factors.
option_explanations.C: Factor VIII is specifically an exception preserved in liver disease per the source; a markedly reduced factor VIII would not be explained by either vitamin K deficiency or typical liver disease and would not help distinguish between them.
option_explanations.D: A markedly reduced platelet count with schistocytes suggests a consumptive process such as DIC, a distinct disorder requiring platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement, not the isolated vitamin K deficiency picture being evaluated here.

questions.teaching_point: Because vitamin K deficiency spares factor V while liver disease does not, a normal factor V activity in a patient with a prolonged PT/PTT helps distinguish isolated vitamin K deficiency from a hepatic synthetic coagulopathy.

source_references.locator: advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md, line 35
source_references.supports: Liver disease decreases synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except factor VIII and vWF, implying factor V is also depressed in liver disease.

source_references.locator: advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md, line 39
source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency is stated to affect only factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA), implying factor V is spared, used to infer the factor V differentiator.

source_references.locator: advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md, line 37
source_references.supports: DIC is described as requiring platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement, supporting the platelet/schistocyte distractor as pointing to a consumptive process rather than vitamin K deficiency.

review.notes: The core logic (factor V is spared in vitamin K deficiency but depressed in liver disease) is not stated as a single differentiating pearl anywhere in the sources -- it is synthesized by combining two separate sentences (SRC-002 line 35: liver disease depresses 'nearly all' factors except VIII/vWF; line 39: vitamin K deficiency affects only II/VII/IX/X) and inferring factor V's behavior by exclusion in each. The inference is medically standard and internally consistent with the sources, but it is not an explicit source statement, matching the item author's own 'low' support_confidence self-rat

questions.subdomain: Other Causes of Vitamin K Deficiency
questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with several months of severe fat malabsorption from short bowel syndrome, and no anticoagulant use, is admitted with active gastrointestinal bleeding and a markedly prolonged PT/INR attributed to vitamin K deficiency. Intravenous vitamin K is administered, but because bleeding is active and ongoing, the transfusion medicine service is asked to select a blood product for immediate correction of her coagulopathy while the vitamin K takes effect. Which of the following is the most appropriate product?

options.text: Albumin
Recombinant antithrombin (rhAT)
Fresh frozen plasma
Cryoprecipitate
Prothrombin complex concentrate

questions.explanation_correct: Fresh frozen plasma supplies the full range of coagulation factors, including the vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, and X, and can provide urgent factor replacement for a multi-factor coagulopathy that is not caused by a vitamin K antagonist. The sources describe prothrombin complex concentrate's role specifically as urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin, a labeled indication that does not apply here since this patient is not on a VKA; the other listed products target unrelated deficiencies.

option_explanations.C: Correct: FFP provides broad-spectrum coagulation factor replacement, addressing deficient vitamin K-dependent factors regardless of the underlying cause of the vitamin K deficiency, unlike products with narrower or unrelated indications.
option_explanations.E: PCC is described as the treatment of choice specifically for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists like warfarin in bleeding patients; this patient's vitamin K deficiency is from malabsorption, not VKA therapy, so this labeled indication does not apply.
option_explanations.D: Cryoprecipitate replenishes fibrinogen, factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, and factor XIII, none of which are the vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) deficient in this patient.
option_explanations.B: Recombinant antithrombin is indicated for patients with congenital antithrombin deficiency undergoing surgery, an unrelated clinical scenario with no role in vitamin K deficiency.
option_explanations.A: Albumin is a colloid used as replacement fluid in therapeutic plasma exchange and contains no coagulation factors, so it cannot correct a coagulation factor deficiency.

questions.teaching_point: PCC's stated indication in these sources is urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists like warfarin; for vitamin K deficiency from non-anticoagulant causes such as malabsorption, FFP remains the appropriate acute source of multi-factor replacement while vitamin K repletion takes effect.

source_references.locator: advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md, line 45
source_references.supports: States PCC is preferred over FFP specifically for urgent warfarin (VKA) reversal due to lower volume and faster INR normalization, implying FFP is the alternative factor-replacement product outside that specific indication.

source_references.locator: specialized-transfusion-medicine-and-clinical-quality-systems.md, line 93
source_references.supports: States PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists like warfarin in bleeding patients, restricting its stated labeled use.

source_references.supports: Describes fibrinogen concentrate/cryoprecipitate's role in fibrinogen replacement and rhAT's indication for congenital antithrombin deficiency in surgery, and albumin's role as a non-coagulant TPE replacement fluid, used to build distractors.

source_references.supports: DIC management requiring platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement supports plasma's general role as a source of coagulation factor replacement.

review.notes: Core reasoning is well grounded: SRC-051 line 93 explicitly restricts PCC's stated indication to 'urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin,' and SRC-002 line 45 frames PCC vs FFP specifically in the warfarin-reversal context, so a non-anticoagulant-related vitamin K deficiency (malabsorption) falls outside PCC's sourced indication, leaving FFP as the correct broad-spectrum factor replacement -- rhAT (line 97) and albumin (line 103) distractors are directly sourced. However, the cryoprecipitate rationale claims it 'replenishes fibrinogen, factor VIII, von Willebrand factor,

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is transfused a unit of RBCs that is later discovered to be ABO-incompatible due to a bedside identification error. Within an hour he develops fever, flank pain, and hypotension; his post-reaction plasma is pink-tinged compared to baseline, and the DAT shows a mixed-field pattern. Over the next several hours he develops diffuse oozing from IV sites and surgical drains. Coagulation studies now show a prolonged PT/PTT, a falling platelet count, and a falling fibrinogen level. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of this new coagulopathy?

options.text: Vitamin K deficiency producing nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA proteins)

Complement fixation by the incompatible antibody-antigen reaction has caused intravascular hemolysis and cytokine release, driving simultaneous pathologic activation of the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems
Decreased hepatic synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, combined with impaired clearance of activated factors
Development of a high-titer autoantibody inhibitor against Factor VIII activity
Formation of anti-HPA-1a antibodies causing immune-mediated destruction of the patient's platelets

questions.explanation_correct: The source material states that in AHTR, IgM or IgG antibodies fix complement, leading to intravascular hemolysis, cytokine release, and DIC. DIC itself is defined as a consumption coagulopathy in which both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically activated simultaneously, which accounts for the falling platelets, falling fibrinogen, and prolonged PT/PTT with diffuse bleeding seen here.

option_explanations.B: Correct: this is the sourced pathophysiologic chain linking AHTR (complement fixation, cytokine release) to DIC as a consumption coagulopathy with pathologic activation of both procoagulant and fibrinolytic pathways.
option_explanations.C: This describes the mechanism of liver disease coagulopathy (decreased synthesis of most factors except VIII/vWF, impaired clearance of activated factors), a distinct entity from the acute post-transfusion process described here.
option_explanations.E: This describes post-transfusion purpura, which is immune-mediated, occurs 1-2 weeks after transfusion, and typically affects parous females — a different timeline and mechanism than the hours-later coagulopathy following AHTR.
option_explanations.A: This describes vitamin K deficiency, an isolated defect in synthesis of the vitamin K-dependent factors; it does not explain the acute, multi-pathway consumption coagulopathy (falling platelets and fibrinogen together) precipitated by a hemolytic reaction.
option_explanations.D: This describes an acquired Factor VIII inhibitor, a distinct entity managed with bypassing agents; it does not account for the combined thrombocytopenia, hypofibrinogenemia, and prolonged PT/PTT following an AHTR.

questions.teaching_point: DIC following AHTR arises from complement-driven cytokine release causing simultaneous consumption of procoagulant and fibrinolytic factors, distinct from liver disease, vitamin K deficiency, PTP, or isolated factor inhibitors.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 31 (AHTR Workup) - Pathophysiology; Subdomain Node 33 (Delayed Reactions) - PTP
source_references.supports: AHTR pathophysiology: complement fixation by IgM/IgG antibodies causes intravascular hemolysis, cytokine release, and DIC; PTP occurs 1-2 weeks post-transfusion in parous females via anti-HPA-1a.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 - Coagulation Defects & Management
source_references.supports: DIC defined as a consumption coagulopathy with simultaneous pathologic activation of procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems; liver disease spares Factor VIII/vWF; vitamin K deficiency produces PIVKA; Factor VIII inhibitors are a distinct managed entity.

review.notes: Core mechanism is well-grounded: SRC-058 Node 31 states IgM/IgG complement fixation causes intravascular hemolysis, cytokine release, and DIC; SRC-002 Node 47 defines DIC as simultaneous pathologic activation of procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems requiring platelet/plasma/cryo replacement. All four distractor rationales (liver disease sparing FVIII/vWF, PTP timeline/anti-HPA-1a, vitamin K deficiency/PIVKA, FVIII inhibitor managed with bypassing agents) are directly and accurately sourced from SRC-002 Node 47 and SRC-058 Node 33. However, 'flank pain' as a presenting AHTR symptom is not stat

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with septic shock and DIC has diffuse mucosal bleeding and a platelet count of 8,000/µL. He receives one apheresis platelet unit. The 1-hour posttransfusion corrected count increment (CCI) is adequate, but by 18-24 hours the platelet count has fallen back toward baseline. Which of the following is the best interpretation of this laboratory pattern?

options.text: A discordant 1-hour versus 24-hour CCI is not a recognized pattern and should prompt repeat testing before any interpretation
The pattern reflects nonimmune platelet consumption related to the patient's underlying DIC; HLA antibody testing is not indicated at this time
The pattern is diagnostic of HLA Class I alloimmunization; obtain an HLA antibody screen and provide HLA-matched platelets
The pattern indicates anti-HPA-1a-mediated platelet destruction; obtain HPA-antigen-negative platelet units
The result confirms platelet crossmatch incompatibility; perform solid-phase platelet crossmatching before further transfusions

questions.explanation_correct: The source material specifically states that a good 1-hour CCI with a poor 24-hour result suggests nonimmune consumption (e.g., sepsis/DIC) rather than HLA antibodies, and that approximately 80% of platelet refractoriness cases are nonimmune, with DIC explicitly listed as a cause. Immune-mediated refractoriness, by contrast, is defined by a poor 1-hour CCI (<5,000-7,500/µL) on two consecutive transfusions, which is not the pattern seen here.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the sourced timing logic (adequate 1-hour recovery, poor 24-hour survival) is the classic pattern of nonimmune consumption such as DIC or sepsis, not alloimmunization, so HLA workup is not indicated.
option_explanations.C: Immune refractoriness from HLA Class I antibodies characteristically produces a poor 1-hour CCI, which is the opposite of what is described; this option misapplies the timing logic.
option_explanations.D: HPA antibody-mediated (e.g., anti-HPA-1a) destruction is also an immune mechanism expected to impair the early (1-hour) increment, not selectively the 24-hour survival; this pattern does not match.
option_explanations.A: The good-1-hour/poor-24-hour pattern is explicitly described in the source as a recognized and interpretable finding indicating nonimmune consumption, not a testing artifact.
option_explanations.E: Platelet crossmatching is a tool used to evaluate suspected immune-mediated refractoriness, not a confirmatory finding produced by this consumption pattern; it is not the next indicated step here.

questions.teaching_point: In platelet refractoriness, the 1-hour versus 24-hour CCI pattern distinguishes immune (poor 1-hour) from nonimmune consumption such as DIC or sepsis (adequate 1-hour, poor 24-hour); this determines whether HLA/HPA workup is warranted.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 10 - Multifactorial Etiology; Diagnostic Approach; Management; Common Board Traps (1-hour vs 24-hour)
source_references.supports: Approximately 80% of platelet refractoriness is nonimmune (sepsis, DIC, splenomegaly); a good 1-hour CCI with poor 24-hour result indicates nonimmune consumption rather than HLA antibodies; immune refractoriness = poor 1-hour CCI (<5,000-7,500/µL) on two consecutive transfusions; platelet crossmatching (SPRCA) is used for suspected immune refractoriness.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations - Platelet Refractoriness
source_references.supports: Platelet refractoriness can be immune-mediated (HLA or HPA antibodies) or nonimmune-mediated (e.g., sepsis, fever, splenomegaly, DIC).

review.notes: Directly and precisely sourced: SRC-026 Node 10 explicitly states 'poor results [at 18-24h] with a good 1-hour count suggest nonimmune consumption' and 'a good 1-hour increment but a bad 24-hour result... is nonimmune (e.g., sepsis/DIC), not HLA antibodies'; ~80% nonimmune figure and DIC as a listed nonimmune cause are both directly sourced (SRC-026 and SRC-031). Distractors B/C correctly invert the timing logic per source; option E's SPRCA description matches source use-case (immune workup tool, not a diagnostic conclusion). Option D ('not a recognized pattern') is a fairly weak/throwaway dis

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with septic shock and DIC has diffuse oozing from surgical sites and IV catheters. Laboratory studies show a fibrinogen level of 80 mg/dL, a platelet count of 15,000/µL, and prolonged PT and PTT. Which of the following is the most appropriate transfusion strategy?

options.text: Transfuse plasma alone, since Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor levels remain preserved and cryoprecipitate is unnecessary
Transfuse cryoprecipitate, plasma, and platelets, with dosing guided by serial monitoring of fibrinogen and platelet counts
Administer prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) as first-line therapy, since it is preferred over plasma for rapid correction of coagulopathy
Administer IVIG as first-line treatment for the thrombocytopenia
Withhold platelet transfusion, since DIC-associated thrombocytopenia is immune-mediated and transfused platelets will be rapidly destroyed, similar to post-transfusion purpura

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that DIC management requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate, with hemostatic support directed by monitoring fibrinogen and platelet levels. This patient's fibrinogen of 80 mg/dL falls below the 100-150 mg/dL threshold associated with diffuse microvascular bleeding, supporting cryoprecipitate use (a pool of 10 units raises fibrinogen by roughly 50-70 mg/dL) alongside plasma and platelets for the active bleeding and consumptive coagulopathy.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the sourced DIC management approach — combined platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement guided by serial fibrinogen and platelet monitoring, appropriate given the bleeding and fibrinogen below the 100-150 mg/dL bleeding threshold.
option_explanations.A: The preserved-Factor-VIII/vWF rationale describes liver disease, not DIC; in DIC, fibrinogen is also consumed, so plasma alone would not adequately or rapidly correct this patient's hypofibrinogenemia the way cryoprecipitate would.
option_explanations.E: DIC-associated thrombocytopenia is explicitly a nonimmune-mediated cause of platelet consumption, unlike post-transfusion purpura, which is immune-mediated (anti-HPA-1a); withholding platelet support in an actively bleeding patient with DIC is not supported.
option_explanations.C: PCC is specifically preferred over plasma for urgent warfarin reversal, not as first-line therapy for DIC, which the sources state requires platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement rather than a factor concentrate alone.
option_explanations.D: IVIG is the treatment of choice for post-transfusion purpura, not for DIC-associated thrombocytopenia, which is managed with component replacement rather than immunomodulatory therapy.

questions.teaching_point: DIC with active bleeding and hypofibrinogenemia below ~100-150 mg/dL is managed with combined platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement guided by serial monitoring — not with PCC (reserved for warfarin reversal) or IVIG (reserved for PTP).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 - Coagulation Defects & Management
source_references.supports: DIC requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate; PCC is preferred over FFP specifically for urgent warfarin reversal; liver disease (not DIC) spares Factor VIII and vWF synthesis.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 31 - Management; Subdomain Node 33 - Management
source_references.supports: Hemostatic support for suspected DIC = monitor fibrinogen and platelets, transfusing as indicated; PTP is treated with IVIG as first-line therapy.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations; Management - Cryoprecipitate Dosing
source_references.supports: Hypofibrinogenemia below 100-150 mg/dL causes diffuse microvascular bleeding; a cryoprecipitate pool of 10 units raises fibrinogen by approximately 50-70 mg/dL; DIC is listed as a nonimmune cause of platelet consumption/refractoriness, distinct from immune mechanisms.

review.notes: Fully grounded and squarely on-topic for the DIC blueprint node. SRC-002 Node 47 explicitly states DIC 'requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate'; SRC-058 Node 31 management states hemostatic support = 'monitor fibrinogen and platelets, transfusing as indicated'; SRC-031 gives the 100-150 mg/dL bleeding threshold and the 10-unit cryo pool raising fibrinogen ~50-70 mg/dL, matching the stem's fibrinogen of 80 mg/dL with active bleeding. Distractors are each correctly and specifically grounded as belonging to other entities: plasma-alone/FVIII-vWF-sparing is the liver-diseas

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is started on antithrombotic therapy following an acute myocardial infarction. On hospital day 3 he develops oozing from a peripheral IV site and gingival bleeding. His platelet count, PT, aPTT, and fibrinogen level are all within normal limits. Which of the following mechanisms best explains this patient's bleeding?

options.text: Autoantibody-mediated platelet destruction, as occurs in immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)
A coagulation factor deficiency related to vitamin K deficiency or liver disease
Alloantibody-mediated platelet destruction directed against the HPA-1a antigen
A qualitative platelet defect induced by the antithrombotic drug, impairing platelet function without reducing platelet number
Formation of heparin-platelet factor 4 immune complexes causing an immune thrombocytopenia

questions.explanation_correct: Because the platelet count, PT, aPTT, and fibrinogen are all normal, mechanisms that reduce platelet number (immune-mediated platelet destruction) or that impair coagulation factors are excluded. The bleeding is best explained by a qualitative platelet function defect caused by the antithrombotic agent — recognized as a distinct bleeding mechanism from factor-deficiency states such as those due to liver disease or vitamin K deficiency.

option_explanations.D: Correct. With a normal platelet count and normal coagulation studies, a drug-induced functional (qualitative) platelet defect from antithrombotic therapy is the best-fitting mechanism, distinct from factor-deficiency causes of bleeding.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. ITP is defined by autoantibodies against platelet antigens producing thrombocytopenia (a decreased count); this patient's platelet count is normal, arguing against an immune destructive process.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Factor deficiency from vitamin K deficiency or liver disease would be expected to prolong PT and/or aPTT; this patient's coagulation studies are normal.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. HPA-1a alloantibodies mediate platelet destruction in neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia and post-transfusion purpura, both of which lower the platelet count; this patient's count is preserved.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Heparin-PF4 immune complexes cause an immune-mediated thrombocytopenia, which would be expected to lower the platelet count rather than leave it normal.
