questions.teaching_point: Drug-induced platelet dysfunction is a qualitative defect (normal count, normal coagulation studies) and must be mechanistically distinguished from quantitative, antibody-mediated platelet disorders (ITP, NAIT/PTP, HIT), which lower the platelet count.

source_references.supports: States bleeding management requires identifying whether the mechanism is factor deficiency or platelet dysfunction, listing antithrombotic therapy among the causes of platelet dysfunction.
Defines ITP as thrombocytopenia caused by autoantibodies directed against platelet antigens.
Identifies HPA-1a as the primary antigen target in neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (NAIT) and post-transfusion purpura (PTP).
Lists heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) alongside ITP as immune thrombocytopenia entities under the Clinical Practice domain.

review.notes: Core premise is grounded only by a single generic sentence in SRC-019 (clinical-foundations, line 9): 'Hemostasis Disorders: Management of bleeding relies on identifying specific factor deficiencies or platelet dysfunction resulting from liver disease, Vitamin K deficiency, or antithrombotic therapy.' None of the sources explicitly state that antithrombotic-drug platelet dysfunction preserves platelet count/normal coagulation studies (a reasonable clinical inference, not literally sourced). ITP definition (SRC-019 line 15) and HPA-1a/NAIT/PTP link (SRC-044 line 67) are well grounded. Option E'

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman taking antithrombotic therapy for atrial fibrillation presents with new-onset easy bruising. Her clinicians are trying to distinguish antithrombotic drug-induced platelet dysfunction (a functional/qualitative defect) from an antibody-mediated thrombocytopenia. Which laboratory/serologic finding would best support an antibody-mediated process rather than a drug-induced functional platelet defect?

options.text: Normal PT, aPTT, and fibrinogen accompanying a normal platelet count
Platelet function testing showing impaired aggregation with a preserved platelet count
A decreased platelet count together with detectable platelet-reactive autoantibodies
A platelet count within normal limits that persists despite continued antithrombotic therapy
A negative result on HLA and HPA platelet antibody testing

questions.explanation_correct: Immune thrombocytopenia is defined by a decreased platelet count caused by autoantibodies directed against platelet antigens. Detecting both thrombocytopenia and platelet-reactive antibodies is the finding that specifically points to an antibody-mediated process, whereas a preserved platelet count with only functional impairment instead points toward a drug-induced qualitative defect.

option_explanations.C: Correct. A low platelet count plus detectable platelet-reactive autoantibodies matches the definition of immune thrombocytopenia and directly indicates an antibody-mediated mechanism.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. A platelet count that stays normal during antithrombotic therapy is the pattern expected with a drug-induced functional defect, not an antibody-mediated process, since immune destruction lowers the count.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Normal coagulation studies and a normal platelet count are nonspecific findings that do not point toward either an antibody-mediated or a drug-induced mechanism.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. A negative HLA/HPA antibody panel argues against, not for, an antibody-mediated process, since these assays are the tools used to identify immune-mediated platelet destruction.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Impaired platelet function testing with a preserved platelet count is the expected pattern of a drug-induced qualitative defect, not an antibody-mediated thrombocytopenia.

questions.teaching_point: The serologic hallmark of antibody-mediated platelet disorders (ITP, NAIT/PTP) is thrombocytopenia plus detectable platelet-reactive antibodies; a normal count with isolated functional impairment instead indicates a drug-induced qualitative defect, for which antibody testing is not the discriminating tool.

source_references.supports: Defines ITP as thrombocytopenia caused by autoantibodies against platelet antigens, establishing the antibody-plus-low-count pattern.
States that HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical for investigating ITP-type platelet destruction.

Identifies HPA-1a as the target antigen in alloantibody-mediated platelet destruction (NAIT/PTP), reinforcing that immune platelet disorders are characterized by a detectable antibody plus a reduced count.

review.notes: ITP definition (autoantibodies + thrombocytopenia, SRC-019) and 'HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical for investigation' (SRC-010) directly support the keyed answer A and option D's rationale. However, option E's premise ('platelet function testing showing impaired aggregation with a preserved platelet count') references a lab methodology (platelet aggregation testing) never mentioned in any of the four sources — an unsourced but plausible clinical detail. Also flagging duplicate-coverage: this item tests essentially the same core discrimination (antibody-mediated thrombocytopenia vs. drug

questions.stem: A patient with antithrombotic drug-induced platelet dysfunction and a platelet count within normal limits is scheduled for an urgent major surgical procedure with substantial expected blood loss, and the surgical team is actively managing intraoperative bleeding. Which of the following best reflects the appropriate approach to platelet support in this patient?

options.text: Defer all blood product support until the platelet count falls below the prophylactic threshold used for stable, non-bleeding patients
Give cryoprecipitate instead, since cryoprecipitate is the appropriate product whenever a patient is bleeding perioperatively
Withhold platelet transfusion because the platelet count exceeds the listed numeric transfusion thresholds

Platelet transfusion can be considered based on clinical bleeding and procedural risk, since the numeric platelet-count thresholds are designed for quantitative thrombocytopenia and do not account for a qualitative (functional) platelet defect
Give fresh frozen plasma instead, since plasma is the appropriate product whenever a patient is bleeding perioperatively

questions.explanation_correct: The standard platelet transfusion triggers (prophylactic <10,000/µL; procedural <50,000/µL for major surgery, <100,000/µL for neurosurgery/ophthalmic surgery) are defined purely by platelet count and are built for quantitative thrombocytopenia. A patient with a normal count but a drug-induced functional platelet defect will not meet any of these numeric triggers, yet may still carry meaningfully increased bleeding risk during major surgery; management should be guided by the clinical bleeding picture and procedural risk rather than by the count-based thresholds alone.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This mechanically applies count-based transfusion thresholds to a qualitative defect they were not designed to address — a normal count does not exclude clinically significant bleeding risk from impaired platelet function.
option_explanations.D: Correct. Because the listed transfusion triggers are quantitative (count-based), they do not directly capture a functional platelet defect; the transfusion decision here should rest on the clinical bleeding and the procedure's bleeding risk.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Plasma transfusion is indicated for a PT or aPTT greater than 1.5x the mid-normal range with active bleeding, not as a default substitute for platelet support in a primary platelet functional defect.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Cryoprecipitate is indicated for a fibrinogen level below 100 mg/dL, not as a routine substitute for platelet support.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The prophylactic threshold (<10,000/µL) applies to stable, non-bleeding patients; this patient is both actively bleeding and undergoing major surgery, so deferring support until that low a count is reached is inappropriate.

questions.teaching_point: Numeric platelet transfusion thresholds are built for quantitative thrombocytopenia; a normal count does not rule out clinically important bleeding risk from a qualitative, drug-induced platelet defect, and product selection (platelets vs. plasma vs. cryoprecipitate) must match the specific hemostatic deficit.

source_references.supports: Gives explicit platelet transfusion triggers: prophylactic <10,000/µL for stable non-bleeding patients; procedural <50,000/µL for major surgery and <100,000/µL for neurosurgery/ophthalmic surgery.
States plasma is indicated for PT or aPTT >1.5x mid-normal range with active bleeding, and cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL.

Frames platelet dysfunction from antithrombotic therapy as a bleeding mechanism distinct from factor deficiency, supporting that its management differs from a purely count-driven quantitative approach.

review.notes: The numeric facts are accurately and fully sourced from SRC-044 (blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-board-curriculum.md, Hemotherapy Triggers section): prophylactic <10,000/µL, procedural <50,000/µL major surgery, <100,000/µL neurosurgery/ophthalmic, plasma for PT/aPTT >1.5x mid-normal with active bleeding, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL — all verified verbatim. However, the item's central inferential claim underlying the keyed answer — that these numeric thresholds 'are designed for quantitative thrombocytopenia and do not account for a qualitative (functional) platelet defect'

questions.stem: The BB/TM curriculum classifies heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) alongside immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) under the same subdomain, "classification and pathogenesis of Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) and Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)." A 60-year-old man receiving unfractionated heparin for five days develops an unexplained fall in platelet count with no active bleeding. Which mechanism best explains the platelet destruction shared by HIT and ITP, distinguishing it from a nonimmune cause of thrombocytopenia?

options.text: Suppression of megakaryocyte platelet production within the bone marrow
Humoral antibody-mediated destruction of platelets, producing shortened platelet survival
Mechanical shearing/fragmentation of platelets, analogous to nonimmune red cell destruction from pumps or small-bore needles
Sequestration of platelets within an enlarged spleen
Dilution of the measured platelet count following large-volume crystalloid resuscitation

questions.explanation_correct: The sources define the core mechanism of immune-mediated cytopenias as humoral antibodies targeting cell antigens and shortening cell survival, and they classify HIT together with ITP under this same immune-thrombocytopenia pathogenesis heading, so both are best explained by antibody-mediated peripheral platelet destruction rather than a production, dilutional, or sequestration problem.

option_explanations.B: Correct — matches the source's description of immune-mediated destruction as humoral-antibody-driven, applied to platelets in the ITP/HIT classification grouping.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — the sources describe immune thrombocytopenias as peripheral antibody-mediated destruction, not a marrow production defect; no marrow-suppression mechanism is described for ITP/HIT.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — this describes a nonimmune process analogous to the mechanical (pump/needle) red cell destruction noted for nonimmune hemolysis, not the antibody-mediated mechanism attributed to ITP/HIT.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — dilution is a nonimmune numerical artifact of fluid administration, not the antibody-mediated destructive mechanism the sources attribute to ITP/HIT.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — splenic sequestration is a distributional problem, not the humoral antibody-mediated destruction mechanism described for immune thrombocytopenias in the sources.

source_references.locator: line 7
source_references.supports: Groups HIT with ITP under the same 'classification and pathogenesis of Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) and Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)' heading, implying a shared immune framework.

source_references.locator: line 5
source_references.supports: States the primary mechanism of immune-mediated destruction is humoral antibodies targeting RBC or platelet antigens, leading to shortened cell survival.

source_references.locator: line 75
source_references.supports: Describes nonimmune mechanical destruction (pumps, small needles) as a distinct, non-antibody mechanism, used here by analogy to build the mechanical-shearing distractor.

review.notes: SRC-010 line 7 does group ITP and HIT under one 'classification and pathogenesis' major-concept heading, and SRC-019 line 5/15 supports 'humoral antibody-mediated destruction, shortened cell survival' as the general immune-cytopenia mechanism for ITP specifically. But no source states that HIT shares this exact peripheral-destruction mechanism with ITP — co-listing under one heading is not the same as a stated shared pathophysiology, and real HIT pathophysiology (PF4/heparin neoantigen, FcyRIIa-mediated platelet activation/consumption, thrombosis risk) is materially different from simple antib

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with a documented history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) has severe, statin-refractory familial hypercholesterolemia and is referred for a course of LDL (lipoprotein) apheresis. The apheresis service is selecting the extracorporeal circuit anticoagulant for his procedure. Which of the following best reflects the correct consideration for this decision?

options.text: Lipoprotein apheresis, unlike most therapeutic apheresis procedures, is typically anticoagulated with heparin rather than ACD-A (citrate), so this patient's HIT history is directly relevant to the anticoagulant choice
Heparin is never used during therapeutic apheresis, so no special precaution regarding his HIT history is needed
The patient's platelet count must first be normalized with platelet transfusion before any apheresis anticoagulant can be selected
Because ACD-A (citrate) is the anticoagulant used for all apheresis procedures, this patient's HIT history has no bearing on the anticoagulant selected
Standard therapeutic plasma exchange, rather than lipoprotein apheresis, is the procedure of concern for a patient with a HIT history

questions.explanation_correct: The apheresis source states that ACD-A (citrate) is the primary anticoagulant for therapeutic apheresis in general, but heparin is specifically used for certain procedures, naming lipoprotein apheresis as an example — making this patient's planned lipoprotein apheresis, and therefore his HIT history, directly relevant to anticoagulant selection.

option_explanations.A: Correct — the source specifically names lipoprotein apheresis as a procedure that uses heparin instead of the usual citrate anticoagulant, making a HIT history pertinent to this decision.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — the source explicitly carves out lipoprotein apheresis as an exception where heparin, not citrate, is used.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — contradicts the source, which explicitly states heparin is used for lipoprotein apheresis.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — the source associates standard TPE with citrate (ACD-A), not heparin, so lipoprotein apheresis, not standard TPE, is the heparin-relevant procedure for this patient.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — no source describes prophylactic platelet transfusion as a prerequisite for apheresis anticoagulant selection; this option does not address the actual heparin-related consideration in the stem.

source_references.locator: line 51
source_references.supports: States ACD-A (citrate) is the primary apheresis anticoagulant while heparin is used for specific procedures such as lipoprotein apheresis.

source_references.locator: line 7
source_references.supports: Establishes HIT as a recognized clinical entity within the curriculum's immune thrombocytopenia classification, providing the clinical premise for the vignette.

review.notes: SRC-023 line 51 directly states 'ACD-A (citrate) is the primary anticoagulant; heparin is used for specific procedures like lipoprotein apheresis,' which squarely supports option A and the explanation. Options B-E are all directly contradicted by or unaddressed in the source (citrate is not universal; heparin is used in some procedures; TPE, not lipoprotein apheresis, is the citrate-anticoagulated procedure; no prophylactic-platelet-transfusion requirement is described anywhere). SRC-010's mention of HIT as a recognized entity provides reasonable premise support. No credible alternative answer

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with newly diagnosed isolated thrombocytopenia is being evaluated for immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The blood bank/reference laboratory is asked to perform HLA and HPA antibody assays as part of the work-up for immune-mediated platelet destruction. Which of the following best explains the biologic basis for including these two antibody assays in this investigation?

options.text: Platelets carry no surface antigens; the thrombocytopenia work-up in ITP therefore relies on bone marrow evaluation rather than antibody testing
Platelets predominantly express HLA class II molecules, so Class II antibody detection is the principal target of the work-up
HPA antibody assays are designed to detect the glycoprotein defect underlying Bernard-Soulier syndrome, which explains the platelet destruction seen in ITP
Immune platelet destruction is confirmed with a Donath-Landsteiner test identifying a biphasic antibody, analogous to the work-up for paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria
Platelets display ABO, HLA class I (A and B), and human platelet antigen (HPA) determinants on their surface, any of which can be bound by antibody and targeted for immune-mediated clearance

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that ITP involves platelet destruction for which HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical, and separately note that platelets carry ABO, HLA class I (A and B), and HPA antigens on their surface. Because these are the antigenic targets actually expressed on the platelet membrane, antibodies directed against them are the biologic rationale for testing HLA and HPA antibodies when investigating immune-mediated thrombocytopenia.

option_explanations.E: Correct: matches the source statement that platelets carry ABO, HLA class I (A and B), and HPA antigens, and that HLA/HPA antibody assays are critical to ITP investigation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources specify HLA class II expression is restricted to antigen-presenting cells (e.g., B lymphocytes, macrophages), not platelets, so Class II is not the relevant platelet target.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect and contradicted by the sources, which explicitly describe platelet surface antigens (ABO, HLA class I, HPA) that make antibody testing relevant.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sources classify Bernard-Soulier syndrome as a congenital platelet function (glycoprotein) disorder, distinct from antibody-mediated destruction detected by HPA antibody assays.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources link the Donath-Landsteiner test and biphasic IgG anti-P antibody specifically to paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria, an RBC-directed process, not platelet antibody investigation.

questions.teaching_point: Platelets express ABO, HLA class I (not class II), and HPA antigens, which is why HLA and HPA antibody assays — not HLA class II or RBC-specific tests like Donath-Landsteiner — are the relevant serologic tools for investigating immune platelet destruction.

source_references.locator: Domain I: Clinical Practice, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States ITP involves platelet destruction where HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical for investigation; also describes the Donath-Landsteiner test/anti-P specificity for PCH used as a distractor.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping
source_references.supports: States platelets carry ABO, HLA class I (A and B), and HPA antigens; classifies Bernard-Soulier and Glanzmann's as congenital platelet function disorders.

source_references.locator: Section 1, MHC Organization (Class I / Class II)
source_references.supports: States HLA class I is expressed on nearly all nucleated cells and platelets, while HLA class II expression is restricted to antigen-presenting cells.

review.notes: Core reasoning is well grounded: SRC-006 states platelets carry ABO, HLA class I (A,B), and HPA antigens; SRC-010 states HLA/HPA antibody assays are critical to ITP investigation; SRC-028 confirms HLA class II is restricted to APCs (correctly sinks option B); SRC-010's Donath-Landsteiner/anti-P/PCH fact correctly sinks option E. However, option D's rationale claims 'the sources classify Bernard-Soulier syndrome as a congenital platelet function (glycoprotein) disorder' — the word 'glycoprotein' is never used in any of the four sources (verified by grep); SRC-006 only says 'congenital (e.g., Be

questions.stem: A patient with suspected immune thrombocytopenia has a platelet antibody detected by solid-phase serologic testing, but the reaction is weak and the reference laboratory has exhausted its panel of HPA-typed reagent platelets needed to confirm antigen specificity and locate compatible donors. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to further characterize the target antigen and identify compatible platelet donors?

options.text: Perform molecular HPA genotyping of donor (and patient) platelets
Repeat the identical serologic panel with the same exhausted reagent set until a stronger reaction is obtained
Order testing for Bernard-Soulier syndrome to rule out a glycoprotein defect
Proceed directly to gamma irradiation of donor platelet units to eliminate the offending antigen
Perform a virtual crossmatch comparing the patient's HLA antibody profile to the donor's documented HLA type

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that molecular HPA genotyping is essential for finding compatible donors precisely in the two scenarios described here: when an antibody reaction is weak or when HPA (serologic) reagents are unavailable. Genotyping bypasses the need for exhausted antisera by identifying the antigen and donor compatibility at the DNA level.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the sources specifically identify genotyping as the tool for finding compatible donors when an antibody is weak or HPA reagents are unavailable — exactly this scenario.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: reusing the same exhausted reagent set does not resolve the reagent shortage or weak-reaction problem described.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: a virtual crossmatch as described in the sources compares HLA antibody profiles to donor HLA type, not HPA specificity, so it does not address an HPA reagent shortage.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: Bernard-Soulier syndrome is a congenital platelet glycoprotein (function) disorder in the sources, unrelated to characterizing an antibody-defined HPA target.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: gamma irradiation in the sources is used to inactivate donor T lymphocytes to prevent TA-GVHD, not to eliminate platelet surface antigens.

questions.teaching_point: When serologic HPA typing is limited by weak reactivity or reagent unavailability, molecular HPA genotyping is the appropriate escalation for confirming antigen specificity and locating compatible platelet donors.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping
source_references.supports: States genotyping is essential for finding compatible donors when an antibody is weak or when HPA reagents are unavailable, and used to resolve antigen-typing discrepancies from weak/partial antigens.

source_references.locator: Section 2, Crossmatching and Antibody Detection (Expert Note)
source_references.supports: Defines the virtual crossmatch as comparison of a recipient's HLA antibody profile to a donor's documented HLA type, distinguishing it from HPA-specific testing.

source_references.locator: Domain V: Hazards of Transfusion, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, used to show irradiation is unrelated to antigen elimination.

review.notes: Correct answer and core distractors are well grounded: SRC-006 explicitly states genotyping is essential for finding compatible donors 'when an antibody is weak or when HPA reagents are unavailable' — matching the stem's exact scenario; SRC-028 correctly defines virtual crossmatch as an HLA (not HPA) tool, sinking option C; SRC-010's TA-GVHD/irradiation fact correctly sinks option E. As in Q0, option D's rationale again asserts the sources describe Bernard-Soulier as a 'glycoprotein (function) disorder' — 'glycoprotein' is not present anywhere in the four sources, an embellished/unsupported at

questions.stem: A patient with immune-mediated thrombocytopenia fails to achieve the expected platelet increment despite receiving more than one ABO-compatible platelet transfusion. The corrected count increment (CCI) — which accounts for the patient's body surface area and the number of platelets transfused — is calculated and found to be low, and subsequent antibody testing identifies HLA antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in platelet product selection for this patient?

options.text: Order gamma-irradiated platelets, since irradiation resolves antibody-mediated refractoriness
Repeat the CCI calculation after transfusing a larger dose of random-donor platelets, without changing donor selection
Switch to platelets from a different random donor without HLA selection
Continue transfusing random-donor platelet products at the same dose without any change in selection
Provide HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelet products

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe using the CCI (based on body surface area and platelets transfused) to evaluate transfusion response, and state that when patients fail to achieve expected incremental increases and are found to have alloimmunization, the appropriate consultation is to provide HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelet products.

option_explanations.E: Correct: the sources state that alloimmune refractoriness (failure to achieve expected increments) is managed by consulting on provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: continuing unselected random-donor products does not address a documented HLA-antibody-mediated refractory state.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: switching donors without HLA selection does not target the identified HLA alloantibody and would be expected to yield a similarly poor increment.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources link gamma irradiation to inactivating donor T lymphocytes for TA-GVHD prevention, not to overcoming HLA-antibody-mediated platelet refractoriness.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: simply increasing the dose of unselected random-donor platelets does not address the underlying HLA alloimmunization identified on antibody testing.

questions.teaching_point: A low CCI on repeated platelet transfusions with documented HLA antibodies defines alloimmune platelet refractoriness, which is managed by providing HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelet products rather than continued random-donor transfusion or irradiation.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 65 – Intravascular Cell Kinetics & Expected Increments
source_references.supports: Defines platelet increment logic via the Corrected Count Increment (CCI), which accounts for body surface area and the number of platelets transfused.

source_references.locator: Section 2, Clinical Consultation and Regulatory Aspects – Alloimmune Refractoriness
source_references.supports: States that alloimmune refractoriness work-up involves consulting on the provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets for patients failing to achieve expected incremental increases.

source_references.locator: Domain V: Hazards of Transfusion, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, used to show irradiation does not address antibody-mediated refractoriness.

review.notes: All asserted facts are accurately and specifically grounded: SRC-006 (Node 65) defines CCI exactly as 'accounts for the patient's body surface area and the number of platelets transfused'; SRC-028 (Section 2, Alloimmune Refractoriness) states the appropriate consult is 'provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets for patients failing to achieve expected incremental increases' — a near-verbatim match to option A; SRC-010's TA-GVHD/gamma-irradiation fact correctly sinks option D. No invented or contradicted facts found. However, this question is materially about managing alloimmune platel

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with atrial fibrillation on long-term warfarin therapy is admitted with gingival bleeding and diffuse bruising. Coagulation studies reveal a markedly prolonged PT/INR. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism of his coagulopathy?

options.text: Warfarin blocks vitamin K-dependent carboxylation, producing nonfunctional ('PIVKA') Factors II, VII, IX, and X
Decreased hepatic synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, as occurs in chronic liver disease
Pathologic activation of both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems with consumption of platelets and factors, as in DIC
Development of a high-titer autoantibody that neutralizes Factor VIII activity

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources describe vitamin K deficiency/antagonism as producing nonfunctional factors II, VII, IX, and X, termed PIVKA (proteins induced by vitamin K absence), because these factors require vitamin K-dependent carboxylation for functional activity. Warfarin is a vitamin K antagonist (VKA), so this is the mechanism underlying its anticoagulant/bleeding effect.

option_explanations.A: Correct: matches the source's description of vitamin K deficiency producing nonfunctional PIVKA factors II, VII, IX, and X, which is the mechanism of warfarin (a VKA).
option_explanations.B: Distractor: this describes chronic liver disease pathophysiology per the source (decreased synthesis of nearly all factors except VIII/vWF), a broader defect than warfarin's selective vitamin K-dependent factor effect — a classic board trap for confusing the two entities.
option_explanations.C: Distractor: this describes DIC per the source, a consumption coagulopathy with simultaneous fibrinolytic activation, not the mechanism of a vitamin K antagonist.
option_explanations.D: Distractor: this describes an acquired Factor VIII inhibitor per the source, managed with bypassing agents (PCC or recombinant FVIIa), not the mechanism of warfarin.

questions.teaching_point: Warfarin's coagulopathy arises from impaired vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of Factors II, VII, IX, and X (producing nonfunctional PIVKA proteins) — a distinct mechanism from liver disease (global factor synthesis loss sparing VIII/vWF), DIC (consumption plus fibrinolysis), or Factor VIII inhibitors (autoantibody neutralization).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management, Core Concepts: Vitamin K Deficiency
source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency/antagonism leads to nonfunctional PIVKA factors II, VII, IX, X

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts: Liver Disease
source_references.supports: Liver disease decreases synthesis of nearly all factors except VIII and vWF (used as mechanism distractor)

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Core Concepts: DIC
source_references.supports: DIC is a consumption coagulopathy with concurrent procoagulant and fibrinolytic activation (used as mechanism distractor)

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Management Logic: Factor VIII Inhibitors
source_references.supports: High-titer Factor VIII autoantibody inhibitors are managed with bypassing agents (used as mechanism distractor)

review.notes: SRC-002 Node 47 explicitly states 'Vitamin K Deficiency: Leads to the production of nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA)' and separately lists 'Urgent Warfarin Reversal' under the same node's Management Logic, but the source never explicitly states the molecular mechanism 'warfarin blocks vitamin K-dependent carboxylation' or explicitly equates warfarin's action with the 'Vitamin K Deficiency' bullet — that link is standard external medical knowledge (warfarin = VKA) rather than a sentence actually present in the source. The downstream effect (nonfunctional PIVKA factors II/VII/IX/

questions.stem: A patient on chronic warfarin therapy presents with an intracranial hemorrhage and an INR of 6.1. The transfusion medicine physician must select the product that will most rapidly normalize the INR while minimizing the infused volume. Which product should be selected?

options.text: Albumin
Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC)
Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP)
Recombinant Factor VIIa
Fibrinogen Concentrate

questions.explanation_correct: Per the approved sources, PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) such as warfarin in bleeding patients, and it is preferred over FFP specifically because of its lower infused volume and faster normalization of the INR.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the sources explicitly identify PCC as the treatment of choice for urgent VKA/warfarin reversal, providing faster INR normalization with lower volume than FFP.
option_explanations.C: Distractor: FFP is a traditional reversal option, but per the source PCC is preferred over FFP due to FFP's larger volume and slower INR correction — a classic board trap.
option_explanations.D: Distractor: per the source, recombinant Factor VIIa is used as a bypassing agent for high-titer Factor VIII inhibitors, not identified as the reversal product of choice for warfarin/VKA-related bleeding.
option_explanations.A: Distractor: per the source, albumin is used as a replacement fluid in therapeutic plasma exchange because it is virus-safe and low-allergy-risk; it has no described role in correcting a vitamin K antagonist-elevated INR.
option_explanations.E: Distractor: per the source, fibrinogen concentrate is a low-volume, pathogen-reduced alternative to cryoprecipitate that boosts fibrinogen; it is not described as containing the vitamin K-dependent factors needed to correct a warfarin-elevated INR.

questions.teaching_point: For urgent warfarin/VKA reversal in life-threatening bleeding, PCC is favored over FFP because it corrects the INR faster and requires a much smaller infused volume; recombinant FVIIa, albumin, and fibrinogen concentrate serve different, non-overlapping indications.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 39 – Derivatives & Recombinant Proteins, Key Products: PCC
source_references.supports: PCC is the treatment of choice for urgent reversal of VKAs like warfarin in bleeding patients

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Management Logic: Urgent Warfarin Reversal
source_references.supports: PCC is preferred over FFP due to lower volume and faster INR normalization

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47, Management Logic: Factor VIII Inhibitors
source_references.supports: Recombinant Factor VIIa is a bypassing agent for Factor VIII inhibitors, not the described first-line VKA reversal agent

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 39, Clinical Application: Albumin
source_references.supports: Albumin's described role is as TPE replacement fluid, unrelated to factor/INR correction

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 39, Key Products: Fibrinogen Concentrate
source_references.supports: Fibrinogen concentrate is a low-volume alternative to cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen boost, distinct from vitamin K-dependent factor replacement

review.notes: Both sources directly and unambiguously support the key: SRC-051 Node 39 states PCC is 'Treatment of choice for the urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin in bleeding patients,' and SRC-002 Node 47 states 'PCC is preferred over FFP due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR' — this is an almost verbatim match to the stem's framing ('most rapidly normalize the INR while minimizing the infused volume'). Distractors are each correctly grounded: recombinant FVIIa is tied only to Factor VIII inhibitor bypassing (not VKA reversal) per source; albumin's only de

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with a lifelong history of easy bruising and prolonged bleeding after dental extractions is diagnosed with a congenital deficiency of von Willebrand factor. She develops significant mucosal bleeding after a minor surgical procedure, and her physician selects cryoprecipitate — prepared as the cold-insoluble portion of fresh frozen plasma — as part of her management. Which of the following best explains the physiologic basis for choosing this specific component to control her bleeding?

options.text: Cryoprecipitate corrects her mildly elevated INR prior to the procedure
Cryoprecipitate supplies ADAMTS13, the protease required to cleave pathogenic ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers
Cryoprecipitate is a concentrated source of von Willebrand factor, directly replacing the protein she is deficient in
Cryoprecipitate raises her fibrinogen level, correcting a co-existing hypofibrinogenemia responsible for her bleeding
Cryoprecipitate expands her circulating plasma volume to compensate for surgical blood loss

questions.explanation_correct: Cryoprecipitate is defined as the cold-insoluble portion of FFP and is described as a concentrated source of fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor. In a patient whose bleeding diathesis is caused by a congenital deficiency of von Willebrand factor, cryoprecipitate corrects the hemostatic defect because it directly replaces the deficient protein in concentrated form — the mechanism is replacement of the missing factor, not an ancillary effect such as volume expansion or INR correction.

option_explanations.C: Correct: cryoprecipitate is explicitly described as a concentrated source of von Willebrand factor (along with fibrinogen, Factor VIII, and Factor XIII), so it works by replacing the deficient protein.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: ADAMTS13 replacement is the rationale for using plasma in TTP, a distinct disorder in which a deficient cleaving protease allows pathogenic ultra-large vWF multimers to accumulate — this is a different mechanism from a primary vWF deficiency state and is a classic board trap conflating the two vWF-related conditions.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the source specifically warns that transfusing plasma to correct a mildly elevated INR in a non-bleeding patient before a procedure is usually ineffective and not recommended; this is not the mechanism by which cryoprecipitate helps an actively bleeding vWD patient.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the source explicitly states plasma products should never be used for simple volume expansion; this is not why cryoprecipitate is selected here.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: while cryoprecipitate does raise fibrinogen levels, the vignette describes a von Willebrand factor deficiency, not hypofibrinogenemia, so fibrinogen replacement is not the operative mechanism in this case.

questions.teaching_point: Cryoprecipitate's efficacy in a vWF-deficient bleeding disorder rests on it being a concentrated source of vWF (plus Factor VIII, fibrinogen, Factor XIII) — do not confuse this replacement mechanism with the distinct ADAMTS13-replacement rationale used for plasma in TTP, a different disease of vWF multimer handling.

source_references.locator: Cryoprecipitate definition, line ~19
source_references.supports: Cryoprecipitate is the cold-insoluble portion of FFP and a concentrated source of fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor.

source_references.locator: ADAMTS13 kinetics pearl, line ~77
source_references.supports: Plasma treats TTP by supplying the vWF-cleaving protease ADAMTS13 to break down pathogenic ultra-large vWF multimers, distinct from replacing deficient vWF itself.

source_references.locator: ADAMTS13 and TTP pearl, line ~79
source_references.supports: TPE is first-line in TTP because it removes the inhibitor and ultra-large vWF multimers while replacing the deficient ADAMTS13 protease.

source_references.locator: Volume Expansion Distractor, line ~61
source_references.supports: Plasma products should never be used for simple volume expansion, ruling out that mechanism.

source_references.locator: INR Distractor, line ~65
source_references.supports: Transfusing plasma to correct a mildly elevated INR pre-procedure in a non-bleeding patient is usually ineffective and not recommended.

review.notes: Core mechanism is solidly grounded: SRC-031 line 19 explicitly defines cryoprecipitate as 'the cold-insoluble portion of FFP; a concentrated source of Fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand Factor,' which directly supports key A. The ADAMTS13/TTP distractor (B) is well supported by SRC-031 line 77 and SRC-023 line 79 (plasma/TPE replace ADAMTS13 to cleave ultra-large vWF multimers in TTP, a distinct disease from primary vWF deficiency). However, distractors C (INR) and D (volume expansion) are grounded only in statements the source makes about 'plasma'/'plasma products' gener

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with alcohol-associated cirrhosis is found on preoperative testing to have a prolonged PT/INR and thrombocytopenia consistent with his hepatic synthetic dysfunction. His hematologist explains that, unlike most other coagulation-related proteins, two specific hemostatic proteins are typically preserved (or even elevated) in this setting rather than decreased — a laboratory pattern that helps distinguish hepatic coagulopathy from a primary congenital deficiency state such as von Willebrand disease, in which these same two proteins would instead be expected to be low. Which two proteins does this describe?

options.text: Fibrinogen and Factor XIII
Protein C and Protein S
Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor
Factor IX and Factor X
Factor II and Factor VII

questions.explanation_correct: Liver disease is characterized by decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, which are typically preserved. Because these two are the exception to the generalized hepatic synthetic decline, a low Factor VIII/vWF level in a bleeding patient argues against liver disease alone and instead points toward a primary deficiency state such as congenital von Willebrand disease, where vWF (and secondarily Factor VIII) would be expected to be low.

option_explanations.C: Correct: Factor VIII and vWF are explicitly identified as the exception that remains preserved despite the globally decreased factor synthesis of liver disease.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: fibrinogen and Factor XIII are both concentrated in cryoprecipitate but are not identified as spared in liver disease; they fall under the 'nearly all clotting factors' that are decreased.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: Factors II and VII are vitamin K-dependent factors that become nonfunctional (via PIVKA) in vitamin K deficiency and are decreased along with the other hepatically synthesized factors in liver disease, not spared.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: Factors IX and X are also vitamin K-dependent factors listed as affected by vitamin K deficiency/decreased hepatic synthesis, not preserved.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: Protein S is noted to be present at reduced levels in PF24 relative to fresh FFP, consistent with it being subject to depletion rather than being the specific exception spared in hepatic synthetic dysfunction.

questions.teaching_point: A coagulation panel showing globally low factors except a preserved (or high) Factor VIII/vWF favors hepatic synthetic dysfunction; conversely, an isolated low vWF (with otherwise unremarkable synthetic factors) should raise suspicion for a primary congenital vWF deficiency state such as von Willebrand disease rather than liver disease.

source_references.locator: Liver Disease core concept, line ~35
source_references.supports: Liver disease decreases synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and vWF, and impairs clearance of activated factors.

source_references.locator: Vitamin K Deficiency core concept, line ~39
source_references.supports: Vitamin K deficiency leads to nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X, supporting that these are affected/decreased factors rather than spared ones.

source_references.locator: Cryoprecipitate definition, line ~19
source_references.supports: Fibrinogen and Factor XIII are cryoprecipitate constituents alongside Factor VIII and vWF, but are not stated to be spared in liver disease.

source_references.locator: PF24 definition, line ~15
source_references.supports: Protein S is present at slightly lower levels in PF24 versus FFP, indicating it is a labile/depletable protein rather than one specifically preserved in hepatic dysfunction.

review.notes: The keyed fact is explicitly and strongly grounded: SRC-002 line 35 states liver disease is 'characterized by decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors (except Factor VIII and vWF)' -- directly supporting answer A over B/C/D, since SRC-002 line 39 separately confirms Factors II, VII, IX, and X become nonfunctional/decreased in vitamin K deficiency (the vitamin-K-dependent factors named in distractors C and D), and fibrinogen/FXIII (option B) are reasonably read into the 'nearly all clotting factors' decrease. However, the exclusion of option E (Protein C and Protein S) is weakly sourc

questions.stem: A 5-week-old exclusively breastfed infant who did not receive vitamin K prophylaxis at birth presents with gastrointestinal bleeding. Laboratory evaluation shows: platelet count 250,000/uL (normal), INR 1.8, aPTT 70 seconds, and fibrinogen 220 mg/dL (normal). Based on standard laboratory transfusion triggers, which blood component is indicated to correct this infant's coagulopathy?

options.text: Platelet concentrate
Fresh frozen plasma
Cryoprecipitate
Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)

questions.explanation_correct: This infant's INR of 1.8 and aPTT of 70 seconds both exceed the sourced plasma transfusion triggers (INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds), while the platelet count and fibrinogen are normal, arguing against a platelet-consumptive or hypofibrinogenemic process. This isolated prolongation of INR/aPTT with normal platelets and fibrinogen is the laboratory pattern corrected by fresh frozen plasma.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the sourced laboratory trigger for plasma transfusion is INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds, both of which are met here, with normal platelets/fibrinogen indicating an isolated coagulation factor problem best addressed by plasma.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sourced platelet transfusion trigger is a count <50,000/uL; this infant's platelet count of 250,000/uL is normal, arguing against a platelet-destructive process such as FNAIT.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sourced cryoprecipitate trigger is fibrinogen <100 mg/dL; this infant's fibrinogen of 220 mg/dL is normal, arguing against a consumptive fibrinogen-depleting process.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: IVIG is used in HDFN management to block Fc receptors and reduce hemolysis/hyperbilirubinemia, not to correct an isolated prolongation of INR/aPTT.

questions.teaching_point: Interpreting a neonatal bleeding laboratory panel requires checking platelet count, PT/INR, aPTT, and fibrinogen together: an isolated INR/aPTT prolongation with normal platelets and fibrinogen selects plasma over platelets, cryoprecipitate, or IVIG.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16, Diagnostic and Management Approach / Laboratory Triggers
source_references.supports: Laboratory transfusion triggers: plasma for INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 sec; platelets for count <50,000/uL; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL.

source_references.locator: Domain VII, Management
source_references.supports: IVIG is used in neonatal therapy for HDFN to block Fc receptors, distinguishing its indication from a coagulation factor deficiency.

review.notes: The lab-trigger logic (INR>1.5/aPTT>60 -> plasma; platelet <50,000 -> platelets; fibrinogen <100 -> cryo) is accurately and directly quoted from SRC-032 Node 16, and the IVIG/HDFN rationale is accurately sourced to SRC-017 Domain VII Management, so the arithmetic/threshold-matching in this item is well grounded. However, SRC-032's triggers are explicitly an adult massive-transfusion-protocol framework, not established anywhere in the sources as applicable to a 5-week-old infant with suspected late vitamin K deficiency bleeding, and no source discusses vitamin K deficiency or late HDN onset/pat

questions.subdomain: Immunogenetic and Frequency of Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman, gravida 3 para 3, receives an apheresis platelet transfusion before an elective orthopedic procedure. Eight days later she develops abrupt, severe thrombocytopenia (platelet count 3,000/µL) with new petechiae and mucosal bleeding. There is no evidence of sepsis, disseminated intravascular coagulation, or a drug reaction, and she has no personal or family history of a platelet function disorder. Laboratory workup confirms this is a delayed (>24 hour), antibody-mediated transfusion complication. Which of the following best explains the immunogenetic mechanism underlying this reaction?

options.text: Alloantibody directed against the HPA-1a antigen on donor platelet glycoproteins — the antigen most frequently implicated in immune platelet destruction in both FNAIT/NAIT and post-transfusion purpura (PTP)
Alloantibody against donor Human Neutrophil Antigens (HNA) cross-reacting with platelet surface glycoproteins
Nonimmune platelet consumption due to splenic sequestration
Autoantibody against the patient's own glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex, the defect underlying congenital Glanzmann thrombasthenia
Alloantibody directed against HLA Class II (DR) antigens expressed on the platelet membrane

questions.explanation_correct: The vignette (a multiparous woman, delayed onset days after a platelet-containing transfusion, confirmed antibody-mediated mechanism) is the classic presentation attributed to post-transfusion purpura. The approved sources identify HPA-1a as the antigen most frequently targeted by alloantibody in both Neonatal/Fetal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT/FNAIT) and post-transfusion purpura (PTP), and PTP is explicitly classified as a delayed (>24 hour), immune-mediated transfusion reaction caused by HPA-1a antibody.

option_explanations.A: Correct — the sources state HPA-1a is the primary immunogenic target antigen in NAIT/FNAIT and PTP, and classify PTP as a delayed immune reaction caused specifically by HPA-1a antibody.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — the sources enumerate only ABO, HLA Class I (A and B), and Human Platelet Antigens (HPA) as the antigen systems carried on platelets; HLA Class II is not listed among platelet-expressed antigen systems in the source material.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — glycoprotein IIb/IIIa deficiency (Glanzmann thrombasthenia) is described in the sources as a congenital platelet function disorder, not an antibody-mediated alloimmune process; the stem explicitly excludes a personal/family history of a platelet function disorder and confirms an antibody-mediated mechanism.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — per the sources' reaction classification, HNA antibodies are linked to TRALI, an acute (not delayed) immune reaction, not to alloimmune platelet destruction.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — splenomegaly is listed in the sources among the nonimmune causes of poor platelet response; the stem specifies a confirmed antibody-mediated (immune) reaction with no evidence of splenomegaly.

questions.teaching_point: HPA-1a is the platelet alloantigen most classically implicated as the immunogenic target in both FNAIT/NAIT and post-transfusion purpura; both are delayed, antibody-mediated processes distinct from congenital platelet function disorders, HLA/HNA-mediated reactions, and nonimmune causes of thrombocytopenia.

source_references.locator: Platelet and Granulocyte Immunohematology section
source_references.supports: States HPA-1a is the primary target antigen in Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT) and Post-Transfusion Purpura (PTP).

source_references.locator: Transfusion Reactions: Classification table
source_references.supports: Transfusion reaction classification table lists PTP (HPA-1a Ab) under Delayed (>24h) Immune reactions, and TRALI under Acute (HLA/HNA Ab), separating the two antigen/antibody targets.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Enumerates the antigen systems carried by platelets as ABO, HLA Class I (A and B), and HPA, and notes HPA are critical to investigating FNAIT and PTP.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62, Laboratory and Diagnostic Logic
source_references.supports: States platelet function disorders such as Glanzmann's and Bernard-Soulier are congenital (or acquired from causes like uremia/bypass/drugs), distinguishing them from alloimmune antibody-mediated destruction.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations – Platelet Refractoriness
source_references.supports: Lists splenomegaly among the nonimmune causes of an inadequate posttransfusion platelet response, contrasted with immune-mediated (HLA/HPA antibody) causes.

review.notes: Core teaching point is well grounded: SRC-044 states verbatim 'HPA-1a: Primary target in Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT) and Post-Transfusion Purpura (PTP)' and its Transfusion Reactions classification table lists 'PTP (HPA-1a Ab)' under Delayed (>24h) Immune and 'TRALI (HLA/HNA Ab)' under Acute Immune, directly supporting option A and refuting D. SRC-006 confirms platelets carry only ABO, HLA Class I (A and B), and HPA (no Class II listed), supporting exclusion of B, and separately classifies Glanzmann/Bernard-Soulier as congenital/acquired platelet function disorders (not antibod

questions.subdomain: Immunogenetic and Frequency of Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia
questions.stem: A patient with a documented history of anti-HPA-1a antibody requires HPA-1a-antigen-negative apheresis platelets before an elective procedure. When the blood bank attempts to phenotype potential platelet donors using available HPA-1a antisera, the reactions are weak and inconsistent, and the laboratory's supply of HPA typing antisera is limited. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to identify compatible donors for this patient?

options.text: Substitute HLA crossmatching for HPA phenotyping in the donor selection process
Perform a Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT) on the candidate donor platelets
Issue random, unmatched whole-blood-derived pooled platelet concentrates
Select apheresis platelet donors based solely on ABO compatibility with the recipient
Perform molecular (DNA-based) genotyping of potential donors for the HPA-1 locus

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that molecular genotyping is essential for identifying compatible platelet donors specifically in the situation described here: when antibody/antisera reactivity is weak or when HPA typing reagents are unavailable or limited. Genotyping bypasses the serologic reagent limitation by determining the donor's HPA-1 genotype directly.

option_explanations.E: Correct — the sources specifically identify molecular (genotyping) testing as essential for finding compatible platelet donors when an antibody is weak or when HPA typing reagents are unavailable, which matches this scenario exactly.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — the sources discuss the DAT as a test for RBC-bound antibody (e.g., in AIHA and HDFN workups), not as a method for platelet antigen typing or donor selection.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — ABO is only one of three antigen systems the sources describe as carried on platelets (ABO, HLA Class I, HPA); matching on ABO alone does not address this patient's HPA-1a alloantibody and antigen-negative donor requirement.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — whole-blood-derived pooled platelets are drawn from multiple donors, increasing rather than reducing the chance of exposure to HPA-1a-positive units, and the sources note apheresis (single-donor) platelets are preferred when matching is needed.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — the sources describe HLA crossmatching/matching as the approach for HLA-mediated immunization and refractoriness, a distinct antigen system from HPA; it does not identify HPA-1a-negative donors for this patient's documented HPA-specific antibody.

questions.teaching_point: When serologic HPA phenotyping is unreliable due to weak reactivity or limited antisera, molecular genotyping is the source-supported approach for identifying antigen-negative platelet donors; this is distinct from HLA-directed matching, which addresses a different antigen system and does not resolve HPA-specific alloimmunization.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping, Genotyping Utility
source_references.supports: States molecular testing (genotyping) is essential for finding compatible platelet donors when an antibody is weak or when HPA reagents are unavailable.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62, Core Concepts
source_references.supports: Enumerates ABO, HLA Class I, and HPA as the three antigen systems carried by platelets, supporting why ABO-only matching is insufficient for an HPA-specific antibody.

source_references.locator: High-Yield Comparison Points – Apheresis vs. Pooled Platelets
source_references.supports: Notes apheresis platelets reduce donor exposure compared to pooled whole-blood-derived platelets and are easier to match when a patient requires antigen/antibody-directed selection.

source_references.locator: The Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT) and Hemolysis section
source_references.supports: Discusses the DAT only in the context of RBC-bound antibody/hemolysis workups (AIHA, DIIHA), supporting that it is not the appropriate tool for platelet antigen donor selection.

review.notes: Correct answer is well grounded: SRC-006 states verbatim under Subdomain Node 62 'Genotyping Utility: Molecular testing (genotyping) is essential for finding compatible donors when an antibody is weak or when HPA reagents are unavailable,' which matches the stem's weak/inconsistent antisera reactions plus limited reagent supply, and clearly supports A over B (DAT is only discussed in the sources for RBC-bound antibody/AIHA workups), C (ABO is only one of three platelet antigen systems per SRC-006), and D (SRC-031 confirms apheresis reduces donor exposure vs. pooled 5-donor products). One overc

questions.subdomain: Polymorphism of HLA System and Nomenclature
questions.stem: A hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplant program is discussing why it is often difficult to find a fully HLA-matched unrelated donor for a patient, even when searching large volunteer donor registries. Which of the following best explains this difficulty?

options.text: The difficulty is best attributed to polymorphism of non-HLA determinants such as human neutrophil antigens (HNA) and MHC class I-related chain A (MICA), rather than to the HLA loci themselves.
HLA class III genes, which encode the classic transplantation antigens recognized in graft rejection, mutate at an unusually high rate compared with class I and class II genes.

HLA class II molecules are expressed on all nucleated cells and platelets and show little allelic variation, while HLA class I molecules are restricted to antigen-presenting cells and account for essentially all of the observed polymorphism.

The HLA loci (HLA-A, -B, -C, -DR, -DQ, -DP) are among the most polymorphic regions of the genome, and because these closely linked genes are typically co-inherited as a block (linkage disequilibrium), the number of possible allele combinations across the population is extremely large.

questions.explanation_correct: The HLA region is the most polymorphic region of the human genome, with numerous alleles possible at each of the class I (HLA-A, -B, -C) and class II (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) loci. Because these loci are closely linked on chromosome 6, they are typically inherited together as haplotypes (linkage disequilibrium) rather than independently, so the allele combination a person carries is highly individualized. This combination of extreme polymorphism and linkage disequilibrium is what makes finding a fully HLA-matched unrelated donor statistically difficult.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The source describes extreme polymorphism and linkage disequilibrium as inherent properties of the HLA locus, meaning many alleles exist at each locus and closely linked loci tend to be inherited together, drastically reducing the chance of an exact match between unrelated individuals.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Class III genes encode complement components (C2, C4) and cytokines rather than classic histocompatibility (transplantation) antigens; class I and class II are the loci responsible for graft recognition.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This reverses the actual expression pattern: class I (HLA-A, -B, -C) is expressed on most nucleated cells and platelets, while class II (HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP) expression is restricted to antigen-presenting cells such as B lymphocytes and macrophages; polymorphism is a described feature of the HLA locus generally, not confined to one class.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. HNA and MICA are described as non-HLA determinants evaluated separately from the HLA system; the extreme polymorphism driving matching difficulty is specifically attributed to the HLA locus itself.

questions.teaching_point: HLA polymorphism plus linkage disequilibrium across linked class I/II loci explains why unrelated-donor HLA matching is statistically difficult; class III is not a classic transplantation-antigen locus.

source_references.locator: Section 2, Critical Components of the HLA System - Genetics of the MHC
source_references.supports: Genetics of the MHC is characterized by extreme polymorphism and linkage disequilibrium at the HLA locus

source_references.locator: Section 1, MHC Organization table
source_references.supports: Class I loci (A,B,C) on nucleated cells/platelets; Class II (DR,DQ,DP) restricted to APCs; Class III encodes complement/cytokines, not classic histocompatibility antigens

source_references.locator: Section 2, Non-HLA Determinants
source_references.supports: HNA and MICA are listed as non-HLA determinants distinct from the HLA system

review.notes: Fully grounded in SRC-028: extreme polymorphism + linkage disequilibrium at the HLA locus; Class I (A,B,C) on nucleated cells/platelets vs Class II (DR,DQ,DP) restricted to APCs; Class III encodes complement/cytokines 'rather than classic histocompatibility antigens'; HNA/MICA explicitly listed as separate non-HLA determinants. All three distractors are directly contradicted by source text, leaving A as the unambiguous single best answer. Fits the blueprint node (polymorphism) well.

questions.subdomain: Polymorphism of HLA System and Nomenclature
questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with myelodysplastic syndrome has received numerous platelet transfusions. His most recent pretransfusion antibody screen is positive, and the antibody identification panel shows weak, inconsistent (nebulous) reactivity against nearly all reagent red cells, with no pattern matching a specific RBC alloantibody. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to determine whether this reactivity represents anti-HLA (Bg) activity rather than a clinically significant RBC alloantibody?

options.text: Perform testing by immediate spin only, since antibodies to Knops system antigens (anti-KN, directed against CR1) are weak and are not reactive by the indirect antiglobulin test (IAT).
Attribute the pattern to anti-JMH without further workup, since JMH antibodies are frequently seen in patients over age 50 and are generally not clinically significant.
Treat the reagent red cells with ficin or papain, since CH/RG antigens are destroyed by proteolytic enzymes, and loss of reactivity would confirm anti-CH/RG rather than anti-Bg.

Treat the reagent red cells with chloroquine diphosphate or EDTA/glycine acid to remove HLA class I (Bg) antigen expression, then repeat testing; loss of reactivity supports a Bg/HLA-related antibody.

questions.explanation_correct: Bg antigens are HLA class I antigens that are weakly and inconsistently expressed on red cells, which explains the nebulous, broadly reactive but weak pattern seen against nearly all panel cells. Because Bg reactivity can be removed by treating reagent red cells with chloroquine diphosphate or EDTA/glycine acid, repeating the panel after such treatment and observing loss of reactivity is the recognized way to confirm the antibody is HLA (Bg)-related rather than a clinically significant RBC-specific alloantibody.

option_explanations.D: Correct. Bg antigens are HLA class I antigens weakly and variably expressed on red cells, producing the described nebulous panel reactivity; the source notes this reactivity can be removed by chloroquine treatment or EDTA/glycine.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. CH/RG antigens are plasma-adsorbed complement (C4d) fragments destroyed by ficin/papain, but the described diffuse weak reactivity and multiply-transfused history point to HLA/Bg; enzyme treatment is not the technique the source associates with resolving Bg reactivity.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The source states KN antibodies are reactive only by IAT (not immediate spin), so restricting testing to immediate spin would miss rather than confirm a KN-related explanation; this option misstates the testing requirement.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. While JMH antibodies are indeed common after age 50 and generally not clinically significant, jumping to this conclusion without performing a technique-based confirmatory step (such as antigen removal) is not the appropriate laboratory approach.

questions.teaching_point: Bg (HLA class I) antigens on RBCs cause weak, variable panel reactivity; chloroquine or EDTA/glycine treatment of panel cells removes Bg reactivity, distinguishing it from clinically significant RBC alloantibodies.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors - The Bg Distractor
source_references.supports: Bg antigens are HLA class I antigens weakly expressed on RBCs; reactivity removed by chloroquine treatment or EDTA/glycine

source_references.locator: High-Yield Specific Systems - CH/RG (017)
source_references.supports: CH/RG antigens are complement C4d fragments adsorbed from plasma and are destroyed by ficin/papain

source_references.locator: High-Yield Specific Systems - KN (022)
source_references.supports: KN antibodies are reactive only by IAT and are not clinically significant

source_references.locator: High-Yield Specific Systems - JMH (026)
source_references.supports: JMH antibodies are frequently seen in patients over age 50 and are generally not clinically significant

review.notes: Core facts trace directly to SRC-003 (Bg = HLA class I weakly expressed on RBCs, removed by chloroquine treatment or EDTA/glycine; CH/RG destroyed by enzymes; KN reactive only by IAT and not clinically significant; JMH common >50 and not clinically significant). Option C correctly inverts the source's actual KN statement, making it a valid distractor. Key A is well supported and is the best answer among options. Two quality concerns: (1) the underlying Bg fact in the source is a single terse sentence, yet the question adds unsourced specificity ('chloroquine diphosphate,' 'EDTA/glycine acid')

questions.subdomain: Polymorphism of HLA System and Nomenclature
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia has required multiple platelet transfusions over the past month. Despite receiving ABO-identical, fresh apheresis platelet units, his post-transfusion platelet count increments remain poor. Fever, disseminated intravascular coagulation, and splenomegaly have been excluded, and HLA antibody testing is positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Issue any available donor platelet unit as compatible, since a virtual crossmatch predicts compatibility without requiring a physical mixture of cells and serum, regardless of the donor's HLA type.
Test for and select platelet donors based on human neutrophil antigen (HNA) compatibility instead of HLA.
Provide HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelet products.
Continue transfusing random-donor platelets at a higher dose to overcome antibody-mediated clearance.

questions.explanation_correct: Once non-immune causes of poor platelet increments (fever, DIC, splenomegaly, ABO mismatch, unit age) have been excluded and HLA antibodies are demonstrated, the source-directed management for alloimmune platelet refractoriness is to provide HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelet products, since these are selected to avoid the specific HLA antigens the patient has become sensitized against.

option_explanations.C: Correct. The source specifically identifies provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets as the consultative management strategy for patients who fail to achieve expected platelet increments due to alloimmune refractoriness.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Simply increasing the dose of random-donor (non-HLA-selected) platelets does not address the underlying alloimmune mechanism causing rapid antibody-mediated clearance.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. HNA and MICA are described as non-HLA determinants evaluated separately from the HLA system; the source's specific management approach for alloimmune platelet refractoriness is HLA-matched/selected platelets, not HNA-based donor selection.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. This misapplies the virtual crossmatch, which requires comparing the recipient's known HLA antibody profile against the donor's documented HLA type to predict compatibility; it does not permit bypassing donor HLA typing altogether.

questions.teaching_point: Platelet refractoriness due to HLA alloimmunization, once non-immune causes are excluded, is managed with HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelet products.

source_references.locator: Section 2, Clinical Consultation and Regulatory Aspects - Alloimmune Refractoriness
source_references.supports: Consulting on provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets for patients failing to achieve expected incremental increases (alloimmune refractoriness)

source_references.locator: Section 2, Non-HLA Determinants
source_references.supports: Non-HLA determinants (HNA, MICA) are evaluated separately from the HLA system

source_references.locator: Section 2, Expert Note - Virtual Crossmatch
source_references.supports: Virtual crossmatch compares recipient's HLA antibody profile against donor's documented HLA type to predict compatibility without physical mixture

review.notes: Fully grounded in SRC-028: 'Consulting on the provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets for patients failing to achieve expected incremental increases' directly supports key A; the Virtual Crossmatch expert note (requires donor's documented HLA type) correctly refutes option D; HNA/MICA as separate non-HLA determinants correctly refutes option C. No invented or contradicted facts. However, this question tests clinical management of alloimmune platelet refractoriness, not 'polymorphism of the HLA system and nomenclature' as named by the assigned blueprint node - accurate HLA content bu

questions.subdomain: Transfusion Service Support of Tissue Transplantation
questions.stem: A hospital transfusion service also functions as the institution's tissue bank, procuring and distributing musculoskeletal (bone) and skin allografts to surgical services. A resident preparing for a rotation asks why FDA requires this tissue-banking activity to meet the same rigorous, single-source-traceable regulatory framework that governs blood component manufacturing. Which of the following best explains this regulatory requirement?

options.text: Fresh, unprocessed bone and skin allografts contain viable donor T-lymphocytes that must be gamma-irradiated to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease in the recipient, exactly as is done for cellular blood components.
Leukocytes present in donor tissue must be removed by leukoreduction filtration to prevent cytomegalovirus transmission and febrile nonhemolytic-type reactions in the allograft recipient.

Bone and skin allografts are classified as human cells, tissues, and cellular/tissue-based products (HCT/Ps) that, like blood, can transmit communicable disease from donor to recipient; FDA regulation under 21 CFR Part 1271 exists specifically to prevent this transmission and requires donor-to-recipient traceability.

Tissue allografts must be tracked so that any manufacturing deviation discovered after distribution can be reported to CBER on Form FDA 3486, and this reporting requirement is the primary reason the regulation exists.

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that FDA 21 CFR Part 1271 regulates tissue banking specifically to prevent transmission of communicable disease, and that tissue allografts (bone, skin) are HCT/Ps requiring the same vein-to-vein, bidirectional donor-recipient traceability used for blood components. This disease-transmission-prevention rationale, not a specific processing step or a downstream reporting mechanism, is the underlying reason for the regulatory rigor.

option_explanations.C: Correct — this is the stated FDA rationale for regulating tissue banking under 21 CFR 1271 and the reason bidirectional donor-recipient traceability is required.
option_explanations.A: Irradiation to inactivate donor T-lymphocytes and prevent TA-GVHD is described in the sources as a blood-component modification measure; it is not identified as the rationale for 1271 oversight of tissue allografts.
option_explanations.B: Leukoreduction is described as a blood-component processing step that reduces FNHTR, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission risk; the sources do not describe it as part of tissue-banking regulation.
option_explanations.D: BPD reporting via Form FDA 3486 is a described mechanism for reporting distributed-product deviations, but the sources identify prevention of disease transmission — not deviation reporting — as the reason 21 CFR 1271 regulates tissue banking.

source_references.locator: Section II, Domain: Cell and Tissue Therapy
source_references.supports: States FDA 21 CFR Part 1271 regulates tissue banking to prevent disease transmission, and that tissue service activities include bidirectional donor-recipient traceability.

source_references.locator: Section V, Domain 4: Advanced Technologies & Specialized Testing; Section III/IV regulatory nodes
source_references.supports: States hospital services must track human tissue allografts (bone, skin) vein-to-vein and comply with 21 CFR 1271 for HCT/Ps; also lists irradiation for TA-GVHD prevention and leukoreduction for FNHTR/CMV prevention as blood-component measures, and BPD reporting via Form FDA 3486.

source_references.locator: Section 7, Biological Product Deviation (BPD) Reporting
source_references.supports: Describes BPD reporting (Form FDA 3486) as required for distributed products with manufacturing deviations affecting safety, purity, or potency.

review.notes: SRC-010 (blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-board-curriculum.md, 'II. Domain: Cell and Tissue Therapy') states verbatim: 'FDA 21 CFR Part 1271 regulates tissue banking to prevent disease transmission' and 'Tissue service activities include bidirectional traceability between donor and recipient,' with HCT/Ps defined in the same subdomain block. This directly grounds option A. SRC-043 line 53 ('Pretransfusion Processing: Irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention) and leukoreduction (FNHTR/CMV prevention)') confirms B and C describe blood-component measures, not a stated tissue-banking rationale, so th

questions.subdomain: Transfusion Service Support of Tissue Transplantation
questions.stem: The hospital tissue bank distributes a femoral head bone allograft for use in a hip revision surgery. Six weeks postoperatively, the surgeon reports a deep wound infection suspicious for an organism transmitted by the graft and asks the transfusion service to investigate the source. Per FDA 21 CFR Part 1271 requirements for tissue banking, which record-keeping capability must the transfusion service's tissue-tracking system provide to support this investigation?

options.text: Calculation of Hardy-Weinberg allele frequencies for the donor's blood group phenotype to estimate the statistical probability that the donor carried the suspected organism.
Documentation that the case was reported to CBER as a transfusion-associated fatality, since FDA fatality-reporting rules apply to any suspected tissue-transmission event regardless of outcome.

Results of an antihuman-globulin (AHG) crossmatch performed on the recipient's pretransfusion serum against the donor's red cells, since bone allografts require the same compatibility testing as red blood cell transfusion.

Bidirectional traceability that allows the distributed allograft to be traced from the specific recipient back to its donor, and forward again to identify any other recipients of tissue recovered from that same donor.

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that tissue service activities include bidirectional traceability between donor and recipient, and that hospital services must track tissue allografts vein-to-vein. Investigating a suspected transmission event requires exactly this capability: tracing the implicated allograft back to its donor and forward to identify any other recipients of tissue from that same donor.

option_explanations.D: Correct — bidirectional donor-recipient traceability is the specific record-keeping capability the sources describe for tissue banking under 21 CFR 1271.
option_explanations.C: The sources describe immediate-spin (ABO-only) and AHG crossmatch as pretransfusion testing for red blood cell compatibility; no source states that bone allografts require this same serologic crossmatch.
option_explanations.B: The sources reserve mandatory FDA fatality reporting for deaths related to collection or transfusion; this scenario is a nonfatal suspected infection, so mandatory fatality reporting is not the traceability capability being asked about.
option_explanations.A: Hardy-Weinberg calculations are described in the sources as a tool for estimating blood group phenotype frequencies in population genetics, not for investigating a suspected tissue-transmitted infection.

source_references.locator: Section II, Domain: Cell and Tissue Therapy
