questions.stem: A D-negative patient with a previously identified anti-D alloantibody (from a prior D-positive pregnancy) now requires RBC transfusion for symptomatic anemia. The antibody screen is positive and antibody identification confirms anti-D. Which crossmatch and unit-selection strategy is most appropriate before red cells are released for this patient?

options.text: Perform only an immediate-spin crossmatch, since ABO compatibility is all that needs to be confirmed
Give the patient RhIg before transfusion to prevent hemolysis from the existing anti-D
Perform a full AHG-phase crossmatch and select D-negative RBC units
Select red cells from a partial D donor, since partial D donors carry a reduced D antigen load
Select D-positive RBC units, since the patient is already alloimmunized and further D exposure will not change the outcome

questions.explanation_correct: Because anti-D is an IgG alloantibody, an AHG-phase crossmatch (not immediate-spin alone) is required to detect potential incompatibility. Unit-selection guidance specifies that D-negative recipients should receive D-negative red cells, which is essential here to avoid re-exposing an already-sensitized patient to the D antigen and precipitating hemolysis, since Rh antibodies typically cause extravascular hemolysis.

option_explanations.C: Correct: combines the AHG-phase requirement for IgG alloantibody detection with the unit-selection rule that D-negative recipients receive D-negative red cells.
option_explanations.A: Immediate-spin alone detects only ABO incompatibility and would miss reactivity from the clinically significant IgG anti-D already identified in this patient.
option_explanations.B: RhIg is indicated to prevent primary D sensitization (e.g., at 28 weeks' gestation, after delivery of a D-positive infant, or after D-positive platelet exposure), not to treat or blunt hemolysis in a patient who has already formed anti-D.
option_explanations.E: Transfusing D-positive units to a patient with existing anti-D risks a hemolytic transfusion reaction, since Rh antibodies (IgG) typically cause extravascular hemolysis; further D exposure is not inconsequential.
option_explanations.D: Partial D donors must be classified as Rh-positive specifically to prevent sensitizing D-negative recipients, making their red cells inappropriate for a D-negative, anti-D-alloimmunized patient.

questions.teaching_point: A patient with a known clinically significant alloantibody (e.g., anti-D) requires a full AHG-phase crossmatch plus selection of antigen-negative (D-negative) units; RhIg is a preventive measure for D-negative individuals at risk of primary sensitization and has no role once anti-D alloimmunization has already occurred.

source_references.locator: Management: Unit Selection; Common Board Traps: Partial D Donors; Prevention: RhIg Protocol
source_references.supports: States that D-negative recipients should receive D-negative red cells, that partial D donors must be classified as Rh-positive to avoid sensitizing recipients, and lists RhIg indications (28 weeks, post-delivery of D-positive infant, post D-positive platelet exposure) that do not include treating an already-alloimmunized transfusion recipient.

source_references.locator: Domain 2: Pretransfusion Testing; Adverse Reaction Recognition (Allergic & Febrile / Delayed Reactions section)
source_references.supports: Distinguishes immediate-spin (ABO only) from AHG crossmatch (alloantibody) and states Rh antibodies are primarily IgG causing extravascular hemolysis.

review.notes: Well grounded. SRC-020 explicitly states 'D-negative recipients should receive D-negative red cells' (Unit Selection) and 'Partial D Donors: Must be classified as Rh-positive to prevent sensitizing recipients, even if they type weakly,' directly falsifying option E. SRC-020's RhIg Protocol section (28 weeks' gestation, within 72 hours of delivery of a D-positive infant, post D-positive platelet transfusion) contains no indication for treating a patient who already has anti-D, correctly falsifying option C. SRC-043's IS-vs-AHG distinction supports the crossmatch method choice, and SRC-020's sta

questions.stem: A hospital transfusion service uses a validated Blood Establishment Computer System (BECS) to release compatible RBC units to eligible, non-alloimmunized patients without performing a physical antihuman globulin (AHG)-phase serologic crossmatch. Which of the following best describes the underlying principle that allows this validated computer-based ("electronic") process to substitute for the physical serologic crossmatch?

options.text: The computer detects complement (C3d) fixation on donor RBCs via polyspecific antihuman globulin reagent to flag incompatible units
The system uses polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based potentiation to increase antibody uptake before generating a compatibility result

The validated software compares the patient's previously confirmed ABO/Rh typing data against the donor unit's confirmed ABO/Rh group to predict compatibility, without physically mixing patient serum and donor cells
The computer system digitally performs the indirect antiglobulin test (IAT) reaction, internally simulating hemagglutination between reagent cells and the patient's serum

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe the general logic of a computer-based compatibility check (illustrated for HLA as the "Virtual Crossmatch") as comparing a recipient's known, documented profile against the donor's documented type to predict compatibility without a physical mixture of cells and serum. Applied to RBC release, a validated BECS substitutes this data comparison of confirmed ABO/Rh records for the physical serologic step, which is why such software must itself be fully validated before live use.

option_explanations.C: Correct: mirrors the source's description of computer-based compatibility prediction via comparison of documented/confirmed typing data rather than physical mixture of cells and serum, run on software (BECS) that must be validated before clinical use.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources define the IAT as an in-vitro physical reaction between reagent RBCs and serum/plasma (the basis of manual antibody screening/crossmatching) — it is not something a computer system internally simulates as part of the electronic process.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: PEG is described as a physical enhancement medium that concentrates antibody by removing water of hydration during manual/automated serologic AHG testing; it has no role in a computer-based record comparison.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: detection of complement (C3d) coating requires polyspecific AHG reagent in physical antiglobulin testing, per the sources; this is not part of the computer-based verification mechanism.

questions.teaching_point: Electronic (computer) crossmatch substitutes validated computer comparison of already-established ABO/Rh compatibility data for the physical serologic (AHG-phase) mixing step; it depends entirely on the reliability and validation of the underlying computer software, not on any in-vitro antigen-antibody reaction.

source_references.locator: Section 2, HLA System – Crossmatching and Antibody Detection / Virtual Crossmatch expert note
source_references.supports: General principle that a computer-based compatibility check compares a recipient's known profile against a donor's documented type to predict compatibility without physically mixing cells and serum, illustrated via HLA virtual crossmatching.

source_references.locator: Definitions – Blood Establishment Computer System (BECS); Validation Timeline bullet
source_references.supports: Defines BECS as software regulated as a medical device for blood bank/transfusion service functions and states such critical computer software must be fully validated before live clinical use.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20 – Definitions, Indirect Antiglobulin Test (IAT)
source_references.supports: Defines the indirect antiglobulin test (IAT) as the physical in-vitro method detecting reactions between reagent RBCs and unknown serum/plasma, used for antibody screening and serologic crossmatching.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20 – Enhancement Media (Potentiators), PEG
source_references.supports: Describes PEG as a physical enhancement medium that concentrates antibody by removing water of hydration around the RBC, used in manual/automated serologic testing.

review.notes: Option A is correct BB/TM knowledge (validated BECS-based electronic crossmatch works by comparing already-confirmed ABO/Rh records rather than physically mixing serum and cells), and the three distractors are correctly refuted using solid, directly-quoted source definitions (SRC-032 Node 20: IAT is an in-vitro reagent-cell/serum reaction, not something a computer 'simulates'; PEG is a physical potentiator; polyspecific AHG detects C3d in a physical test). However, the core mechanistic claim in the key/explanation is not actually drawn from any RBC-specific electronic-crossmatch passage in the

questions.stem: A transfusion service's policy allows compatible RBC units to be released through a computer-based (electronic) verification process, rather than a full serologic crossmatch, once ABO/Rh compatibility has been reliably established in the patient's record. A 52-year-old woman is admitted for elective surgery. This is her first admission to the facility, and no historical ABO/Rh record exists in the laboratory information system. Her current sample types as Group A, Rh-positive, with a negative antibody screen. Two units of non-group-O RBCs are ordered for possible intraoperative use. What is the most appropriate next step before these units can be issued?

options.text: Issue the Group A RBC units now, since the current ABO/Rh type and antibody screen are both unremarkable
Issue Group O RBC units instead, since without any historical record the ABO type can never be considered verified
Collect a second sample by a separate venipuncture, at a different time, to independently confirm the ABO group before issuing the non-group-O RBC units
Repeat the antibody screen using PEG enhancement to increase sensitivity before issuing the ordered units

questions.explanation_correct: Per the source material, when no historical ABO/Rh record exists for a patient, many facilities require a second sample, collected at a different time, to independently verify the ABO group before non-group-O RBC units are issued. This confirmatory step establishes the reliable ABO record on which any subsequent computer-based (electronic) release ultimately depends; a single current typing plus a negative antibody screen is not, by itself, described as sufficient in the absence of any historical record.

option_explanations.C: Correct: directly reflects the sourced rule that absent a historical record, a second, separately collected sample is required to verify ABO before issuing non-group-O RBCs.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: a board trap — a single current ABO/Rh type and negative screen are not described as sufficient substitutes for the required historical-record confirmation when no prior record exists.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source reserves Group O RBC issuance specifically for situations where an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved before an urgent transfusion need — this scenario (elective surgery, no discrepancy, simply no prior record) does not meet that description.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: PEG enhancement addresses antibody-screen sensitivity, a separate laboratory concern from the specific ABO-confirmation requirement being tested here.

questions.teaching_point: A reliable ABO record — established via two independent determinations when no history exists — is a prerequisite for confidently issuing non-group-O RBCs, and by extension for any subsequent computer-based release; lacking history is not itself grounds for defaulting to Group O.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 29 – Management and Prevention, Confirmation of ABO
source_references.supports: States that when no historical ABO record exists, many facilities require a second sample obtained at a separate time to verify the ABO group before issuing non-group-O RBCs.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 29 – Common Board Traps and Distractors, Group O RBCs
source_references.supports: Clarifies that Group O RBC issuance is reserved for situations where an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved before an urgent transfusion need, not for a routine absence of historical records.

review.notes: Well grounded: SRC-003 Subdomain Node 29 states verbatim that 'If no historical record exists, many facilities require a second sample collected at a different time to verify the ABO group before issuing non-Group O RBCs,' which directly supports key A and correctly refutes distractor B (a single current type + negative screen is explicitly framed as insufficient absent history). The Group O rationale for option C is also directly and correctly sourced ('If an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved before an urgent transfusion is needed, only Group O RBCs should be issued' -- this scenario has no

questions.stem: A transfusion service that routinely uses a validated Blood Establishment Computer System (BECS) to perform computer-based (electronic) release of compatible RBC units experiences a sudden, prolonged computer system outage during a busy on-call shift. A stable, non-bleeding inpatient with a historical Group A, Rh-positive type already on file and a negative current antibody screen requires 1 unit of RBCs for symptomatic anemia. What is the most appropriate immediate course of action?

options.text: Follow the facility's back-up paper-based procedure and issue compatible RBC units using conventional documented methods until the computer system is restored
Proceed with the electronic release based on the last-known valid computer record, since the historical type was already confirmed before the outage began
Delay the transfusion entirely until the computer system is restored, since compatibility cannot be documented without it
Issue Group O RBC units to this patient because the computer system's records cannot presently be trusted

questions.explanation_correct: The source material specifies that facilities must maintain back-up paper documents and procedures for use when computerized information systems are unavailable. During a BECS outage, the electronic release process itself cannot be relied upon, so the transfusion service should revert to its validated manual/back-up documentation procedure to safely continue issuing compatible units rather than defaulting to delay, unsupported verbal release, or an unnecessary switch to Group O.

option_explanations.A: Correct: directly reflects the sourced requirement that facilities maintain back-up paper documents/procedures specifically for use when computerized systems are down.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the existence of a mandated back-up paper procedure is meant to prevent exactly this kind of unnecessary delay in a stable, non-emergent but clinically indicated transfusion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: a board trap — Group O issuance is described specifically for an unresolved ABO discrepancy in an urgent setting, not for a computer outage in a patient with an already-established, undisputed historical type.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the electronic (computer-based) process depends on the operative computer system; when that system is unavailable, the source directs use of the back-up manual procedure rather than continued reliance on the (currently inaccessible) electronic pathway.

questions.teaching_point: Electronic/computer-based release is only usable while the underlying validated computer system is operative; facility back-up paper procedures exist precisely to keep compatible units flowing safely during downtime, without unnecessary delay or an unwarranted switch to Group O.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 27 – Regulatory/Quality Approach, Computer Downtime
source_references.supports: States that facilities must maintain back-up paper documentation and procedures for use when computerized information systems are unavailable.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 29 – Common Board Traps and Distractors, Group O RBCs
source_references.supports: Clarifies that Group O RBC issuance applies specifically to an unresolved ABO discrepancy with an urgent transfusion need, a distinct circumstance from a computer outage with an already-known historical type.

source_references.locator: Definitions – BECS; Validation Timeline
source_references.supports: Notes that BECS/computer software used for transfusion service functions is regulated as a medical device and must be validated before use, underscoring dependence of the electronic process on the operative computer system.

review.notes: Directly and clearly grounded: SRC-003 Node 27 states verbatim 'Facilities must maintain back-up paper documents and procedures for use when computerized information systems are unavailable,' which is an exact match for key A. Option C is correctly refuted using the sourced Group O rule (reserved for unresolved ABO discrepancy with urgent need -- not applicable to a stable patient with an established historical type during a downtime event). Option B's refutation ('back-up procedures exist to prevent exactly this delay') and option D's refutation (electronic release cannot proceed without the

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man develops fever, chills, hypotension, and hemoglobinuria within minutes of starting an RBC transfusion. The transfused unit is group A. Retrospective review shows that his pretransfusion (admission) specimen also typed as group A with a properly matching antibody screen, but a freshly redrawn specimen obtained emergently during the reaction now types as group O. No technical problems are identified with the reagents, equipment, or the technologist's interpretation of either specimen.

options.text: Transient mixed-field agglutination from artificial chimerism related to a remote prior transfusion produced a false group A result on the original specimen
A quality-control failure in the ABO antisera produced a false group A forward-typing result on the original specimen

Wrong blood in tube (WBIT): the original pretransfusion specimen was drawn from, or labeled as belonging to, a different (group A) patient, so testing was performed on blood that did not actually represent this (group O) recipient
Weak expression of the high-prevalence Bg (HLA Class I) antigen on the patient's red cells was misread as anti-A reactivity during forward typing
A technical error during the antiglobulin-phase crossmatch failed to detect ABO incompatibility between the group A unit and the patient

questions.explanation_correct: The source states that most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions result from clerical or identification errors rather than laboratory testing failures, and defines WBIT as blood in a pretransfusion specimen that does not belong to the recipient identified on the label. Here the stem excludes technical/reagent/interpretation error, and the original specimen's group A result is discordant with the patient's true group O type shown on repeat draw — the classic WBIT pattern underlying fatal ABO-incompatible hemolytic transfusion reactions.

option_explanations.C: Correct: matches the source's WBIT definition and its statement that clerical/identification error, not testing failure, is the predominant mechanism of fatal ABO-incompatible transfusion.
option_explanations.B: The stem specifies no reagent or QC problem was found, and the source attributes these fatal events chiefly to identification error rather than reagent failure.
option_explanations.A: Chimerism is described in the source as producing mixed-field agglutination, not a clean discordant single-population type change between two draws, and it is not linked in the source to fatal ABO-incompatible reactions.
option_explanations.E: The stem excludes technical testing error, and the source specifically contrasts identification error against laboratory testing failure as the dominant cause.
option_explanations.D: The source describes Bg (HLA Class I) reactivity as a nuisance finding in antibody workups, not a mechanism that converts a true group O forward type into a false group A result.

questions.teaching_point: Fatal ABO-incompatible transfusion is most commonly caused by specimen/patient identification (labeling) errors — i.e., wrong blood in tube — rather than by failures of serologic testing technique.

source_references.supports: Defines WBIT and states most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions stem from clerical/identification error rather than lab testing failure (Subdomain Node 29).
Describes artificial chimerism from transfusion producing mixed-field agglutination, used as a grounded but incorrect distractor mechanism.

review.notes: SRC-003 (Node 29) directly defines WBIT as blood in a pretransfusion specimen not belonging to the labeled recipient and states most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions arise from clerical/identification error rather than testing failure - matches the stem's exclusion of technical/reagent/interpretation error and the clean A-to-O discordance on repeat draw. SRC-012 (Node 51) confirms chimerism characteristically causes mixed-field agglutination (not a clean single-population type flip), correctly grounding rejection of option C. Option E (Bg/HLA Class I antigen misread as anti-A) is a very wea

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient with no prior ABO/Rh record in the laboratory information system requires RBC transfusion for symptomatic anemia. The current pretransfusion specimen, collected and labeled at the bedside with two independent patient identifiers, types as group A, D-positive, with a negative antibody screen; forward and reverse typing are concordant. The ordering team requests release of group A (non-group O) RBCs for this patient.

options.text: Proceed directly to an immediate-spin crossmatch, since the antibody screen is negative and no additional ABO confirmation is required
Release the group A, D-positive RBCs now, since forward and reverse typing were internally concordant on the current specimen
Relabel the original specimen tube if any identifier discrepancy is noted, then proceed with testing on that tube
Issue group AB RBCs instead, since group AB is considered compatible with recipients of any ABO type
Collect a second specimen at a separate collection event and confirm the ABO group before releasing the group A RBCs

questions.explanation_correct: The source states that when no historical ABO record exists, many facilities require a second sample collected at a different time to verify the ABO group before issuing non-group O RBCs. This guards against WBIT, since a single specimen — even with internally concordant, technically correct results — could still belong to the wrong patient.

option_explanations.E: Correct: directly matches the source's stated practice of requiring a second, separately collected sample to confirm ABO before non-group O release when no historical type exists.
option_explanations.B: Internal concordance of forward/reverse typing does not rule out WBIT; an entirely different patient's blood could type consistently as group A, which is exactly why the source recommends a second independently collected sample.
option_explanations.C: The source specifies that specimen labels must be affixed to the container at the bedside before the phlebotomist leaves the patient; correcting or relabeling an already-collected tube is not the safeguard described — recollection under proper identification is the accepted response to any identity concern.
option_explanations.A: Proceeding to crossmatch without addressing the lack of ABO confirmation skips the specific safeguard the source describes for patients without a historical record who need non-group O units.
option_explanations.D: This reverses the source's guidance: when ABO status cannot yet be safely confirmed before an urgent need, the source specifies issuing group O RBCs (compatible with all recipients for red cells), not group AB.

questions.teaching_point: A technically clean, internally concordant ABO type on a single specimen does not exclude WBIT; absent a historical record, a second independently collected and labeled specimen is the recommended safeguard before releasing non-group O RBCs.

source_references.supports: States that without a historical record, many facilities require a second sample at a different time to verify ABO before issuing non-group O RBCs, and describes bedside labeling and two-identifier requirements (Subdomain Node 29).

review.notes: SRC-003 (Node 29) states verbatim that when no historical ABO record exists, many facilities require a second sample collected at a different time to verify the ABO group before issuing non-group O RBCs - exact match to the stem and key. The same node's bedside-labeling and two-identifier requirements support rejecting option C (relabeling a tube is never the accepted remedy; recollection is). Option E's claim that group AB is compatible with any recipient is factually backwards for RBCs and is correctly contrasted against the source's group-O guidance. No contradicted or invented facts found;

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman arrives in the trauma bay in hemorrhagic shock from a pelvic fracture. She has no historical blood type on file, and ABO/Rh testing on her freshly collected specimen cannot be completed before she requires immediate transfusion. The trauma attending requests emergency release of RBCs.

options.text: Issue uncrossmatched RBCs based on the partially completed current-specimen ABO typing, without obtaining a signed physician statement
Withhold all RBC transfusion and provide crystalloid resuscitation only until the complete antibody screen and crossmatch are finalized
Issue uncrossmatched group O RBCs under a signed physician statement documenting the emergency, with the units clearly labeled to indicate that pretransfusion testing is incomplete
Delay all RBC transfusion until a second specimen is drawn and ABO/Rh typing is fully completed and confirmed
Issue uncrossmatched group AB RBCs, since group AB is considered compatible with recipients of any ABO type

questions.explanation_correct: The source defines Emergency Release as the standard for urgent issuance of uncrossmatched group O RBCs, requiring a signed physician's statement and clear labeling that testing is incomplete. It also specifies that when an ABO discrepancy or incomplete testing situation cannot be resolved before an urgent transfusion is needed, only group O RBCs should be issued — matching this trauma scenario precisely.

option_explanations.C: Correct: matches the Emergency Release standard exactly, including the physician statement and the labeling requirement flagging incomplete testing.
option_explanations.D: Delaying transfusion in life-threatening hemorrhage contradicts the purpose of emergency release, which exists specifically to allow urgent issuance before testing is complete.
option_explanations.A: Bypassing the signed physician statement violates the Emergency Release requirement, and releasing group-specific (non-O) units before ABO is confirmed contradicts the source's directive to issue group O RBCs when testing cannot be completed before urgent need.
option_explanations.E: The source specifies group O, not group AB, as the RBC of choice when ABO status is unresolved before an urgent transfusion; group AB is not identified in the source as the emergency RBC option.
option_explanations.B: Withholding RBCs entirely is not supported by the source, which establishes the emergency-release pathway precisely so that transfusion is not withheld while testing is finalized.

questions.teaching_point: Emergency release of uncrossmatched group O RBCs requires both a signed physician statement and clear unit labeling stating that pretransfusion testing is incomplete — labeling is an integral, non-optional part of the safety mechanism, not paperwork after the fact.

source_references.supports: Defines Emergency Release as urgent issuance of uncrossmatched group O RBCs requiring a signed physician statement and labeling indicating incomplete testing (Subdomain Node 55).
States that if an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved before an urgent transfusion, only group O RBCs should be issued (Subdomain Node 29 board trap).

review.notes: SRC-012 (Node 55) defines Emergency Release precisely as urgent issuance of uncrossmatched group O RBCs requiring a signed physician's statement and clear labeling that testing is incomplete - matches option A word-for-word in substance. SRC-003 (Node 29 board trap) independently corroborates that group O (not AB) is issued when ABO status can't be resolved before an urgent transfusion, supporting rejection of option D. Options B/E (withholding transfusion) and C (skipping the physician statement / using unconfirmed group-specific blood) are all directly contradicted by the Emergency Release d

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision with a blood pressure of 70/40 mmHg. He has no prior blood bank record at this facility, and there is no time to result a type and screen before transfusion is needed. The trauma team requests immediate red cells, and the blood bank issues uncrossmatched Group O RBCs under the emergency release protocol. Which of the following best explains the immunologic reason Group O red cells specifically prevent an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction in this scenario, regardless of the patient's actual (unknown) ABO type?

options.text: Group O red cells come only from donors previously confirmed to lack any red cell alloantibodies of clinical significance
Group O red cells prevent activation of donor leukocyte antibodies, thereby avoiding transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) in the recipient

Group O red cells lack both A and B antigens, so there is no antigen present for the recipient's anti-A and/or anti-B to bind, preventing the IgM/complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis that is the hallmark of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
Group O units have already undergone a full antihuman globulin crossmatch against panel cells, so no further serologic testing is needed regardless of the recipient's antibodies
Group O red cells lack the D antigen, preventing anti-D-mediated intravascular hemolysis in an Rh-negative recipient

questions.explanation_correct: Group O red cells express neither the A nor the B antigen (per ABO transferase biochemistry). Because acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are classically driven by IgM anti-A/anti-B triggering the complement cascade to produce the visible (intravascular) hemolysis that is the hallmark of AHTR, red cells lacking A/B antigens cannot serve as a target for those antibodies no matter what ABO type the recipient turns out to be. This is why uncrossmatched Group O RBCs are the emergency-release standard while ABO/Rh typing and full compatibility testing are completed in parallel.

option_explanations.C: Correct — absence of A/B antigens removes the substrate for anti-A/anti-B-driven, complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis regardless of the recipient's true ABO group.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — ABO group and Rh(D) type are independent; not all Group O units are D-negative, and anti-D-mediated hemolysis is a delayed, IgG/extravascular mechanism, not the acute intravascular process being avoided here.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — this directly contradicts the definition of emergency release, which specifies uncrossmatched units are issued precisely because there has been no time for AHG crossmatch.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — conflates donor antibody status with the actual mechanism; the risk being mitigated is the recipient's anti-A/anti-B reacting against donor antigens, not donor-derived alloantibodies.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — TRALI results from donor antibodies reacting against recipient leukocyte/HLA antigens, an unrelated mechanism distinct from ABO-incompatible hemolysis.

questions.teaching_point: Group O RBCs are the emergency-release default because their absence of A/B antigens makes complement-mediated ABO-incompatible intravascular hemolysis (the AHTR mechanism) impossible, independent of the unknown recipient's true ABO type.

review.notes: Key A is correct and clearly the best answer among the five options. However, source grounding is thin: SRC-043's bullets are terse topic headers ('ABO System (001): Biochemistry of H, A, and B transferases'; 'Basic Immunology: Complement pathways and IgG vs. IgM clinical kinetics'; 'Acute Hemolytic Reactions (AHTR): Clerical error is the #1 cause; hallmark is visual hemolysis'). None of these bullets explicitly states that Group O cells lack A/B antigens, that AHTR is IgM/complement-mediated, or that the hallmark 'visual hemolysis' equals 'intravascular hemolysis.' These are true, standard bl

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with active gastrointestinal hemorrhage requires urgent transfusion. Her current blood bank specimen shows group A, Rh-positive typing. Because of clinical urgency, the technologist selects group A, Rh-positive RBC units and performs only an immediate-spin crossmatch — mixing the patient's plasma with donor red cells, centrifuging, and reading immediately — rather than waiting for the antihuman globulin (AHG) phase before issuing the units. A negative immediate-spin crossmatch in this situation provides reliable evidence of compatibility for which of the following?

options.text: The presence or absence of clinically significant alloantibodies in the recipient's plasma
ABO compatibility between the donor unit and the recipient (major ABO incompatibility)
Complete serologic equivalence to a full AHG crossmatch, so no further testing is needed once the patient stabilizes
Rh(D) compatibility between the donor unit and the recipient
The absence of any risk for a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction

questions.explanation_correct: Pretransfusion testing distinguishes the immediate-spin crossmatch, which detects ABO incompatibility only, from the antihuman globulin (AHG) crossmatch, which is needed to detect clinically significant alloantibodies. A negative immediate-spin result therefore only confirms that no major ABO mismatch exists between this donor unit and this recipient's plasma; it does not evaluate for alloantibodies, which requires carrying the reaction through the AHG phase.

option_explanations.B: Correct — immediate-spin crossmatch is specifically the ABO-only compatibility check.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — detection of clinically significant alloantibodies is the role of the AHG crossmatch phase, not the immediate-spin phase.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — Rh(D) compatibility is established by independent ABO/Rh typing of donor and recipient, not demonstrated by the crossmatch reaction itself.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — the immediate-spin and AHG crossmatch phases test for different things; an immediate-spin-only result does not substitute for full testing, which must still be completed.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — delayed hemolytic reactions relate to anamnestic alloantibody responses that would only be picked up by antibody screening/AHG-phase testing, not by an immediate-spin reading.

questions.teaching_point: An immediate-spin crossmatch only rules out major ABO incompatibility; it cannot substitute for the AHG phase needed to detect clinically significant alloantibodies, a key distinction when abbreviating testing under time pressure.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 node list, 'Pretransfusion Testing' bullet
source_references.supports: States pretransfusion testing distinguishes immediate-spin (ABO only) from AHG crossmatch (detects alloantibody).

source_references.locator: Node 55 – Emergency Management & Continuity, 'Emergency Release' bullet
source_references.supports: Emergency/urgent release framework requires testing to be clearly identified as complete or incomplete on the unit label.

review.notes: Core fact is a near-verbatim match to SRC-043: 'Pretransfusion Testing: Immediate-spin (ABO only) vs. AHG crossmatch (alloantibody).' Key A is correct and the distractors (B alloantibody detection, C Rh compatibility, D full AHG equivalence, E delayed hemolytic reaction risk) are all clearly and correctly excluded by that same distinction — no invented facts found. However, the assigned blueprint node is specifically 'Emergency Release' (node S3.d.ii.4.g), and this stem never invokes emergency release (no unknown/uncrossmatched Group O issuance, no physician statement, no incomplete-testing la

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman develops massive postpartum hemorrhage with hypotension. No blood bank specimen has yet been drawn, and the obstetric team calls the blood bank requesting immediate red cells while a type and screen specimen is being collected and processed. Which of the following is the best next step for the blood bank to support this request?

options.text: Withhold all red cells until a type and screen specimen arrives and ABO/Rh typing is completed

Issue uncrossmatched Group O RBCs immediately, together with a signed statement from the requesting physician documenting the emergency, and label the units to indicate that compatibility testing is incomplete
Issue uncrossmatched RBCs without any physician documentation, since the clinical emergency is self-evident
Administer hemoglobin-based oxygen carriers (HBOCs) instead, since they do not require crossmatching and are readily stocked for routine emergency use
Delay red cell issue until a full massive transfusion protocol with fixed 1:1:1 component ratios can be assembled

questions.explanation_correct: The emergency release standard is designed exactly for this situation: urgent issuance of uncrossmatched Group O RBCs, which requires a signed statement from the physician documenting the emergency and clear labeling that testing is incomplete, so that red cells are not withheld from a hemorrhaging patient while ABO/Rh typing and compatibility testing proceed in parallel.

option_explanations.B: Correct — this is precisely the documented emergency release process: uncrossmatched Group O units, a signed physician statement, and labeling that testing is incomplete.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — withholding transfusion until typing is complete delays life-saving therapy in an actively hemorrhaging patient, defeating the purpose of the emergency release pathway.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — the signed physician statement documenting the emergency is a required element of the emergency release process, not an optional formality.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — while HBOCs do not require crossmatching, they are available primarily through clinical trials or expanded/compassionate access, not as a routinely stocked emergency substitute for RBCs.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — massive transfusion protocol ratios address trauma-induced coagulopathy once transfusion is underway; assembling a full 1:1:1 protocol is not a prerequisite for the immediate red cell need addressed by emergency release.

questions.teaching_point: When a patient is hemorrhaging faster than compatibility testing can be completed, the correct action is immediate emergency release of uncrossmatched Group O RBCs with a signed physician statement and clear 'testing incomplete' labeling — not delaying transfusion or substituting other measures.

source_references.locator: Node 55 – Emergency Management & Continuity, 'Emergency Release' bullet
source_references.supports: Defines emergency release: uncrossmatched Group O RBCs, signed physician statement, and labeling indicating incomplete testing.

source_references.locator: Node 54 – Management of Transfusion Refusal, 'HBOCs' bullet
source_references.supports: Notes HBOCs do not require crossmatching but are currently available mainly via clinical trials or compassionate/expanded access, not routine stock.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 node list, 'Massive Transfusion (MTP)' bullet
source_references.supports: Describes massive transfusion protocol as defined 1:1:1 component ratios for trauma-induced coagulopathy, a distinct process from initial emergency RBC release.

review.notes: Strong, precise match to SRC-012 Node 55: 'Emergency Release: Standard for the urgent issuance of uncrossmatched Group O RBCs, requiring a signed physician's statement and clear labeling indicating testing is incomplete.' Option D's HBOC rationale matches SRC-012 Node 54 verbatim ('Currently available primarily through clinical trials or expanded access (compassionate use)'). Option E's MTP characterization matches SRC-043 ('Defined 1:1:1 ratios to combat trauma-induced coagulopathy'). All distractors are correctly and clearly excluded; this question directly and accurately targets the assigne

questions.stem: During transfusion of a unit of RBCs, a patient develops sudden fever, chills, flank pain, and passage of dark red urine within minutes of the unit being started. The transfusion is stopped immediately. Investigation reveals that the unit was actually intended for a different patient on the same ward with a similar name, and that the bedside identification check that should have caught this mismatch before the unit was hung was not properly performed. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this patient's presentation?

options.text: An anamnestic IgG antibody response to a Kidd (Jk) alloantigen, undetectable at the time of pretransfusion testing, is mediating gradual destruction of the transfused red cells, typically becoming apparent 2 to 14 days after transfusion.
Anaphylaxis due to recipient antibodies against donor plasma proteins, associated with IgA deficiency, is occurring.
Cytokines that accumulated in the red cell unit during storage, or recipient antibodies to donor leukocytes, are producing fever without red cell destruction (febrile non-hemolytic reaction).
This is a transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI), an inflammatory, noncardiogenic pulmonary reaction rather than a hemolytic one.

Recipient IgM and/or IgG anti-A or anti-B antibodies bind antigens on the ABO-incompatible donor red cells and fix complement, causing intravascular hemolysis with cytokine release and risk of DIC and acute renal injury.

questions.explanation_correct: The source material defines the pathophysiology of AHTR as IgM or IgG antibodies fixing complement, producing intravascular hemolysis, cytokine release, DIC, and potential acute renal failure, and states that ABO incompatibility from clerical or patient identification errors is the most common cause. The bedside identification check exists precisely because most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions result from clerical/identification errors rather than laboratory testing failures, and the transfusionist is described as the 'final check' or last line of defense before the unit is started. The dark red urine (hemoglobinuria) and flank pain reflect this intravascular hemolytic process.

option_explanations.E: Correct: matches the source's description of AHTR pathophysiology (complement-fixing IgM/IgG causing intravascular hemolysis, cytokines, DIC, renal injury) and etiology (clerical/ID error causing ABO incompatibility), which is exactly what the bedside check is designed to prevent.
option_explanations.A: Describes the DHTR mechanism (anamnestic IgG, often Kidd), but DHTR is defined as occurring 2 to 14 days post-transfusion, not within minutes, and does not fit this acute presentation.
option_explanations.D: TRALI is described in the sources as an inflammatory, noncardiogenic pulmonary process distinct from TACO and from hemolytic reactions; it does not explain hemoglobinuria and flank pain.
option_explanations.B: Anaphylaxis is described as immediate-onset respiratory distress, wheezing, hypotension, and shock, often linked to IgA deficiency — a different symptom complex than the hemolysis-driven hemoglobinuria and flank pain seen here.
option_explanations.C: FNHTR is caused by recipient antibodies to donor leukocytes or pre-formed cytokines and is a diagnosis of exclusion after hemolysis is ruled out; this patient's dark urine indicates hemolysis is present, not excluded.

questions.teaching_point: The bedside identification check is the final safeguard against ABO-incompatible transfusion, and the resulting acute hemolytic reaction is mediated by complement-fixing antibodies causing intravascular hemolysis — distinct in mechanism and timing from DHTR, TRALI, anaphylaxis, and FNHTR.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 section, Adverse Reaction Recognition
source_references.supports: TRALI described as inflammatory/noncardiogenic versus TACO as hydrostatic/volume overload, used to construct and refute distractor C.

review.notes: Core content is well grounded: SRC-058 Node 31 explicitly states AHTR pathophysiology as 'IgM or IgG antibodies fix complement, leading to intravascular hemolysis, cytokine release, DIC, and potential acute renal failure' and etiology as 'most commonly caused by ABO incompatibility resulting from clerical or patient identification errors' (lines 21-23). SRC-003 Node 29 confirms the 'final check'/last-line-of-defense framing and that most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions stem from clerical/ID errors (lines 133-135). Distractor rationales for B (DHTR, 2-14 days, Kidd/Rh), C (TRALI as inflamma

questions.stem: Shortly after the first unit of RBCs is started as part of a two-unit order, a 52-year-old woman develops fever, low back pain, and passage of red-brown urine. The transfusion is stopped immediately, and the bedside clerical check performed at the start of the infusion is confirmed to have correctly matched the patient's wristband identifiers to the unit tag. In the laboratory, the post-reaction plasma sample is grossly pink compared with the patient's pre-transfusion sample, the DAT on the post-reaction sample shows a mixed-field pattern, and repeat ABO typing of the post-reaction sample yields group A — whereas the pretransfusion specimen originally submitted for type and screen (and used to select this 'ABO-matched' unit) had typed as group B. Which of the following best explains this set of findings?

options.text: The mixed-field DAT and pink plasma reflect a febrile non-hemolytic reaction, and the ABO discrepancy is an unrelated laboratory error requiring specimen recollection.

The pretransfusion specimen originally submitted for type and screen was not actually drawn from this patient (wrong blood in tube), so an ABO-incompatible unit was selected despite a correct bedside identification check at the time of administration.
The patient's ABO type was correctly determined on both occasions, and a technical error in antisera reactivity produced the discrepant post-reaction result.
A clerical error occurred at the bedside when the unit was hung, despite the documented verification, and the wrong unit was infused into this patient.
The findings represent a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction caused by an anamnestic anti-Kidd antibody that was undetectable at the time of the original crossmatch.

questions.explanation_correct: The source defines 'wrong blood in tube' (WBIT) as blood in a pretransfusion sample tube that does not belong to the recipient named on the label, and specifically requires that specimen labels be affixed to the container before the phlebotomist leaves the patient's side to prevent this. Here, the bedside administration check was verified correct, so the failure occurred earlier — at specimen collection/labeling — producing a mismatched pretransfusion type (group B) that led to selection of an ABO-incompatible unit for this actual group A patient. The pink post-reaction plasma, mixed-field DAT, and confirmatory repeat ABO typing are the source-described AHTR workup findings (visual inspection for hemoglobinemia, DAT indicating sensitized donor cells, repeat ABO of the post-sample) that establish an acute intravascular hemolytic process consistent with ABO incompatibility.

option_explanations.B: Correct: fits the source's WBIT definition and the rationale for the specimen-labeling-at-bedside requirement, combined with source-defined AHTR workup findings (mixed-field DAT, hemoglobinemia, repeat ABO confirmation) that establish true intravascular hemolysis from an ABO-incompatible unit.
option_explanations.D: The bedside administration check was explicitly confirmed correct in the stem, so a mismatch at the moment of hanging the unit has already been excluded as the point of failure.
option_explanations.C: Antisera reactivity and specificity are subject to day-of-use QC per the sources; the combination of hemolysis markers (pink plasma, mixed-field DAT) and a genuine ABO discrepancy between the original specimen and the patient's true type is the pattern the sources associate with WBIT, not a reagent artifact.
option_explanations.E: DHTR is described as an anamnestic response typically to Kidd or Rh antigens presenting 2 to 14 days after transfusion; it does not explain an acute presentation within minutes or an ABO discrepancy.
option_explanations.A: FNHTR is described as a diagnosis of exclusion made only after hemolysis is ruled out; the pink plasma and mixed-field DAT are the source-defined evidence of hemolysis, so dismissing the ABO discrepancy as unrelated ignores these findings.

questions.teaching_point: A correct bedside administration check does not rule out an upstream WBIT event at specimen collection; mixed-field DAT, hemoglobinemia, and a genuine ABO discrepancy between the original type-and-screen specimen and the patient's confirmed type point to mislabeling at the point of collection, which is exactly what bedside specimen-labeling rules are designed to prevent.

AHTR workup steps (visual inspection for hemoglobinemia, DAT/mixed-field, repeat ABO of patient and unit) are directly supported by SRC-058 Node 31 (lines 39-43). DHTR timing/mechanism (distractor D) and FNHTR-as-exclusion (distractor E) are correctly grounded. The antisera Day-of-Use QC fact used to weigh against distractor C is correctly grounded in SRC-043's QC table (line 201). Two

questions.stem: At the bedside, prior to starting an RBC transfusion, two qualified staff members perform the required verification. The patient's wristband reads: Maria Alvarez, DOB 03/14/1965, MRN 445890. The tag attached to the unit reads: Maria Alvarez, DOB 03/14/1965, MRN 445980. No other patient care is urgent at this moment. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Document the discrepancy as a likely transcription error and proceed with the transfusion, monitoring the patient closely for a reaction.
Relabel the unit tag with the wristband's MRN and proceed with the transfusion.
Have a single staff member re-verify the identifiers alone, and proceed if that person confirms a match.
Do not begin the transfusion; hold the unit and notify the blood bank to resolve the identifier discrepancy before proceeding.
Proceed with the transfusion because the name and date of birth — two of the three identifiers — are identical.

questions.explanation_correct: The sources require two independent identifiers — the patient's first and last name plus a unique number such as MRN — to be present and matching on the request, specimen label, and patient's wristband, and require two-person verification (or one person using a validated electronic system) to confirm identity and match the unit to the patient before initiating a transfusion. The transfusionist is described as the last line of defense against clerical/identification errors, which are the leading cause of fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions. An MRN mismatch is a discrepancy in one of the two required unique identifiers, so the transfusion must not proceed until the blood bank resolves which record is correct.

option_explanations.D: Correct: this is exactly the function of the two-person bedside verification requirement — detecting identifier mismatches and resolving them through the blood bank before the unit is started, since the transfusionist is the final safeguard against clerical/ID errors.
option_explanations.E: The unique numeric identifier (MRN) is one of the two required independent identifiers and must match; the source's standard does not permit proceeding on partial identifier agreement, since this is the exact type of clerical error the check is designed to catch.
option_explanations.C: The source specifies verification by two qualified individuals, or by one person using a validated electronic system — not a single person performing an unaided manual re-check, which does not meet the described standard.
option_explanations.A: Assuming the discrepancy is a benign transcription error and proceeding defeats the stated purpose of the bedside check, which exists because most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions result from exactly this kind of clerical/identification error.
option_explanations.B: Altering the unit tag to force a match bypasses verification entirely and does not establish which record (wristband or tag) is actually correct, so it does not resolve the identification error.

questions.teaching_point: Bedside verification requires exact agreement of both required independent identifiers (name and a unique number) between the patient's wristband and the unit tag, performed by two qualified individuals or a validated electronic system; any discrepancy — even a single-digit MRN mismatch — must halt the transfusion until resolved by the blood bank.

source_references.supports: Two-person verification requirement, definition of two independent identifiers (name plus unique number such as MRN/DOB), and the requirement to match wristband identifiers to the unit label/tag at the bedside before transfusion.

review.notes: Fully grounded in SRC-003 Node 29: two-independent-identifier requirement (name + unique number such as DOB or MRN) present on wristband/request/specimen (line 143), two-qualified-individuals verification (or one person with a validated electronic system) before initiating transfusion (line 137), bedside matching of armband identifiers to unit tag (line 153), and the 'final check'/last-line-of-defense rationale explaining why any discrepancy must halt the transfusion (lines 133-135). All distractors are cleanly excluded: B contradicts the stated single-person-insufficient standard; C proceedin

questions.stem: A hospital's hemovigilance program is reviewing a case in which a patient receiving a unit of plasma developed acute dyspnea, hypoxemia, and new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates within a few hours of the transfusion, with no clinical evidence of volume overload. To correctly classify this reaction for hemovigilance reporting, which of the following best describes its underlying pathophysiologic mechanism?

options.text: A cytokine-mediated (febrile-type) reaction
An inflammatory process causing noncardiogenic pulmonary edema
Immune-mediated hemolysis following an ABO-incompatible transfusion due to a clerical/identification error
Hydrostatic pulmonary edema resulting from volume overload
An allergic reaction related to IgA deficiency in the recipient

questions.explanation_correct: The vignette describes noncardiogenic pulmonary edema (respiratory distress with infiltrates and no volume overload) occurring shortly after transfusion. The source material distinguishes TRALI as an inflammatory, noncardiogenic process from TACO, which is hydrostatic/volume-overload-driven; TRALI and TACO are both among the leading transfusion-related fatalities that hemovigilance surveillance is designed to capture and correctly classify.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the source's characterization of TRALI as an inflammatory, noncardiogenic mechanism, consistent with pulmonary edema occurring without volume overload.
option_explanations.D: This describes TACO, the hydrostatic/volume-overload mechanism explicitly contrasted with TRALI in the source; the absence of volume overload in the stem argues against this choice.
option_explanations.E: This describes the mechanism the source attributes to allergic reactions (IgA deficiency in the recipient), not a pulmonary-infiltrate presentation.
option_explanations.A: This describes the mechanism attributed to febrile reactions (cytokines), which produce fever rather than the noncardiogenic pulmonary edema picture described.
option_explanations.C: This describes the mechanism of acute hemolytic transfusion reactions (clerical-error-driven ABO incompatibility per the source), an unrelated hemolytic process rather than a respiratory presentation.

questions.teaching_point: Hemovigilance classification depends on correctly matching a reaction's clinical picture to its underlying mechanism (TRALI vs. TACO vs. allergic vs. febrile vs. hemolytic) because each category has a distinct reporting pathway.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 section, Adverse Reaction Recognition bullet list
source_references.supports: States 'TRALI vs. TACO: TRALI (inflammatory, noncardiogenic) vs. TACO (hydrostatic, volume overload)' and 'Allergic & Febrile Reactions: Pathophysiology of IgA deficiency (Allergic) vs. cytokines (Febrile)' and 'Acute Hemolytic Reactions (AHTR): Clerical error is the #1 cause.'

source_references.supports: Identifies TRALI, TACO, and hemolytic reactions as the most frequent transfusion-related fatalities reported to the FDA, and clerical error as the leading cause of acute hemolytic reactions.

source_references.locator: Domain IX, Clinical/Managerial Presentations section
source_references.supports: Confirms TRALI and TACO as the leading causes of transfusion-related fatalities reported to the FDA.

review.notes: SRC-043 (line 81) explicitly states 'TRALI (inflammatory, noncardiogenic) vs. TACO (hydrostatic, volume overload)' and (line 83) 'Pathophysiology of IgA deficiency (Allergic) vs. cytokines (Febrile)'; SRC-032 and SRC-033 corroborate TRALI/TACO/hemolytic as leading FDA-reported transfusion fatalities. The vignette (acute dyspnea/hypoxemia/bilateral infiltrates, no volume overload, plasma transfusion) maps cleanly onto TRALI's inflammatory/noncardiogenic mechanism; TACO, allergic, febrile, and hemolytic distractors are each excluded by specific stem details (no volume overload, respiratory not u

questions.stem: As part of a hospital's hemovigilance case review, a patient develops fever and rigors during an RBC transfusion. Investigation confirms the unit was correctly ABO/Rh-matched to the intended recipient with no clerical error, the post-reaction direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is negative, and there is no visual hemolysis in the post-transfusion plasma. The patient is also found to have a newly positive urine culture obtained the same day, and the treating clinician judges a urinary tract infection to be at least as likely a cause of the fever as the transfusion. Using the NHSN imputability framework used for hemovigilance reporting, how should this event be classified?

options.text: Near-Miss Event
Probable
Doubtful/Ruled Out
Possible
Definitive

questions.explanation_correct: The NHSN imputability scheme grades how likely it is that a reported reaction was actually caused by transfusion. 'Possible' applies when transfusion cannot be ruled out but another cause is considered at least as likely — exactly the situation here, where a competing UTI diagnosis is judged equally plausible. The negative DAT, absence of visual hemolysis, and confirmed correct ABO match argue against a hemolytic process, and the case does not meet the higher-certainty 'Definitive' or 'Probable' thresholds, nor does it meet the 'Doubtful/Ruled Out' threshold, which requires evidence that clearly favors or conclusively proves a non-transfusion cause.

option_explanations.E: Definitive requires the reaction to fulfill all case-definition criteria; the ambiguous, competing-diagnosis scenario here does not meet that threshold.
option_explanations.B: Probable requires transfusion to be judged the most likely cause; here the clinician considers the UTI at least equally likely, which is inconsistent with 'most likely.'
option_explanations.D: Correct: transfusion cannot be excluded, but another potential cause (the UTI) is present and considered likely, matching the source's definition of 'Possible.'
option_explanations.C: Doubtful/Ruled Out requires evidence that clearly favors or conclusively proves a cause other than transfusion; a merely competing, equally likely alternative does not conclusively exclude transfusion.
option_explanations.A: A Near-Miss Event is an occurrence that had the potential for harm but did not result in an adverse outcome (e.g., an error caught before reaching the patient); this case involves an actual reaction in a transfused patient, not an intercepted error.

questions.teaching_point: Imputability classification in hemovigilance reporting requires weighing serologic/clinical evidence (DAT, hemolysis, ABO verification) against competing clinical explanations, not simply whether a reaction occurred during transfusion.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 18 - Hemovigilance & Adverse Event Monitoring, NHSN Imputability Designations and Adverse Event Classifications sections
source_references.supports: Defines the four NHSN imputability designations (Definitive, Probable, Possible, Doubtful/Ruled Out) and the Near-Miss Event category as a distinct adverse-event classification.

source_references.locator: Domain IX, Clinical/Managerial Presentations section
source_references.supports: Corroborates the Near-Miss Event definition (unexpected occurrence that did not result in harm but could have).
