source_references.supports: Corroborates the Near-miss Event glossary definition and states clerical error is the primary cause of acute hemolytic reactions with a visual-hemolysis hallmark, supporting ruling out a hemolytic mechanism in this case.

review.notes: SRC-032 (lines 103-111) gives verbatim NHSN imputability definitions: Definitive (fulfills all case-definition criteria), Probable (transfusion most likely cause), Possible (transfusion cannot be ruled out but other potential causes are likely), Doubtful/Ruled Out (evidence clearly favors or conclusively proves a non-transfusion cause); SRC-033 and SRC-043 corroborate the Near-Miss definition used to exclude option E. The stem's negative DAT/no visual hemolysis/confirmed ABO match correctly excludes a hemolytic mechanism, and 'UTI at least as likely as transfusion' precisely satisfies the 'Pos

questions.stem: A hospital transfusion service confirms that a patient died shortly after receiving an ABO-incompatible RBC unit that was issued and administered because of a bedside patient-identification (clerical) error. Per hemovigilance and federal reporting requirements, what is the most appropriate immediate reporting action by the facility?

options.text: Complete a full root-cause analysis first, and notify FDA only if the analysis confirms the transfusion caused the death
Submit only a Biological Product Deviation Report to FDA, which may be filed up to 45 calendar days after discovery
Notify FDA (CBER) as soon as possible by phone or electronic means, followed by a written report within 7 days
Report the event only to the blood collection facility/supplier, since the hospital has no independent obligation to notify FDA
Withhold external notification until a confirmatory autopsy establishes transfusion as the cause of death

questions.explanation_correct: The source material specifies that fatalities related to collection or transfusion must be reported to the FDA (CBER) as soon as possible by oral or electronic means, with a written follow-up report required within 7 days — a distinct, more urgent pathway than the general Biological Product Deviation reporting timeline. Clerical error is identified as the leading, largely preventable cause of acute hemolytic transfusion reactions, which is precisely the type of suspected transfusion fatality that triggers this expedited reporting requirement rather than waiting for full confirmation.

option_explanations.C: Correct: matches the source's stated FDA (CBER) fatality reporting requirement (ASAP oral/electronic notification plus a written report within 7 days).
option_explanations.B: This confuses the fatality reporting pathway with the separate Biological Product Deviation reporting requirement, which the source states allows up to 45 calendar days from discovery and applies to manufacturing deviations, not the urgent fatality-specific timeline.
option_explanations.E: The requirement is to report a suspected fatality as soon as possible; waiting for autopsy confirmation contradicts the mandated expedited (ASAP) timeline.
option_explanations.D: Reporting only to the supplier does not satisfy the facility's independent, mandated FDA (CBER) fatality-reporting obligation described in the sources.
option_explanations.A: Delaying notification until a root-cause analysis is complete is inconsistent with the 'as soon as possible' oral/electronic reporting requirement for suspected transfusion fatalities.

questions.teaching_point: Suspected transfusion fatalities require an expedited FDA (CBER) notification pathway (ASAP + 7-day written report) distinct from the 45-day Biological Product Deviation reporting timeline used for manufacturing deviations.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 18, Common Board Traps and Distractors section
source_references.supports: States suspected transfusion-related deaths must be reported to FDA (CBER) as soon as possible (oral/electronic) with a written report within 7 days, and that clerical error is the primary, preventable cause of acute hemolytic reactions.

source_references.locator: Domain IX, Management section (Fatality Reporting and Biological Product Deviation Reporting)
source_references.supports: States fatality reporting to FDA CBER must occur ASAP (oral/electronic) with a written report within 7 days, and distinguishes this from Biological Product Deviation reporting, which is required as soon as possible but not to exceed 45 calendar days from discovery.

source_references.locator: Domain 3: Regulatory, Quality & Operations section
source_references.supports: States FDA fatality reporting requirements for deaths during collection or transfusion, and that Biological Product Deviations are reported via Form FDA 3486 as a separate mechanism.

review.notes: All three of SRC-032 (line 125), SRC-033 (line 57), and SRC-043 (line 155) state that suspected transfusion-related deaths/fatalities must be reported to FDA (CBER) as soon as possible (oral/electronic) with a written report within 7 days, distinct from Biological Product Deviation reporting which SRC-033 (line 55) and SRC-043 (line 153) place at up to 45 calendar days from discovery via Form FDA 3486. Clerical error as the #1 cause of acute hemolytic reactions is confirmed in SRC-043 (line 79) and SRC-032 (line 127). Option A is clearly the single best, fully sourced answer; distractors B-E a

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is being typed prior to an elective procedure. Forward grouping shows no agglutination with either anti-A or anti-B reagent. Reverse grouping shows strong agglutination of the patient's own serum against reagent A1 cells, reagent B cells, and even reagent group O screening cells, at both room temperature and after the antihuman globulin phase. There is no history of transfusion or transplant, and the family history is notable for parental consanguinity. Molecular testing confirms that the patient inherited a functional A-transferase allele from one parent. Which of the following best explains why this patient's red cells fail to express any ABH antigens despite carrying a functional A-transferase gene?

options.text: Homozygosity for amorphic (silent) alleles at the FUT1 (H) locus prevents synthesis of H antigen, leaving no fucosylated precursor substrate for the inherited A transferase to act upon
The patient is genetically A2, a subgroup in which the A transferase has reduced but not absent enzymatic activity
Loss of Se gene function converts the erythrocyte's H antigen into Lewis antigen through passive adsorption
A homozygous nonsecretor genotype at the FUT2 locus eliminates H substance from the red cell membrane while sparing secreted body fluids
Acquired modification of red cell antigens by intestinal bacteria, as in the acquired B phenomenon, has eliminated antigen expression

questions.explanation_correct: This is the Bombay (Oh) phenotype. The H gene (FUT1) encodes an alpha-2-L-fucosyltransferase that adds L-fucose to the precursor chain to create H antigen; H antigen is the obligatory substrate that A and B transferases must have available before they can add their respective terminal sugars. When both FUT1 alleles are silent (amorphic 'h' alleles), no H substance is made, so even a fully functional inherited A-transferase allele has nothing to act on and no A antigen is expressed. The resulting red cells type as O on forward grouping but, unlike true group O, the serum contains a potent anti-H that agglutinates essentially all other red cells, including reagent group O cells, because virtually all non-Bombay cells display H antigen.

option_explanations.B: A2 is a true A subgroup with weaker but still present A-transferase activity; A2 red cells still react (often weakly) with anti-A and would not produce an antibody that agglutinates every group O reagent cell tested.
option_explanations.D: FUT2 (the secretor gene) governs H substance in secretions/body fluids, distinct from FUT1 (the erythroid gene) that governs red cell H antigen; a nonsecretor genotype at FUT2 does not remove H antigen from the red cell membrane, so this does not explain the phenotype.
option_explanations.A: Correct: silent alleles at the FUT1 locus prevent H antigen formation, removing the substrate A and B transferases require, which is the defining biochemical mechanism of the Bombay phenotype.
option_explanations.C: The Lewis system involves passive adsorption of soluble Lewis substance onto red cells and depends on Le, Se, and H gene interactions, but there is no mechanism by which loss of Se converts existing H antigen into Lewis antigen; this misapplies the Lewis passive-adsorption concept.
option_explanations.E: The acquired B phenomenon is an acquired, disease-associated alteration of existing A antigen described with intestinal disease; it modifies antigen structure rather than eliminating H substance genetically, and it does not fit a lifelong, consanguinity-associated presentation with panreactive anti-H.

questions.teaching_point: H antigen (from FUT1) is the obligatory biosynthetic precursor for both A and B antigens; loss of functional H gene, not loss of the A/B transferase itself, produces the Bombay phenotype and its characteristic panreactive anti-H.

source_references.locator: "The H Gene Necessity" teaching pearl
source_references.supports: States that FUT1 is a prerequisite for A/B antigen expression and that without H substance (fucose), A/B transferases have no substrate to act on.

source_references.locator: Domain III high-yield facts
source_references.supports: States that the H gene (FUT1) encodes alpha-2-L-fucosyltransferase, which adds L-fucose to the precursor chain to form H antigen.

source_references.locator: Domain 1, "The H System (018) & Secretors"
source_references.supports: Distinguishes FUT1 (erythroid) from FUT2 (secretory) genes, grounding why a secretor-gene defect does not remove red cell H antigen.

source_references.locator: "Bombay Phenotype (Oh)" board trap and ABO discrepancy patterns
source_references.supports: Confirms Bombay presents as a group O discrepancy with potent anti-H, and separately notes the acquired B phenomenon is linked to intestinal disease, grounding distractor E.

review.notes: Core mechanism is well grounded: SRC-024 ('The H Gene Necessity': FUT1 is a prerequisite for A/B expression; without H substance/L-fucose, A/B transferases have no substrate) and SRC-010 (FUT1 encodes alpha-2-L-fucosyltransferase adding L-fucose to form H antigen) directly support the keyed answer C. SRC-043's 'FUT1 (erythroid) vs FUT2 (secretory)' line supports refuting distractor B. However, several stem/rationale details are not verifiable in these four sources and appear to be reasonable but unsourced elaboration: (1) the specific term 'amorphic/silent alleles' tied to FUT1/Bombay (SRC-043

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with colon adenocarcinoma is typed before surgery. Forward grouping shows 4+ agglutination with anti-A reagent and 2+ (weak, mixed-field) agglutination with anti-B reagent. Reverse grouping shows no reactivity of the patient's serum with reagent A1 cells but strong 4+ reactivity with reagent B cells. He has no transfusion or transplant history. Which of the following best accounts for this ABO typing discrepancy?

options.text: The patient has a true AB genotype with a weak B-subgroup allele
Missing/weak antigen expression due to an underlying leukemic process is suppressing B-antigen synthesis
An acquired B phenomenon, associated with his intestinal disease, has modified his group A red cell antigen to produce a false-positive weak reaction with anti-B reagent
Rouleaux formation from an underlying paraproteinemia is mimicking true agglutination in the forward grouping
A cold autoagglutinin (anti-I) is causing nonspecific agglutination

questions.explanation_correct: The reverse grouping is internally consistent with a true group A patient: strong anti-B is present in serum and there is no anti-A1. A genuine group AB or B-subgroup individual would not produce anti-B, so the weak, mixed-field forward reactivity with anti-B reagent is a false-positive 'extra reactivity' discrepancy rather than a true B antigen. This pattern is specifically described as the acquired B phenomenon associated with intestinal disease, which fits this patient's colon adenocarcinoma, and forward-grouping discrepancies are typically attributed to red cell (antigen-side) problems such as disease-related antigen changes, consistent with what is seen here.

option_explanations.A: A true AB or B-subgroup patient with functioning B transferase would not produce anti-B antibody; the strong 4+ reactivity of the patient's serum against reagent B cells contradicts a genuine B antigen and argues against this option.
option_explanations.E: Cold autoagglutinins are a reverse-grouping (serum-side) problem that typically causes nonspecific reactivity affecting multiple reagent cell types at the reverse phase; here the reverse grouping is the expected, consistent portion of the result, while the discrepancy lies in the weak forward anti-B reaction, which this option does not explain.
option_explanations.B: Missing/weak antigen expression is described in association with leukemia, not colon cancer, and it explains under-reactivity, not the unexpected extra (false-positive) forward reactivity with anti-B seen in this patient.
option_explanations.C: Correct: extra reactivity in ABO discrepancies is described as arising from the acquired B phenomenon in the setting of intestinal disease, which matches this patient's colon adenocarcinoma and the classic false-positive weak anti-B forward reaction with a reverse grouping consistent with true group A.
option_explanations.D: Rouleaux is a reverse-grouping (serum protein) problem associated with paraproteinemia (e.g., multiple myeloma) that mimics agglutination in serum-based testing; it does not account for a weak, mixed-field forward-typing reaction with anti-B reagent in a colon cancer patient.

questions.teaching_point: When forward and reverse ABO groupings appear to conflict, check internal consistency of the reverse (antibody) pattern first; a genuine antigen would suppress the corresponding antibody, so unexplained extra forward reactivity in a patient with intestinal disease should raise suspicion for the acquired B phenomenon rather than a true subgroup.

source_references.locator: "ABO Discrepancy Patterns" (Diagnostic Approach/Laboratory Findings)
source_references.supports: Describes 'extra reactivity' discrepancies as caused by the acquired B phenotype in intestinal disease, cold autoantibodies, or rouleaux, and 'missing/weak antigen' discrepancies as seen in leukemia, elderly, or newborns.

source_references.locator: "Forward vs. Reverse" high-yield comparison point
source_references.supports: States forward typing discrepancies are usually RBC (antigen-side) problems such as disease, while reverse typing discrepancies are usually serum-side problems such as cold antibodies, supporting the reasoning used to localize this discrepancy.

review.notes: The central fact is well grounded: SRC-024 explicitly lists 'Extra Reactivity: Caused by acquired B phenotype (intestinal disease), cold autoantibodies, or rouleaux (multiple myeloma)' and 'Forward vs. Reverse: Forward typing discrepancies are usually RBC problems (subgroups, disease); reverse typing discrepancies are usually serum problems,' which supports keying D and explains why B/C/E are wrong-mechanism distractors, and SRC-024's leukemia/missing-antigen line supports ruling out C. However, the detailed serologic-logic rationale for distractor A (that a true AB/B-subgroup patient could no

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man requires emergent RBC transfusion after trauma. Pretransfusion testing shows no reactivity with anti-A or anti-B reagents (types as group O), but his serum contains a potent antibody that agglutinates every reagent and donor red cell tested, including group O cells, at room temperature and after the antihuman globulin phase. Antigen studies confirm the Bombay (Oh) phenotype. Group O, D-negative RBC units are readily available in the blood bank's emergency inventory. What is the most appropriate transfusion approach for this patient?

options.text: Withhold routine group O RBC units and obtain crossmatch-compatible Oh (Bombay) red cells, since this patient's anti-H will react with any red cells expressing H antigen, including group O
Transfuse group AB plasma together with group O RBCs to neutralize the patient's unexpected antibody before infusion
Transfuse the available group O, D-negative RBCs after first performing a saline-replacement technique on the patient's serum to eliminate the interfering antibody
Perform therapeutic plasma exchange to remove the anti-H antibody, then proceed with standard group O RBC transfusion
Transfuse emergency-release group O, D-negative RBCs immediately, since group O red cells are compatible with all ABO phenotypes

questions.explanation_correct: The Bombay (Oh) phenotype is described as presenting as a group O discrepancy, but these patients possess a potent anti-H antibody that reacts with essentially all group O cells (and all other ABO cells) because those cells express H antigen; the only compatible red cells are rare Oh (Bombay) units, which themselves lack H antigen. Giving 'universal donor' group O red cells here would provide antigen-positive cells that the patient's own anti-H would attack, so routine group O release must be withheld in favor of true Oh-compatible units.

option_explanations.E: This is the classic board trap for Bombay phenotype: group O is only a universal donor with respect to A and B antigens, but group O red cells still carry H antigen, which this patient's potent anti-H will target, making this choice unsafe.
option_explanations.A: Correct: the source material specifically states that Bombay phenotype patients require transfusion with rare Oh units because their anti-H reacts with all group O cells; crossmatch-compatible Oh blood is the appropriate product.
option_explanations.C: Saline replacement/cell washing is described as a laboratory technique for resolving in-vitro ABO discrepancies (e.g., clearing interfering serum proteins from a test system), not a method for neutralizing a genuine, clinically significant circulating antibody like anti-H before in-vivo transfusion.
option_explanations.B: No mechanism for using group AB plasma to neutralize a patient's own clinically significant antibody prior to RBC transfusion is described; antibody neutralization of this kind is not a supported transfusion practice in the reviewed material.
option_explanations.D: Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) is described in the source material as an established treatment for conditions such as TTP by removing pathologic factors like ADAMTS13 inhibitors, not as a technique to enable transfusion of H-antigen-positive red cells to a Bombay-phenotype patient; the described management for Bombay patients is simply to use Oh-compatible units.

questions.teaching_point: Because Bombay phenotype red cells lack H antigen, these patients form a potent, clinically significant anti-H that reacts with essentially all non-Bombay red cells, including group O; 'universal donor' group O blood is therefore unsafe, and only rare Oh (Bombay) units are compatible.

source_references.locator: "Bombay Phenotype (Oh)" common board trap
source_references.supports: States Bombay phenotype presents as a group O discrepancy with a potent anti-H reacting with all group O cells, requiring transfusion with rare Oh units.

source_references.locator: "Discrepancy Resolution" diagnostic approach
source_references.supports: Describes cell washing/saline techniques and extended incubation as laboratory methods for resolving typing discrepancies, supporting why this is misapplied as a therapeutic antibody-removal step in distractor C.

source_references.locator: Domain IV: Apheresis high-yield facts
source_references.supports: States TPE is a primary treatment for TTP by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors, establishing TPE's grounded indication and supporting why using it to enable Bombay-incompatible transfusion (distractor E) is not the described management.

review.notes: Strongly and almost verbatim grounded: SRC-024 states 'Bombay Phenotype (Oh): Presents as a group O discrepancy; these patients have a potent anti-H that reacts with all O cells, requiring transfusion with rare Oh units,' which directly supports the stem setup and keyed answer B. Distractor E is grounded by SRC-010's statement that TPE is a primary treatment for TTP via ADAMTS13-inhibitor removal (not an antibody-clearance technique for enabling incompatible transfusion), and distractor C's rebuttal is reasonably grounded by SRC-024's discrepancy-resolution methods (cell washing/extended incub

questions.subdomain: Rh Blood Group System – General Information & Nomenclature
questions.stem: A transfusion service investigates a patient whose red cells show markedly weakened expression of all Rh system antigens (D, C, c, E, and e) on serologic testing, despite DNA sequencing that confirms intact, non-mutated RHD and RHCE coding sequences. Which of the following genes is most likely responsible for this phenotype, given its established role in Rh antigen biology?

options.text: RHCE
RHD
KLF1
RHAG

questions.explanation_correct: RHAG is classified in the source material as a regulator gene for the Rh blood group system — a gene that does not itself encode the antigen protein but instead controls its expression on the red cell membrane. Because the vignette specifies that the structural genes RHD and RHCE are sequence-intact, yet Rh antigen expression is nonetheless depressed, the defect must lie in a non-structural, regulatory component; RHAG is the gene the source pairs specifically with control of Rh antigen expression.

option_explanations.B: RHD is a structural gene that encodes the D antigen protein itself. A defect here would be expected to alter or eliminate the D antigen through changes to its coding sequence, but the vignette specifies RHD sequence is intact, and structural genes are not described in the source as controlling expression independent of their own product.
option_explanations.A: RHCE is the structural gene encoding the C/c and E/e antigen proteins, analogous to RHD for the D antigen. Like RHD, it is described as coding for antigen protein rather than functioning as a regulator of expression, and the vignette specifies its sequence is intact.
option_explanations.D: Correct. The source explicitly identifies RHAG as a regulator gene for Rh antigens — one that does not code for the antigen itself but controls its expression — distinguishing it from the structural RHD/RHCE genes.
option_explanations.C: KLF1 is identified in the source as the regulator gene paired with the Lutheran system, not Rh. While KLF1 mutations are noted to potentially affect multiple blood group systems broadly, the source specifically links RHAG, not KLF1, to control of Rh antigen expression.

questions.teaching_point: Rh antigen expression depends not only on the structural RHD/RHCE genes but also on regulator genes such as RHAG, which do not encode antigen protein but are required for its membrane expression.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 26 - Definitions: Regulator Genes
source_references.supports: Defines RHAG as a regulator gene controlling Rh antigen expression and KLF1 as the regulator gene paired with Lutheran, distinguishing regulator genes (control expression) from structural genes (encode the antigen).

review.notes: The core fact - RHAG is a 'regulator gene' for Rh antigens that does not code for the antigen but controls its expression, contrasted with KLF1 as the Lutheran regulator gene - is directly supported by SRC-003 (Subdomain Node 26, Definitions: 'Regulator Genes: Genes that do not code for the antigen itself but control its expression (e.g., RHAG for Rh antigens; KLF1 for Lutheran)'). That single parenthetical line is the entire source basis for the question. The elaborate clinical vignette built on top of it - 'markedly weakened expression of all Rh system antigens,' DNA sequencing 'confirming i

questions.subdomain: Rh Blood Group System – General Information & Nomenclature
questions.stem: A patient with sickle cell disease is being worked up for a chronic RBC transfusion program that will require extended antigen matching. Routine pretransfusion tube testing with anti-D reagent types the patient's red cells as D-positive. The transfusion medicine physician is concerned that this routine serologic result may not fully characterize the patient's true Rh status before donor unit selection. Which of the following best explains the basis for this concern and the recommended next step?

options.text: The D-positive result most likely reflects a transient serologic artifact from a prior transfusion and requires no further Rh-specific investigation.
Because the patient already types D-positive, RhIG-related considerations and further Rh workup are not applicable to this transfusion scenario.

The D-positive result may reflect an RHD variant allele that reacts with routine anti-D reagents yet still carries a risk that the patient will form an antibody with anti-D specificity; RHD genotyping should be performed to characterize the variant and guide selection of antigen-matched units.
Weak D testing by indirect antiglobulin testing is unnecessary once tube testing shows a clear D-positive reaction, so no further Rh characterization is indicated.
Extended antigen matching concerns in sickle cell disease relate primarily to ABO subgrouping rather than to Rh variant alleles, so the D-positive result requires no additional scrutiny.

questions.explanation_correct: The source material specifically notes that in sickle cell disease, molecular testing is essential for identifying Rh variants that appear as conventional D on routine serology but nonetheless place the patient at risk for forming an antibody with anti-D specificity (allo-anti-D). This is a key reason extended, molecular-level antigen matching is emphasized for chronically transfused SCD patients: RHD genotyping resolves what serology alone cannot.

option_explanations.D: This describes appropriate use of weak D/IAT testing for D-negative or discrepant tube results in general, but it does not address the specific SCD concern described in the source: a variant RHD allele can react as clearly D-positive on routine tube testing and still carry alloimmunization risk, so this option misapplies a true but unrelated testing rule.
option_explanations.C: Correct. The source identifies molecular RHD genotyping as essential in sickle cell disease specifically because some Rh variants type as conventional D-positive by serology yet remain at risk for allo-anti-D formation.
option_explanations.A: The source does not attribute this concern to a transfusion-related serologic artifact; the concern it describes is genetic Rh variation detectable by molecular methods, not a transient technical issue.
option_explanations.E: The source's stated concern for extended matching and Rh variants in sickle cell disease relates specifically to the Rh system, not to ABO subgrouping; this option misdirects the underlying issue to an unrelated blood group system.
option_explanations.B: This conflates prenatal RhIG management (a separate clinical scenario) with the sickle cell disease chronic-transfusion Rh-variant concern described in the source, and incorrectly concludes no further Rh evaluation is needed.

questions.teaching_point: In sickle cell disease, a routine D-positive serologic result does not exclude an underlying RHD variant allele; molecular RHD genotyping is indicated to detect variants that pose a risk for allo-anti-D despite a conventional D-positive phenotype.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 41 - Clinical Applications: Sickle Cell Disease
source_references.supports: States that in sickle cell disease, extended antigen matching requires identifying Rh variants that type as conventional D by serology but carry allo-anti-D risk, supporting the need for molecular clarification.

source_references.locator: Section 1 - Primary Areas of Genetic and Molecular Analysis
source_references.supports: Notes that DNA/molecular analysis, including RHD genotyping, is used for blood group gene mapping in transfusion medicine, supporting genotyping as the follow-up test.

review.notes: Near-verbatim source match: SRC-004 (Subdomain Node 41, Clinical Applications: Sickle Cell Disease) states testing is 'Essential for providing extended antigen matching and identifying Rh variants that appear as conventional D by serology but are at risk for allo-anti-D,' which directly grounds the stem, the correct answer, and its rationale. SRC-028's DNA Analysis section ('used for blood group gene mapping and RHD genotyping') reasonably supports RHD genotyping as the follow-up test named in option B. Distractors A, C, D, E are all correctly identified as unsupported or contradicted by the s

questions.subdomain: Rh Blood Group System – General Information & Nomenclature
questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman who typed Rh(D)-negative on her first-trimester prenatal panel presents at 24 weeks gestation. Her partner's RhD status is unknown. The obstetric team wants to determine whether the fetus is at risk for RhD alloimmunization in order to decide on antenatal RhIg administration, while avoiding an invasive procedure. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Obtain a maternal blood sample and test cell-free fetal DNA (cffDNA) in the maternal plasma to noninvasively determine fetal RhD status, and use the result to guide administration or withholding of RhIg.
Perform amniocentesis to obtain fetal cells directly for RhD antigen typing, since maternal plasma cannot be used to assess fetal Rh status.
Repeat the maternal indirect antiglobulin test (antibody screen) alone, since a negative screen confirms the fetus is RhD-negative and RhIg can be withheld.
Administer RhIg to all Rh(D)-negative pregnant women at the standard antenatal time point without attempting to determine fetal RhD status, since fetal RhD status cannot be assessed before delivery.

questions.explanation_correct: The source material specifically describes testing cell-free fetal DNA (cffDNA) in maternal plasma as the method used to determine fetal RhD status non-invasively during prenatal care, with the result used to guide RhIg administration. This allows an individualized, fetal-status-informed prophylaxis decision without resorting to an invasive procedure.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The source directly states that cffDNA testing in maternal plasma is used to determine fetal RhD status non-invasively and to guide RhIg administration.
option_explanations.B: Amniocentesis is an invasive procedure that itself carries a risk of fetomaternal hemorrhage and further alloimmunization; the source specifically describes a non-invasive plasma-based method (cffDNA), making this an unnecessary and riskier alternative.
option_explanations.D: This describes a blanket 'treat all' approach without fetal-specific information, which is not the approach described in the source; the source specifically notes fetal RhD status can be determined non-invasively via cffDNA to guide (i.e., individualize) RhIg administration rather than defaulting to universal administration.
option_explanations.C: A maternal antibody screen detects whether the mother has already formed red cell alloantibodies; it does not determine the RhD status of the fetus and therefore cannot be used to decide whether RhIg is needed.

questions.teaching_point: Cell-free fetal DNA testing in maternal plasma allows non-invasive determination of fetal RhD status, enabling individualized (rather than blanket) antenatal RhIg administration in RhD-negative pregnant women.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 41 - Clinical Applications: Prenatal Care
source_references.supports: States that cffDNA testing in maternal plasma determines fetal RhD status non-invasively and guides RhIg administration.

review.notes: Directly supported: SRC-004 (Subdomain Node 41, Clinical Applications: Prenatal Care) states 'Testing cell-free fetal DNA (cffDNA) in maternal plasma to determine fetal RhD status non-invasively, guiding RhIg administration,' which matches the stem and keyed answer essentially verbatim. Distractors (amniocentesis, blanket RhIg administration, maternal antibody screen alone) are all correctly and non-controversially identified as inferior/incorrect per the source and basic transfusion medicine logic; no invented facts detected. As with Q1, the topic fits under 'Clinical Applications: Prenatal C

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is being evaluated prior to elective surgery. Routine immediate-spin Rh typing shows weak/discrepant reactivity with anti-D reagents. Reference laboratory molecular testing identifies an RHD/RHCE hybrid gene producing a red cell phenotype in which specific D epitopes are missing while other D epitopes remain intact. Which of the following best explains why this patient should be selected to receive D-negative red blood cells for transfusion?

options.text: The weak reactivity reflects passively acquired anti-D from a prior dose of Rh immune globulin, which will resolve spontaneously without further precautions.
She lacks specific D epitopes because of a hybrid RHCE/RHD gene and can become immunized to form alloanti-D if exposed to conventional D-positive red cells expressing the missing epitopes.
She has a complete deletion of the RHD gene, eliminating D antigen expression entirely, so any exposure to D-positive cells will inevitably cause immunization.
She has a quantitative reduction in D antigen sites caused by mutations in the intracellular or transmembrane regions of RHD, placing her at risk for anti-D formation.
The weak reactivity is a position effect from inheritance of the C antigen in trans to the D gene, a transient finding that does not raise concern for alloimmunization.

questions.explanation_correct: Partial D is a qualitative variant, typically arising from RHD/RHCE hybrid genes, in which one or more D epitopes are absent from the red cell membrane while others remain. Because the patient's immune system has never been exposed to the missing epitopes, she can form alloanti-D if transfused with conventional D-positive red cells carrying those epitopes. For this reason, partial D recipients are managed as D-negative for both transfusion and RhIg candidacy, even though her own cells type D-positive (or weakly so).

option_explanations.D: Incorrect - this describes the mechanism of weak D (a quantitative reduction in D sites from transmembrane/cytoplasmic mutations), not partial D; weak D individuals are unlikely to form anti-D, which is the opposite risk profile described in the stem's molecular finding of a hybrid gene.
option_explanations.B: Correct - partial D reflects qualitative epitope loss from an RHCE/RHD hybrid gene, and such patients can form alloanti-D against the missing epitopes, which is why they are managed as Rh-negative for transfusion.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - complete RHD gene deletion is the mechanism underlying most ordinary D-negative phenotypes, not partial D; the stem specifically describes a hybrid gene with retained (not entirely absent) D epitopes.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - this scenario describes intrinsic molecular variation (hybrid gene) identified on reference testing, not a history of RhIg administration; passively acquired anti-D is a distinct clinical concept unrelated to the discrepant typing described here.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - position effect refers to reduced D expression when C is inherited in trans to D (e.g., Dce/dCe), a different mechanism from the hybrid-gene epitope loss described in the stem, and does not carry the same alloimmunization implications as partial D.

questions.teaching_point: Partial D (qualitative epitope loss, often from RHD/RHCE hybrid genes) carries real alloanti-D risk and is managed as Rh-negative for transfusion and RhIg candidacy; weak D (quantitative reduction in D sites from transmembrane/cytoplasmic mutations) rarely forms anti-D and can typically be managed as Rh-positive.

source_references.locator: Definitions; High-Yield Comparison Points (Weak D vs Partial D)
source_references.supports: Defines partial D as qualitative epitope loss from RHD/RHCE hybrid genes with risk of alloanti-D formation, versus weak D as quantitative reduction from transmembrane/cytoplasmic mutations with low anti-D risk.

source_references.locator: Management (Unit Selection); Rh-Negative Mechanism
source_references.supports: States that D-negative recipients (including partial D patients) should receive D-negative red cells, and that most D-negative phenotypes arise from complete RHD gene deletion.

Distractor A is well-refuted because SRC-020/059 state weak D patients are 'unlikely to make anti-D' (opposite of A's claim), distractor C is refuted because complete RHD deletion is the

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy develops sudden dark-colored urine and pallor several days after a resolving viral upper respiratory illness. Laboratory evaluation shows evidence of brisk intravascular hemolysis. The direct antiglobulin test is positive with anti-C3 reagent but negative with anti-IgG reagent. A Donath-Landsteiner test is positive, and the causative antibody is shown to react with essentially all reagent red cells tested except the rare p and Pk phenotypes. His hemoglobin continues to fall and transfusion is being considered. Which of the following is the most appropriate approach to red cell selection for this patient?

options.text: Provide only IgM-depleted red cell units, since the causative antibody is an IgM cold agglutinin.
Select red cells that lack the I antigen, since the DAT and Donath-Landsteiner findings indicate a high-titer cold agglutinin with anti-I specificity.
Treat donor red cells with proteolytic enzymes before crossmatch, since enzyme treatment is necessary to identify red cells compatible with this autoantibody.

Transfuse crossmatch-compatible standard donor red cells while keeping the patient and the infused blood warmed, since standard red cells are typically tolerated once the antibody can no longer bind in vivo.
Withhold all transfusion until p-negative red cells can be sourced, since standard red cells will be immediately destroyed by the circulating antibody regardless of temperature.

questions.explanation_correct: The clinical picture (post-viral, biphasic hemolysin confirmed by a positive Donath-Landsteiner test, DAT positive for complement only, and reactivity with all cells except rare p/Pk phenotypes) is classic for paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria (PCH), caused by an IgG biphasic hemolysin with anti-P specificity that binds RBCs in the cold and lyses them at body temperature. Although p-negative blood is theoretically ideal, it is extremely rare, so transfusion should not be withheld in urgent cases; standard crossmatch-compatible blood is generally tolerated once the patient (and infused blood) is warmed, preventing further cold-dependent antibody binding.

option_explanations.D: Correct - the source explicitly states that although p-negative blood is ideal for PCH, it is extremely rare (~1:200,000), transfusion should not be withheld in urgent cases, and standard blood may be tolerated once the patient is warmed.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - this contradicts the source's guidance that transfusion should not be withheld while awaiting rare p-negative units in urgent PCH cases.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect - anti-I is the specificity associated with cold agglutinin syndrome (reacting with adult but not cord cells), not PCH; the Donath-Landsteiner-positive, pan-reactive-except-p/Pk pattern described here corresponds to anti-P, the specificity associated with PCH.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - this reflects the classic board trap of assuming the PCH antibody is IgM because it is cold-reactive; the biphasic hemolysin of PCH is actually IgG, and the DAT being C3-positive/IgG-negative reflects the IgM cold autoantibody pattern of dissociation on washing, not the PCH antibody itself, which is IgG.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - enzyme treatment enhances in vitro detection of anti-P reactivity for antibody identification purposes; the source does not describe enzyme-treated donor units as part of transfusion/blood-selection management, and this autoantibody (not alloantibody) is managed with standard compatible blood and warming rather than enzyme pretreatment of donor cells.

questions.teaching_point: In PCH, serologic findings (DAT positive for C3 only, positive Donath-Landsteiner test, anti-P specificity reacting with all cells except rare p/Pk) confirm the diagnosis and guide transfusion decisions: p-negative blood is theoretically ideal but too rare to require, so urgent transfusion should proceed with standard compatible blood plus warming rather than being withheld.

source_references.locator: Biphasic Mechanism; Laboratory Findings (Anti-P); Common Board Traps (PCH Isotype)
source_references.supports: Describes PCH as caused by an IgG biphasic hemolysin (Donath-Landsteiner antibody) with anti-P specificity reacting with all cells except rare p/Pk phenotypes, and notes the classic board trap that the antibody is actually IgG despite being cold-reactive.

source_references.locator: Management (Transfusion in PCH); Diagnostic Approach (DAT Results)
source_references.supports: States that p-negative blood is ideal but extremely rare (~1:200,000) and that transfusion should not be withheld in urgent PCH cases since standard blood may be tolerated once the patient is warmed; also notes DAT is typically complement-only positive because IgM dissociates on washing.

review.notes: Correct answer A is essentially verbatim from SRC-056: 'Although p-negative blood is ideal, it is extremely rare (~1:200,000). Transfusion should not be withheld in urgent cases, as standard blood may be tolerated once the patient is warmed.' Stem details (post-viral acute intravascular hemolysis/hemoglobinuria in a child, positive Donath-Landsteiner test, pan-reactivity except rare p/Pk = anti-P) match SRC-056's PCH/anti-P definitions closely. Distractor B contradicts the same 'do not withhold' guidance; C is refuted because anti-I (not anti-P) is the CAS specificity and does not produce the

questions.stem: During a departmental teaching session, a hematopathology fellow explains the biochemical basis of the red blood cell (RBC) storage lesion. She notes that ATP levels in a unit of stored RBCs decline progressively over the storage period and that the cells have no way to compensate for this decline once glycolytic flux slows. Which of the following best explains why mature RBCs cannot compensate for declining glycolytic ATP production by switching to oxidative phosphorylation?

options.text: Mature erythrocytes shift ATP production to the citric acid (Krebs) cycle within retained mitochondria
Mature erythrocytes preferentially oxidize free fatty acids via mitochondrial beta-oxidation once glycolysis slows
Mature erythrocytes generate the majority of their ATP through the hexose monophosphate (pentose phosphate) shunt
Mature erythrocytes lack a nucleus and mitochondria, so anaerobic glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway) is essentially their sole source of ATP production
Mature erythrocytes upregulate nuclear gene transcription to increase electron transport chain protein synthesis

questions.explanation_correct: Mature RBCs are anucleate and lack mitochondria, so oxidative phosphorylation, the Krebs cycle, and beta-oxidation are structurally unavailable to them. Anaerobic glycolysis via the Embden-Meyerhof pathway is essentially their sole source of ATP (approximately 90%), which is why glycolytic decline during storage cannot be offset by any mitochondrial pathway.

option_explanations.B: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids occurs in mitochondria; mature RBCs have no mitochondria, so this pathway is not available as a compensatory ATP source.
option_explanations.C: The source identifies anaerobic glycolysis, not the hexose monophosphate shunt, as the pathway providing about 90% of RBC ATP; the sources do not describe the HMP shunt as an ATP-generating pathway in RBCs.
option_explanations.D: Correct: mature RBCs lack a nucleus and mitochondria, so anaerobic (Embden-Meyerhof) glycolysis is essentially their only ATP source, per the source material; this is the core mechanistic constraint on RBC energy metabolism and on storage-related ATP decline.
option_explanations.A: Mature RBCs do not retain mitochondria after enucleation, so the mitochondrial citric acid (Krebs) cycle is not available to them.
option_explanations.E: Mature RBCs are anucleate, so no nuclear gene transcription (including for electron transport chain proteins) can occur.

questions.teaching_point: Mature RBCs are anucleate and lack mitochondria, making anaerobic glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway) essentially their only ATP source; this structural feature is the reason RBC ATP inevitably declines during storage with no mitochondrial pathway available to compensate.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts, 'RBC Metabolism'
source_references.supports: States mature RBCs lack a nucleus and mitochondria and rely on anaerobic (Embden-Meyerhof) glycolysis for about 90% of their ATP

source_references.locator: Domain III, Subdomains/Major Concepts
source_references.supports: Lists 'Metabolism' as a major RBC subdomain, providing curriculum-level context for the topic

review.notes: SRC-024 line 7 states verbatim: 'Mature RBCs lack a nucleus and mitochondria, relying on anaerobic glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway) for 90% of their ATP' — this directly and fully grounds the stem and the keyed answer C. Distractors B (HMP shunt as major ATP source), D (Krebs cycle in retained mitochondria), and E (nuclear transcription) are all correctly falsified by the same source sentence (anucleate, amitochondrial, glycolysis-dependent). Distractor A (beta-oxidation) is not explicitly named in the source but is a standard, non-contradicted extension of 'lack mitochondria' and is a rea

questions.stem: A transfusion service investigates a report of poor tissue oxygen delivery following transfusion of RBC units nearing the end of their approved storage period (day 35 in CPDA-1). Biochemical testing of the units shows substantially depleted 2,3-diphosphoglycerate (2,3-DPG) compared to values at collection. Based on this finding, which of the following best describes the expected effect on the transfused cells' oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve?

options.text: Rightward shift with an increased P50, reflecting increased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity
Leftward shift with a decreased P50, reflecting increased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity and reduced oxygen release to tissue
Rightward shift with an increased P50, reflecting decreased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity and increased oxygen release to tissue
No shift in the curve, because 2,3-DPG depletion does not alter hemoglobin-oxygen affinity
Leftward shift with an increased P50, reflecting decreased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity

questions.explanation_correct: The source defines P50 as the partial pressure of oxygen at which hemoglobin is 50% saturated, a direct measure of oxygen affinity, and states that depletion of 2,3-DPG during red cell storage produces a leftward shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve. By definition, a leftward shift corresponds to a decreased P50 and increased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity, so less oxygen is released to peripheral tissue at a given oxygen tension.

option_explanations.A: This pairs a rightward shift/increased P50 with increased affinity, which is physiologically inconsistent (a higher P50 denotes lower, not higher, affinity), and it also names the wrong direction of shift for 2,3-DPG depletion.
option_explanations.B: Correct: 2,3-DPG depletion during storage causes a leftward shift of the dissociation curve; per the source's P50 definition, a leftward shift means a decreased P50 and increased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity, reducing oxygen unloading to tissue.
option_explanations.C: This option correctly pairs an increased P50 with decreased affinity and increased oxygen release, but a rightward shift is the opposite of what the source describes as occurring with 2,3-DPG depletion (a leftward shift).
option_explanations.D: The source explicitly states that 2,3-DPG depletion during storage causes a leftward shift with increased oxygen affinity, contradicting the claim of no effect.
option_explanations.E: This option correctly identifies a leftward shift but incorrectly pairs it with an increased P50 and decreased affinity; a leftward shift corresponds by definition to a decreased P50 and increased affinity.

questions.teaching_point: 2,3-DPG is depleted as part of the RBC storage lesion; because it lowers hemoglobin's oxygen affinity under normal conditions, its depletion shifts the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve leftward (decreased P50), impairing oxygen release to tissue from older stored units.

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls ('Shift to the Left'); Definitions ('P50')
source_references.supports: States that a leftward shift with increased oxygen affinity (less oxygen released to tissue) occurs when 2,3-DPG is depleted during storage, and defines P50 as the pO2 at 50% hemoglobin saturation (a measure of oxygen affinity)

source_references.locator: Domain III, Major Concepts
source_references.supports: Lists the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve as a major concept within the RBC domain, providing curriculum-level context

review.notes: SRC-024 directly supports the content: 'Shift to the Left: Occurs when 2,3-DPG is depleted (storage lesion); oxygen affinity increases, meaning less oxygen is released to tissues' plus the P50 definition ('partial pressure of oxygen at which hemoglobin is 50% saturated; a measure of oxygen affinity'). This unambiguously supports B (leftward shift, decreased P50, increased affinity, reduced O2 release) and correctly falsifies A/C (wrong direction), D (contradicts source), and E (internally inconsistent affinity/P50 pairing). The CPDA-1 35-day storage detail is also correctly sourced (line 79: '

questions.stem: A hematopathology fellow reviews a stable patient with chronic anemia and is asked to identify which physiologic system is responsible for adjusting the release of oxygen already bound to circulating hemoglobin to peripheral tissue beds — as distinct from the systems that regulate red cell production, ex vivo red cell preservation, and red cell antigen (immunologic) identity. Which of the following is that mechanism?

options.text: Red cell storage lesion accumulated during blood bank storage
Position/affinity of the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve
Biochemistry of carbohydrate (e.g., ABH) and protein (e.g., Rh) blood group antigens
Leukoreduction of cellular blood components
Erythropoietin-driven erythropoiesis

questions.explanation_correct: The source curriculum map pairs each RBC subdomain with its governing major concept: Red Cell Production with erythropoietin regulation, Oxygen Delivery with the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve, Preservation with red cell storage lesions, and Immunology/Blood Group Systems with blood group antigen biochemistry. The dissociation curve is therefore the concept explicitly mapped to oxygen delivery/release to tissue, distinguishing it from marrow production, ex vivo storage change, and immunologic identity.

option_explanations.B: Correct — mapped in the source to the 'Oxygen Delivery' subdomain as the concept governing how hemoglobin releases bound oxygen.
option_explanations.E: Erythropoietin regulation is mapped to the 'Red Cell Production' subdomain (marrow output of red cells), not to regional oxygen release from already-circulating hemoglobin.
option_explanations.A: Red cell storage lesion is mapped to the 'Preservation' subdomain — changes that accumulate in red cells during blood bank storage — not an in vivo mechanism of oxygen delivery regulation.
option_explanations.C: Blood group antigen biochemistry is mapped to the 'Immunology/Blood Group Systems' subdomain, governing antigen expression and antibody formation, not oxygen delivery.
option_explanations.D: Leukoreduction is a component modification step (reducing WBC content to prevent FNHTR, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission) and is unrelated to the physiologic regulation of oxygen delivery.

questions.teaching_point: Within the RBC domain, the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve (not erythropoietin regulation, storage lesion, or blood group biochemistry) is the concept the source curriculum specifically assigns to oxygen delivery/release.

source_references.locator: Domain III: RBCs and RBC Components
source_references.supports: Lists Domain III RBC subdomains (Production, Oxygen Delivery, Metabolism, Preservation, Immunology/Blood Group Systems) paired with major concepts (erythropoietin regulation; hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curves; red cell storage lesions; blood group biochemistry).

source_references.supports: Confirms leukoreduction (<5x10^6 WBCs/unit) prevents FNHTR/HLA sensitization/CMV transmission, supporting it as a plausible but unrelated distractor.

review.notes: SRC-010 (III. Domain: RBCs and RBC Components) lists 'Subdomains: Red Cell Production, Oxygen Delivery, Metabolism, Preservation, Immunology, and Blood Group Systems' followed by 'Major Concepts: Erythropoietin regulation; hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curves; red cell storage lesions; biochemistry of carbohydrate and protein blood groups' as two adjacent but separately-worded lists — the source never states an explicit one-to-one correspondence table pairing each subdomain to a specific concept. The question's explanation asserts 'the source curriculum map pairs each RBC subdomain with its g

questions.stem: A 68-year-old hospitalized man with known coronary artery disease develops symptomatic dyspnea and fatigue. He is hemodynamically stable with no evidence of active bleeding. A CBC shows hemoglobin 7.5 g/dL, platelet count 180,000/uL, and normal PT/aPTT. Applying current threshold-based hemotherapy strategy to this laboratory result in the context of his comorbidity, which interpretation is most appropriate?

options.text: His hemoglobin is below the liberal transfusion threshold (Hb <8 g/dL) used for patients with symptomatic anemia or pre-existing cardiovascular disease, so RBC transfusion is appropriate
His hemoglobin is above the restrictive threshold (Hb <7 g/dL) used for stable ICU/orthopedic patients, so RBC transfusion should be withheld
Cryoprecipitate should be given first because his fibrinogen is presumed to be below 100 mg/dL
RBC transfusion should be withheld because PT/aPTT are not prolonged beyond 1.5x the mid-normal range
RBC transfusion should be withheld because his platelet count is not below 10,000/uL

questions.explanation_correct: The source hemotherapy trigger framework specifies a restrictive Hb <7 g/dL threshold for stable hospitalized patients without cardiovascular disease, but a liberal Hb <8 g/dL threshold for patients with symptomatic anemia or pre-existing cardiovascular disease. This patient's known coronary artery disease and symptomatic dyspnea place him in the liberal-strategy category, and his Hb of 7.5 g/dL is below that 8 g/dL threshold, so transfusion is indicated to support oxygen delivery to a heart with reduced reserve to compensate for anemia.

option_explanations.A: Correct — his Hb (7.5 g/dL) falls below the liberal threshold (<8 g/dL) that applies to patients with symptomatic anemia or pre-existing cardiovascular disease.
option_explanations.B: This is the board trap: it misapplies the restrictive Hb <7 g/dL threshold, which is reserved for stable patients without cardiovascular disease, to a patient whose coronary artery disease places him in the liberal-strategy group.
option_explanations.D: PT or aPTT >1.5x the mid-normal range with active bleeding is the trigger for plasma transfusion, not RBC transfusion; his RBC trigger is Hb-based, not coagulation-based.
option_explanations.E: A platelet count below 10,000/uL is the prophylactic platelet transfusion trigger for stable non-bleeding patients, not an RBC transfusion criterion; his platelet count is also normal.
option_explanations.C: Fibrinogen <100 mg/dL is the trigger for cryoprecipitate, not RBC transfusion, and no fibrinogen value is provided or presumed for this patient.

questions.teaching_point: RBC transfusion thresholds are stratified by cardiovascular reserve — Hb <7 g/dL (restrictive) for stable patients without cardiac disease versus Hb <8 g/dL (liberal) for those with symptomatic anemia or pre-existing cardiovascular disease — reflecting differing capacity to tolerate reduced regional oxygen delivery.

source_references.locator: Domain II: Clinical Practice & Patient Management - Hemotherapy Triggers & Strategy
source_references.supports: States RBC restrictive strategy Hb <7 g/dL for stable ICU/orthopedic patients vs. liberal strategy Hb <8 g/dL for symptomatic anemia or pre-existing cardiovascular disease, plus plasma, platelet, and cryoprecipitate trigger criteria used as distractors.

review.notes: Fully and directly grounded in SRC-044's Domain II 'Hemotherapy Triggers & Strategy' table: 'Restrictive Strategy: Hb <7 g/dL for stable hospitalized patients (ICU/Orthopedic). questions.subdomain: Reagent Red Cells for Antibody Detection
questions.stem: During antibody identification workup, a technologist is trying to exclude anti-Jkb as a possible specificity in a patient whose plasma reacts by the antiglobulin (indirect Coombs) technique with several reagent panel cells. One nonreactive panel cell types as Jk(a+b+) — i.e., heterozygous for the Jkb antigen — rather than Jk(a–b+), which would be homozygous for Jkb. According to the standard exclusion logic (the "Rule of Three"/"3+3" rule) applied to reagent red cell panels, why can this nonreactive heterozygous cell NOT be used to exclude anti-Jkb?

options.text: The Rule of Three specifically requires three heterozygous, antigen-positive reactive cells to exclude a specificity.
Ficin/papain enzyme treatment of the cell would convert its heterozygous antigen expression into homozygous expression, resolving the discrepancy.
A Jk(a+b+) heterozygous cell does not carry the Jkb antigen at all, so it cannot be used to test for anti-Jkb.
Only the potency of the anti-Jkb typing antiserum used to characterize the panel cell matters; the zygosity of the reagent red cell itself is irrelevant to exclusion logic.

Antigens carried in single dose (heterozygous expression) may be present at lower site density and react weakly or not at all with some clinically significant antibodies, so a negative reaction with a heterozygous cell does not reliably rule the antibody out; only a nonreactive homozygous (double-dose) cell satisfies valid exclusion logic.

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that antibody exclusion logic in panel interpretation requires homozygous-expressing reagent red cells (the "Rule of Three"/"3+3" rule). This exists because single-dose (heterozygous) antigen expression can yield weaker or negative reactions with certain antibodies, so a negative result on a heterozygous cell is not a valid basis to exclude a specificity — only a true negative on a homozygous-expressing cell provides confident exclusion.

option_explanations.E: Correct: this is the underlying rationale the sources give for requiring homozygous, not heterozygous, cells in exclusion logic.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — a Jk(a+b+) cell does express Jkb, just in single dose; it is not antigen-negative.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — the source-based exclusion logic calls for homozygous expressing cells, not heterozygous cells, to be used for valid exclusion.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — enzyme treatment changes antigen reactivity/exposure on the cell surface; it does not alter the cell's underlying genotype or zygosity.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — while reagent antisera/red cell reactivity and specificity are checked with day-of-use QC, the exclusion logic itself is specifically tied to whether the panel cell's antigen expression is homozygous or heterozygous, not to antisera potency.

questions.teaching_point: Reagent red cells used to exclude an antibody specificity must be homozygous for the corresponding antigen; a negative reaction on a heterozygous (single-dose) cell is not valid grounds for exclusion.

source_references.locator: Domain 1 section, 'Identification of Antibodies' and 'Basic Identification' bullets
source_references.supports: States that identification/exclusion of antibodies uses homozygous cells under the 'Rule of Three'/'3+3 rule' logic.

review.notes: SRC-043 explicitly supports the core fact tested: 'Identification of Antibodies: Exclusion logic using homozygous cells ("Rule of Three")' and 'Basic Identification: Application of the "3+3" rule, exclusion of antibodies using homozygous cells.' This confirms the WHAT (homozygous cells required for exclusion) and correctly rules out B/C/D/E, which are all independently wrong on basic serology grounds (B misreads Jk(a+b+) phenotype notation; C inverts the rule; D confuses enzyme treatment with genotype change; E ignores the zygosity issue entirely). However, the WHY given in the correct answer

questions.subdomain: Reagent Red Cells for Antibody Detection
questions.stem: An antibody screen is positive by the indirect antiglobulin test (IAT). Using untreated reagent panel cells, the patient's plasma reacts with several Fy(a+) cells and is nonreactive with Fy(a–) cells, a pattern consistent with anti-Fya. To help confirm specificity, the technologist repeats testing after treating the same panel cells with ficin. After ficin treatment, all previously reactive Fy(a+) cells become nonreactive, while reactions with D+ cells (only weakly positive on the untreated panel) become markedly stronger. What is the best interpretation of this enzyme panel result?

options.text: The antibody is actually anti-Jka, because ficin treatment enhances Kidd antigen reactivity.
Enzyme-treated panels have no role in antibody identification and are used only for ABO discrepancy workups.
The original anti-Fya reaction must have been a false positive, since Duffy antibodies cannot be detected by antiglobulin (IAT) testing.
Ficin nonspecifically destroys all reagent red cell antigens, so the enzyme panel result cannot be interpreted.

The pattern is consistent with an underlying anti-Fya together with an Rh antibody: ficin/papain treatment destroys Duffy antigens (explaining loss of the Fy(a+) reactions) while it enhances Rh antigens (explaining the stronger D+ reactions).

questions.explanation_correct: Per the approved sources, ficin/papain enzyme treatment destroys MNS and Duffy antigens on reagent red cells while enhancing Rh, Kidd, and P1 antigens. Loss of reactivity with Fy(a+) cells after ficin treatment is therefore expected for anti-Fya, while the concurrent strengthening of D+ reactions points to a coexisting Rh antibody being enhanced by the same enzyme treatment — a classic enzyme panel pattern used to help resolve antibody specificity.

option_explanations.E: Correct: matches the source-stated fact that ficin destroys Duffy antigens and enhances Rh antigens, accounting for both observed changes.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — the untreated panel's dosage-appropriate Fy(a+)/Fy(a–) pattern by IAT is a valid, expected way to detect anti-Fya; nothing in the sources suggests Duffy antibodies evade IAT detection.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — ficin's effect is antigen-system specific (destroys MNS/Duffy, enhances Rh/Kidd/P1), not a uniform, nonspecific destruction of every antigen.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect/trap — enhancement of Kidd antigens would produce stronger, not absent, reactions; loss of reactivity specifically implicates an antigen system that ficin destroys (Duffy), not one it enhances (Kidd).
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — enzyme (one-stage/two-stage) procedures are listed as a standard technique within antibody identification and compatibility testing, not limited to ABO discrepancy workups.

questions.teaching_point: Enzyme (ficin/papain) treatment of reagent red cells destroys MNS and Duffy antigens but enhances Rh, Kidd, and P1 antigens; loss vs. gain of reactivity after enzyme treatment helps localize antibody specificity to the correct blood group system.

source_references.locator: Domain 1 section, 'Enzyme Treatment' bullet
source_references.supports: States that ficin/papain destroys MNS and Duffy antigens while enhancing Rh, Kidd, and P1 antigens on reagent red cells.

source_references.locator: Section 7, 'Antibody Identification & Compatibility' roadmap
source_references.supports: Lists one-stage/two-stage enzyme procedures as part of the antibody identification and compatibility testing serological subdomain, confirming enzyme panels are a recognized identification tool.

review.notes: Directly and fully grounded in SRC-043: 'Enzyme Treatment: Ficin/Papain destroys MNS and Duffy antigens; enhances Rh, Kidd, and P1 antigens.' The stem's pattern (loss of Fy(a+) reactivity + enhancement of D+ reactivity after ficin) maps exactly onto this stated fact, correctly supporting option A and correctly refuting D (which inverts destroy/enhance for Kidd) and E (SRC-052's roadmap explicitly lists 'One-stage/Two-stage Enzyme procedures' under 'Antibody Identification & Compatibility,' not ABO discrepancy workups, refuting E). B and C are reasonable, unsupported-by-source distractors that

questions.subdomain: Reagent Red Cells for Antibody Detection
questions.stem: A multiply transfused, multiparous patient's antibody screen and identification panel show reactivity that does not resolve to a single common specificity on standard commercial reagent red cell panels; the suspected antibody targets a low-prevalence antigen (e.g., Jsa) that is not represented on routinely available antisera or panel cells, so antigen-negative donor units cannot be reliably identified by standard serologic phenotyping. What is the most appropriate next step to resolve donor antigen typing and identify compatible red cells for this patient?

options.text: Proceed to autologous adsorption of the patient's plasma to remove the interfering antibody before further testing.

Perform molecular genotyping on candidate donor red cells to determine status for the low-prevalence antigen, since genotyping is used to type donors for reagent/panel red cells and to locate compatible units when corresponding antisera are unavailable or the antigen is weakly/partially expressed.
Treat all reagent panel cells with ficin/papain and repeat testing, since enzyme treatment resolves antigen-typing gaps caused by low-prevalence antigens.
Perform urine neutralization studies to confirm the antibody specificity.
Repeat testing using only the immediate-spin phase, which is sufficient to detect all clinically significant alloantibodies.

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that molecular genotyping is used specifically to type donors for reagent red cells used in antibody identification panels (citing low-prevalence antigens such as Jsa, V, and VS) and to find compatible donors when an antibody is weak or corresponding antisera/reagents are unavailable, and also to resolve antigen-typing discrepancies from weak or partial antigen expression. This makes donor genotyping the most appropriate next step in this scenario.

option_explanations.B: Correct: directly matches the source-stated indication for genotyping donor red cells for low-prevalence-antigen panels and compatible-unit identification.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — immediate-spin testing is described as an ABO-only pretransfusion technique; alloantibody detection/exclusion (including for low-prevalence antigens) requires the AHG (antiglobulin) phase, not immediate-spin alone.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect/trap — autologous adsorption is a technique for removing autoantibody and is only valid if the patient has not been transfused in the preceding 3 months; this patient is multiply transfused, so donor (allogeneic) cells could adsorb out the very alloantibody being investigated, producing a false-negative result rather than resolving low-prevalence antigen typing.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — enzyme treatment selectively destroys or enhances specific antigen systems (MNS/Duffy destroyed; Rh/Kidd/P1 enhanced); it does not create or reveal a low-prevalence antigen absent from available panel cells or antisera.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect/trap — neutralization with urine is specifically used to confirm anti-Sda, not to resolve reactivity due to unrelated low-prevalence antigens such as Jsa.

questions.teaching_point: Molecular genotyping of donor red cells is the source-supported approach for typing and locating compatible units when a suspected antibody targets a low-prevalence antigen not covered by routine reagent panels or antisera.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62, 'Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping' — Laboratory and Diagnostic Logic
source_references.supports: States that genotyping is used to type donors for reagent red cells for antibody identification panels (e.g., Jsa, V, VS), to find compatible donors when antibody/antigen expression is weak or reagents are unavailable, and to resolve typing discrepancies from weak/partial antigens.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 14, 'Common Board Traps and Distractors' — Transfusion Timing
source_references.supports: States autologous adsorption requires the patient not be transfused within the prior 3 months, since donor cells can adsorb out recipient alloantibody and falsely negate the screen.

source_references.locator: Domain 1 section, 'Neutralization' bullet; Domain 2 section, 'Pretransfusion Testing' bullet
source_references.supports: States urine is used as a neutralizing substance specifically for anti-Sda, and that immediate-spin testing is used for ABO only.

review.notes: Core claim is well grounded: SRC-006 states 'Genotyping Indications: Used to type donors for reagent red cells for antibody identification panels (e.g., Jsa, V, VS) and to resolve antigen-typing discrepancies caused by weak or partial antigens' — this directly names Jsa and matches the question's scenario, correctly supporting option A. Distractors are also well supported: SRC-043 states immediate-spin is 'ABO only' (refutes B) and urine neutralization is specifically for anti-Sda (refutes E); SRC-061 confirms autoadsorption is invalid if the patient was transfused within the prior 3 months an

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman's pretransfusion sample shows red blood cells that fail to react with anti-A, anti-B, or anti-H reagents on forward typing, while her serum strongly agglutinates reagent A1, B, and group O screening cells. She is subsequently confirmed to have the Bombay phenotype. Genetic studies show she inherited a normal A-transferase allele at the ABO locus from her father. Which of the following BEST explains why her red cells still fail to express A antigen on their surface?

options.text: Anti-H autoantibody is coating her own red cells and destroying newly synthesized A antigen before it can be detected by forward typing, analogous to a "blocked" antigen pattern
She has inherited two nonfunctional (amorphic) alleles at the ABO locus itself, so despite the family history, no functional A-transferase is actually being expressed

She has inherited two nonfunctional H-gene (FUT1) alleles, so no H substance (fucosylated precursor) is produced on RBC membrane type 2 precursor chains, leaving the inherited A-transferase with no substrate to convert into A antigen
Her RBC membrane precursor chains are exclusively type 1 chains, which require the secretor (FUT2) gene product rather than H-transferase for conversion to A antigen
A KLF1 regulator-gene mutation is silencing transcription of her inherited A-transferase allele, similar to its documented effect on Lutheran antigen expression

questions.explanation_correct: The H gene (FUT1) adds L-fucose to the type 2 precursor chain that scaffolds RBC membrane antigens, generating H substance. H substance is the obligatory substrate for the A- and B-glycosyltransferases. In the Bombay (hh) genotype, no functional H-transferase is produced, so no H substance exists on the membrane; even an inherited, fully functional A-transferase allele has nothing to convert, and the cell types as A-, B-, and H-negative regardless of the underlying ABO genotype.

option_explanations.C: Correct: absent H-transferase activity removes the obligatory substrate for A-transferase, explaining A-antigen absence despite a normal inherited A allele.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect and inconsistent with the stem: the patient is stated to have inherited a normal A-transferase allele; an ABO-locus defect would not explain a combined A-, B-, AND H-negative phenotype, since ordinary group O cells (also lacking functional ABO transferase) still express H antigen.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources identify type 2 chains, not type 1 chains, as the scaffold for RBC membrane ABH antigens; type 1 chains and the secretor (FUT2) gene govern soluble/secreted ABH and Lewis substances, not the RBC membrane conversion tested here.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this misapplies the "blocked antigen" concept described for antibody-coated fetal cells in severe HDFN to an adult ABO/H typing scenario; there is no described mechanism by which the patient's own circulating anti-H would selectively destroy newly synthesized self A-antigen.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: KLF1 is described as a regulator gene affecting expression of systems such as Lutheran, not the ABO/H pathway; this option borrows a real regulator-gene concept from the sources but misapplies it to this scenario.

questions.teaching_point: H substance is the shared biochemical precursor for A and B antigens; loss of H-transferase (hh genotype) abolishes A/B expression regardless of the inherited ABO alleles, which is the mechanistic basis of the Bombay phenotype.

source_references.locator: "The 'H Gene' Necessity" teaching pearl, Domain III RBC note
source_references.supports: States the H gene (FUT1) is a prerequisite for A and B antigen expression because without H substance (L-fucose), the A and B transferases have no substrate to act upon.

source_references.locator: "Bombay vs. Para-Bombay" comparison, Subdomain Node 26
source_references.supports: Describes Bombay individuals as lacking H on RBCs and contrasts them with Para-Bombay, who retain a functional secretor (FUT2) gene, establishing the hh genotype concept underlying true Bombay.

source_references.locator: "Type 1 vs. Type 2 Precursors" bullet, Subdomain Node 26
source_references.supports: Defines type 1 chains (secretions/plasma, ABH and Lewis) versus type 2 chains (RBC membrane scaffold), the basis for the type-1/type-2 distractor.

source_references.locator: Definitions / Regulator Genes, Subdomain Node 26
source_references.supports: Defines regulator genes (e.g., KLF1 for Lutheran) that control antigen expression without coding for the antigen itself, the basis for the KLF1 distractor.

source_references.locator: "Blocked Rh Distractor" bullet
source_references.supports: Describes the 'blocked Rh' phenomenon in which heavy antibody coating of fetal cells produces a false-negative D type, the basis for the blocked-antigen distractor.

review.notes: Core mechanism is directly and strongly grounded: SRC-024's 'The H Gene Necessity' pearl states FUT1/H substance is the obligatory substrate for A/B transferases, and SRC-003's 'Bombay vs. Para-Bombay' entry establishes the hh-genotype basis for Bombay independent of ABO genotype. Distractor B is correctly eliminated by the stem's own given fact (inherited functional A-transferase). Distractor C is correctly refuted by SRC-003's Type 1 vs Type 2 precursor distinction (type 2, not type 1, scaffolds RBC membrane ABH antigens). Distractor E's KLF1/Lutheran basis and Distractor D's 'blocked' antig

questions.stem: During pretransfusion testing, a patient's red cells fail to react with anti-A and anti-B reagents (consistent with group O), and forward typing with anti-H reagent is also non-reactive. Reverse (serum) typing shows unexpected agglutination not only with reagent A1 and B cells, but also with the reagent group O cells used in the antibody screen. Which of the following is the MOST likely explanation for this overall pattern of reactivity?

options.text: Rouleaux formation secondary to an underlying plasma cell dyscrasia such as multiple myeloma
Acquired B phenomenon secondary to underlying intestinal disease, producing extra reactivity against the reagent cells
Cold autoagglutinin (anti-I) causing spontaneous agglutination that mimics a broader ABO reaction pattern
Bombay phenotype (Oh), in which a potent anti-H antibody reacts with the H antigen present on essentially all reagent red cells, including the group O screening cells

questions.explanation_correct: The key discriminating finding is that the patient's OWN forward typing is anti-H negative, not just anti-A/anti-B negative. Acquired B phenomenon, cold autoagglutinins, and rouleaux all produce extra or mimicking reactivity in ABO/antibody testing but do not make a patient's forward anti-H reaction negative. An anti-H-negative forward type combined with serum agglutination of ALL reagent cells (including group O screening cells, which normally carry abundant H antigen) is the classic pattern of the Bombay phenotype, whose potent anti-H reacts with the H antigen that essentially all non-Bombay red cells display.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect: acquired B phenomenon is a recognized cause of extra reactivity in ABO discrepancies, but it does not explain a negative anti-H forward type and classically involves apparent extra B reactivity, not agglutination of reagent group O cells by the patient's serum.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: cold autoagglutinins (anti-I) are a recognized cause of spontaneous agglutination that can mimic an ABO discrepancy, but this mechanism does not account for the patient's own anti-H-negative forward typing result.
option_explanations.D: Correct: Bombay phenotype produces a group O-appearing forward type with anti-H-negative typing, and a potent anti-H in the serum that reacts with the H antigen on essentially all conventional reagent red cells, including reagent group O cells.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: rouleaux from a plasma cell dyscrasia is a recognized cause of extra reactivity mimicking agglutination in ABO discrepancies, but it does not explain the negative anti-H forward typing and is typically resolved by saline replacement rather than reflecting a true antibody.

questions.teaching_point: A negative anti-H forward type combined with serum reactivity against ALL reagent cells (including group O screening cells) should prompt suspicion for Bombay phenotype, distinguishing it from acquired B, cold autoagglutinins, or rouleaux, which do not affect anti-H forward typing.
