source_references.locator: "Bombay Phenotype (Oh)" bullet, Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States that Bombay phenotype presents as a group O discrepancy with a potent anti-H that reacts with all group O reagent cells, requiring rare Oh units for transfusion.

source_references.locator: "ABO Discrepancy Patterns" and "Cold Agglutinins vs. ABO" bullets
source_references.supports: Lists acquired B phenomenon (intestinal disease), cold autoantibodies, and rouleaux (multiple myeloma) as recognized causes of 'extra reactivity' ABO discrepancy patterns, forming the basis of the three distractors.

source_references.locator: "The 'H Gene' Necessity" teaching pearl
source_references.supports: States the H gene is required for A/B/H antigen synthesis on RBC membranes, implying that ordinary (non-Bombay) reagent red cells, including group O cells, normally express H antigen that can be targeted by anti-H.

review.notes: The core diagnostic pattern is directly supported: SRC-024's 'Bombay Phenotype (Oh)' bullet states these patients have a potent anti-H reacting with 'all O cells,' matching the stem's reagent-group-O-cell reactivity, and the phenotype is definitionally H-antigen-negative on RBCs (supporting a negative forward anti-H). However, several explanatory details in the option rationales go beyond what the 4 sources state: the source only lists acquired B, cold autoantibodies, and rouleaux as causes of 'extra reactivity' in ABO discrepancies without describing their mechanisms in the detail asserted he

questions.stem: A patient has been serologically confirmed to have the Bombay phenotype (Oh) with a potent anti-H antibody in her serum. She now requires red cell transfusion for symptomatic anemia. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate transfusion approach?

options.text: Washed group O RBCs, since washing removes the H antigen from the donor red cell membrane and renders the units compatible
Group O RBCs selected by extended phenotype matching for Rh, Kell, Duffy, and Kidd antigens
Emergency-release group O, Rh-negative RBCs, since group O units lack A and B antigens and are considered universally compatible
Rare Bombay (Oh) phenotype RBCs obtained through a rare-donor registry or prior autologous/directed donation, since routine group O units still carry the H antigen targeted by her antibody

questions.explanation_correct: Confirmed Bombay (Oh) patients possess a potent anti-H that reacts with the H antigen expressed on essentially all conventional red cells, including group O units, which are not safe for transfusion despite lacking A and B antigens. These patients require transfusion with rare Oh (Bombay) phenotype red cells, sourced from a rare-donor registry or prior autologous/directed donation.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this reflects the general emergency-release rule of issuing group O RBCs when an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved in time, but once Bombay phenotype is confirmed, group O units are known to be incompatible because they express the H antigen her anti-H targets.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: extended Rh/Kell/Duffy/Kidd phenotype matching is the standard alloimmunization-prevention strategy for chronically transfused patients, but it does not address the fundamental incompatibility caused by H antigen present on any group O unit.
option_explanations.D: Correct: rare Oh units lack H antigen entirely and are the only red cells serologically safe for a patient with a potent anti-H.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: H antigen is intrinsically synthesized on the RBC membrane's own precursor chains by H-transferase, unlike passively acquired/adsorbed plasma antigens; routine washing removes residual plasma proteins but does not strip an intrinsic membrane antigen, so washed group O cells remain incompatible.

questions.teaching_point: Once Bombay phenotype is confirmed, the usual 'group O is universally compatible' rule does not apply; the potent anti-H reacts with H antigen on any conventional (non-Bombay) red cell, so only rare Oh units are transfusion-safe.

source_references.locator: "Bombay Phenotype (Oh)" bullet, Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States that Bombay (Oh) patients have a potent anti-H reacting with all group O cells and require transfusion with rare Oh units.

source_references.locator: "Group O RBCs" bullet, Subdomain Node 29
source_references.supports: States that when an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved before urgent transfusion, only group O RBCs should be issued, forming the basis for the emergency-release distractor which does not apply once Bombay is confirmed.

source_references.locator: "Alloimmunization Mitigation" bullet
source_references.supports: Describes extended antigen matching (C, E, K) as the standard alloimmunization-mitigation strategy for chronically transfused patients, the basis for the extended-phenotype distractor.

source_references.locator: "Adsorbed Antigens" definition and "Bg Distractor" bullet, Subdomain Node 26
source_references.supports: Distinguishes antigens acquired/adsorbed from plasma (e.g., Lewis, Chido, Rodgers, Bg/HLA) that can be diminished by specific treatments, supporting the contrast that H antigen is instead an intrinsically synthesized membrane structure not removed by simple washing.

review.notes: The keyed answer is directly and strongly supported: SRC-024's 'Bombay Phenotype (Oh)' bullet explicitly states these patients require transfusion with rare Oh units because ordinary group O cells still carry H antigen. However, distractor B's option text ('extended phenotype matching for Rh, Kell, Duffy, and Kidd antigens') overstates its own cited source: SRC-024's 'Alloimmunization Mitigation' bullet supports matching only for C, E, and K (Rh/Kell) for chronically transfused patients -- Duffy and Kidd antigen matching as a described policy do not appear anywhere in any of the 4 approved sou

questions.stem: During a resident teaching session on carbohydrate blood group biochemistry, a blood bank supervisor contrasts the Lewis system with the Rh system. She explains that Rh antigens are transmembrane proteins encoded and directly assembled by genes expressed within erythroid precursor cells, whereas Lewis-positive individuals also have abundant Lewis substance detectable in their saliva and plasma, and their Lewis phenotype depends on interactions among the Le, Se, and H genes. The resident asks how Lewis antigen actually comes to be present on the mature red cell membrane. Which of the following best explains this mechanism?

options.text: Lewis glycolipids are synthesized directly within erythroid precursor cells on a Type 2 precursor chain, in the same manner as ABH antigen biosynthesis on the red cell membrane.
Lewis antigen expression on red cells depends solely on inheritance of the Le gene and is unaffected by the individual's ABH secretor (Se/FUT2) status.
Erythroid expression of Lewis antigen requires the RHAG regulator gene, which transcriptionally controls Lewis antigen production in the same way it controls Rh protein expression.
Lewis antigens are carried on a GPI-linked semaphorin protein synthesized within the red cell membrane, analogous to the biochemistry described for the JMH antigen.

Lewis antigens are synthesized by non-erythroid (secretory) tissue on a Type 1 precursor chain, released into the plasma as soluble substance, and then passively adsorbed onto the red cell membrane from the plasma.

questions.explanation_correct: The sources classify Lewis antigens as 'adsorbed antigens' that are not produced by erythroid cells but are acquired from plasma, alongside Chido/Rodgers. Lewis and ABH soluble substances are built on Type 1 precursor chains (beta1->3 linkage), which are associated with secretions and plasma, in contrast to Type 2 chains, which scaffold antigens synthesized within the RBC membrane. The Lewis system entry explicitly describes 'passive adsorption of antigens' driven by Le, Se, and H gene interactions, and secretor (FUT2/Se) status is separately linked to Le(b) expression on red cells, confirming that RBC Lewis phenotype is not independent of secretor status.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect: Type 2 chains are the scaffold for antigens synthesized within the RBC membrane; Lewis/ABH soluble substances instead ride on Type 1 chains associated with secretions and plasma, not on-membrane erythroid synthesis.
option_explanations.E: Correct: Lewis antigens are produced by secretory tissue on Type 1 chains, secreted as soluble substance, and passively adsorbed onto RBCs from plasma, matching the sources' description of Lewis as an adsorbed antigen with passive adsorption driven by Le/Se/H interactions.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sources tie RHAG specifically to Rh antigen expression, not Lewis; misapplying a Rh-specific regulator to Lewis is a classic cross-system distractor.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources explicitly link FUT2 (Se gene) status to Le(b) expression on red cells, so secretor status does affect the RBC Lewis phenotype rather than being irrelevant.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: GPI-linked semaphorin on CD108 describes the JMH antigen in the sources, an unrelated system; this option borrows JMH biochemistry and misapplies it to Lewis.

source_references.supports: Lewis antigens are adsorbed (not erythroid-synthesized) antigens acquired from plasma; Type 1 chains carry Lewis/ABH soluble substances in secretions/plasma vs. Type 2 chains scaffolding RBC membrane antigens; RHAG regulates Rh; JMH is a GPI-linked semaphorin on CD108.

source_references.locator: line 27
source_references.supports: The Lewis System entry: passive adsorption of antigens; Le, Se, and H gene interactions.

source_references.locator: line 31
source_references.supports: Secretor Status links FUT2 (Se gene) to expression of ABH in secretions and Le(b) on red cells.

review.notes: Core claim (Lewis = adsorbed antigen, not erythroid-synthesized; Type 1 chain scaffold vs Type 2 for RBC-membrane antigens; RHAG regulates Rh not Lewis; JMH is the GPI-linked semaphorin, not Lewis; Le/Se/H interaction and FUT2-Le(b) link) is directly supported: SRC-003 line 17 ('Adsorbed Antigens: ... not produced by erythroid cells but acquired from plasma (e.g., Lewis, Chido, Rodgers)'), line 45 (Type 1 vs Type 2 precursor comparison), line 29 (JMH/CD108/GPI-semaphorin), and line 15 (RHAG regulates Rh, KLF1 regulates Lutheran); SRC-043 line 27 ('Passive adsorption of antigens; Le, Se, and H

questions.stem: A technologist is working up a patient's serum that produces weak agglutination with several reagent red cells during antibody screening. When the same panel is repeated using ficin-treated reagent red cells, the reactions become noticeably stronger. To help confirm specificity, the technologist preincubates an aliquot of the patient's serum with the patient's own saliva before testing it against a Le(a+) reagent red cell; the previously observed agglutination is abolished. Which antibody specificity is most consistent with these findings?

options.text: Anti-P1
Anti-Fya
Anti-Jka
Anti-Ch (Chido)
Anti-Lea

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that ficin/papain enzyme treatment enhances Lewis (as well as Rh, Kidd, P1, and I) antigen reactivity, and that Lewis antibodies (anti-Lea/Leb) can be neutralized by soluble Lewis substance found in saliva or plasma. This patient's antibody shows both the expected enzyme-enhancement pattern and loses reactivity after preincubation with saliva against a Le(a+) cell, which together point specifically to anti-Lea. Other listed antibodies either do not fit the enzyme pattern or are not neutralized by saliva per the sources.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources list Fya as an antigen destroyed (not enhanced) by ficin/papain, so enhanced reactivity with enzyme-treated cells argues against anti-Fya; no saliva neutralization is described for Duffy.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: Kidd antigen reactivity is enhanced by ficin/papain like Lewis, but the sources describe no saliva-based neutralization method for anti-Jka, so it would not be abolished by preincubation with saliva.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: P1 antigen reactivity is also enhanced by ficin/papain, but the sources specify hydatid cyst fluid or pigeon egg whites, not saliva, as the neutralization source for anti-P1.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources state Ch/Rg antigens are destroyed by proteolytic enzymes (ficin/papain), the opposite of the enhancement seen here, and Ch/Rg is neutralized by pooled human plasma rather than saliva.
option_explanations.E: Correct: Lewis antigen reactivity is enhanced by ficin/papain, and anti-Lea/Leb activity is specifically neutralized by Lewis substance present in saliva or plasma, matching both findings in the vignette.

source_references.supports: Ficin/papain enhances Rh, Kidd, P1, Lewis, and I; destroys M, N, S, Fya, Fyb; neutralization sources include Lewis (saliva or plasma), P1 (hydatid cyst fluid or pigeon egg whites), and Ch/Rg (pooled human plasma).

source_references.locator: line 21
source_references.supports: CH/RG antigens are acquired from plasma and are destroyed by proteolytic enzymes (ficin/papain).

source_references.locator: line 41
source_references.supports: Neutralization: Lewis substance is used to mask anti-Lea/Leb antibody activity.

review.notes: Fully grounded and precisely cited. SRC-001 lines 41/43 confirm ficin/papain destroys M,N,S,Fya,Fyb and enhances Rh,Kidd,P1,Lewis,I; lines 53/55/57 confirm neutralization sources: Lewis by saliva or plasma, P1 by hydatid cyst fluid/pigeon egg whites, Ch/Rg by pooled human plasma. SRC-003 line 21 confirms Ch/Rg is destroyed (not enhanced) by proteolytic enzymes, correctly refuting option D. SRC-043 line 41 confirms Lewis substance masks anti-Lea/Leb activity, matching the saliva-neutralization clue. Because the enzyme-enhancement pattern is shared by Rh/Kidd/P1/Lewis/I but the saliva-neutraliza

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 42-year-old practicing Jehovah's Witness presents with acute gastrointestinal bleeding and a hemoglobin of 6.5 g/dL. After being counseled on risks and benefits, she declines allogeneic red blood cell (RBC) transfusion. Her physician initiates bloodless medicine measures (IV iron, erythropoietin, meticulous endoscopic hemostasis) and considers a hemoglobin-based oxygen carrier (HBOC) as bridging therapy. Unlike allogeneic RBC units, HBOCs can be administered without pretransfusion crossmatching. Which of the following best explains this difference?

options.text: HBOCs undergo pathogen-inactivation treatment that strips away all immunogenic donor proteins before release.
HBOCs are cell-free, synthetic oxygen-carrying solutions and therefore lack the red cell membrane blood group antigens that crossmatching is designed to detect antibodies against.
HBOCs are manufactured exclusively from group O donor blood, making them universally compatible without testing.
HBOCs are irradiated during production, which prevents antigen-antibody interactions after infusion.
HBOCs are leukocyte-reduced during manufacturing, which removes the antigens responsible for alloimmunization.

questions.explanation_correct: The source material characterizes HBOCs as synthetic oxygen-carrying solutions that do not require crossmatching. Because they are cell-free products rather than intact donor red cells, they do not carry the red cell membrane blood group antigens (e.g., ABO, Rh) that crossmatching exists to detect incompatibility against — removing the immunologic basis for the pretransfusion antibody testing required for allogeneic RBC units.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect: HBOCs are described as synthetic (cell-free) products, not donor-derived Group O red cells; 'universal donor' logic in the sources applies to Group O RBC units in ABO-discrepancy management, not to HBOCs.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: pathogen inactivation (e.g., amotosalen/UVA) is described as a plasma/platelet technology aimed at reducing transfusion-transmitted disease risk, not as the reason HBOCs skip crossmatching.
option_explanations.B: Correct: HBOCs are cell-free synthetic solutions, so they carry no red cell membrane antigens for antibodies to react against, eliminating the need for crossmatching required for cellular RBC units.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: leukoreduction is described as a strategy to remove CMV-carrying white cells (CMV mitigation), not as the mechanism underlying HBOC crossmatch avoidance.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: irradiation is described as a method to prevent TA-GVHD by inactivating viable donor T lymphocytes, not as a mechanism related to antigen-antibody crossmatch testing.

questions.teaching_point: HBOCs are cell-free synthetic oxygen carriers; lacking red cell membrane antigens lets them bridge anemia in transfusion-refusing patients without crossmatching, unlike allogeneic RBC units.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 54 – Management of Transfusion Refusal
source_references.supports: States competent adults may refuse transfusion (e.g., Jehovah's Witnesses), that HBOCs are synthetic oxygen carriers not requiring crossmatching and are available mainly via trials/compassionate use, and that bloodless medicine includes EPO, iron, cell salvage, and hemostasis.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 52 – CMV Mitigation Strategies
source_references.supports: Describes leukoreduction as the mitigation strategy for CMV-carrying leukocytes, grounding why leukoreduction (distractor D) is unrelated to HBOC crossmatch avoidance.

source_references.locator: Prevention section, Pathogen Inactivation
source_references.supports: Describes pathogen inactivation (amotosalen/UVA) as a plasma/platelet technology to reduce transfusion-transmitted disease risk, grounding why distractor B misapplies this concept to HBOCs.

source_references.locator: Prevention section, TA-GVHD
source_references.supports: Describes irradiation of blood components as protection against TA-GVHD via inactivation of donor T lymphocytes, grounding why distractor E misstates irradiation's purpose.

source_references.locator: Management section, Group O Universalism
source_references.supports: Describes Group O RBC units as the universal fallback in ABO discrepancy management, grounding why distractor A's Group-O-donor explanation is a plausible but incorrect conflation.

review.notes: SRC-012 Node 54 supports 'HBOCs are synthetic oxygen-carrying solutions that do not require crossmatching' verbatim, and separately supports informed refusal (JW) and bloodless medicine (EPO, iron, cell saver, hemostasis). However, the correct-answer's explanatory mechanism -- that HBOCs are 'cell-free' and therefore 'lack the red cell membrane blood group antigens that crossmatching is designed to detect antibodies against' -- is not stated anywhere in the source; the source gives the fact (no crossmatch needed) but not this causal mechanism, so it is an inferred/synthesized rationale layered

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 38-year-old man received an allogeneic HSCT 10 weeks ago from a major ABO-mismatched donor (recipient blood group O, donor blood group A). His absolute neutrophil count recovered on schedule and remains normal, confirming myeloid engraftment, and platelet counts are adequate. However, he continues to require RBC transfusion support. A bone marrow biopsy shows a reticulocyte count <1% with absence of erythroid precursors; myeloid and megakaryocytic lineages are unaffected. Which of the following best explains this patient's continued RBC transfusion dependence?

options.text: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction resulting from failure to red cell-reduce the graft before infusion.
Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) from infusion of non-irradiated cellular blood components.
Pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) caused by persistent recipient anti-donor isoagglutinins suppressing donor-derived erythropoiesis despite successful myeloid engraftment.
Graft failure/rejection, which by definition would also present with failure of neutrophil (ANC) engraftment.
Passenger lymphocyte syndrome, in which donor-derived lymphocytes produce antibodies against the recipient's original RBC antigens.

questions.explanation_correct: The source material states that PRCA occurs in 3%–29% of major ABO-mismatched transplants and is characterized by reticulocytes <1% and absence of erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment — a pattern that matches this patient exactly (major mismatch, intact ANC/myeloid recovery, isolated erythroid failure). PRCA reflects ongoing recipient isoagglutinins (here, anti-A, since the recipient is group O and the donor is group A) suppressing donor-derived red cell precursors even after myeloid and megakaryocytic lineages have engrafted.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the source ties red cell reduction of a major-mismatched graft to prevention of an acute hemolytic reaction at the time of infusion, not to a marrow-level erythroid aplasia presenting weeks later.
option_explanations.C: Correct: this is the classic source-described picture of PRCA after major ABO mismatch — reticulocytes <1%, absent erythroid precursors, myeloid engraftment intact.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: TA-GVHD is prevented by irradiating transfused cellular components and is not described as producing an isolated erythroid marrow defect with preserved myeloid/megakaryocytic lines.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources define engraftment by ANC recovery; since this patient's ANC recovered on schedule, graft failure/rejection (which would manifest as failure of that same ANC engraftment) is inconsistent with the findings.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the source specifies passenger lymphocyte syndrome occurs in minor (not major) ABO mismatches, typically 5–16 days post-infusion, a different mechanism and timeline than described here.

questions.teaching_point: Isolated reticulocytopenia with absent erythroid precursors but intact myeloid/megakaryocytic engraftment after major ABO-mismatched HSCT points to PRCA from persistent recipient isoagglutinins, not graft failure or an acute hemolytic event, and explains ongoing RBC transfusion dependence.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings section, Pure Red Cell Aplasia (PRCA)
source_references.supports: Defines PRCA after major ABO-mismatched transplant, including its incidence and its characteristic finding of reticulocytes <1% with absent erythroid precursors despite intact myeloid engraftment.

source_references.locator: Management section, ABO Incompatibility Management – Major mismatch
source_references.supports: States that red cell reduction of a major-mismatched graft is performed specifically to prevent acute hemolysis, grounding why an acute hemolytic reaction (distractor A) does not fit a delayed, isolated erythroid-aplasia picture.

source_references.locator: Definitions section, Engraftment; Core Concepts, Major Histocompatibility Barrier
source_references.supports: Defines engraftment as typically indicated by ANC recovery and describes graft rejection as related to HLA disparity, grounding why intact ANC recovery argues against graft failure/rejection (distractor D).

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls, Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome
source_references.supports: Describes passenger lymphocyte syndrome as occurring in minor (not major) ABO mismatches, typically 5–16 days post-infusion, distinguishing it from the case described.

source_references.locator: Prevention section, TA-GVHD
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD prevention requires irradiated blood components for HSCT recipients, grounding why TA-GVHD (distractor C) is a different entity than an isolated marrow erythroid defect.

review.notes: SRC-038 directly and precisely supports the diagnostic picture: 'PRCA: Occurs in 3%-29% of major ABO-mismatched transplants; characterized by reticulocytes <1% and absence of erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment.' This is a strong, near-verbatim match to the vignette. Two residual concerns: (1) the correct answer's causal claim -- that PRCA is caused by 'persistent recipient anti-donor isoagglutinins suppressing donor-derived erythropoiesis' -- is standard teaching but is not explicitly stated in the source (the source only names/characterizes the entity, it does not state the mech

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 68-year-old hemodynamically stable, non-bleeding adult inpatient has anemia and is being evaluated for RBC transfusion prior to a routine outpatient procedure scheduled for the following week. Vital signs are normal, and there is no evidence of active bleeding or hemodynamic compromise. Per patient blood management (PBM) principles, which of the following is the most appropriate transfusion approach?

options.text: Transfuse one unit of RBCs, then clinically reassess the patient's symptoms and hemoglobin before deciding whether a second unit is needed.
Transfuse RBCs to a fixed target hemoglobin, regardless of the patient's ongoing symptoms or clinical status.
Use a hemoglobin-based oxygen carrier (HBOC) instead of allogeneic RBCs, since HBOCs avoid crossmatching delays.
Defer RBC transfusion and instead administer fresh frozen plasma to support the hemoglobin and expand intravascular volume.
Order two units of RBCs as a standard transfusion order to minimize the number of separate blood bank releases.

questions.explanation_correct: The source material describes a core PBM principle — 'why give 2 when 1 will do' — under which single-unit RBC (or platelet) transfusions should be the default for stable patients, with clinical reassessment before any additional unit is given. This stable, non-bleeding patient fits that scenario: transfusing one unit and reassessing avoids unnecessary exposure to a second unit whose need has not yet been established.

option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this is the classic board trap the sources warn against — routine two-unit ordering contradicts the 'why give 2 when 1 will do' single-unit-then-reassess principle for stable patients.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the PBM pearl emphasizes reassessment of the individual patient after each unit rather than transfusing to a fixed numeric target irrespective of clinical status.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sources describe HBOCs as available primarily through clinical trials or compassionate use, not as a routine substitute for standard crossmatched RBCs in a patient who has not refused transfusion.
option_explanations.A: Correct: this directly reflects the sources' single-unit, reassess-before-repeating PBM principle for stable, non-bleeding patients.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources explicitly flag this as a distractor — plasma should never be used for simple volume expansion, and it does not address the patient's anemia.

questions.teaching_point: For stable, non-bleeding adults, PBM favors single-unit RBC transfusion with reassessment before further transfusion, rather than routine multi-unit orders, fixed hemoglobin targets, or inappropriate use of plasma/HBOCs.

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls, 'Why give 2 when 1 will do?'
source_references.supports: States the PBM principle that single-unit RBC or platelet transfusions should be the default for stable patients, followed by clinical reassessment, directly supporting the correct answer and refuting the two-unit distractor.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors, Volume Expansion Distractor
source_references.supports: States plasma should never be used for simple volume expansion, grounding why deferring RBCs in favor of FFP for volume support is a named board-trap distractor.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 54 – Management of Transfusion Refusal, HBOCs
source_references.supports: States HBOCs are available primarily through clinical trials or compassionate use rather than as routine clinical alternatives to allogeneic RBCs, grounding why HBOC use is inappropriate here.

review.notes: Strongly grounded: SRC-031 states the PBM pearl verbatim -- "'Why give 2 when 1 will do?': ... for stable patients, single-unit transfusions (RBCs or Platelets) should be the default, followed by clinical reassessment" -- which directly supports D and directly refutes A (routine two-unit ordering). E is grounded by the explicit 'Volume Expansion Distractor: Plasma should never be used for simple volume expansion.' C is grounded by SRC-012's statement that HBOCs are available 'primarily through clinical trials or compassionate use' rather than as a routine substitute. B (fixed Hgb target regard

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A fetus at 28 weeks' gestation is diagnosed with severe anemia from hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn and requires an intrauterine transfusion (IUT). The transfusion service issues an irradiated, leukoreduced group O, Rh-negative RBC unit that was collected 5 days earlier rather than an otherwise compatible unit nearing the end of its dating period.

options.text: Reduce the risk of transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease in the fetus
Maximize 2,3-DPG content and minimize extracellular potassium, supporting oxygen delivery and reducing the risk of hyperkalemia during the large-volume infusion
Reduce the risk of transfusion-transmitted cytomegalovirus infection
Reduce the citrate load delivered with the unit, preventing hypocalcemia
Ensure ABO compatibility with any passively acquired maternal antibody

questions.explanation_correct: For large-volume or intrauterine transfusion, units should be less than 7-10 days old specifically to maximize 2,3-DPG levels (preserving oxygen unloading capacity) and to minimize extracellular potassium, since rapid infusion of an older, more potassium-rich stored unit risks life-threatening hyperkalemia in a small-volume recipient.

option_explanations.B: Correct: freshness (<7-10 days) is specifically selected to maximize 2,3-DPG and minimize potassium, addressing both oxygen-delivery efficacy and hyperkalemia risk during large-volume/IUT transfusion.
option_explanations.A: TA-GVHD prevention is achieved by irradiation of cellular components, not by selecting a fresher unit; irradiation is a separate, mandatory safety standard for at-risk recipients.
option_explanations.C: CMV transmission risk is mitigated by leukoreduction, not by unit age; this is a distinct safety standard from the freshness requirement.
option_explanations.D: Citrate-related hypocalcemia is a plasma-volume/rate-of-infusion issue, not something reduced by choosing a fresher RBC unit.
option_explanations.E: ABO/Rh selection (group O, Rh-negative unless type-specific is confirmed safe) is governed by maternal antibody status, not by how old the unit is.

questions.teaching_point: Freshness (<7-10 days) of RBC units for large-volume/IUT pediatric transfusion is specifically about preserving 2,3-DPG (oxygen delivery efficacy) and limiting potassium load, distinct from irradiation (TA-GVHD), leukoreduction (CMV), and ABO/Rh selection logic.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36, RBC Requirements
source_references.supports: States units for large-volume or IUT should be <7-10 days old to maximize 2,3-DPG and minimize potassium.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36, Safety Standards
source_references.supports: States cellular components must be irradiated (TA-GVHD prevention) and leukoreduced (CMV risk reduction) as separate safety standards.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36, Metabolic Risks
source_references.supports: Describes rapid infusion of stored RBCs causing hyperkalemia and citrate in plasma causing hypocalcemia as distinct metabolic risks.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36, ABO/Rh Logic
source_references.supports: States group O, Rh-negative RBCs are standard for neonates unless type-specific cells are confirmed safe based on maternal antibody status.

review.notes: SRC-051 Node 36 'RBC Requirements' directly states units for large-volume/IUT should be <7-10 days old to maximize 2,3-DPG and minimize potassium — matches option A and the stem's 5-day-old unit vs. near-expiration alternative exactly. Distractors B (irradiation->TA-GVHD), C (leukoreduction->CMV), D (citrate/hypocalcemia as a distinct risk, not freshness-mitigated), and E (ABO/Rh selection governed by maternal antibody status) are each correctly separated from the freshness rationale per the same source node. Minor flaw: the stem never states an explicit question (e.g., 'What is the rationale

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 6-week-old male infant, blood group A, Rh-positive, requires an RBC transfusion for symptomatic anemia. He has never been transfused. Pretransfusion testing shows a positive antibody screen; workup identifies anti-A reactivity in the infant's plasma. Since birth, the blood bank has been issuing group O, Rh-negative RBCs to this infant.

options.text: The infant has synthesized his own anti-A alloantibody; group O RBCs must be continued indefinitely with no further evaluation needed
The anti-A is most likely passively acquired from the mother; a transfusion physician should be consulted before switching from group O to type-specific (group A) RBCs
A positive antibody screen at this age is necessarily a testing artifact, since neonates cannot passively acquire or retain maternal antibodies this long
The anti-A is passively acquired from the mother, and group A RBCs may be issued immediately without further consultation because the infant is past the immediate newborn period
This result mandates discontinuing any testing exemption policy and performing full antibody identification with every future encounter regardless of screen result

questions.explanation_correct: Neonates generally do not produce their own RBC alloantibodies; antibodies detected, including anti-A/anti-B, are typically passively acquired from the mother. When passively acquired maternal anti-A or anti-B is identified, a transfusion physician should be consulted before switching the infant from group O to type-specific RBCs.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the stated principle that neonatal antibodies are passively maternal in origin and that a transfusion physician must be consulted before any switch from group O to type-specific units.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: neonates are not expected to synthesize their own RBC alloantibodies; this option also wrongly forecloses further evaluation, contrary to the recommended physician consultation.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: a positive screen from passively acquired maternal antibody is an expected, not artifactual, finding in infants; the source's testing exemption applies only when the initial screen is negative, implying a positive result at this age is meaningful and expected in some infants.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the source specifies consultation with a transfusion physician before switching from group O to type-specific RBCs when a passively acquired maternal anti-A/anti-B is found, regardless of the infant's exact age within the neonatal window.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect and unsupported: the source describes an exemption tied to a negative initial screen, not an escalation to mandatory lifelong full identification triggered by a single positive maternal-antibody result.

questions.teaching_point: A positive antibody screen in a young infant is usually passively acquired maternal antibody rather than true neonatal alloimmunization; this affects whether type-specific RBCs can safely replace group O units, and the source directs consultation with a transfusion physician before making that switch.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36, Immune Immaturity
source_references.supports: States neonates generally do not produce their own RBC alloantibodies; detected antibodies are typically passively acquired from the mother.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36, PAS-Acquired Antibodies
source_references.supports: States that if passively acquired maternal anti-A or anti-B is detected, a transfusion physician should be consulted before switching from group O to type-specific RBCs.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36, Testing Exemption
source_references.supports: States that if a neonate's initial antibody screen is negative, repeat ABO/Rh and antibody screening can be omitted for the rest of the admission or until 4 months of age, implying a positive screen does not fall under this exemption.

review.notes: The core tested facts are directly grounded in SRC-051 Node 36: neonates generally do not synthesize their own RBC alloantibodies (antibodies are typically passively acquired from the mother), and if passively acquired maternal anti-A/anti-B is detected, a transfusion physician should be consulted before switching from group O to type-specific RBCs — this exactly matches option A and correctly invalidates B, D, and E. However, the stem contains an internal contradiction: 'He has never been transfused' is immediately followed by 'Since birth, the blood bank has been issuing group O, Rh-negative

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents 10 days after an upper respiratory viral illness with pallor and dark urine that began after he played outside in cold weather. Laboratory evaluation shows brisk intravascular hemolysis. The direct antiglobulin test is positive with anti-C3d and negative with anti-IgG, and a Donath-Landsteiner test is positive. His hemoglobin is critically low and immediate RBC transfusion is required. The reference laboratory reports that p-negative RBC units are not available.

options.text: Transfuse standard RBCs without a blood warmer, because the causative antibody is IgM and warming would increase agglutination
Perform cold autoadsorption with the patient's own RBCs to remove the autoantibody from his plasma before selecting a unit for transfusion
Withhold transfusion until p-negative units can be located, since standard units will be immediately destroyed by the circulating antibody
Transfuse standard, ABO/Rh-compatible RBCs using a blood warmer, and keep the patient warm; do not delay transfusion while awaiting rare antigen-negative units
Delay transfusion to first administer corticosteroids, which are first-line therapy for this antibody-mediated hemolysis

questions.explanation_correct: This presentation (child, post-viral illness, cold-induced hemoglobinuria, DAT positive for C3d only, positive Donath-Landsteiner test) is classic for paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria (PCH). Although p-negative blood is ideal, it is extremely rare (~1:200,000), so transfusion should not be withheld in urgent cases; standard blood may be tolerated once the patient is warmed, and blood warmers are indicated for potent, high-thermal-amplitude cold autoantibodies to prevent in-vivo binding.

option_explanations.D: Correct: matches the guidance that p-negative blood, though ideal, is too rare to wait for, and that standard blood with warming (patient and blood warmer) is an appropriate urgent approach in PCH.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the source explicitly states transfusion should not be withheld in urgent cases while awaiting rare antigen-negative units.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect on two points: PCH is caused by an IgG biphasic hemolysin (a classic board trap, not IgM despite being cold-reactive), and blood warmers are specifically indicated for potent, high-thermal-amplitude cold autoantibodies.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: cold autoadsorption is a serologic workup technique to unmask alloantibodies in patients with cold autoantibodies who have not been recently transfused; it is not described as the required step before urgent transfusion in acute PCH.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: corticosteroids are described as first-line for warm AIHA, not specifically for PCH, and delaying urgently needed transfusion for a critically low hemoglobin contradicts the guidance not to withhold transfusion.

questions.teaching_point: In pediatric PCH, transfusion efficacy and safety are optimized by not delaying urgent transfusion for rare p-negative units and by using warming (patient and blood warmer) with standard compatible blood, since the causative antibody is an IgG biphasic hemolysin, not IgM.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 13, Transfusion in PCH
source_references.supports: States p-negative blood is ideal but rare (~1:200,000); transfusion should not be withheld urgently, and standard blood may be tolerated once the patient is warmed.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 13, Blood Warmers
source_references.supports: States blood warmers are indicated for transfusion in patients with potent, high-thermal-amplitude cold autoantibodies to prevent in-vivo binding.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 13, PCH Isotype and Cold Autoadsorption
source_references.supports: Clarifies PCH is IgG (not IgM) as a common board trap, and describes cold autoadsorption as a technique for unmasking alloantibodies in non-recently-transfused patients.

source_references.locator: Domain I, Clinical Presentations and Management
source_references.supports: Describes PCH as typically seen in children following viral infection, presenting with acute rapid intravascular hemolysis and hemoglobinuria; corticosteroids listed as first-line for WAIHA specifically.

review.notes: Fully grounded: SRC-056 Node 13 states p-negative blood is ideal but rare (~1:200,000), transfusion should not be withheld urgently, standard blood may be tolerated once the patient is warmed, and blood warmers are indicated for potent/high-thermal-amplitude cold autoantibodies — matching option A and refuting B (withhold) and C (no warmer, IgM claim; source explicitly flags PCH-is-IgG as a board trap). SRC-019 Domain I confirms the PCH clinical picture (post-viral, brisk intravascular hemolysis, DAT C3d+/IgG-, Donath-Landsteiner positive) and that corticosteroids are first-line specifically f

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay after a high-speed motor vehicle collision with a grade IV splenic laceration and ongoing intra-abdominal hemorrhage. Over the first hour he receives 6 units of RBCs and 2 liters of crystalloid, with no plasma or platelets administered. Despite an adequately maintained hemoglobin, he continues to ooze diffusely from IV sites, the surgical incision, and mucosal surfaces. Which of the following mechanisms best explains his ongoing bleeding?

options.text: Citrate-induced hypocalcemia from rapid infusion of anticoagulated blood components
Hyperkalemia from potassium leaching out of the stored RBC units during rapid infusion
Transfusion-related acute lung injury from donor anti-HLA antibodies
Storage lesion with decreased 2,3-DPG in the transfused RBC units impairing tissue oxygen delivery
Dilutional coagulopathy from replacing lost whole blood with RBCs and crystalloid alone, depleting coagulation factors and platelets

questions.explanation_correct: Dilutional coagulopathy is the hemorrhagic state produced when lost whole blood is replaced with RBCs and crystalloid alone, progressively depleting coagulation factor and platelet levels. This patient received a large RBC/crystalloid load with no plasma or platelet replacement, which is the exact scenario the source material identifies as the cause of this coagulopathy and the rationale for balanced, formula-based MTP ratios.

option_explanations.E: Correct: replacing whole-blood loss with RBCs and crystalloid only depletes clotting factors and platelets, producing diffuse microvascular oozing.
option_explanations.A: Citrate toxicity arises from rapid infusion of citrate-anticoagulated plasma/platelets (which carry roughly 5x more citrate than RBCs) and manifests as perioral paresthesias, tetany, and arrhythmias from hypocalcemia, not primarily as diffuse surgical oozing; this patient received predominantly RBCs and crystalloid, not large plasma/platelet volumes.
option_explanations.B: Hyperkalemia results from potassium leaking into the supernatant during RBC storage and can cause cardiac arrest with rapid infusion, but it is a metabolic/cardiac complication, not a mechanism of impaired hemostasis.
option_explanations.D: The storage lesion (decreased 2,3-DPG) is a recognized phenomenon affecting oxygen delivery, but large trials have not shown it significantly affects outcomes in massive transfusion, and it does not explain a coagulopathic bleeding pattern.
option_explanations.C: TRALI is a pulmonary complication arising from donor antibodies (e.g., anti-HLA/HNA) reacting with recipient leukocytes, producing pulmonary edema rather than diffuse bleeding/oozing.

questions.teaching_point: In the actively bleeding/massively transfused patient, diffuse microvascular oozing despite adequate hemoglobin should prompt consideration of dilutional coagulopathy from unbalanced RBC/crystalloid-only resuscitation, not metabolic complications or storage lesion effects, which have different clinical signatures.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Core Concepts (Balanced Resuscitation) and Definitions (Dilutional Coagulopathy)
source_references.supports: Defines dilutional coagulopathy as replacement of lost whole blood with RBCs/crystalloid alone, depleting factors/platelets, and describes balanced 1:1:1 MTP ratios to prevent it.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Clinical Presentations (Citrate Toxicity)
source_references.supports: Describes citrate toxicity mechanism (hypocalcemia, tetany, arrhythmia) from rapid infusion of anticoagulated plasma/platelet components.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Clinical Presentations (Hyperkalemia) and Common Board Traps (RBC Age Distractor)
source_references.supports: Describes hyperkalemia as a metabolic storage-related complication distinct from coagulopathy, and the 'RBC Age Distractor' noting no proven survival difference from storage lesion in massive transfusion.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 46 - Mechanics of the Shift (Shift to the Left)
source_references.supports: Describes 2,3-DPG decrease in stored blood as a shift-to-left phenomenon affecting oxygen delivery, not hemostasis.

source_references.locator: Domain X - Prevention (TRALI Risk Reduction)
source_references.supports: Describes TRALI as related to donor HLA/HNA antibodies, mitigated by male-donor plasma selection, distinguishing it as a pulmonary rather than hemostatic complication.

review.notes: Dilutional coagulopathy definition ('replacing lost whole blood with only RBCs and crystalloids, leading to depleted factor and platelet levels') matches SRC-032 Node 16 verbatim. Citrate toxicity (hypocalcemia sx: perioral paresthesias/tetany/arrhythmia, driven by plasma/platelets carrying 5x more citrate than RBCs), hyperkalemia (potassium leak into supernatant during storage), 2,3-DPG/shift-to-left (SRC-002 Node 46), and TRALI/donor HLA-HNA antibodies causing pulmonary rather than hemostatic injury (SRC-031) are all accurately represented as distinct, non-hemostatic mechanisms. Scenario (6u

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman develops postpartum hemorrhage and is undergoing an activated massive transfusion protocol. She has received 8 units of RBCs so far and continues to have diffuse microvascular oozing from venipuncture sites. Current laboratory results are: platelet count 38,000/µL, INR 1.2, aPTT 30 seconds, fibrinogen 220 mg/dL, and ionized calcium 1.15 mmol/L. Based on these results, which blood component is most indicated at this time?

options.text: Cryoprecipitate
No additional component is indicated; continue RBCs alone
Platelets
Calcium gluconate
Fresh frozen plasma

questions.explanation_correct: Per the laboratory triggers used during massive transfusion, platelet transfusion is indicated when the count falls below 50,000/µL. This patient's platelet count of 38,000/µL meets that threshold, while her INR (1.2), aPTT (30 sec), fibrinogen (220 mg/dL), and ionized calcium (1.15 mmol/L) are all within the ranges that do not meet the stated triggers for plasma, cryoprecipitate, or calcium replacement.

option_explanations.C: Correct: platelet count of 38,000/µL is below the 50,000/µL trigger used for platelet transfusion during massive transfusion in a bleeding patient.
option_explanations.E: Plasma is indicated when INR exceeds 1.5 or aPTT exceeds 60 seconds; this patient's INR (1.2) and aPTT (30 sec) are both well below those thresholds, so plasma is not indicated by these values.
option_explanations.A: Cryoprecipitate replacement is triggered by fibrinogen below roughly 100 mg/dL (with bleeding described once fibrinogen falls below 100-150 mg/dL); at 220 mg/dL her fibrinogen is well above threshold.
option_explanations.D: Ionized calcium should be maintained above 1.0 mmol/L during MTP monitoring; her level of 1.15 mmol/L is within the acceptable range, so calcium replacement is not indicated at this time.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: her platelet count of 38,000/µL is below the stated 50,000/µL platelet transfusion trigger in this actively bleeding patient, so continuing RBCs alone would not be appropriate.

questions.teaching_point: During massive transfusion in a bleeding patient, component selection should be driven by specific laboratory triggers (platelets <50,000/µL; plasma for INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 sec; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL) rather than reflexively transfusing all components or none.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Diagnostic and Management Approach (Laboratory Triggers)
source_references.supports: States the laboratory triggers used in massive transfusion: platelets if count <50,000/µL, plasma if INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 sec.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Diagnostic and Management Approach (Fibrinogen; Monitoring)
source_references.supports: Notes cryoprecipitate is indicated when fibrinogen falls below 100 mg/dL, and that ionized calcium should be maintained above 1.0 mmol/L during monitoring.

source_references.locator: Domain X - Clinical Presentations (Hypofibrinogenemia)
source_references.supports: Describes hypofibrinogenemia as causing diffuse microvascular bleeding when fibrinogen falls below 100-150 mg/dL, supporting the fibrinogen threshold used to rule out cryoprecipitate need here.

review.notes: All four lab-trigger thresholds are directly and correctly sourced: platelets <50,000/µL (pt at 38,000, meets trigger), plasma for INR>1.5 or aPTT>60s (pt's 1.2/30s both below), cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL (pt at 220, well above; SRC-031 corroborates 100-150 mg/dL bleeding threshold), ionized calcium maintain >1.0 mmol/L (pt at 1.15, within range) — all verbatim from SRC-032 Node 16. Only platelets meet a stated trigger, making A unambiguous.

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man arrives in hemorrhagic shock after a stab wound to the abdomen with active bleeding, hypotension, and tachycardia. The trauma team anticipates that he will require large-volume transfusion during emergent surgical control of bleeding. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial transfusion strategy for this patient?

options.text: Administer tranexamic acid alone in place of blood component transfusion to control the hemorrhage
Preferentially select AB plasma for this patient because it is the universal plasma type
Activate the massive transfusion protocol and administer RBCs, plasma, and platelets in a fixed ratio (e.g., 1:1:1)
Withhold plasma and platelets, giving RBCs and crystalloid alone until formal coagulation studies result
Apply the restrictive hemoglobin transfusion threshold (7-8 g/dL) used for stable, non-bleeding patients

questions.explanation_correct: Modern massive transfusion protocols use a fixed, formula-based ratio of plasma, platelets, and RBCs (e.g., 1:1:1) to approximate the composition of whole blood and prevent dilutional coagulopathy. In a patient with active hemorrhage anticipated to need large-volume transfusion, this balanced approach is the appropriate initial strategy, rather than waiting on lab results, applying a stable-patient threshold, favoring a scarce plasma type, or substituting an adjunct for component therapy.

option_explanations.C: Correct: balanced, formula-based ratios of RBCs, plasma, and platelets are the modern MTP standard to restore oxygen-carrying capacity, oncotic pressure, and hemostasis while preventing dilutional coagulopathy.
option_explanations.D: Giving RBCs and crystalloid alone while withholding plasma/platelets produces the dilutional coagulopathy that balanced MTP ratios are specifically designed to prevent.
option_explanations.E: The restrictive hemoglobin threshold (7-8 g/dL) is a patient blood management strategy intended for stable, non-bleeding patients; transfusion decisions in an actively hemorrhaging patient should integrate clinical signs and anticipated needs rather than a stable-patient laboratory threshold.
option_explanations.B: AB plasma is the universal type, but because AB donors are scarce, many trauma protocols instead use group A plasma for emergent release rather than reserving AB plasma preferentially.
option_explanations.A: Tranexamic acid is listed as one of several technological/pharmacologic tools within patient blood management, used alongside (not instead of) blood component transfusion in a patient anticipated to need large-volume transfusion.

questions.teaching_point: The actively bleeding patient anticipated to need large-volume transfusion should be managed with MTP activation and balanced component ratios rather than a stable-patient hemoglobin threshold, RBC-only resuscitation, plasma-type preference that ignores supply constraints, or substitution of an adjunct agent for component therapy.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Core Concepts (Balanced Resuscitation; Physiological Goals)
source_references.supports: Describes balanced formula-based 1:1:1 resuscitation ratios in MTP to prevent dilutional coagulopathy and restore oxygen-carrying capacity, oncotic pressure, and hemostasis.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 17 - Definitions (Restrictive Strategy) and Common Board Traps (Treating the Patient, Not the Value)
source_references.supports: Defines the restrictive strategy (Hgb 7-8 g/dL) as a PBM approach and notes transfusion decisions should integrate clinical signs rather than lab nadirs alone.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 - Common Board Traps and Distractors (Group A Plasma)
source_references.supports: Notes that many trauma protocols use group A plasma for emergent release due to scarcity of AB donors, despite AB being the universal plasma type.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 17 - Management and Strategies (Technological Tools)
source_references.supports: Lists tranexamic acid as a technological/pharmacologic tool within patient blood management strategies alongside Cell Saver and viscoelastic testing.

review.notes: Balanced 1:1:1 MTP resuscitation to prevent dilutional coagulopathy, restrictive Hgb 7-8 g/dL as a stable-patient PBM strategy, Group A plasma used for emergent release due to AB donor scarcity, and TXA listed as an adjunct 'technological tool' within PBM (not a component substitute) are all directly supported by SRC-032 Nodes 16-17. Scenario (anticipated large-volume transfusion for active hemorrhage) squarely matches the MTP activation indication. A is clearly the single best answer among the distractors offered.

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman underwent an uncomplicated cesarean section 8 days ago, during which she received a single prophylactic dose of cefotetan (a detail she does not recall reporting). She now presents with new fatigue and jaundice, and laboratory testing confirms a significant, previously absent anemia. Workup identifies a drug-dependent red cell antibody as the cause of hemolysis, and the drug is classified as producing the 'hapten-type' pattern of drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia.

options.text: The drug induces formation of a true autoantibody that reacts with red cells even when the drug is absent from the test system
The drug and antibody form a loosely attached immune complex on the red cell surface that activates complement, producing acute intravascular hemolysis
The drug binds firmly to the red cell membrane, and IgG antibody directed against the drug-coated membrane mediates extravascular hemolysis
The drug nonspecifically alters the red cell membrane so that plasma proteins adsorb passively, without a true antibody-antigen interaction
Preformed alloantibody from prior red cell sensitization reacts with a foreign antigen introduced by an earlier transfusion

questions.explanation_correct: The source classifies cefotetan (with penicillin) as producing the hapten-type pattern of drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia: the drug binds firmly to the red cell membrane, IgG forms against the drug-membrane complex, and destruction occurs extravascularly. This is contrasted with the immune-complex type (e.g., ceftriaxone/quinine), where loose drug-membrane association triggers complement-mediated acute intravascular hemolysis with renal risk. The source specifically notes cefotetan can trigger profound anemia 7-10 days after a single prophylactic surgical dose such as at cesarean section, exactly matching this vignette.

option_explanations.C: Correct. Firm drug-membrane binding with IgG-mediated extravascular hemolysis is the defined hapten-type mechanism, and the source explicitly places cefotetan/penicillin in this category.
option_explanations.B: This describes the immune-complex mechanism (loose drug-membrane association, complement activation, acute intravascular hemolysis with renal risk), which the source attributes to ceftriaxone/quinine, not cefotetan's hapten-type pattern -- a classic board trap for testing whether the two mechanisms are confused.
option_explanations.A: This describes the drug-independent (autoantibody) mechanism, serologically identical to warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, exemplified by methyldopa (and more recently fludarabine) -- not the firm-binding hapten mechanism at play here.
option_explanations.D: This describes nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA), in which no true antibody specificity exists and the eluate/serum are negative -- inconsistent with a confirmed drug-dependent antibody causing hemolysis.
option_explanations.E: Nothing in the vignette indicates a prior transfusion or alloimmunization; the workup specifically identified a drug-dependent antibody, not an alloantibody to a foreign red cell antigen.

questions.teaching_point: Hapten-type DIIHA (e.g., cefotetan, penicillin) involves firm drug-membrane binding with IgG-mediated extravascular hemolysis, distinct from the immune-complex type (e.g., ceftriaxone, quinine), which involves loose drug-membrane association with complement-mediated acute intravascular hemolysis and renal risk.

source_references.locator: Node 23 - Clinical Presentations and High-Yield Comparison Points
source_references.supports: Classifies cefotetan/penicillin as hapten-type DIIHA (firm drug-membrane binding, IgG-mediated extravascular hemolysis) versus ceftriaxone/quinine as immune-complex type (loose binding, intravascular hemolysis, renal risk); notes cefotetan can trigger anemia 7-10 days after a single prophylactic surgical dose such as C-section.

review.notes: All asserted facts are grounded in SRC-040 Node 23: hapten-type (firm drug-membrane binding, IgG-mediated extravascular hemolysis, cefotetan/penicillin exemplars) vs immune-complex type (loose binding, complement, acute intravascular hemolysis with renal risk, ceftriaxone/quinine) vs drug-independent autoantibody (reacts without drug present, e.g. methyldopa) vs NIPA (nonspecific plasma protein adsorption, no true antibody). The '7-10 days after a single prophylactic surgical dose (e.g., C-section)... without knowing they received the drug' detail matches the source almost verbatim, and 8 days

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A patient recovering from cesarean section, who received prophylactic cefotetan intraoperatively, is being evaluated for new-onset anemia with a positive direct antiglobulin test (DAT). Results: DAT is positive for IgG and negative for C3d. The antibody screen using standard (untreated) reagent red cells is nonreactive, and an eluate prepared from the patient's own red cells is also nonreactive against untreated reagent red cells. However, both the serum and the eluate react strongly when tested against red cells that have been pretreated (coated) with the drug.

options.text: Alloantibody from prior transfusion sensitization
Immune complex-type drug-dependent antibody pattern
Nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA) pattern
Drug-independent (autoantibody) pattern
Hapten-type drug-dependent antibody pattern

questions.explanation_correct: This result set matches the hapten pattern described in the source: DAT positive for IgG (not complement), with serum and eluate nonreactive against untreated reagent cells but reactive specifically against drug-treated (drug-coated) cells. This reflects firm binding of the drug to the red cell membrane, requiring the drug to be pre-coated onto the test cells to demonstrate reactivity -- consistent with a cefotetan hapten-type mechanism.

option_explanations.E: Correct. IgG-only DAT with serum/eluate reactive only against drug-treated (not untreated) cells is the defined hapten pattern in the source.
option_explanations.B: The immune-complex pattern requires a DAT positive for complement (C3) only, with serum reacting against untreated red cells only when the drug is added directly to the test system -- not matching this IgG-positive, drug-coated-cell-dependent result.
option_explanations.D: The drug-independent (autoantibody) pattern is serologically identical to warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, with serum and eluate panreactive against normal red cells even without the drug present -- inconsistent with this drug-treated-cell-restricted reactivity.
option_explanations.C: NIPA produces a DAT positive for multiple plasma proteins with both serum and eluate negative, reflecting no true antibody specificity -- inconsistent with the strong, drug-specific reactivity seen here.
option_explanations.A: An alloantibody from prior transfusion would typically react with untreated antigen-positive reagent cells rather than requiring the cells to be coated with the drug, and no transfusion history is given.

questions.teaching_point: Differentiate the four DIIHA serologic patterns by DAT specificity (IgG vs C3d vs multiple proteins) and by whether serum/eluate reactivity requires drug-coated cells, drug added in solution, no drug at all, or is entirely absent (NIPA).

source_references.locator: Node 23 - Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: Describes the four DIIHA serologic patterns (hapten, immune complex, drug-independent/autoantibody, NIPA) with their characteristic DAT reactivity and serum/eluate behavior against drug-treated versus untreated red cells.

review.notes: Serologic pattern facts are all directly supported by SRC-040 Node 23 Laboratory Findings: hapten pattern = DAT IgG-positive, serum/eluate reactive only with drug-treated cells; immune-complex pattern = DAT C3-only, serum reactive with untreated cells only when drug added to the test system; autoantibody pattern = panreactive without drug; NIPA = DAT positive for multiple proteins, serum/eluate both negative. Option A is the clear single best answer and matches the key. Same two concerns as question_index 0: this is fundamentally a DIIHA serology-interpretation item (Node 23 content) rather th

questions.domain: 4. Anemia and Red Blood Cell Transfusion
questions.stem: A competent adult who is a Jehovah's Witness requires elective major surgery with anticipated moderate blood loss and has clearly refused any allogeneic blood transfusion, including in the event of intraoperative bleeding. The surgical and blood bank teams are planning her perioperative care.

options.text: Postpone the surgery indefinitely and take no active steps until her hemoglobin normalizes on its own
Optimize preoperative hemoglobin with erythropoietin and iron, plan for intraoperative cell salvage (Cell Saver), and ensure meticulous surgical hemostasis
Proceed with routine type and crossmatch and transfuse allogeneic RBCs intraoperatively whenever hemoglobin falls below the usual institutional threshold
Arrange for emergency consent to transfuse allogeneic blood intraoperatively if massive hemorrhage occurs, overriding her prior refusal
Administer a hemoglobin-based oxygen carrier (HBOC) electively before surgery as routine prophylaxis against anticipated blood loss

questions.explanation_correct: The source frames management of transfusion refusal around the three pillars of Patient Blood Management -- optimizing hemoglobin, minimizing blood loss, and improving anemia tolerance -- operationalized through 'bloodless medicine' tools: erythropoietin, high-dose iron, intraoperative cell salvage (Cell Saver), and meticulous surgical hemostasis. This approach respects the patient's legal right to informed refusal while actively reducing her need for allogeneic blood.

option_explanations.B: Correct. This combination directly implements the three-pillar PBM framework and the specific bloodless medicine tools (EPO, iron, Cell Saver, hemostasis) described in the source for managing patients who refuse transfusion.
option_explanations.E: HBOCs are described as synthetic oxygen carriers available primarily through clinical trials or compassionate/expanded access, not as a routine elective prophylactic measure before every such surgery.
option_explanations.D: The source states competent adults have the legal right to refuse transfusion based on religious or personal beliefs; arranging to override a clear, informed refusal is inconsistent with this principle.
option_explanations.C: Proceeding with routine crossmatch-and-transfuse protocol disregards the patient's explicit refusal and does not reflect the bloodless medicine approach the source describes for such patients.
option_explanations.A: Indefinite postponement without active intervention is not the source's described strategy; the three pillars call for actively optimizing hemoglobin and minimizing blood loss rather than passive delay.

questions.teaching_point: For surgical patients who refuse allogeneic transfusion, apply the Patient Blood Management three-pillar framework and bloodless medicine strategies (erythropoietin, iron, intraoperative cell salvage, meticulous hemostasis) rather than defaulting to standard crossmatch-and-transfuse protocols or overriding a competent adult's informed refusal.

source_references.locator: Node 54 - Core Concepts and Therapeutic Alternatives
source_references.supports: Describes management of transfusion refusal via the Patient Blood Management three pillars (optimize hemoglobin, minimize blood loss, improve anemia tolerance) using bloodless medicine tools (EPO, iron, Cell Saver, hemostasis), notes informed refusal is a legal right for competent adults, and states HBOCs are currently available mainly through trials or compassionate use.

review.notes: Fully grounded in SRC-012 Node 54 (Management of Transfusion Refusal): informed refusal is a legal right for competent adults (religious/personal beliefs), management follows the PBM 'Three Pillars' (optimize hemoglobin, minimize blood loss, improve anemia tolerance) via bloodless medicine tools (EPO, high-dose iron, Cell Saver/intraoperative salvage, meticulous surgical hemostasis), and HBOCs are currently available mainly through clinical trials/compassionate use rather than as routine elective prophylaxis. All distractor rationales (B-E) are accurately characterized against the source and a

questions.stem: A 29-year-old healthy repeat donor is midway through an automated plateletpheresis procedure on a centrifugal cell separator using ACD-A anticoagulant. He develops circumoral and fingertip paresthesias with a mild metallic taste; his vital signs remain stable and he is not lightheaded. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of these symptoms?

options.text: Citrate anticoagulant returned to the donor chelates ionized calcium, producing acute hypocalcemia
Rapid extracorporeal blood volume shifts trigger a vasovagal reflex with peripheral vasodilation
Turbulence within the centrifuge bowl causes mechanical shearing and hemolysis of the donor's red cells
An IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to the plastic apheresis tubing set
Potassium released from platelets damaged during centrifugation produces transient hyperkalemia

questions.explanation_correct: The approved curriculum lists citrate toxicity presenting as hypocalcemia among the common adverse effects of apheresis. Citrate anticoagulant is infused into the donor throughout the collection to prevent clotting in the extracorporeal circuit; when it is returned faster than it can be metabolized (chiefly hepatically), it chelates ionized calcium, lowering free serum calcium and producing the classic circumoral/acral paresthesias and metallic taste described in the vignette.

option_explanations.A: Correct: matches the source's statement that citrate toxicity/hypocalcemia is a common apheresis adverse effect.
option_explanations.C: Mechanical hemolysis is not described as a common apheresis adverse effect in the source, and hemolysis does not classically produce circumoral/acral tingling.
option_explanations.B: Vasovagal reactions classically present with lightheadedness, diaphoresis, and hypotension rather than isolated perioral/acral paresthesias; the source attributes this symptom pattern specifically to citrate-induced hypocalcemia.
option_explanations.E: Platelets contain comparatively little intracellular potassium, and hyperkalemia is not listed in the source as an apheresis donor adverse effect.
option_explanations.D: No hypersensitivity reaction to apheresis set components is described in the source; the described symptom complex is the recognized presentation of citrate toxicity.

questions.teaching_point: Citrate returned to a donor during apheresis chelates ionized calcium; circumoral/acral paresthesias and a metallic taste are the hallmark early symptoms of citrate-induced hypocalcemia.

source_references.locator: Domain IV: Apheresis - High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that citrate toxicity, presenting as hypocalcemia, is a common adverse effect of apheresis procedures.

review.notes: Core mechanism is well-grounded: SRC-010 curriculum line 61 states verbatim 'Common adverse effects include citrate toxicity, presenting as hypocalcemia' under Domain IV: Apheresis - High-Yield Facts, exactly as cited. However, the specific symptom complex asserted in the stem/explanation (circumoral and fingertip paresthesias, metallic taste, and the explanation's claim that these are 'the classic circumoral/acral paresthesias and metallic taste described in the vignette') is NOT stated anywhere in any of the four approved sources -- I grepped all four files for citrate/hypocalcemia/paresthes

questions.stem: A patient with acute myeloid leukemia and a platelet count of 8 x 10^9/L receives a pooled whole blood-derived platelet concentrate. The corrected count increment (CCI), which incorporates the patient's body surface area and the number of platelets transfused, remains persistently low after this and prior transfusions. An antibody screen demonstrates broadly reactive antibodies against HLA class I antigens. Which apheresis-derived product is most appropriate to improve this patient's future platelet increments?

options.text: Single-donor platelets collected by apheresis, selected for HLA compatibility with the patient
Fresh frozen plasma collected by apheresis (plasmapheresis)
Additional pooled whole blood-derived platelet concentrates from random, unselected donors
Packed red blood cells collected by conventional whole blood donation
A granulocyte concentrate collected by apheresis

questions.explanation_correct: Platelets carry HLA Class I antigens in addition to ABO and HPA antigens, and HLA Class I antigens are broadly expressed on nearly all nucleated cells (and platelets). A persistently low CCI despite adequate platelet dosing indicates immune (antibody-mediated) refractoriness, and the source identifies molecular/serologic genotyping as the tool used to find a compatible donor once such an antibody is identified. Because random, unselected donors will generally share the same broadly reactive HLA class I antigens, the appropriate product is single-donor apheresis platelets selected for HLA compatibility.

option_explanations.A: Correct: apheresis allows collection of a single-donor product that can be matched to a specific donor for HLA compatibility, addressing the identified HLA antibody.
option_explanations.C: Random, unselected whole blood-derived platelet donors are unlikely to lack the broadly reactive HLA class I antigens targeted by the patient's antibodies, so this would be expected to fail again.
option_explanations.B: Plasma contains no platelets and cannot correct platelet refractoriness.
option_explanations.E: Granulocyte concentrates are collected for neutropenic infectious complications, not to correct platelet increments.
option_explanations.D: Whole blood-derived red cells address anemia, not thrombocytopenia, and are not an apheresis product, making them irrelevant to this scenario.

questions.teaching_point: A persistently low CCI with a positive HLA class I antibody screen points to immune platelet refractoriness, managed with HLA-matched/compatible single-donor apheresis platelets rather than random pooled product.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 - Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping
source_references.supports: Platelets carry ABO, HLA Class I, and HPA antigens; genotyping is used to find compatible donors when an antibody is identified.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 65 - Intravascular Cell Kinetics & Expected Increments
source_references.supports: Corrected count increment (CCI), incorporating body surface area and platelets transfused, is used to evaluate platelet transfusion response.

source_references.locator: Domain II: Cell and Tissue Therapy - High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: HLA Class I antigens (HLA-A, -B, -C) are found on nearly all nucleated cells, underlying HLA-mediated refractoriness logic.

review.notes: Factual content is broadly accurate but the source citations are mislabeled. Two of the three source_references are attributed to SRC-011 (blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-board-review-nodes.md), but I read that entire file (97 lines) and it contains ONLY Subdomain Nodes 66-70 (neurologic apheresis, rheumatic/renal apheresis, RCA, IECs/cell therapy, therapeutic cytapheresis/parasite deferrals) -- there is no 'Node 62' or 'Node 65' in SRC-011 at all. The cited content ('Platelets carry ABO, HLA Class I, and HPA antigens'; genotyping utility; CCI/body-surface-area definition) actually appe

questions.stem: A regular apheresis platelet donor presents for a scheduled donation. During screening she reports that she was treated for malaria 18 months ago while living in an endemic country and has been asymptomatic ever since completing therapy; she otherwise meets all donation criteria. What is the most appropriate donor management decision?

options.text: Permanently defer her from all future blood and apheresis donation
Defer her for only 6 months since her malaria was treated and resolved
Defer her from apheresis donation; she is not yet eligible until she has been asymptomatic for a total of 3 years
Defer her for a total of 2 years from becoming asymptomatic, the interval used for a reactive Babesia test
Accept her for plateletpheresis today because she is currently asymptomatic

questions.explanation_correct: The source specifies that prospective donors with a history of malaria are deferred for 3 years after becoming asymptomatic. At 18 months asymptomatic, this donor has completed only half of the required interval, so she must continue to be deferred from apheresis platelet donation until the full 3 years has elapsed.

option_explanations.C: Correct: matches the source's stated 3-year deferral interval for a history of malaria, measured from becoming asymptomatic.
option_explanations.D: The 2-year ("at least 2 years") deferral interval in the source applies to a reactive Babesia spp. test result, not to a history of malaria — a classic board trap conflating the two parasite deferral intervals.
option_explanations.E: Current absence of symptoms does not by itself satisfy the required 3-year deferral period after becoming asymptomatic.
option_explanations.A: The source describes a time-limited 3-year deferral for malaria history, not a permanent deferral.
option_explanations.B: Six months is shorter than the 3-year interval specified in the source and would not adequately mitigate residual parasitemia risk.

questions.teaching_point: Malaria history requires a 3-year asymptomatic deferral before donation (including apheresis donation), distinct from the shorter ≥2-year deferral triggered by a reactive Babesia test — a frequently confused pair of intervals.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 70 - Therapeutic Cytapheresis & Emerging Pathogens - Parasite Deferrals
source_references.supports: Donors with a history of malaria are deferred 3 years after becoming asymptomatic; a reactive Babesia spp. test results in a deferral of at least 2 years.

review.notes: Fully and precisely grounded: SRC-011 (review-nodes.md), Subdomain Node 70, lines 91 and 93 state verbatim 'Malaria: Prospective donors with a history of malaria are deferred for 3 years after becoming asymptomatic' and 'Babesiosis: A reactive test for Babesia spp. results in a deferral of at least 2 years' -- exactly matching the stem, key, and the option-B distractor trap (conflating the 2-year Babesia interval with malaria). At 18 months asymptomatic, the donor has not completed the required 3-year interval, so A is unambiguously correct and no other option is defensible. Minor blueprint co

questions.subdomain: Adverse Effects on Donors and Recipients
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) undergoes emergent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) using fresh frozen plasma (FFP) as the replacement fluid. Roughly two hours into the exchange, she develops acute dyspnea, hypoxemia, and new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, without clinical evidence of volume overload. Which mechanism best explains this reaction?

options.text: A "two-hit" process in which her critical illness primes pulmonary neutrophils and biologic response modifiers (e.g., leukocyte antibodies) carried in the transfused donor plasma trigger capillary leak
