review.notes: All facts verified verbatim in SRC-004 'Subdomain Node 43 – HNA & Clinical Syndromes' (HNA-3a/CTL2 -> most severe/fatal TRALI; HNA-1/FcγRIIIb -> most common cause of NAN) and distractor facts confirmed in SRC-004 Node 42 (PNH/GPI/CD55/CD59) and SRC-028 (HLA Class II restricted to APCs) and SRC-032 (AHG/Coombs' principle). Key B is unambiguously the single best answer. Concerns: (1) the entire source basis for HNA-TRALI content is exactly two sentences in SRC-004, a thin anchor for a question with five detailed option rationales; (2) the blueprint node is 'Pathophysiology > Mechanisms of Lung D

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: During investigation of two separate transfusion-associated adverse events, the antibody identification laboratory reports the following: a term neonate with unexplained neutropenia has maternal serum containing an antibody reactive with paternal neutrophils, typed as anti-HNA-1a; separately, a plasma donor implicated in a fatal case of acute hypoxemic respiratory failure in an adult recipient has serum containing an antibody typed as anti-HNA-3a. Which of the following correctly interprets these two serologic findings?

options.text: Anti-HNA-3a targets the Fcγ receptor IIIb and causes NAN, while anti-HNA-1a targets CTL2 and causes the most severe TRALI reactions
Both antibodies reflect maternal or donor sensitization to HLA class I antigens expressed on platelets and most nucleated cells

Anti-HNA-1a targets the Fcγ receptor IIIb and is the most common antibody specificity implicated in neonatal alloimmune neutropenia (NAN), whereas anti-HNA-3a targets the choline transporter-like protein 2 (CTL2) and is associated with the most severe and fatal TRALI reactions
Anti-HNA-1a and anti-HNA-3a are two allelic variants within the same single antigen system and carry identical clinical significance
The identification of either antibody reflects a single antigen system encompassing all ten described neutrophil-specific antigens

questions.explanation_correct: The sources classify neutrophil-specific antigens into five distinct systems (HNA-1 through HNA-5). Within this nomenclature, HNA-1 is located on the Fcγ receptor IIIb and antibodies to HNA-1a/1b are the most common cause of NAN, while HNA-3a is located on CTL2 and its antibodies are associated with the most severe and fatal TRALI reactions — two distinct antigen-antibody pairs with distinct clinical associations, not interchangeable findings.

option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the source states there are five separate HNA systems (HNA-1 through HNA-5), not one system with allelic variants, and the two antibodies have distinct clinical associations (NAN vs. severe TRALI).
option_explanations.C: Correct: matches the source's explicit pairing of HNA-1/FcγRIIIb with NAN and HNA-3a/CTL2 with the most severe and fatal TRALI reactions.
option_explanations.A: Reverses the correct antigen-location and clinical-association pairings given in the source.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source describes HNA (neutrophil-specific) antigens for these two findings, not HLA class I antigens, which are a separate system located on most nucleated cells and platelets.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the source specifies ten neutrophil-specific antigens divided across five distinct systems (HNA-1 through HNA-5), not a single unified system.

questions.teaching_point: Correctly interpreting HNA antibody identification requires matching the specific antigen system to its clinical syndrome: HNA-1/FcγRIIIb antibodies are the leading cause of NAN, while HNA-3a/CTL2 antibodies are specifically tied to the most severe, fatal TRALI reactions — the two are not interchangeable despite both belonging to the broader HNA nomenclature.

source_references.locator: advanced-immunohematology-and-transfusion-medicine-review.md, Subdomain Node 43 – HNA & Clinical Syndromes
source_references.supports: States that ten neutrophil-specific antigens are classified into five systems (HNA-1 through HNA-5), that HNA-1 (FcγRIIIb) antibodies are the most common cause of NAN, and that HNA-3a (CTL2) antibodies are associated with the most severe and fatal TRALI reactions.

source_references.locator: domain-4-advanced-technologies-specialized-testing-study-guide.md, Section 1 MHC Organization table
source_references.supports: Describes HLA Class I (HLA-A/B/C) as expressed on most nucleated cells and platelets, used to build a plausible-but-wrong distractor distinguishing HLA from HNA systems.

review.notes: Facts verified: SRC-004 states 10 neutrophil antigens in 5 systems (HNA-1 to HNA-5), HNA-1/FcγRIIIb -> most common cause of NAN, HNA-3a/CTL2 -> most severe/fatal TRALI; SRC-028 confirms HLA Class I distribution used in distractor D. Key B is the single best answer; options A/C/D/E are all correctly excluded by source. Concerns: (1) this question and Q1 draw on the identical two-sentence source passage and test the same underlying discrimination (HNA-1/NAN vs HNA-3a/TRALI), just reframed as a side-by-side comparison rather than a single vignette - substantial duplicate coverage within this 3-qu

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 34-year-old man is undergoing massive transfusion for hemorrhagic shock after a motor vehicle collision, receiving RBCs, plasma, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio through a rapid infuser. After his fourth unit of plasma, he develops perioral tingling, hand tremors progressing to muscle twitching, and the cardiac monitor shows new prolongation of the QT interval. Which of the following mechanisms is the most likely explanation for this patient's findings?

options.text: Citrate anticoagulant in the plasma and platelet units is chelating the patient's ionized calcium
Replacement of blood loss with RBCs and crystalloid alone is diluting clotting factors and platelets
Citrate from the anticoagulated units is being metabolized by the liver into bicarbonate
Potassium released from red cells during cold storage is producing hyperkalemia
Rapid infusion of refrigerated components is lowering core body temperature and impairing clotting enzyme activity

questions.explanation_correct: Plasma and platelet units carry roughly five times more citrate anticoagulant than RBC units. During rapid, high-volume infusion of plasma/platelets in massive transfusion, this citrate load chelates ionized calcium faster than it can be mobilized, producing hypocalcemia. The classic clinical picture is perioral paresthesias and tremor/muscle twitching progressing toward tetany, with a prolonged QT interval and cardiac arrhythmia on monitoring — exactly what this patient shows.

option_explanations.A: Correct: citrate-driven ionized-calcium chelation is the source-defined mechanism for perioral paresthesias, tremor/tetany, and QT prolongation during rapid plasma/platelet infusion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: potassium leaking from stored RBCs causes hyperkalemia with peaked T waves and risk of cardiac arrest, not perioral tingling with QT prolongation; it is a distinct metabolic complication of massive transfusion.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: hypothermia from cold component infusion causes arrhythmias and worsens coagulopathy by impairing platelet function and clotting-enzyme activity, but it does not produce perioral paresthesias or tetany, which specifically reflect calcium chelation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: dilutional coagulopathy from RBC/crystalloid-only replacement presents as bleeding/oozing from depleted factors and platelets, not paresthesias or tetany.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: citrate is eventually metabolized hepatically to bicarbonate, which can later contribute to metabolic alkalosis, but this delayed process is not what produces the acute hypocalcemic symptoms described; those arise from citrate's direct calcium-chelating effect before metabolism.

questions.teaching_point: Citrate toxicity during massive transfusion is driven by plasma/platelet citrate load chelating ionized calcium; its symptom complex (paresthesias, tetany, QT prolongation) is mechanistically distinct from hyperkalemia (K+ leak) and hypothermia (enzymatic/platelet impairment).

source_references.supports: States plasma/platelets contain ~5x more citrate than RBCs and that rapid infusion causes hypocalcemia with perioral paresthesias, tetany, and arrhythmias; also describes hyperkalemia and hypothermia as distinct MTP metabolic complications.

Defines citrate toxicity as calcium chelation with perioral paresthesias, shivering, muscle twitching, tetany/arrhythmias, and a prolonged QT interval with low ionized calcium on ECG/labs; also notes hyperkalemia's peaked T waves and hypothermia's effect on platelet function and clotting-enzyme activity, and the delayed citrate-to-bicarbonate metabolic alkalosis.

review.notes: Core mechanism (citrate 5x higher in plasma/platelets, chelates ionized Ca, causing perioral paresthesias/tetany/arrhythmia and prolonged QT with low ionized Ca) is directly stated in SRC-032 and SRC-060. Distractors B (hyperkalemia: K+ leak from stored RBCs, peaked T waves, cardiac arrest risk in neonates/renal failure), C (hypothermia: arrhythmia + impaired platelet/enzyme function), D (dilutional coagulopathy: RBC/crystalloid-only replacement depletes factors/platelets), and E (citrate->bicarbonate->delayed metabolic alkalosis) all match source text. Minor unsupported embellishment: stem/ex

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is receiving a massive transfusion protocol for postpartum hemorrhage and has diffuse oozing from her IV sites and surgical field. Current labs return: platelet count 62,000/µL, INR 1.3, aPTT 45 seconds, fibrinogen 85 mg/dL, and ionized calcium 1.1 mmol/L. Based on these results, which blood component is most immediately indicated?

options.text: Platelet concentrate
Additional RBC units alone
Fresh frozen plasma
Intravenous calcium gluconate
Cryoprecipitate

questions.explanation_correct: During massive transfusion, cryoprecipitate replacement is indicated once fibrinogen falls below 100 mg/dL. Her fibrinogen of 85 mg/dL is the only value in this panel that crosses a defined laboratory trigger for component replacement — her platelet count, INR, aPTT, and ionized calcium all remain within the source-defined acceptable ranges — making cryoprecipitate the most immediately indicated product for her ongoing oozing.

option_explanations.E: Correct: fibrinogen 85 mg/dL is below the <100 mg/dL trigger for cryoprecipitate replacement during massive transfusion.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the platelet trigger during massive transfusion is <50,000/µL; at 62,000/µL she does not meet this threshold.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: plasma is indicated when INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds; her INR of 1.3 and aPTT of 45 seconds are both below these thresholds.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the monitoring target for ionized calcium during MTP is to keep it above 1.0 mmol/L; at 1.1 mmol/L she is within target, so empiric calcium is not the most immediate need.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: additional RBCs restore oxygen-carrying capacity and volume but do not correct the hypofibrinogenemia that is most directly implicated in her ongoing oozing.

questions.teaching_point: Component replacement during massive transfusion should be driven by defined laboratory triggers (platelets <50,000/µL, INR >1.5 or aPTT >60s for plasma, fibrinogen <100 mg/dL for cryoprecipitate, ionized calcium >1.0 mmol/L) rather than reflexively transfusing every product.

source_references.supports: Provides the specific laboratory triggers used during MTP: platelets <50,000/µL, plasma if INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, and an ionized calcium monitoring target of >1.0 mmol/L.

review.notes: All four lab triggers used in the rationales are explicitly given in SRC-032 (Node 16): platelets <50,000/µL, plasma if INR >1.5 or aPTT >60s, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, ionized calcium target >1.0 mmol/L. Patient values (platelets 62k, INR 1.3, aPTT 45, ionized Ca 1.1) all fall on the 'acceptable' side of each threshold except fibrinogen (85 <100), making cryoprecipitate the only source-justified immediate need. SRC-044 independently corroborates the fibrinogen <100 mg/dL cryoprecipitate trigger (and even notes a higher pregnancy/massive-hemorrhage target of 150-200 mg/dL, whic

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A neonate requires a large-volume, rapid RBC transfusion for hemorrhagic shock. The transfusion medicine physician is asked to select the RBC product that minimizes the risk of transfusion-associated hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest in this setting. Which of the following is the most appropriate product selection?

options.text: RBC units that are less than 7 days old, or washed RBC units
Washed RBC units that were washed 4 days earlier and held since
Any in-date RBC unit, rapidly warmed in a microwave to shorten equilibration time before infusion
Standard-issue RBC units nearing the end of the 42-day storage period, to conserve younger inventory
No RBC modification; instead give intravenous calcium gluconate before the transfusion

questions.explanation_correct: During RBC storage, extracellular potassium accumulates in the unit supernatant (the storage lesion). In neonates receiving large-volume, rapid transfusion, infusing this potassium-rich supernatant can precipitate life-threatening hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest. The source-directed approach is to select fresh RBC units (<7 days old) or washed RBC units, both of which minimize the potassium load delivered to the neonate.

option_explanations.A: Correct: fresh (<7 day) or washed RBC units are the specific product selections recommended to minimize potassium load in large-volume neonatal transfusion.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: microwaving or using any unapproved warming device is a recognized board trap that causes hemolysis; only validated, FDA-cleared blood warmers are acceptable, and this does nothing to address the potassium load.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: washing removes extracellular potassium but triggers a new leakage cycle, so washed units must be used within 24 hours; a unit washed 4 days earlier would have re-accumulated potassium and lost its protective benefit.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: units nearing the end of the 42-day storage period have undergone the most prolonged storage lesion and therefore carry the highest supernatant potassium concentration, increasing rather than decreasing hyperkalemia risk.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: calcium gluconate treats citrate-related hypocalcemia, not hyperkalemia; it does not reduce the potassium load from stored RBCs and is not a substitute for appropriate product selection.

questions.teaching_point: Neonatal large-volume RBC transfusion carries a specific hyperkalemia/cardiac arrest risk from the RBC storage lesion; mitigation requires selecting fresh (<7 day) or washed RBC units, and washed units must be transfused within 24 hours since washing only transiently removes accumulated potassium.

source_references.supports: States that RBC storage causes potassium accumulation in the supernatant (storage lesion), recommends fresh (<7 day) or washed RBCs to minimize potassium load in large-volume neonatal transfusion, notes washed units must be used within 24 hours because washing triggers a new leakage cycle, and warns against microwave/unapproved warming devices; also identifies IV calcium as the treatment for citra

review.notes: SRC-060 (Node 34) directly states: 'Use fresh RBCs (<7 days old) or washed RBCs for large-volume neonatal transfusions to minimize potassium load' — matches option A verbatim. Distractor B (microwave/unapproved warming causes hemolysis; only FDA-cleared warmers acceptable) and the washing 24-hour re-leakage rule underlying option C are both explicitly stated in the same source. Option D's reasoning (older units = more prolonged storage lesion = higher supernatant K+) is a reasonable direct inference from the source's storage-lesion definition ('storage lesion results in increased plasma potass

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with hemorrhagic shock from blunt trauma is being resuscitated per a massive transfusion protocol with RBCs, plasma, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio. About 30 minutes into rapid infusion of plasma and platelets, she develops perioral tingling, numbness of the fingertips, and a new dysrhythmia on the cardiac monitor. A stat ionized calcium level returns markedly low. Which of the following mechanisms most directly accounts for this presentation?

options.text: Potassium released from stored red cells during refrigeration produces cardiac membrane instability
Donor leukocyte antibodies bind recipient neutrophils, triggering pulmonary capillary leak
Citrate anticoagulant contained in the transfused plasma and platelet units chelates the patient's ionized calcium
Rapid infusion of refrigerated components cools the myocardium and impairs coagulation enzyme activity
Preformed recipient IgE cross-links donor plasma proteins, releasing histamine from mast cells

questions.explanation_correct: Citrate is the anticoagulant used to prevent clotting in collected blood components, and it works by binding (chelating) ionized calcium. Plasma and platelet components contain roughly five times more citrate than RBC units, so rapid infusion of plasma/platelets during a massive transfusion protocol delivers a large citrate load that can overwhelm the patient's capacity to clear it, producing symptomatic hypocalcemia manifesting as perioral paresthesias, tingling, tetany, or arrhythmias.

option_explanations.C: Correct: citrate chelation of ionized calcium is the mechanism of citrate toxicity, and the higher citrate content of plasma/platelets versus RBCs explains why rapid infusion of these components is the trigger.
option_explanations.A: This describes the mechanism of hyperkalemia, a separate metabolic complication of massive transfusion from potassium leak during RBC storage; it produces arrhythmia through hyperkalemia, not calcium chelation, and would show an elevated (not reduced) relevant electrolyte.
option_explanations.D: This describes hypothermia-related coagulopathy from infusing refrigerated components, a distinct massive-transfusion complication that does not cause hypocalcemia via chelation.
option_explanations.B: This describes the mechanism of TRALI, an immune-mediated acute lung injury unrelated to citrate or calcium chelation.
option_explanations.E: This describes an IgE-mediated allergic/anaphylactoid reaction mechanism, unrelated to citrate chelation of calcium.

questions.teaching_point: Citrate toxicity during rapid transfusion results from citrate chelating ionized calcium; the risk is greatest with plasma and platelet components, which carry roughly 5x more citrate than RBC units.

source_references.locator: Massive Transfusion Protocols - Clinical Presentations (Metabolic Complications)
source_references.supports: States that rapid infusion of anticoagulated components causes citrate toxicity/hypocalcemia (perioral paresthesias, tetany, arrhythmias), and that plasma/platelets contain about 5x more citrate than RBCs; also describes hyperkalemia and hypothermia as separate MTP metabolic complications used as distractors.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: Confirms citrate toxicity presents with perioral paresthesias, tingling, and (in severe cases) tetany or arrhythmias, and that citrate chelates ionized calcium.

review.notes: Core claim is strongly sourced: SRC-032 states 'Citrate Toxicity: Rapid infusion of anticoagulated components (plasma/platelets contain 5x more citrate than RBCs) can lead to hypocalcemia (perioral paresthesias, tetany, arrhythmias)' and SRC-023 confirms citrate chelates ionized calcium and the paresthesia/tetany/arrhythmia presentation. Distractor B (hyperkalemia) and C (hypothermia) mechanisms are directly sourced verbatim in SRC-032 lines 23 and 25. However, distractor D's TRALI mechanism ('donor leukocyte antibodies bind recipient neutrophils, triggering pulmonary capillary leak') and dist

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A patient is undergoing a massive transfusion protocol for traumatic hemorrhage, receiving RBCs, plasma, and platelets simultaneously at a rapid rate. Serial point-of-care monitoring shows: ionized calcium 0.8 mmol/L (low), serum potassium 4.3 mEq/L (normal), and venous blood gas pH 7.49 with bicarbonate 27 mEq/L (mildly elevated). Which of the following best explains this combination of laboratory findings?

options.text: Dilutional coagulopathy from crystalloid administration diluting clotting factors
Hypothermic impairment of enzymatic coagulation activity from infusing refrigerated components
Citrate toxicity from the anticoagulant contained in the transfused plasma and platelet components
An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from ABO-incompatible red cells
Hyperkalemia from potassium released by red cells during refrigerated storage

questions.explanation_correct: Citrate anticoagulant in transfused components chelates ionized calcium, producing hypocalcemia, and citrate is also metabolized to bicarbonate, which can produce a mild metabolic alkalosis (elevated pH and bicarbonate). This combined pattern of low ionized calcium plus mild alkalosis is characteristic of citrate toxicity from rapid infusion of citrate-anticoagulated components (particularly plasma and platelets, which carry the highest citrate load).

option_explanations.C: Correct: citrate chelates ionized calcium (explaining the hypocalcemia) and is metabolized to bicarbonate (explaining the mild alkalosis), together fitting the described laboratory pattern.
option_explanations.E: Hyperkalemia would show an elevated serum potassium; the potassium here is normal (4.3 mEq/L), arguing against this as the cause and making it a classic board trap for massive-transfusion metabolic complications.
option_explanations.A: Dilutional coagulopathy reflects depletion of coagulation factors and platelets from crystalloid/RBC-only resuscitation; it does not account for the observed calcium or acid-base abnormalities.
option_explanations.B: Hypothermia from rapidly infused refrigerated components impairs enzymatic clotting activity and can worsen coagulopathy or precipitate arrhythmia, but it does not produce this hypocalcemia/alkalosis laboratory pattern.
option_explanations.D: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction would be expected to show evidence of hemolysis (e.g., visible hemolysis, DIC-type picture) rather than an isolated hypocalcemia with mild metabolic alkalosis.

questions.teaching_point: Citrate toxicity classically produces both a low ionized calcium (from chelation) and a mild metabolic alkalosis (from citrate's conversion to bicarbonate) — a combined lab pattern that helps distinguish it from hyperkalemia, which requires an elevated potassium to diagnose.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings section (Hypocalcemia; Metabolic Alkalosis)
source_references.supports: States that citrate chelates ionized calcium requiring monitoring/replacement, and that metabolism of citrate to bicarbonate can cause mild metabolic alkalosis.

source_references.locator: Massive Transfusion Protocols - Clinical Presentations (Metabolic Complications)
source_references.supports: Describes hyperkalemia as a distinct massive-transfusion metabolic complication from potassium release during RBC storage, used here to justify excluding it when potassium is normal.

review.notes: Well grounded overall: SRC-023 directly supports both halves of the key finding - 'Hypocalcemia: Citrate chelates ionized calcium' and 'Metabolic Alkalosis: Metabolism of citrate to bicarbonate can lead to mild alkalosis' - which together justify picking A. The potassium-normal detail correctly excludes hyperkalemia per SRC-032/SRC-043. Two minor issues: (1) distractor E's rationale adds 'DIC-type picture' for AHTR, which is not stated in the sources (SRC-043 only says 'hallmark is visual hemolysis'); (2) the specific numeric lab values in the stem (ionized Ca 0.8 mmol/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, pH 7.49,

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: During a massive transfusion protocol for postpartum hemorrhage, a patient receiving rapid infusion of RBCs, plasma, and platelets develops perioral paresthesias and a new cardiac dysrhythmia. A stat ionized calcium level is markedly reduced. Besides continuing frequent ionized calcium monitoring for the remainder of the resuscitation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer intravenous calcium replacement
Premedicate with diphenhydramine before continuing the transfusion
Administer intravenous furosemide and restrict further volume
Discontinue the plasma and platelet components and give RBCs alone until she is asymptomatic
Send a repeat crossmatch to evaluate for a hemolytic transfusion reaction

questions.explanation_correct: Hypocalcemia from citrate toxicity necessitates monitoring and possible intravenous (or oral) calcium replacement. In a symptomatic patient with perioral paresthesias and a new arrhythmia in the setting of a confirmed low ionized calcium during massive transfusion, IV calcium replacement is the appropriate step to correct the physiologic derangement and reduce arrhythmia risk, alongside the frequent ionized calcium monitoring already recommended during MTP.

option_explanations.A: Correct: sources state that citrate-mediated hypocalcemia necessitates monitoring and possible IV or oral calcium replacement, which directly treats the symptomatic low ionized calcium and dysrhythmia.
option_explanations.D: Although plasma and platelets carry a higher citrate load than RBCs, simply switching to RBC-only transfusion does not correct the patient's already-symptomatic hypocalcemia and delays needed hemostatic component support during active hemorrhage; it is a plausible but incomplete response to the acute problem.
option_explanations.C: IV furosemide with volume restriction addresses transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO), not citrate-induced hypocalcemia; this patient's presentation (paresthesias, low ionized calcium) is not consistent with volume overload.
option_explanations.E: A repeat crossmatch/hemolytic workup is appropriate for suspected acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, which presents differently and is not supported by this patient's findings.
option_explanations.B: Antihistamine premedication addresses allergic transfusion reactions, not the calcium chelation mechanism responsible for this patient's symptoms.

questions.teaching_point: Symptomatic citrate toxicity (paresthesias, tetany, arrhythmia) with confirmed low ionized calcium during massive transfusion should be treated with IV (or oral) calcium replacement, while ionized calcium continues to be monitored frequently and kept above the target threshold throughout resuscitation.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings - Hypocalcemia
source_references.supports: States that citrate-related hypocalcemia necessitates monitoring and possible IV or oral calcium replacement.

source_references.locator: Massive Transfusion Protocols - Diagnostic and Management Approach
source_references.supports: States that frequent assessment of ionized calcium (maintaining it above 1.0 mmol/L) along with potassium and viscoelastic testing is essential during massive transfusion protocols.

review.notes: Correct answer and its core justification are directly and strongly sourced: SRC-023 states hypocalcemia from citrate 'necessitat[es] monitoring and possible IV or oral replacement,' and SRC-032 states 'frequent assessment of ionized calcium (maintain >1.0 mmol/L)... is essential during MTP,' matching the stem's framing of continued monitoring plus the correct next step. Distractor rationales reference specifics not found in the four sources - furosemide/volume-restriction as TACO treatment, repeat crossmatch as the AHTR workup step, and diphenhydramine premedication for allergic reactions are

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy with chronic granulomatous disease develops an invasive pulmonary mold infection that fails to improve despite several days of maximal antifungal therapy, with an absolute neutrophil count of 80/µL. The transfusion service is asked to provide apheresis-collected granulocyte concentrates from a single donor. Before the unit is issued, it undergoes gamma irradiation rather than leukoreduction. Which of the following best explains the rationale for this specific processing step?

options.text: Irradiation reduces the residual leukocyte count below 5×10⁶ per unit, the threshold required to prevent febrile non-hemolytic reactions, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission
Irradiation destroys donor IgA in the plasma component of the granulocyte concentrate, preventing an anaphylactic reaction in an IgA-deficient recipient

Irradiation inactivates the large number of viable donor T lymphocytes present in the granulocyte concentrate, preventing transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease, whereas leukoreduction would remove the therapeutic granulocytes themselves
Irradiation inactivates donor anti-HLA and anti-HNA antibodies that would otherwise cause transfusion-related acute lung injury in the recipient
Irradiation is required because, like hematopoietic progenitor cell products, granulocyte concentrates must never be leukoreduced in order to preserve engraftment potential

questions.explanation_correct: Granulocyte concentrates contain significant numbers of viable donor T lymphocytes, so the units must be irradiated before issue specifically to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) by blocking donor T-cell proliferation. Leukoreduction is not used for this component because it would strip out the very granulocytes that constitute the therapeutic dose, defeating the purpose of the transfusion.

option_explanations.C: Correct: irradiation targets the viable donor T-lymphocyte population to prevent TA-GVHD without removing the therapeutic granulocytes, unlike leukoreduction.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the <5×10⁶ WBC/unit threshold and its FNHTR/HLA-sensitization/CMV-prevention benefits are attributes of leukoreduction, a distinct processing step from irradiation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: irradiation has no described role in IgA removal; anaphylactic reactions in IgA-deficient recipients are a separate hazard category unrelated to this processing step.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: irradiation does not inactivate antibodies; TRALI is attributed to donor anti-HLA/HNA antibody reacting with recipient antigen, a different mechanism from TA-GVHD prevention.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect and reverses the rule: hematopoietic progenitor cell products must never be irradiated or leukoreduced to preserve engraftment, whereas granulocyte concentrates must be irradiated (this is a classic board trap pairing the two cellular products).

questions.teaching_point: Granulocyte concentrates are irradiated (not leukoreduced) before issue: irradiation neutralizes the abundant viable donor T lymphocytes to prevent TA-GVHD, while leukoreduction is deliberately avoided because it would remove the therapeutic granulocytes themselves.

source_references.locator: Granulocyte Irradiation, line 63
source_references.supports: States granulocytes must be irradiated before issue to prevent TA-GVHD because they contain significant numbers of viable donor T lymphocytes.

source_references.locator: Prevention – TA-GVHD, line 65
source_references.supports: Confirms granulocytes must be irradiated because they contain high numbers of viable lymphocytes, under TA-GVHD prevention.

source_references.locator: Domain IV, Blood Component Modification lines 183-189; Transfusion Reactions Classification lines 133-139
source_references.supports: Defines irradiation (prevents T-cell proliferation/TA-GVHD) and leukoreduction (<5×10⁶ WBC/unit; prevents FNHTR, HLA sensitization, CMV) as distinct component-modification steps, and lists TRALI as HLA/HNA-antibody mediated and anaphylaxis as linked to IgA deficiency.

source_references.locator: Prevention – Processing Rules, line 121
source_references.supports: States HPC products should never be irradiated or leukoreduced, used to construct the paired board-trap distractor contrasting HPC vs granulocyte processing rules.

review.notes: Core mechanism is fully grounded: SRC-031 line 63 and SRC-047 line 65 both state granulocytes must be irradiated before issue to prevent TA-GVHD because they contain significant/high numbers of viable donor lymphocytes. Distractors are well grounded: SRC-044 lines 185-189 cleanly separates irradiation (prevents T-cell proliferation/TA-GVHD) from leukoreduction (<5x10^6 WBC/unit; prevents FNHTR/HLA sensitization/CMV) refuting B; no source discusses IgA removal by irradiation, refuting C; TRALI is attributed to donor HLA/HNA antibody (not antibody inactivation by irradiation) per SRC-044 line 13

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia and an absolute neutrophil count of 120/µL is receiving an apheresis-collected granulocyte transfusion for bacteremia unresponsive to 96 hours of appropriate antibiotics. Twenty minutes into the transfusion she develops acute dyspnea and hypoxia; chest imaging shows new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, and her jugular venous pressure and echocardiogram are normal. Post-reaction investigation identifies an antibody in the donor's plasma directed against the patient's HLA class I antigens. Which type of transfusion reaction does this serologic finding support?

options.text: Anaphylactic reaction related to IgA deficiency, an immune reaction independent of donor leukocyte antibodies
Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO), a non-immune reaction caused by excessive transfusion volume
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR), an immune reaction caused by ABO incompatibility
Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction (FNHTR), an immune reaction attributed to cytokines rather than donor antibody against recipient antigen
Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI), an immune-mediated reaction attributed to donor antibody directed against recipient leukocyte antigens

questions.explanation_correct: The source's transfusion reaction classification identifies TRALI as an acute immune reaction specifically attributed to donor HLA/HNA antibody. The demonstrated donor anti-HLA class I antibody matching the recipient's antigens, together with acute hypoxia and bilateral infiltrates with a normal cardiac exam (excluding volume overload), fits this HLA/HNA-antibody-mediated mechanism rather than the other listed reaction categories.

option_explanations.E: Correct: the classification table attributes TRALI to donor HLA/HNA antibody, matching the serologic finding and the respiratory presentation with normal cardiac filling.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: TACO is a non-immune, volume-related reaction; it would not be explained by a donor anti-HLA antibody and is further argued against by the normal JVP/echocardiogram.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: FNHTR is attributed to cytokines, not to donor antibody against recipient leukocyte antigens, and does not correspond to the antibody finding described.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: AHTR is attributed to ABO incompatibility (hemolysis), an entirely different antigen-antibody system than the HLA finding reported here.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: anaphylactic reactions are linked to IgA deficiency, not to donor anti-HLA antibody, and this mechanism is not supported by the serologic finding described.

questions.teaching_point: When a granulocyte transfusion recipient develops acute respiratory distress with bilateral infiltrates and normal cardiac filling, a demonstrated donor anti-HLA/HNA antibody against recipient antigen confirms an immune-mediated TRALI mechanism, distinguishing it serologically from cytokine-mediated FNHTR, ABO-mediated AHTR, IgA-related anaphylaxis, and volume-mediated TACO.

source_references.locator: Domain II, Transfusion Reactions: Classification, lines 133-139
source_references.supports: Provides the acute transfusion reaction classification table pairing AHTR with ABO, FNHTR with cytokines, anaphylaxis with IgA deficiency, TRALI with HLA/HNA antibody (immune), and TACO with volume (non-immune).

review.notes: The determinative fact -- a demonstrated donor anti-HLA/HNA antibody against the recipient maps to TRALI -- is directly grounded in SRC-044's acute reaction classification table (line 137: 'TRALI (HLA/HNA Ab)'), and the distractors are grounded by the same row/table: FNHTR->cytokines, AHTR->ABO, anaphylaxis->IgA deficiency (all line 137), and TACO->volume, listed as Non-Immune (line 139), consistent with using the normal JVP/echo to exclude TACO. No other option is defensible against this table, so independent read agrees with A. One structural note: SRC-044's own table classifies TRALI under

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy with chronic granulomatous disease has a necrotizing pulmonary mold infection that has not improved after several days of maximal antifungal therapy; his absolute neutrophil count remains under 500/µL. The transfusion medicine and infectious disease teams agree that granulocyte transfusion support is appropriate. Which of the following represents the most appropriate transfusion plan for this patient?

options.text: Provide irradiated apheresis granulocyte concentrates daily, targeting at least 1×10¹⁰ granulocytes per transfusion, continued for a minimum of five days or until infection resolves or marrow recovery occurs
Initiate daily therapeutic plasma exchange with albumin replacement instead of granulocyte transfusion to control the infection
Withhold irradiation, since granulocyte concentrates pose no meaningful risk of transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease
Leukoreduce the apheresis granulocyte product before issue to lower the risk of febrile and HLA-alloimmunization complications
Provide a single unirradiated granulocyte transfusion, since granulocytes' short circulating lifespan makes irradiation and repeated dosing unnecessary

questions.explanation_correct: This patient meets the described indication for granulocyte transfusion: severe neutropenia (ANC <500/µL) with a documented infection unresponsive to standard antimicrobial therapy. A therapeutic regimen requires irradiated units (to prevent TA-GVHD from the viable donor lymphocytes they contain) dosed at a minimum of 1×10¹⁰ granulocytes per day for at least five days, continued until infection resolves or marrow recovery occurs.

option_explanations.A: Correct: matches the stated therapeutic dose (≥1×10¹⁰/day) and minimum five-day duration, plus the mandatory irradiation step to prevent TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect on two counts: irradiation is mandatory (not optional) for granulocyte concentrates regardless of circulating lifespan, and the regimen requires daily dosing for at least five days rather than a single transfusion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: leukoreducing a granulocyte concentrate would strip out the therapeutic granulocytes that constitute the entire purpose of the product, contradicting the goal of delivering a ≥1×10¹⁰ granulocyte dose.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the source explicitly states granulocyte concentrates must be irradiated because they contain high numbers of viable donor lymphocytes capable of causing TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: therapeutic plasma exchange is indicated for antibody/toxin-mediated conditions (e.g., TTP, Guillain-Barré syndrome, Goodpasture's syndrome), not as a substitute for cellular granulocyte support in refractory neutropenic infection.

questions.teaching_point: Appropriate granulocyte transfusion support requires: correct candidacy (ANC <500/µL with infection refractory to antimicrobials), irradiated units to prevent TA-GVHD, avoidance of leukoreduction (which would remove the therapeutic cells), and sustained daily dosing (≥1×10¹⁰/day for ≥5 days) rather than a single transfusion.

source_references.locator: Granulocyte Role, line 9; Granulocyte Irradiation, line 63
source_references.supports: Defines granulocyte transfusion candidacy as severely neutropenic patients (ANC <500/µL) with documented infections unresponsive to standard antimicrobial therapy, and states granulocytes must be irradiated before issue.

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls – Granulocyte Dose, line 85; Prevention – TA-GVHD, line 65
source_references.supports: States a therapeutic granulocyte dose requires ≥1.0×10¹⁰ granulocytes/day for at least 5 days, and that granulocytes must be irradiated due to high numbers of viable lymphocytes.

source_references.locator: Therapeutic Apheresis: ASFA Category I Indications, lines 121-131
source_references.supports: Lists ASFA Category I (first-line) indications for therapeutic plasma exchange (TTP, Guillain-Barré, CIDP, Goodpasture's), none of which correspond to refractory neutropenic infection, supporting exclusion of option E.

review.notes: Candidacy criteria (ANC <500/uL with infection unresponsive to standard antimicrobials) is grounded in SRC-031 line 9; the >=1x10^10 granulocytes/day for at least 5 days dose is grounded in SRC-047 line 85; the mandatory-irradiation-to-prevent-TA-GVHD fact is grounded in SRC-031 line 63 and SRC-047 line 65; and the TPE exclusion (option E) is grounded in SRC-044 lines 121-131 (ASFA Category I list: TTP, GBS, CIDP, Goodpasture's -- none matching this indication). However, the correct answer's added qualifier 'continued for a minimum of five days or until infection resolves or marrow recovery oc

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man predonates a unit of his own red blood cells in anticipation of elective hip arthroplasty and receives this autologous RBC unit intraoperatively. Twenty minutes into the transfusion, he develops a temperature rise of 2.5°C above his preinfusion baseline, shaking rigors, tachycardia, and a drop in systolic blood pressure. The transfusion is stopped, and culture of the residual unit (obtained from the bag itself) grows a cold-growing gram-negative organism consistent with Yersinia enterocolitica. Which of the following best explains why this reaction occurred despite the patient receiving his own blood?

options.text: Autologous transfusion reduces the risk of alloimmune-mediated reactions because the donor and recipient are the same person, but it does not eliminate non-immune hazards such as bacterial contamination introduced during collection, processing, or storage
The autologous unit was not leukoreduced, triggering a febrile nonhemolytic reaction from cytokines that accumulated in the stored unit
Donor-derived passenger lymphocytes within the unit produced antibodies against the patient's own red cell antigens, causing hemolysis and fever
The patient generated antibodies against his own red cell antigens during the storage interval between donation and transfusion
Because the unit was autologous, routine pretransfusion compatibility testing was omitted, permitting an ABO-incompatible reaction to occur

questions.explanation_correct: Autologous transfusion uses the patient as both donor and recipient, which removes the alloantigen mismatch that drives immune-mediated reactions. However, autologous units are collected, processed, and stored using the same methods as allogeneic units, so non-immune hazards -- most notably bacterial contamination -- are not eliminated by autologous status. The clinical picture (fever with a rise ≥2°C, rigors, tachycardia, hypotension) together with a cold-growing gram-negative organism on culture matches the described pattern of transfusion-associated bacterial sepsis from an RBC unit, a non-immune process unrelated to donor-recipient antigen matching.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A cytokine-mediated febrile mechanism does not explain the rigors, profound hypotension, and positive bacterial culture described; this constellation matches transfusion-transmitted bacterial sepsis rather than a cytokine-driven febrile reaction.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Passenger lymphocyte syndrome is a recognized complication of allogeneic transplantation, where donor lymphocytes are genetically distinct from the recipient and can make antibody against recipient antigens; it does not apply to autologous transfusion, where donor and recipient are the same person.
option_explanations.A: Correct. Because donor and recipient are identical, autologous transfusion removes the alloantigen mismatch that drives immune-mediated reactions, but collection/processing/storage-related hazards such as bacterial contamination are unaffected by donor identity and can still occur.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Autologous status relaxes donor eligibility criteria and changes unit labeling, not the routine safety practices used to prevent ABO-incompatible transfusion; this option also does not fit a clinical picture of sepsis with a positive unit culture.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. There is no described phenomenon of a patient's own red cells becoming antigenically foreign to him during routine storage; this option is biologically implausible and unsupported by the sources.

questions.teaching_point: Autologous transfusion removes alloimmune-mediated reaction risk (donor = recipient) but does not protect against non-immune hazards like bacterial contamination, which arise from collection, processing, or storage regardless of donor identity.

source_references.locator: Node 7 – Transplantation Immunohematology > High-Yield Comparison Points; Definitions (Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome)
source_references.supports: Autologous settings carry the lowest risk of immunologic complications because donor and recipient are the same person, contrasted with allogeneic settings where donor-derived lymphocytes/antibodies can react against recipient antigens (e.g., passenger lymphocyte syndrome).

source_references.locator: Domain VI – Infectious Hazards of Transfusion > Clinical Presentations; High-Yield Comparison Points (RBC vs Platelet Contaminants)
source_references.supports: Transfusion-associated bacterial sepsis presents with a temperature rise ≥2°C, rigors, tachycardia, and profound hypotension; RBC units are classically contaminated by cold-growing gram-negative organisms such as Yersinia enterocolitica.

review.notes: Bacterial-sepsis clinical facts (>=2C rise, rigors, tachycardia, hypotension; RBC units classically contaminated by cold-growing gram-negatives like Yersinia; culture the bag not segments) are explicitly and strongly grounded in SRC-015/SRC-013. However, the key rationale for C -- 'autologous transfusion reduces alloimmune-mediated reaction risk because donor and recipient are the same person' -- is sourced only from SRC-035's 'Autologous vs. Allogeneic HSCT' comparison point, which sits in the transplantation/HSCT graft-immunology node (donor lymphocyte/PLS context), not in a blood-component

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a hematologic malignancy predonates autologous apheresis platelets before an elective surgical procedure. Twenty minutes into transfusion of this autologous platelet unit, she develops a 2.3°C rise in temperature, rigors, a heart rate of 132 beats/min, and a fall in systolic blood pressure from 128 to 76 mmHg. The transfusion is stopped immediately, and transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection is suspected. Which of the following is the most appropriate next laboratory step?

options.text: Conclude that no microbiologic workup is indicated, since autologous components cannot become contaminated with bacteria
Culture only the pilot tubing segments attached to the bag, since these are considered representative of the sterility of the entire unit
Send the patient's blood for culture and culture the residual platelet product taken from the bag itself, not the attached segments
Repeat ABO/Rh typing on the recipient to evaluate for a major ABO-incompatible hemolytic reaction
Perform a direct antiglobulin test (DAT) with eluate to look for an antibody directed against donor red cell antigens

questions.explanation_correct: When transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection is suspected, the transfusion should be stopped immediately and both the patient's blood and the remaining component should be cultured -- specifically the product taken from the bag itself rather than the attached pilot tubing segments, since segment sampling is not validated to confirm contamination of the implicated unit. This workup applies regardless of whether the unit was autologous or allogeneic because bacterial contamination is introduced during collection, processing, or storage and is not affected by donor identity.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The recommended workup specifies culturing the residual product in the bag itself, not the pilot segments, because segment sampling does not reliably confirm contamination of the implicated unit.
option_explanations.C: Correct. This matches the recommended suspected-bacterial-contamination workup: stop the transfusion and culture both the patient's blood and the residual unit contents from the bag, not the segments.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. DAT and eluate testing identifies antibody-mediated hemolysis (as seen in passenger lymphocyte syndrome after allogeneic transplantation), which is not the mechanism suggested by fever, rigors, and hypotension after platelet transfusion, and is not relevant to a single-donor autologous platelet unit.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Autologous components are collected, processed, and stored the same way as allogeneic components and are equally susceptible to bacterial contamination; a septic-appearing reaction still requires a full microbiologic workup.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. An autologous unit is by definition ABO/Rh identical to the recipient, so a major ABO-incompatible hemolytic reaction is not the relevant concern here; the clinical picture (fever, rigors, hypotension) is characteristic of a septic reaction rather than a hemolytic one.

questions.teaching_point: Workup of a suspected septic transfusion reaction requires culturing the patient's blood and the residual product from the bag itself (not the segments), regardless of whether the implicated unit was autologous or allogeneic.

source_references.locator: Domain VI – Infectious Hazards of Transfusion > Diagnostic Approach (Suspected TTBI Workup)
source_references.supports: When bacterial contamination is suspected, transfusion should be stopped and both patient blood and the residual component from the bag itself (not the tubing segments) should be cultured to confirm the diagnosis.

source_references.locator: Node 7 – Transplantation Immunohematology > Laboratory Findings (PLS Serology)
source_references.supports: DAT and eluate testing with specificity for recipient antigens is used to diagnose passenger lymphocyte syndrome after allogeneic transplantation, an antibody-mediated hemolytic process distinct from bacterial sepsis.

review.notes: Near-verbatim match to SRC-015: 'Suspected TTBI Workup: Stop transfusion; culture both the patient's blood and the remaining component in the bag (not the segments).' All distractors are correctly refuted using source content (segment-only culture explicitly contradicted; DAT/eluate belongs to PLS in allogeneic HSCT per SRC-035 and does not apply to autologous platelets or hemolysis mechanism; no exemption from microbiologic workup for autologous components is described; ABO/Rh incompatibility is definitionally excluded in autologous transfusion). Only minor note: thematically overlaps with Q0

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A patient predonates two units of autologous red blood cells at Hospital A in preparation for elective spinal surgery. The surgery is subsequently rescheduled and will now take place at Hospital B, so the units must be shipped there for the procedure. Which of the following statements about handling these units before transfer is correct?

options.text: The units are exempt from ABO/Rh confirmation testing because they are labeled "For Autologous Use Only"
Because the units are autologous, no additional infectious disease testing is required regardless of the shipping destination
The units must be relabeled as allogeneic once they leave the collecting facility
The units must be irradiated before release because all autologous components require irradiation
Because the units are being shipped to another facility, they must undergo infectious disease testing before transfer

questions.explanation_correct: Autologous units are not automatically exempt from infectious disease marker testing; testing is specifically required when the unit will be shipped to a facility other than the one that collected it. This is a frequently tested distinction from the general assumption that autologous status alone waives infectious disease testing requirements.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect. This is the classic misconception flagged as a board trap: while autologous donor eligibility criteria are relaxed compared with allogeneic donation, infectious disease testing is specifically required when an autologous unit is shipped to another facility.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Autologous units must remain labeled 'For Autologous Use Only' and are intended solely for the donor-patient; they are not relabeled as allogeneic product when transferred between facilities.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Irradiation is required for cellular blood components given to specific at-risk recipients (such as HSCT/immunocompromised patients) to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease; it is not a blanket requirement triggered simply by a unit being autologous or shipped between facilities.
option_explanations.E: Correct. Autologous units shipped to another facility must undergo infectious disease testing before transfer, unlike units used only at the collecting facility.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. No exemption from ABO/Rh confirmation testing for autologous units is described; comparison of current typing with historical records remains part of standard pretransfusion safety practice.

questions.teaching_point: Autologous units are not exempt from infectious disease testing when shipped to another facility for use; they retain their required 'For Autologous Use Only' label and are not subject to a blanket irradiation requirement.

source_references.locator: Node 8 – Infectious Safety & Look-back > Common Board Traps and Distractors (Autologous Testing)
source_references.supports: Autologous units require infectious disease testing if they are shipped to a facility other than the collecting site, a commonly tested exception to typical autologous testing exemptions.

source_references.locator: Domain VI – Infectious Hazards of Transfusion > Common Board Traps and Distractors (Autologous Testing)
source_references.supports: Reiterates that autologous units still require infectious disease testing when shipped to another facility.

source_references.locator: Node 30 – Donor Selection, Deferrals, and Physicals > Common Board Traps and Distractors (Autologous vs Allogeneic)
source_references.supports: Autologous units must be labeled 'For Autologous Use Only,' distinguishing them from standard allogeneic donations, which have more stringent donor eligibility criteria.

source_references.locator: Node 7 – Transplantation Immunohematology > Prevention (TA-GVHD)
source_references.supports: Irradiation of cellular blood components is required specifically to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease in HSCT/immunocompromised recipients, not as a general rule applied to all autologous units.

review.notes: Keyed fact stated nearly verbatim in two independent sources: SRC-013 'Autologous units do require infectious disease testing if they are shipped to another facility' and SRC-015 'Autologous units still require infectious disease testing if they are shipped to another facility.' Distractors are well supported: SRC-003 Node 30 explicitly requires 'For Autologous Use Only' labeling (refutes B); SRC-035 ties irradiation specifically to TA-GVHD prevention in HSCT/immunocompromised recipients, not a blanket autologous rule (refutes C); SRC-003 Node 29 documents ABO/Rh comparison against historical

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A patient who received a unit of non-irradiated red blood cells several weeks earlier is diagnosed with transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD), a delayed transfusion reaction associated with an approximately 90% fatality rate. Which of the following best explains the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism of TA-GVHD?

options.text: Anti-IgA antibodies in an IgA-deficient recipient reacted against donor plasma IgA, producing an anaphylactic-type reaction
An anamnestic (secondary) recipient alloantibody response targeted antigens on the transfused donor red cells, causing delayed extravascular hemolysis
Viable donor T lymphocytes contained in the transfused cellular component were not inactivated before transfusion and mounted an immune-mediated attack against the recipient's tissues
Donor leukocyte-derived cytokines that accumulated in the component during storage triggered a systemic febrile response in the recipient
A clerical error caused an ABO-incompatible transfusion, producing complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis

questions.explanation_correct: TA-GVHD is prevented specifically by gamma irradiation of cellular blood components, described in the sources as acting to inactivate donor T lymphocytes; granulocyte components are separately flagged as especially high risk because they contain significant numbers of viable donor T lymphocytes. Together these statements indicate that surviving, non-inactivated donor T lymphocytes mounting an immune attack on recipient tissue is the mechanism attributed to TA-GVHD, distinct from the antibody-, cytokine-, and clerical-error-driven mechanisms described for other classic transfusion reactions.

option_explanations.C: Correct. The sources state irradiation prevents TA-GVHD by inactivating donor T lymphocytes and that granulocyte components carry significant numbers of viable donor T lymphocytes, indicating surviving donor T lymphocytes attacking host tissue is the causative mechanism.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibody reaction against donor plasma is the mechanism the sources attribute to allergic transfusion reactions, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Accumulated donor leukocyte cytokines is the mechanism the sources attribute to febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Clerical error causing ABO-incompatible, hallmark-hemolysis transfusion is described as the classic acute hemolytic transfusion reaction mechanism, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. An anamnestic alloantibody response against donor red cell antigens is the mechanism the sources attribute to delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions, a separate delayed-reaction category from TA-GVHD.

questions.teaching_point: TA-GVHD results from viable, non-inactivated donor T lymphocytes in a cellular blood product; this distinguishes it mechanistically from antibody-mediated (allergic, hemolytic) and cytokine-mediated (febrile) transfusion reactions.

source_references.locator: line 75
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, establishing donor T lymphocytes as the causative agent.

source_references.locator: line 63
source_references.supports: States granulocyte components must be irradiated before issue because they contain significant numbers of viable donor T lymphocytes, reinforcing the T-lymphocyte mechanism.

Classifies TA-GVHD as a delayed reaction with ~90% fatality, and separately attributes allergic reactions to IgA deficiency, febrile reactions to cytokines, and AHTR to clerical error/visual hemolysis, grounding the distractor mechanisms.

review.notes: Core mechanism claim is grounded: SRC-010 line 75 states TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, and SRC-031 line 63 states granulocytes carry significant viable donor T lymphocytes and must be irradiated to prevent TA-GVHD. The 90% fatality figure matches SRC-043 line 85 verbatim ('TA-GVHD (90% fatality)'). Distractor mechanisms are each grounded in SRC-043 line 79 (AHTR/clerical error/visual hemolysis), line 83 (IgA deficiency->allergic; cytokines->febrile), and line 85 (anamnestic->delayed hemolytic). The phrase 'mounted an immune-mediated attack against

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A transfusion medicine physician is asked to help confirm a diagnosis of transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) in a patient who received a non-irradiated cellular blood component. Which of the following laboratory findings would most strongly support this diagnosis?

options.text: Visual hemolysis identified in the post-transfusion plasma specimen
A positive bacterial culture obtained from the residual segment of the implicated unit
A biphasic IgG antibody demonstrating anti-P specificity on the Donath-Landsteiner test
Identification of a second, donor-derived cell population in the recipient's blood or affected tissue (chimerism), analogous to the dual cell populations seen in post-transplant settings
A positive HLA antibody screen explaining an inadequate posttransfusion platelet count increment

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe chimerism as dual (donor plus recipient) cell populations occurring in post-transplant scenarios. Because TA-GVHD results from viable donor T lymphocytes persisting and proliferating in the recipient, demonstrating a distinct donor-derived cell population in the recipient is the laboratory correlate that fits this process, distinguishing it from the antibody- and culture-based tests used to work up other transfusion complications described in the sources.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The sources define chimerism as dual donor-and-recipient cell populations occurring in post-transplant scenarios; TA-GVHD is fundamentally an unintended engraftment of donor T lymphocytes, so demonstrating a donor-derived cell population supports the diagnosis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. A biphasic Donath-Landsteiner antibody with anti-P specificity is the diagnostic test the sources describe for paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. A positive HLA antibody screen is described as explaining immune-mediated platelet refractoriness, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Visual hemolysis is described as the hallmark of acute hemolytic transfusion reactions, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A positive bacterial culture from a unit segment supports transfusion-transmitted bacterial contamination, a separate hazard from TA-GVHD in the sources.

questions.teaching_point: TA-GVHD can be supported by demonstrating persistent donor-derived (chimeric) cells in the recipient, reflecting the same dual-cell-population principle used for post-transplant chimerism, and should be distinguished from serologic/culture tests used for other transfusion complications (Donath-Landsteiner for PCH, HLA antibody screen for platelet refractoriness, unit culture for bacterial contamination).

source_references.locator: line 21
source_references.supports: Defines chimerism as dual cell populations occurring in twins or post-transplant scenarios, the basis for using chimerism analysis to identify persisting donor cells.

source_references.locator: lines 13, 75
source_references.supports: States PCH is diagnosed via the Donath-Landsteiner test with anti-P specificity, and that TA-GVHD is prevented by irradiation inactivating donor T lymphocytes, framing TA-GVHD as a donor-cell-persistence process distinct from PCH.

source_references.locator: lines 25, 53
source_references.supports: States platelet refractoriness can be HLA/HPA-antibody mediated and that platelets require mandatory bacterial culture due to contamination risk, grounding two distractor lab findings.

source_references.locator: line 79
source_references.supports: States visual hemolysis is the described hallmark of acute hemolytic transfusion reactions caused by clerical error.

review.notes: The four distractors are solidly grounded and correctly excluded: SRC-010 line 13 ties Donath-Landsteiner/anti-P to PCH (not TA-GVHD); SRC-031 line 25 ties HLA/HPA antibodies to platelet refractoriness; SRC-043 line 79 ties visual hemolysis to AHTR; SRC-031 line 53 ties bacterial culture to platelet contamination screening. However, the positive justification for the keyed answer A is weak: the sources' only mention of 'chimerism' is a single, isolated one-line definition in SRC-043 line 21 ('Chimerism: Dual cell populations in twins vs. post-transplant scenarios'), located in the Domain 1 mol

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A patient with severe neutropenia (ANC <500/µL) and a documented bacterial infection unresponsive to antimicrobial therapy is to receive a granulocyte transfusion as bridge therapy while awaiting marrow recovery. Which of the following processing steps should the blood bank perform before releasing this component in order to reduce the risk of TA-GVHD?

options.text: Leukoreduction of the component by prestorage filtration
Selection of a donor unit with reduced CMV transmission risk
Treatment of the component with amotosalen/UVA pathogen-inactivation technology
Gamma irradiation of the granulocyte component to inactivate donor T lymphocytes
Saline washing of the component to remove plasma and cellular debris

questions.explanation_correct: Granulocyte components are specifically highlighted as high risk for TA-GVHD because they contain significant numbers of viable donor T lymphocytes, and the sources state granulocytes must be irradiated before issue for this reason. Irradiation is the processing step the sources tie to TA-GVHD prevention, while leukoreduction, CMV-risk-reduction selection, washing, and amotosalen/UVA pathogen-inactivation technology are each linked instead to FNHTR/CMV prevention, CMV transmission prevention, debris/factor removal, and plasma/platelet transfusion-transmitted-disease reduction, respectively.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The sources state granulocytes must be irradiated before issue to prevent TA-GVHD because they contain significant numbers of viable donor T lymphocytes, and that irradiation of cellular components is the specific TA-GVHD prevention measure.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The sources pair leukoreduction with prevention of febrile non-hemolytic reactions and CMV transmission, explicitly distinguishing it from irradiation, which is the measure tied to TA-GVHD prevention.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Selecting a reduced-CMV-risk donor unit is described as a practice to prevent CMV transmission (e.g., in neonatal transfusion), not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Saline washing is described as a means of removing debris, free hemoglobin, and activated factors from recovered blood; it is not described as a method of inactivating leukocytes to prevent TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Amotosalen/UVA pathogen-inactivation technology is described as treating plasma and platelets to reduce transfusion-transmitted disease risk; it is not the process the sources describe for granulocyte components, for which irradiation is specified.

questions.teaching_point: Irradiation, not leukoreduction, CMV-risk-reduction donor selection, washing, or pathogen-inactivation technology (as applied to plasma/platelets), is the specific processing step that prevents TA-GVHD by inactivating donor T lymphocytes; this is especially critical for granulocyte components given their high viable T-lymphocyte content.

source_references.locator: lines 9, 63
source_references.supports: States granulocytes are indicated as a bridge for severely neutropenic patients (ANC <500) with infections unresponsive to antimicrobials, and must be irradiated before issue to prevent TA-GVHD due to viable donor T lymphocytes.

source_references.locator: line 53
source_references.supports: Pairs irradiation with TA-GVHD prevention and leukoreduction with FNHTR/CMV prevention as distinct pretransfusion processing steps.

source_references.locator: lines 75, 103
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, and separately notes CMV-reduced-risk components are used in neonatal transfusion practice.

source_references.locator: line 57
source_references.supports: States pathogen-inactivation technologies (amotosalen/UVA) treat plasma and platelets to reduce transfusion-transmitted disease risk.

review.notes: Well grounded throughout. SRC-031 line 9 matches the stem's clinical scenario (granulocytes as bridge therapy for ANC <500 with antimicrobial-refractory infection) nearly verbatim. SRC-031 line 63 and SRC-010 line 75 directly state irradiation of granulocytes/cellular components prevents TA-GVHD by inactivating viable donor T lymphocytes. Distractors are each correctly grounded to a different purpose: leukoreduction to FNHTR/CMV prevention (SRC-043 line 53), CMV-reduced-risk donor selection to neonatal CMV prevention (SRC-010 line 103), saline washing to debris/free-Hb/activated-factor removal

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A 940-g neonate born at 27 weeks' gestation requires a double-volume exchange transfusion for severe hyperbilirubinemia. The blood bank director specifies that the RBC unit selected must be gamma-irradiated before release. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which this processing step prevents the transfusion complication of greatest concern in this patient?

options.text: Irradiation inactivates viable donor T lymphocytes, preventing their proliferation and engraftment in the recipient
Irradiation reduces residual donor leukocytes, decreasing the risk of febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions
Irradiation lyses donor granulocytes, preventing recipient sensitization to HLA antigens
Irradiation reduces intact donor leukocytes capable of transmitting cytomegalovirus
Irradiation eliminates donor antibodies directed against recipient neutrophil antigens, preventing acute lung injury

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components specifically to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, which otherwise can survive, proliferate, and engraft in a susceptible host such as a premature neonate undergoing exchange transfusion — one of the explicitly listed high-risk indications for irradiation.

option_explanations.A: Correct: irradiation's stated purpose is inactivation of donor T lymphocytes so they cannot proliferate/engraft and attack recipient tissue.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: prevention of febrile nonhemolytic reactions is attributed to leukocyte reduction, not irradiation.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: TRALI mitigation is attributed to donor selection (male or never-pregnant female donors for plasma-containing products), not irradiation.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: reduction of CMV transmission risk is attributed to leukoreduction/CMV-safe component selection, a requirement listed separately from irradiation.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: decreased HLA alloimmunization is attributed to leukocyte reduction; irradiation's described action is inactivating T-lymphocyte proliferative capacity, not lysing granulocytes.

questions.teaching_point: Gamma irradiation, leukoreduction, and donor-sex selection each mitigate a different transfusion hazard (TA-GVHD, FNHTR/CMV/HLA-allo, and TRALI, respectively); boards frequently test whether examinees can match the correct preventive intervention to its mechanism.

source_references.locator: Domain V high-yield facts
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes.

source_references.locator: Prevention section
source_references.supports: Distinguishes irradiation (prevents TA-GVHD) from leukoreduction (prevents FNHTR, CMV transmission, HLA alloimmunization) and from TRALI donor-selection mitigation.

source_references.locator: Prevention section
source_references.supports: Lists gamma irradiation of cellular components for at-risk patients including neonates <1200g and directed donations from relatives, and separately lists male/never-pregnant-female donor selection as TRALI mitigation.

review.notes: Core fact is well-grounded: SRC-010 states 'TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes'; SRC-019 and SRC-045 corroborate and cleanly separate irradiation (TA-GVHD) from leukoreduction (FNHTR/CMV/HLA-allo, SRC-045 line 63) and donor-sex selection (TRALI, SRC-045 line 67 / SRC-019 line 65). The 940g/27-week neonate independently qualifies via both '<1200g' and 'exchange transfusion' criteria (SRC-017 line 57, SRC-019 line 67). Option A's wording ('preventing their proliferation and engraftment') slightly elaborates beyond the literal source

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: A fetus with severe hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn requires an intrauterine transfusion (IUT). The blood bank issues a group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, hemoglobin S-negative RBC unit less than 7 days old. The ordering physician notes the unit will also be leukocyte-reduced and asks whether irradiation adds anything beyond leukoreduction for this fetus.

options.text: An anamnestic (memory) alloantibody response develops days after transfusion and destroys donor red cells extravascularly

Leukoreduction removes leukocytes responsible for febrile reactions and CMV transmission, but viable donor T-lymphocytes capable of surviving, engrafting, and proliferating in a recipient unable to reject them are not reliably eliminated by leukoreduction and require irradiation for inactivation
Cytokines that accumulate in the cellular component during refrigerated storage trigger a recipient febrile response at the time of transfusion
Preformed recipient antibody directed against donor RBC antigens causes rapid intravascular hemolysis, producing visually evident hemolysis in the post-transfusion plasma
Donor plasma IgA reacts with anti-IgA antibody in an IgA-deficient recipient, producing an anaphylactic-type reaction

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources explicitly separate irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention) from leukoreduction (FNHTR/CMV prevention), meaning leukoreduction alone does not protect against TA-GVHD. TA-GVHD is grouped among delayed reactions with 90% fatality. The chimerism node describes dual cell populations arising from transfusion, with the recipient failing to reject/eliminate the foreign (donor) population - consistent with viable donor lymphocytes persisting and proliferating when not inactivated by irradiation.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the source's explicit irradiation-vs-leukoreduction distinction and the chimerism concept of a persisting, non-rejected donor cell population.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes the mechanism of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (clerical error, visible hemolysis), not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this describes the IgA-deficiency mechanism attributed to allergic reactions in the source, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this describes the anamnestic mechanism of a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction, which the source lists alongside but distinct from TA-GVHD under 'Delayed Reactions.'
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this describes the cytokine mechanism attributed to febrile non-hemolytic reactions in the source, not TA-GVHD.

questions.teaching_point: Irradiation, not leukoreduction, is the specific measure that prevents TA-GVHD; leukoreduction targets a different leukocyte-mediated risk (FNHTR/CMV).

source_references.locator: Domain 2 nodes: 'Pretransfusion Processing' and 'Delayed Reactions'; 'Acute Hemolytic Reactions'; 'Allergic & Febrile Reactions'
source_references.supports: States irradiation prevents TA-GVHD while leukoreduction prevents FNHTR/CMV (Pretransfusion Processing); lists TA-GVHD as a delayed reaction with 90% fatality; describes AHTR (clerical error, visual hemolysis), allergic (IgA deficiency), and febrile (cytokine) mechanisms used as distractors.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 51 - Chimerism, Core Concepts and Diagnostic Logic
source_references.supports: Defines chimerism as dual cell populations induced by transfusion, and states that individuals with natural (twin) chimerism are immunologically tolerant to the foreign cell population, supporting the concept of non-rejection of donor cells.

review.notes: The core distinction is explicit in SRC-043 ('Pretransfusion Processing: Irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention) and leukoreduction (FNHTR/CMV prevention)'), and TA-GVHD's 90% fatality under 'Delayed Reactions' is also explicit, so the answer choice itself is correctly keyed. However, option A's detailed mechanism — 'viable donor T-lymphocytes capable of surviving, engrafting, and proliferating in a recipient unable to reject them ... not reliably eliminated by leukoreduction' — is not stated anywhere in the four sources; it is standard TA-GVHD physiology but exceeds the terse source text. questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions.stem: Several weeks after receiving an intrauterine transfusion of irradiated, group O, Rh-negative RBCs, a neonate's postnatal ABO/Rh typing shows a mixed-field agglutination pattern instead of a single clear reaction. The blood bank physician wants to determine whether this reflects true persistence of two distinct cell populations rather than a technical artifact.

options.text: Perform molecular (DNA-based) testing on two different tissue sources, such as peripheral blood and a buccal swab, and compare the results
Perform a direct antiglobulin test (DAT) with anti-IgG reagent
Perform a Kleihauer-Betke (KB) test to quantify the volume of fetomaternal hemorrhage
