Albumin increases plasma colloid oncotic pressure, pulling fluid from the interstitial space into the intravascular compartment

questions.explanation_correct: Among the major plasma proteins, albumin's defining physiologic role is maintenance of colloid oncotic pressure, which is the basis for using an albumin infusion to shift fluid into the vascular space. This is distinct from immunoglobulins (immunity) and coagulation factors (hemostasis), and distinct from the oxygen-carrying-capacity goal met by red cells.

option_explanations.E: Correct: albumin's core plasma-protein function is oncotic pressure maintenance, the physiologic basis for using it to draw fluid intravascularly.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: coagulation factors, a separate plasma protein category responsible for hemostasis, are supplied by plasma or cryoprecipitate, not by albumin.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: passive immunity is conferred by immunoglobulins, a distinct plasma protein class from albumin.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: oxygen-carrying capacity is restored by red blood cell transfusion, a physiologic goal distinct from the oncotic-pressure role of albumin.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: fibrinogen is concentrated in cryoprecipitate, not albumin, which does not contain coagulation proteins.

questions.teaching_point: Albumin's transfusion-medicine role is defined by its oncotic (colloid osmotic) effect, not by hemostatic, immunologic, or oxygen-carrying functions, which belong to other blood components.

source_references.locator: Domain X – Plasma and Platelet Components > Core Concepts > Plasma Composition
source_references.supports: States plasma proteins include Albumin (oncotic pressure), Immunoglobulins (immunity), and Coagulation Factors (hemostasis) as distinct functional categories.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 – MTP > Core Concepts > Physiological Goals
source_references.supports: Lists oxygen-carrying capacity, oncotic pressure, and hemostasis as three separate physiologic goals of transfusion/volume restoration.

review.notes: SRC-031 line 5 explicitly assigns oncotic pressure to albumin, immunity to immunoglobulins, and hemostasis to coagulation factors as distinct plasma-protein functions; SRC-032 line 9 lists oxygen-carrying capacity, oncotic pressure, and hemostasis as separate physiologic goals. Cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen distractor (E) and RBC/oxygen-carrying distractor (D) are both consistent with source definitions elsewhere in SRC-031. Independent read confirms A as the only defensible answer; no competing option is supportable.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man receiving a high-dose intravenous antibiotic associated with drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia (DIIHA) is found to have an incidentally positive DAT on pretransfusion testing. He has no clinical or laboratory evidence of hemolysis (stable hemoglobin, normal LDH and haptoglobin). Polyspecific DAT is positive, and monospecific testing shows the RBCs are coated with IgG, complement (C3d), and albumin. The antibody screen is negative, and an eluate prepared from the patient's RBCs is nonreactive against both untreated reagent RBCs and drug-treated reagent RBCs. Which of the following best explains these findings?

options.text: An immune complex-type drug-dependent antibody reacting only when the drug is present in the test system
A drug-independent autoantibody serologically indistinguishable from warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia
A technical false-positive DAT caused by overcentrifugation
A hapten-type drug-dependent antibody reacting with drug-coated RBCs
Nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA), causing nonspecific coating of the RBC membrane by multiple plasma proteins

questions.explanation_correct: The pattern described - a positive DAT for multiple proteins (IgG, complement, and albumin) with a negative antibody screen and an eluate that is nonreactive against both untreated and drug-treated RBCs, in a patient with no evidence of hemolysis - matches nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA), in which the drug alters the RBC membrane so that plasma proteins adsorb nonspecifically without a true antibody being present.

option_explanations.E: Correct: NIPA is defined by DAT positivity for multiple adsorbed plasma proteins, including albumin, with negative serum and eluate reactivity, exactly as described.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the hapten pattern shows DAT positivity for IgG only, and the eluate specifically reacts with drug-treated RBCs; here the DAT is positive for multiple proteins and the eluate is nonreactive even against drug-treated cells.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the immune complex pattern shows DAT positivity for complement (C3) only, with serum reactive against RBCs only when drug is added to the test system; here DAT positivity involves multiple proteins, not complement alone.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the drug-independent autoantibody pattern is serologically identical to warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, with serum and eluate panreactive against normal RBCs; here the eluate is nonreactive, not panreactive.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: while overcentrifugation is a recognized cause of a false-positive antiglobulin test, it does not account for monospecific reactivity with three distinct proteins (IgG, C3d, and albumin) in a patient on a drug known to cause DIIHA.

questions.teaching_point: A DAT positive for multiple unrelated proteins (IgG, complement, and albumin) with negative serum/eluate testing and no hemolysis should raise suspicion for nonimmunologic protein adsorption rather than true drug-dependent or drug-independent immune hemolysis.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 23 – DIIHA > Definitions (NIPA) and Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: Defines NIPA as the drug altering the RBC membrane so plasma proteins (IgG, albumin, etc.) adsorb nonspecifically, producing a positive DAT without a true antibody; states the NIPA laboratory pattern is DAT positive for multiple proteins with negative serum and eluate.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 23 – DIIHA > Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: Describes the hapten pattern (DAT positive IgG only; eluate reacts only with drug-treated cells), immune complex pattern (DAT positive complement only; serum reacts with untreated cells only when drug added), and drug-independent autoantibody pattern (serum/eluate panreactive with normal cells) for comparison.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20 – Antiglobulin Testing > Laboratory Findings (Sources of Error)
source_references.supports: Lists overcentrifugation, spontaneous autoagglutination, and fibrin clots as causes of a false-positive antiglobulin test.

review.notes: Core NIPA mechanism, hapten pattern, immune-complex pattern, and drug-independent autoantibody pattern are all verbatim-supported in SRC-040 (lines 17, 37, 39, 41, 43). However, the stem's specific claim that 'monospecific testing shows the RBCs are coated with IgG, complement (C3d), and albumin' goes beyond the source: SRC-040 line 17 only names 'IgG, Albumin, etc.' as NIPA-adsorbed proteins (not C3d specifically), and SRC-032 line 191 defines 'Monospecific AHG' as reagent against ONLY IgG or ONLY complement -- it does not include an albumin-detecting monospecific reagent in this source set.

questions.stem: A patient presents with profound thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. ADAMTS13 activity is severely reduced, and a diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is made. Emergent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) is initiated, requiring removal of the patient's plasma (containing ADAMTS13 inhibitors) and replacement with fluid to restore normal ADAMTS13 activity. Which of the following is the most appropriate replacement fluid for this TPE procedure?

options.text: Fresh frozen plasma
Albumin
Packed red blood cells
Cryoprecipitate

questions.explanation_correct: In TTP, plasma is the treatment of choice for TPE because it supplies the ADAMTS13 protease that cleaves pathogenic ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers, while TPE itself works by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors from the patient's circulation. Replacement fluid must therefore restore ADAMTS13 activity, which only plasma provides among the standard options.

option_explanations.A: Correct: plasma is the treatment of choice in TTP because it replenishes the ADAMTS13 protease removed/inhibited in the disease, directly addressing the underlying pathophysiology during TPE.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: albumin is a recognized alternative replacement fluid in apheresis generally, but it does not contain ADAMTS13 or coagulation factors, so it would not correct the protease deficiency driving TTP.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: cryoprecipitate is a concentrated source of fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor; providing additional von Willebrand factor substrate would not address the ADAMTS13 deficiency and is not an appropriate TPE replacement fluid.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: packed red blood cells restore oxygen-carrying capacity but do not supply ADAMTS13 or address the mechanism of TTP, and are not used as a TPE replacement fluid.

questions.teaching_point: Replacement-fluid selection in TPE must match the mechanism of disease: TTP requires plasma to restore ADAMTS13 activity, not albumin, which is oncotically active but biologically inert with respect to coagulation/protease factors.

source_references.locator: Domain IV – Apheresis > Major Concepts / High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Identifies replacement fluid choice (Albumin vs. Plasma) as a core apheresis concept and states TPE treats TTP by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors.

source_references.locator: Domain X – Plasma and Platelet Components > Clinical Presentations / Teaching Pearls (ADAMTS13 kinetics); Definitions (Cryoprecipitate)
source_references.supports: States that in TTP, patients require plasma via exchange to replace the ADAMTS13 protease, and that plasma is the treatment of choice because it supplies the VWF-cleaving protease needed to break down ultra-large VWF multimers; also describes cryoprecipitate as a concentrated source of fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand factor.

review.notes: SRC-010 (lines 55, 59) and SRC-031 (lines 27, 77) both directly state that TPE is the treatment of choice for TTP because it supplies ADAMTS13 (the VWF-cleaving protease) while removing inhibitors, and SRC-010 line 55 explicitly frames 'Albumin vs. Plasma' as the key replacement-fluid decision point in apheresis, supporting the albumin distractor. Cryoprecipitate composition (fibrinogen, FVIII, FXIII, VWF) is correctly sourced (SRC-031 line 19) and correctly reasoned as inappropriate (adding VWF substrate without the cleaving protease). Independent read confirms plasma (A) as the unambiguous c

questions.subdomain: Massive Transfusion, Trauma, and Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with septic shock develops oozing from venipuncture sites, hematuria, and diffuse ecchymoses at all IV sites over several hours. Laboratory testing shows thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT and aPTT, a low fibrinogen level, and an elevated D-dimer, confirming a diagnosis of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this patient's bleeding diathesis?

options.text: Reduced enzymatic activity of clotting factors caused by hypothermia from rapid infusion of refrigerated blood components
Pathologic, simultaneous activation of both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems, consuming platelets, fibrinogen, and coagulation factors
Depletion of coagulation factors and platelets from resuscitation with red blood cells and crystalloid alone, without replacement of plasma or platelet components
Chelation of ionized calcium by the citrate anticoagulant contained in transfused plasma and platelet components
Decreased hepatic synthesis of coagulation factors due to underlying chronic liver disease

questions.explanation_correct: DIC is a consumption coagulopathy in which both the procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically activated simultaneously, driving consumption of platelets, fibrinogen, and coagulation factors and producing the diffuse bleeding, prolonged PT/aPTT, hypofibrinogenemia, and elevated D-dimer seen in this patient. Management requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate.

option_explanations.B: Correct: DIC's bleeding results from simultaneous pathologic activation of procoagulant and fibrinolytic pathways, consuming platelets, fibrinogen, and coagulation factors.
option_explanations.C: Distractor: this describes dilutional coagulopathy, the hemorrhagic state caused by resuscitating with only RBCs and crystalloid without plasma/platelet replacement — a distinct entity from DIC, though both can coexist in trauma.
option_explanations.D: Distractor: this describes citrate toxicity, in which citrate anticoagulant (present at higher levels in plasma/platelets than RBCs) chelates ionized calcium causing hypocalcemia — a metabolic complication of rapid component infusion, not DIC's mechanism.
option_explanations.A: Distractor: this describes hypothermia from refrigerated component infusion, which exacerbates coagulopathy by reducing clotting factor enzymatic activity but is not the mechanism of DIC itself.
option_explanations.E: Distractor: this describes the coagulopathy of liver disease (decreased synthesis of most clotting factors with impaired clearance of activated factors), a separate cause of bleeding diathesis distinct from DIC's consumptive process.

questions.teaching_point: DIC's bleeding is driven by simultaneous consumptive activation of coagulation and fibrinolysis, and must be distinguished from other trauma/massive-transfusion coagulopathy mechanisms (dilutional, citrate-related, hypothermic, hepatic synthetic failure) that can coexist but are pathophysiologically distinct.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 47 – Coagulation Defects & Management
source_references.supports: Defines DIC as a consumption coagulopathy with simultaneous pathologic activation of procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems requiring platelet, plasma, and cryoprecipitate replacement; also describes liver disease coagulopathy mechanism.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 – Massive Transfusion Protocols, Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Describes dilutional coagulopathy (RBC/crystalloid-only resuscitation depleting factors/platelets), citrate toxicity (citrate-mediated hypocalcemia), and hypothermia (reduced enzymatic activity worsening coagulopathy) as distinct mechanisms used here as distractors.

review.notes: Core teaching point is well grounded: SRC-002 Node 47 states verbatim that DIC is 'a consumption coagulopathy where both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically active; requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate' — matches option A. All four distractor mechanisms are explicitly sourced: dilutional coagulopathy (SRC-032 Node 16: 'hemorrhagic state caused by replacing lost whole blood with only RBCs and crystalloids, leading to depleted factor and platelet levels'), citrate toxicity (SRC-032 Node 16: citrate in plasma/platelets chelates calcium causing hypoca

questions.subdomain: Massive Transfusion, Trauma, and Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is receiving massive transfusion for hemorrhagic shock after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. Despite several units each of RBCs, plasma, and platelets, she continues to have diffuse microvascular oozing. Repeat laboratory studies show: platelet count 62,000/µL, INR 1.3, aPTT 42 seconds, fibrinogen 85 mg/dL, and ionized calcium 1.2 mmol/L. Based on these results, which laboratory finding is the indication for empiric cryoprecipitate transfusion?

options.text: Fibrinogen of 85 mg/dL
Platelet count of 62,000/µL
Ionized calcium of 1.2 mmol/L
aPTT of 42 seconds
INR of 1.3

questions.explanation_correct: Cryoprecipitate replacement is indicated when fibrinogen falls below 100 mg/dL; this patient's fibrinogen of 85 mg/dL is below that threshold, consistent with hypofibrinogenemia causing diffuse microvascular bleeding. The other listed values do not cross their respective transfusion-trigger thresholds during massive transfusion.

option_explanations.B: Not the trigger for cryoprecipitate: a platelet count <50,000/µL is the threshold for platelet transfusion, and 62,000/µL is above that cutoff.
option_explanations.E: Not the trigger for cryoprecipitate: plasma transfusion is indicated for an INR >1.5, and 1.3 is below that threshold.
option_explanations.D: Not the trigger for cryoprecipitate: plasma transfusion is indicated when aPTT exceeds 60 seconds, and 42 seconds is within the acceptable range.
option_explanations.A: Correct: cryoprecipitate replacement is indicated when fibrinogen falls below 100 mg/dL, and this value (85 mg/dL) is below that trigger, explaining the ongoing microvascular bleeding.
option_explanations.C: Not the trigger for cryoprecipitate: ionized calcium should be maintained above 1.0 mmol/L during massive transfusion, and 1.2 mmol/L is within the acceptable range, so calcium replacement (not cryoprecipitate) would be the relevant consideration only if this value were low.

questions.teaching_point: Each massive-transfusion laboratory trigger corresponds to a specific component: platelets for count <50,000/µL, plasma for INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 sec, and cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL — recognizing which single abnormal value crosses its own threshold is key to targeted component selection.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 – Massive Transfusion Protocols, Diagnostic and Management Approach
source_references.supports: States the specific massive-transfusion laboratory triggers: platelets <50,000/µL, plasma for INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 sec, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, and a target ionized calcium >1.0 mmol/L.

source_references.locator: Domain X – Plasma and Platelet Components, Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Corroborates that diffuse microvascular bleeding occurs when fibrinogen falls below roughly 100–150 mg/dL, supporting cryoprecipitate as the indicated replacement product.

review.notes: Fully grounded. SRC-032 Node 16 states the exact MTP laboratory triggers verbatim: 'Platelets are indicated if the count is <50,000/µL; plasma is needed if the INR is >1.5 or aPTT exceeds 60 seconds' and 'replacement with cryoprecipitate is often indicated when [fibrinogen] levels fall below 100 mg/dL,' plus 'maintain [ionized calcium] >1.0 mmol/L.' Checking stem values against thresholds: platelets 62,000 (>50k, no trigger), INR 1.3 (<1.5, no trigger), aPTT 42 (<60, no trigger), fibrinogen 85 (<100, triggers cryo — correct answer), ionized calcium 1.2 (>1.0, no trigger). SRC-031 corroborates

questions.subdomain: Massive Transfusion, Trauma, and Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay in hemorrhagic shock after a gunshot wound to the abdomen. His ABO group has not yet been determined, and the massive transfusion protocol is activated. The blood bank's inventory of Group AB plasma is very limited, but Group A and Group O plasma are readily available. Which plasma product should the blood bank issue for emergency release while ABO typing is pending?

options.text: Withhold plasma issue until the patient's ABO type is confirmed
Group B plasma
Group O plasma
Group A plasma, reflecting common trauma protocol practice given the limited supply of AB plasma
Group AB plasma, because it is the traditional universal plasma donor type

questions.explanation_correct: Although Group AB plasma is the traditional universal plasma donor type, AB donors are scarce, so many trauma massive transfusion protocols use Group A plasma for emergency release before the recipient's ABO group is known. Waiting for ABO typing would dangerously delay resuscitation in a patient with active hemorrhagic shock.

option_explanations.E: Plausible but not the practice reflected in current trauma protocols: AB plasma is indeed compatible with all recipients, but its donor pool is scarce, making it impractical to stock in the volumes needed for emergency release.
option_explanations.D: Correct: because AB donors are scarce, many trauma MTPs issue Group A plasma for emergent release pending ABO typing rather than reserving the limited AB supply.
option_explanations.B: Not supported as an emergency-release practice; Group B plasma is not described as a standard emergency-release choice in trauma protocols.
option_explanations.C: Not supported as an emergency-release practice; Group O plasma is not described as the emergency-release plasma of choice (unlike Group O red cells, which are the universal red cell donor).
option_explanations.A: Incorrect and unsafe: a patient in active hemorrhagic shock with dilutional/consumptive coagulopathy risk requires emergent component support; withholding plasma pending ABO typing would delay needed hemostatic resuscitation.

questions.teaching_point: Because AB plasma donors are scarce, real-world trauma massive transfusion protocols commonly substitute Group A plasma for emergency release rather than relying on the textbook 'AB is universal' rule, testing awareness of practical blood bank logistics over rote ABO compatibility recall.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 16 – Massive Transfusion Protocols, Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States that although AB plasma is the universal plasma type, many trauma protocols use Group A plasma for emergent release because of the scarcity of AB donors.

review.notes: Directly and almost verbatim grounded in SRC-032 Node 16 'Common Board Traps and Distractors': 'Group A Plasma: While AB is the universal plasma, many trauma protocols use Group A plasma for emergent release due to the scarcity of AB donors.' This matches both the correct answer (B) and option A's rationale (AB as traditional universal donor). Options C (Group B) and E (withhold plasma) are not discussed in the source as legitimate emergency-release practices — plausible and not contradicted, but comparatively weak/throwaway distractors versus the real A-vs-B tension the question is testing, a

questions.subdomain: Fresh Frozen, Frozen, Cryo-Poor, Thawed & Stored Plasma
questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with a known isolated inherited deficiency of coagulation Factor V develops bleeding after a dental extraction. The blood bank issues a unit of Thawed Plasma that was thawed and relabeled 4 days earlier and has been stored refrigerated since relabeling; the unit is still within its allowable dating period. After transfusion, his prothrombin time (PT) remains prolonged and bleeding continues. Which of the following best explains the poor hemostatic response to this specific unit?

options.text: The unit came from a low-volume (300–404 mL) whole-blood collection, which by definition cannot be processed into a plasma product with adequate factor content

Labile coagulation Factors V and VIII progressively lose activity during the refrigerated liquid storage that Thawed Plasma undergoes after relabeling, so a unit nearing the end of its allowable dating period may no longer provide adequate Factor V activity
His true deficiency was of fibrinogen rather than Factor V, so cryoprecipitate rather than plasma should have been transfused

The originating whole-blood unit was not separated and frozen into FFP within 8 hours of collection (6 hours if collected in ACD), and this delay at the collecting facility caused the observed factor loss
The unit was ABO-incompatible with the recipient, and anti-A/anti-B antibodies in the donor plasma destroyed the transfused clotting factors

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that once Thawed Plasma is relabeled it may be used for up to 5 days but is deficient in labile Factors V and VIII. This reflects the underlying pathophysiology: freezing (FFP is maintained ≤-18°C) halts degradation of these labile proteins, but once a unit is thawed and kept as refrigerated liquid plasma under the extended 'Thawed Plasma' dating period, Factors V and VIII progressively lose activity. A unit issued near the end of that window may therefore fail to correct a Factor V-deficient patient's PT despite being technically within date.

option_explanations.B: Correct: this directly reflects the source fact that relabeled Thawed Plasma, usable up to 5 days, is deficient in labile Factors V and VIII because of the refrigerated post-thaw storage interval.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: ABO considerations for plasma concern donor anti-A/anti-B antibody content reacting against recipient RBC antigens (which is why AB plasma is described as the universal plasma donor type) — not enzymatic or immune destruction of transfused clotting factor proteins, which the sources do not describe.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sources specify cryoprecipitate is used when fibrinogen is <100 mg/dL, not for Factor V deficiency; the vignette describes an isolated Factor V deficiency, so switching products would not address the actual deficiency being treated.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the 8-hour (or 6-hour if ACD) collection-to-freeze rule governs the timing requirement for creating FFP at the collecting facility, a separate regulatory step from the post-thaw refrigerated storage decay of Factors V/VIII described for relabeled Thawed Plasma.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the low-volume (300–404 mL) collection rule bars such units from being made into FFP at all; nothing in the vignette indicates a low-volume collection, and this labeling rule does not describe an ongoing storage-related mechanism of factor loss.

questions.teaching_point: Thawed Plasma differs from FFP in that its extended post-thaw dating period (up to 5 days, refrigerated) comes at the cost of labile Factor V and VIII activity; it should not be relied upon when robust levels of these specific factors are clinically required.

source_references.locator: Plasma and Cryoprecipitate Requirements section
source_references.supports: States that relabeled Thawed Plasma may be used up to 5 days but is deficient in labile Factors V and VIII — the core mechanism tested.

source_references.locator: Plasma and Cryoprecipitate Requirements section
source_references.supports: States FFP must be separated and frozen within 8 hours of collection (6 hours if collected in ACD), used to construct distractor D.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors section
source_references.supports: States low-volume (300–404 mL) whole-blood collections must be labeled low volume and cannot be used to make FFP, used to construct distractor E.

source_references.locator: Plasma & Cryoprecipitate line
source_references.supports: States cryoprecipitate is used for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL while FFP is used for general factor replacement, used to construct distractor C.

review.notes: SRC-032 (essentials-of-transfusion-medicine...) directly states: 'Thawed Plasma: Once relabeled, it may be used for up to 5 days but is deficient in labile Factors V and VIII' - this is the core fact and is well grounded, as are the facts used to build distractors D (8-hour/6-hour ACD freeze rule) and E (300-404mL low-volume rule bars FFP production), and C (cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, from SRC-043). However, option A and the explanation add specificity not verbatim in the source: the source gives a blanket 'deficient in labile Factors V and VIII' statement for the whole 5-day Thawed

questions.subdomain: Fresh Frozen, Frozen, Cryo-Poor, Thawed & Stored Plasma
questions.stem: A 60-year-old man who is not receiving anticoagulant therapy is scheduled for a bedside procedure. He is not actively bleeding. Preoperative laboratory results are: INR 1.3, aPTT 34 seconds, platelet count 180,000/µL, and fibrinogen 250 mg/dL. Based on the described laboratory triggers for plasma transfusion, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold plasma and instead give prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC), which produces faster INR correction than plasma
Transfuse plasma in a fixed 1:1:1 ratio with red cells and platelets, consistent with balanced massive transfusion resuscitation
Withhold plasma transfusion; his INR and aPTT do not exceed the described thresholds (INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds) that indicate a need for plasma
Transfuse plasma now, because a platelet count of 180,000/µL and fibrinogen of 250 mg/dL together indicate an underlying coagulopathy that plasma will correct
Transfuse cryoprecipitate now, because his fibrinogen level is low enough to meet the described threshold for cryoprecipitate transfusion

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources define specific laboratory triggers for plasma transfusion: plasma is needed if the INR is >1.5 or the aPTT exceeds 60 seconds. This patient's INR (1.3) and aPTT (34 seconds) meet neither threshold, and he is not actively bleeding, so plasma transfusion is not indicated based on the stated criteria; it should be withheld and the labs reassessed if his clinical status changes.

option_explanations.C: Correct: neither the INR nor the aPTT crosses the described threshold (INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds) for plasma transfusion, so plasma is not indicated by these criteria.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the described platelet trigger is a count <50,000/µL, and this patient's count of 180,000/µL is far above it; his fibrinogen is also not low. Neither value is described as a trigger for plasma transfusion in the sources, which tie plasma specifically to INR/aPTT elevation.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the sources indicate cryoprecipitate is used when fibrinogen is <100 mg/dL; a fibrinogen of 250 mg/dL is well above this threshold, so cryoprecipitate is not indicated.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the fixed 1:1:1 ratio described in the sources is a formula-based approach used during massive transfusion/damage-control resuscitation to prevent dilutional coagulopathy and approximate whole blood, not a rule applied independent of laboratory values to a stable, non-bleeding pre-procedure patient with near-normal coagulation studies.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources describe PCC as preferred over FFP specifically for urgent warfarin reversal; this patient is explicitly not on anticoagulant therapy, and his labs do not meet any described threshold for treatment in the first place.

questions.teaching_point: Plasma transfusion decisions should be driven by the specific abnormal coagulation values that define the described indication (INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds) rather than by platelet count, fibrinogen level, ratio-based massive transfusion protocols, or reflexive substitution with other hemostatic products, each of which has its own distinct trigger.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Triggers line
source_references.supports: States plasma is needed if the INR is >1.5 or aPTT exceeds 60 seconds — the core threshold tested by the correct answer.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Triggers line
source_references.supports: States platelets are indicated if the count is <50,000/µL, used to construct distractor B.

source_references.locator: Plasma & Cryoprecipitate line
source_references.supports: States cryoprecipitate is used for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, used to construct distractor C.

source_references.locator: Balanced Resuscitation line
source_references.supports: Describes the fixed 1:1:1 balanced resuscitation ratio of plasma, platelets, and RBCs used in massive transfusion, used to construct distractor D.

source_references.locator: Management Logic / Urgent Warfarin Reversal line
source_references.supports: States PCC is preferred over FFP for urgent warfarin reversal due to lower volume and faster INR normalization, used to construct distractor E.

review.notes: The INR>1.5 / aPTT>60s thresholds and the <50,000/uL platelet threshold are verbatim from SRC-032, but that sentence ('Laboratory Triggers: Platelets are indicated if the count is <50,000/uL; plasma is needed if the INR is >1.5 or aPTT exceeds 60 seconds') appears specifically under Subdomain Node 16 'Massive Transfusion Protocols (MTP)' as an MTP monitoring/management trigger, not as a general context-free rule for routine pre-procedure plasma decisions. The question applies this MTP-context trigger to a stable, non-bleeding, non-MTP bedside-procedure patient without acknowledging the source

questions.stem: A transfusion service revises its CMV risk-reduction policy so that, for most CMV at-risk patients (e.g., low-birth-weight neonates), prestorage leukoreduced red cell units are dispensed as 'CMV-safe' components instead of units collected specifically from CMV-seronegative donors, citing evidence that the two strategies confer comparable protection against transfusion-transmitted CMV. Which of the following best explains the physiologic rationale for why leukoreduction reduces CMV transmission risk?

options.text: CMV is harbored primarily within donor platelets, and leukoreduction filters preferentially remove platelets from the red cell unit
Leukoreduction filters expose the unit to ultraviolet light that cross-links CMV nucleic acid and prevents viral replication
CMV is a latent virus that resides within donor monocytes and granulocytes; reducing the residual white blood cell content of the unit removes the principal cellular reservoir of transmissible virus
CMV circulates predominantly as cell-free virions in donor plasma, and leukoreduction filters adsorb free virus along with plasma proteins
Leukoreduction removes donor anti-CMV IgG antibody from the plasma, preventing passive transfer of antibody-virus complexes to the recipient

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that CMV is a latent virus carried within monocytes and granulocytes, and that prestorage leukoreduction (to <5x10^6 WBCs) effectively removes these CMV-carrying cells. This cellular removal is the basis for universal leukoreduction being considered 'CMV-safe' and nearly replacing seronegative unit selection in many institutions.

option_explanations.C: Correct: leukoreduction physically removes the leukocytes (monocytes/granulocytes) that harbor latent CMV, eliminating the principal cell-associated viral reservoir.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources describe CMV as a virus carried within leukocytes, not as a predominantly cell-free plasma virion, so leukofiltration is not targeting free plasma virus.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: UV-based nucleic acid cross-linking describes pathogen-reduction technologies (e.g., amotosalen + UVA), a distinct process from leukoreduction filters, which act by physically removing cells.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: leukoreduction is a cell-removal filtration step, not an antibody-removal process; the source ties donor anti-CMV IgG status to seronegative donor selection, a separate mitigation strategy.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the cellular reservoir for CMV described in the source is monocytes and granulocytes (leukocytes), not platelets.

questions.teaching_point: Leukoreduction mitigates CMV transmission by physically removing the leukocytes that harbor latent, cell-associated CMV -- it is not an antiviral or nucleic-acid-inactivating process.

source_references.locator: Mastery Nodes, Subdomain Node 52 - CMV Mitigation Strategies
source_references.supports: States CMV is a latent virus carried within monocytes and granulocytes, and that prestorage leukoreduction to <5x10^6 WBCs removes CMV-carrying cells, making universal LR 'CMV-safe.'

source_references.locator: Advanced Review Nodes, Subdomain Node 48 - Pathogen Inactivation Technologies
source_references.supports: Describes amotosalen+UVA pathogen reduction as cross-linking DNA/RNA to inactivate pathogens, used to distinguish this UV-cross-linking mechanism from leukoreduction's cell-removal mechanism.

review.notes: SRC-012 (Mastery Nodes, Node 52) directly states CMV is a latent virus carried within monocytes and granulocytes, and that prestorage LR (<5x10^6 WBCs) removes CMV-carrying cells, making universal LR 'CMV-safe' and nearly replacing seronegative selection. SRC-002 (Advanced Review, Node 48) confirms amotosalen+UVA cross-links nucleic acid, correctly distinguishing PI technology (option C) from LR's cell-removal mechanism. Distractors B, D, E (cell-free plasma virions, antibody removal, platelet reservoir) are not supported by and are reasonably inconsistent with the source's description of CMV

questions.stem: A blood bank technologist is asked to reserve units specifically labeled 'CMV-seronegative,' as distinct from routine leukoreduced 'CMV-safe' units, for a protocol that requires true seronegative product. Which donor testing result should the technologist use to identify a unit that qualifies for the 'CMV-seronegative' label?

options.text: The donor's plasma tested negative for anti-CMV IgM antibody, regardless of IgG status
The donor's unit was processed by prestorage filtration to a residual WBC count of <5x10^6
The donor's component underwent pathogen reduction with amotosalen and UVA light
The donor's plasma tested negative (nonreactive) for IgG antibody to CMV
The donor's plasma tested negative by nucleic acid testing (NAT) for CMV

questions.explanation_correct: The approved source explicitly defines CMV-seronegative units as those from donors who lack IgG antibodies to CMV. This antibody-based donor testing result -- not WBC content, pathogen-reduction status, or NAT -- is what qualifies a unit for the seronegative label.

option_explanations.D: Correct: the source defines CMV-seronegative units as coming from donors who lack IgG antibody to CMV, i.e., donor serologic testing is negative for anti-CMV IgG.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: a residual WBC count <5x10^6 defines the leukoreduced 'CMV-safe' strategy, which the source explicitly distinguishes from true CMV-seronegative donor selection.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: pathogen reduction with amotosalen + UVA is described as replacing the need for CMV testing altogether, making it an alternative strategy, not a form of seronegative donor testing.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the source ties CMV donor eligibility to antibody testing; NAT is described in the sources as reducing the window period for HIV, HCV, and HBV, not as the method used to establish CMV-seronegative status.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the source's definition specifies IgG antibody status; a negative IgM result alone does not establish that the donor lacks anti-CMV IgG, the stated criterion.

questions.teaching_point: 'CMV-seronegative' and leukoreduced 'CMV-safe' are two distinct, source-defined mitigation strategies with different qualifying tests -- donor anti-CMV IgG negativity versus residual WBC count -- and should not be conflated.

source_references.locator: Mastery Nodes, Subdomain Node 52 - CMV Mitigation Strategies
source_references.supports: Defines CMV-seronegative units as from donors lacking IgG antibodies to CMV, and separately defines leukoreduction to <5x10^6 WBCs as the basis of the 'CMV-safe' strategy, establishing these as distinct mitigation approaches.

source_references.locator: Advanced Review Nodes, Subdomain Node 48 - Pathogen Inactivation Technologies
source_references.supports: States amotosalen+UVA pathogen reduction effectively replaces the need for CMV testing, supporting that pathogen reduction is an alternative to, not a method of, CMV serologic testing.

source_references.locator: Board Curriculum, Domain VI - Infectious Hazards of Transfusion
source_references.supports: States NAT has significantly reduced the window period for HIV, HCV, and HBV, used to show NAT is described in these sources as applying to other agents rather than to CMV seronegative-status determination.

review.notes: SRC-012 explicitly defines CMV-seronegative units as 'from donors who lack IgG antibodies to CMV,' matching key A. Distractor B (<5x10^6 WBC) is correctly tied to the separate 'CMV-safe' LR strategy per the same node. Distractor C's claim that amotosalen+UVA 'replaces the need for...CMV testing' is directly supported by SRC-002 line 57. Distractor D's NAT claim (reduces window period for HIV/HCV/HBV, not tied to CMV serostatus) is directly supported by SRC-010 (Domain VI, line 87), and the locator label matches the actual source heading. Option E's IgM framing is not itself asserted as a sourc

questions.stem: A CMV-seronegative woman at 26 weeks' gestation requires an intrauterine transfusion for severe fetal anemia due to red cell alloimmunization. Intrauterine transfusion recipients and seronegative pregnant women are among the specific populations for whom CMV mitigation is described as critical, and CMV-seronegative donor units are reserved for protocols where leukoreduced 'CMV-safe' components alone are not deemed sufficient. Which of the following components is the most appropriate choice for CMV risk reduction in this case?

options.text: Washed, non-leukoreduced RBC units
RBC units treated with amotosalen and UVA light (pathogen reduction)
CMV-seronegative, leukoreduced RBC units
Gamma-irradiated, non-leukoreduced RBC units
Standard prestorage-leukoreduced RBC units from CMV serostatus-unknown donors, without additional seronegative selection

questions.explanation_correct: The sources identify intrauterine transfusion recipients and seronegative pregnant women as populations for whom CMV mitigation is critical, and reserve true CMV-seronegative donor units for highly specific protocols where leukoreduction alone is not deemed sufficient. Leukoreduction is additionally described as a standard measure to reduce CMV transmission risk, so a seronegative, leukoreduced unit provides the most complete risk-reduction combination described in the sources for this highest-risk scenario.

option_explanations.C: Correct: combines true seronegative donor selection -- specifically reserved for protocols such as intrauterine transfusion -- with leukoreduction, which is separately described as required to decrease CMV transmission.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: leukoreduced-only 'CMV-safe' units are appropriate for most at-risk patients, but the source specifically reserves true seronegative units for the highest-risk protocols named, including intrauterine transfusion and seronegative pregnant women.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: gamma irradiation is described in the sources as preventing TA-GVHD by inactivating donor T lymphocytes; it is not described as a CMV risk-reduction mechanism.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: washing is not described in the sources as a CMV mitigation strategy, and this option omits leukoreduction, the cell-removal step tied to CMV risk reduction.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: per the sources, pathogen reduction with amotosalen/UVA is currently FDA-cleared only for platelets and plasma, while RBC pathogen-reduction systems remain in clinical trials and are not an available option for RBC units.

questions.teaching_point: For the highest-risk CMV scenarios named in the sources (intrauterine transfusion, seronegative pregnant women), true CMV-seronegative donor units -- combined with standard leukoreduction -- are reserved over leukoreduced-only 'CMV-safe' product; irradiation, washing, and RBC pathogen reduction are not substitutes for CMV mitigation.

source_references.locator: Mastery Nodes, Subdomain Node 52 - CMV Mitigation Strategies
source_references.supports: Identifies seronegative pregnant women and intrauterine transfusion recipients as risk populations for whom CMV mitigation is critical, and states seronegative units are reserved for highly specific protocols where leukoreduced 'safe' status is insufficient.

source_references.locator: Clinical Principles of Erythrocytes and Blood Components, Prevention section
source_references.supports: States leukocyte reduction is required to decrease CMV transmission, supporting leukoreduction as a standard component of CMV risk reduction alongside seronegative selection.

source_references.locator: Board Curriculum, Domain V - Non-infectious Adverse Effects
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, used to establish that irradiation addresses a different hazard (TA-GVHD), not CMV.

source_references.locator: Advanced Review Nodes, Subdomain Node 48 - Pathogen Inactivation Technologies
source_references.supports: States pathogen inactivation is currently FDA-cleared for platelets and plasma while RBC PI systems are in clinical trials, supporting that RBC pathogen reduction is not yet an available option.

review.notes: Underlying facts are each individually sourced: SRC-012 lists intrauterine transfusion recipients and seronegative pregnant women as risk populations for whom CMV mitigation is critical, and separately states seronegative units are 'reserved for highly specific protocols where safe is not deemed sufficient'; SRC-024 states LR is required to decrease CMV transmission generally; SRC-010 supports irradiation-for-TA-GVHD and RBC pathogen-reduction-still-in-trials distractor eliminations. However, the source never explicitly states the combined recommendation 'seronegative AND leukoreduced together

questions.subdomain: Window Period and Risk of Transmission
questions.stem: A blood bank medical director is teaching residents about strategies to reduce transfusion-transmitted cytomegalovirus (CMV). The transfusion service uses universal leukoreduction of all cellular blood components as one such strategy. Which of the following best explains the underlying biological mechanism by which leukoreduction lowers the risk of CMV transmission?

options.text: Leukoreduction filters physically trap free, cell-free CMV virions suspended in the donor's plasma
Leukoreduction stimulates production of anti-CMV antibody within the unit that neutralizes residual virus
Leukoreduction lowers the WBC count enough to prevent HLA alloimmunization, and it is this anti-HLA effect that secondarily eliminates CMV transmission risk
CMV is a cell-associated (leukocyte-associated) virus that persists latently within donor white blood cells; removing the WBCs from the component removes the principal reservoir of infectious virus
Leukoreduction disrupts CMV nucleic acid through riboflavin/psoralen and UV light cross-linking, the same mechanism used for pathogen inactivation of platelets and plasma

questions.explanation_correct: CMV is a leukocyte-associated (cell-associated) virus that establishes latency within donor white blood cells. Leukoreduction (<5x10^6 WBCs/unit) physically removes this WBC reservoir from the component, which is why the sources list leukoreduction as preventing CMV transmission alongside FNHTR and HLA sensitization, and describe universal leukoreduction as reducing the risk of CMV, HTLV, and EBV transmission.

option_explanations.D: Correct: matches the source-grounded rationale that leukoreduction reduces CMV risk by removing the leukocyte reservoir that harbors the latent virus.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources tie leukoreduction's CMV benefit to removal of white blood cells, not to filtration of free plasma virions; this describes an unsupported alternative mechanism.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: riboflavin/psoralen plus UV light cross-linking describes Pathogen Inactivation, a distinct technology the source applies to platelets/plasma, not the physical WBC-removal process of leukoreduction.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: leukoreduction is a filtration process; the sources describe no antibody-generating or neutralizing effect from leukoreduction.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the source lists HLA-sensitization prevention and CMV-transmission prevention as parallel, independent benefits of leukoreduction, not as a single mechanism where one causes the other.

questions.teaching_point: Leukoreduction reduces CMV transmission risk because CMV is a leukocyte-associated virus; distinguish this mechanism from pathogen inactivation (nucleic-acid disruption) and from irradiation (T-cell inactivation for TA-GVHD prevention).

source_references.locator: Domain IV, Blood Component Modification
source_references.supports: Leukoreduction (<5x10^6 WBCs/unit) is listed as preventing FNHTR, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission.

source_references.locator: Prevention section
source_references.supports: Universal leukoreduction reduces the risk of CMV, HTLV, and EBV transmission.

source_references.locator: Domain IV, Blood Component Modification
source_references.supports: Pathogen Inactivation (riboflavin/psoralen + UV light) is described as a separate technology used to disrupt pathogen nucleic acids in platelets/plasma, distinct from leukoreduction.

review.notes: Key A is the only defensible option: B (filters trapping free plasma virions), D (antibody production), and E (HLA-sensitization causally driving CMV prevention) are all clearly refuted, and C is correctly distinguished from Pathogen Inactivation (SRC-044: 'Utilizes Riboflavin/Psoralen + UV light to disrupt pathogen nucleic acids in platelets/plasma'). However, the central mechanistic premise of the correct-answer explanation -- that 'CMV is a cell-associated (leukocyte-associated) virus that persists latently within donor white blood cells' -- is never stated in any of the 4 sources. SRC-044

questions.subdomain: Window Period and Risk of Transmission
questions.stem: A regular platelet donor is tested at the time of donation and found to be CMV IgG-antibody negative. Three weeks before this donation, the donor had an unexplained mild febrile illness that resolved without medical evaluation. Despite the negative CMV serology, the collected unit is still considered to carry some residual risk of transmitting CMV to a susceptible recipient. Which of the following best accounts for this residual risk?

options.text: CMV screening is performed by individual-donation NAT with a defined window period of approximately 7-9 days, identical to that used for HIV
CMV antibody assays detect viral antigen rather than antibody, so by definition no window period can exist for this test
The donor may have been within the window period of a recent primary CMV infection, during which virus can be present in leukocytes before a detectable antibody response has developed
Because CMV is not among the agents on the FDA's mandatory allogeneic donor testing list, the concept of a window period does not apply to CMV
The donor's febrile illness three weeks earlier indicates a laboratory reporting error rather than a true window-period donation

questions.explanation_correct: The window period is defined as the interval between infection and the point at which a test (NAT or serology) can detect the agent, and residual risk of transfusion-transmitted infection stems primarily from window-period donations. Applied to CMV serologic (antibody-based) screening, a donor with a recent primary infection can be antibody-negative (seronegative) yet still harbor virus within leukocytes before seroconversion, explaining continued residual risk despite a negative result.

option_explanations.C: Correct: applies the general window-period/residual-risk framework to a CMV-seronegative donor with a recent unexplained febrile illness consistent with unrecognized primary CMV infection.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this conflates CMV serologic screening with individual-donation NAT and misapplies the approximate HIV NAT window period figure to CMV, which the sources do not establish as an ID-NAT-screened analyte with that window.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: a 'CMV-seronegative' designation refers to absence of detectable antibody, not antigen; this option misstates what the test detects and draws an unsupported conclusion that no window period exists.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: window period is a general property of any test based on time-to-detectability and is independent of whether an agent appears on the mandatory testing list; CMV's absence from that list reflects testing policy, not the absence of a window-period concept.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: introduces an unsupported explanation (lab error) not grounded in the sources, distracting from the correct window-period concept.

questions.teaching_point: The window-period concept (interval between infection and test detectability) applies generally to serologic as well as NAT-based screening; a seronegative result does not exclude very recent infection, which is part of the rationale for leukoreduction as an alternative/complementary CMV-risk-reduction strategy.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts section
source_references.supports: Defines window period as the time between infection and detectability by NAT or serology, and residual risk as remaining risk primarily due to window-period donations.

source_references.locator: Prevention section
source_references.supports: Describes CMV-reduced-risk components as produced via leukoreduction or use of CMV-seronegative donors, implying CMV donor screening is serology (antibody)-based.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach, Mandatory Testing (Allogeneic)
source_references.supports: The mandatory allogeneic donor testing list (HBV, HCV, HIV, HTLV, syphilis, WNV) does not include CMV, distinguishing CMV from agents with mandated universal testing.

review.notes: This item most directly matches the assigned blueprint node. Key A correctly generalizes SRC-013's window-period definition ('duration between infection and the time when a test (NAT or serology) can detect the agent') to CMV serologic screening. B is correctly rejected: the sources establish an HIV NAT window of ~7-9 days (SRC-044) but never establish a CMV ID-NAT window of 7-9 days; SRC-038's 'CMV-seronegative donors' phrasing implies CMV screening (where done) is serology-based, not NAT-based, so B conflates two different agents/technologies. D is correctly rejected using SRC-013's mandator

questions.subdomain: Window Period and Risk of Transmission
questions.stem: A 900-gram neonate born at 27 weeks' gestation requires a small-volume RBC transfusion. The neonate's mother is CMV-seronegative, and the transfusion service follows institutional policy to provide CMV-reduced-risk cellular components for at-risk neonates. Which of the following component selections best satisfies this requirement?

options.text: A washed RBC unit, because washing removes the plasma proteins responsible for harboring CMV
A unit treated with riboflavin/psoralen and UV-based pathogen inactivation, the method described for eliminating CMV from RBC units
An irradiated RBC unit, because irradiation inactivates donor leukocytes and thereby eliminates CMV transmission risk
A leukoreduced RBC unit (a CMV-seronegative unit would also be an acceptable alternative)
No special modification is required, because universal leukoreduction completely eliminates all risk of CMV transmission

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that CMV transmission risk is addressed by providing CMV-reduced-risk components, defined as either leukoreduced units or CMV-seronegative units, and that neonates specifically require dedicated units or CMV-reduced components. A leukoreduced RBC unit (or, equivalently, a CMV-seronegative unit) is therefore the appropriate selection for this at-risk premature neonate.

option_explanations.D: Correct: matches the source-defined strategy of providing CMV-reduced-risk components (leukoreduced or CMV-seronegative) for at-risk neonates.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: a classic board trap. Irradiation (25 Gy) is described as preventing TA-GVHD by blocking donor T-cell proliferation, a distinct purpose from CMV-risk reduction; the sources list irradiation and leukoreduction/CMV-seronegative selection as separate component modifications with different targets.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: pathogen inactivation via riboflavin/psoralen and UV light is described in the sources as applied to platelets and plasma, not RBC units, and is not identified as the CMV-risk-reduction strategy for RBCs.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: overstates the effect of leukoreduction; the sources consistently describe it as reducing CMV risk ('CMV-reduced-risk'), not eliminating it entirely, so a deliberate CMV-reduced-risk selection is still indicated by policy.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: misattributes the mechanism of CMV transmission to plasma proteins; the sources frame CMV as leukocyte-associated (the basis for leukoreduction's benefit), and washing is not described as a CMV-risk-reduction strategy.

questions.teaching_point: For CMV-vulnerable patients (e.g., low-birth-weight neonates), 'CMV-reduced-risk' means leukoreduced or CMV-seronegative components; do not conflate this with irradiation, which is a separate modification aimed at preventing TA-GVHD.

source_references.locator: Prevention section
source_references.supports: CMV transmission risk is reduced through use of CMV-reduced-risk components, defined as leukoreduced or CMV-seronegative units.

source_references.locator: Domain VII, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Neonates (infants <4 months) require specific blood banking practices such as dedicated units or CMV-reduced components.

source_references.locator: Domain IV, Blood Component Modification
source_references.supports: Irradiation (25 Gy) is described as preventing TA-GVHD via inactivation of T-cell proliferation, a purpose distinct from leukoreduction's CMV-transmission-prevention role.

source_references.locator: Domain IV, Blood Component Modification
source_references.supports: Pathogen inactivation (riboflavin/psoralen + UV) is described as used for platelets/plasma, not identified as an RBC CMV strategy.

review.notes: Best-grounded of the three items. SRC-038 states verbatim: 'CMV Transmission: Use of CMV-reduced-risk components (leukoreduced or CMV-seronegative)', directly matching option A, and SRC-010 supports the general neonatal requirement ('Neonates ... require specific blood banking practices, such as the use of dedicated units or CMV-reduced components'). Distractor B (irradiation) is a strong, well-sourced classic trap correctly distinguished via SRC-044/SRC-010's TA-GVHD/T-cell-inactivation framing, distinct from CMV-risk reduction. C is correctly rejected because Pathogen Inactivation is describ

questions.stem: A blood center medical director is asked during a regulatory inspection why the standard infectious disease testing algorithm applied to every allogeneic donation — which includes nucleic acid testing (NAT) for viral RNA/DNA and antibody-based serologic assays — does not include a laboratory test for variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (vCJD). Which of the following best explains the current basis for excluding at-risk donors for this prion disease?

options.text: No FDA-approved laboratory test (serologic or nucleic acid-based) exists to detect vCJD in donated blood, so risk reduction depends solely on donor history/questioning
Universal leukoreduction has been shown to reduce vCJD transmission risk in the same way it reduces CMV, HTLV, and EBV risk, making donor questioning unnecessary
Individual-donation NAT (ID-NAT), which is mandated for Zika virus, is also mandated for vCJD
A repeatedly reactive vCJD screening assay triggers supplemental testing, such as Western blot, analogous to the HIV confirmatory algorithm
Minipool NAT (MP-NAT), the same platform used for other transfusion-transmitted agents, is used to detect the vCJD prion protein in pooled donor samples

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state explicitly that vCJD (along with malaria) relies solely on donor questioning because no FDA-approved screening test exists for donors. This makes donor history/deferral, rather than any laboratory assay, the operative safety mechanism for this agent — unlike HIV, HCV, HBV, WNV, HTLV, syphilis, T. cruzi, and Babesia, all of which have defined serologic or NAT-based testing.

option_explanations.A: Correct — directly matches the source statement that vCJD screening relies solely on donor questioning because no FDA-approved test exists.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — MP-NAT is described only for agents with defined nucleic acid assays (e.g., pooled testing balancing cost/sensitivity); no vCJD NAT exists per the sources.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — supplemental/confirmatory testing (e.g., Western blot for HIV) is described only for agents with an initial reactive screening assay; there is no vCJD screening assay to trigger this pathway.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — ID-NAT is specifically tied to Zika virus in the sources; it is not described as extended to vCJD.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — leukoreduction is credited in the sources only with reducing CMV, HTLV, and EBV transmission risk; no source statement extends this benefit to prion disease.

questions.teaching_point: For vCJD, unlike virtually every other relevant transfusion-transmitted infection, there is no validated laboratory screening assay of any kind (serologic or NAT); donor history and deferral are the sole risk-mitigation mechanism, and this is a repeated board trap.

source_references.locator: Domain VI High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor questioning because no FDA-approved screening tests exist.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach — Questioning Only; Definitions; Prevention
source_references.supports: Repeats that vCJD (and malaria) rely solely on donor history because no FDA-approved screening test exists for donors; also defines NAT, MP-NAT, ID-NAT/Zika, supplemental testing, and leukoreduction's documented benefits (CMV/HTLV/EBV).

review.notes: SRC-013 ('Questioning Only: Malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor history because no FDA-approved screening tests exist for donors') and SRC-010 ('Malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor questioning as no FDA-approved screening tests exist') directly support key A. Distractors are all correctly grounded as false: MP-NAT/ID-NAT are described only for agents with defined NAT assays (HIV/HCV/HBV/Zika), supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot) is tied to reactive screening assays that don't exist for vCJD, and leukoreduction's documented benefit in the sources is explicitly limited to CMV/HTLV/EBV

questions.stem: A regular whole blood donor completes the full mandatory infectious disease testing panel required for every allogeneic donation, with the following results: HBsAg negative, anti-HBc negative, HBV DNA negative, anti-HCV negative, HCV RNA negative, anti-HIV-1/2 negative, HIV-1 RNA negative, anti-HTLV-I/II negative, WNV RNA negative, and syphilis testing negative. Regional Babesia NAT was also negative. Two years later, the donor is diagnosed with vCJD. Which statement about the donor's original testing results is most accurate?

options.text: A fully negative mandatory testing panel does not exclude vCJD infection, because no serologic or nucleic acid test for the prion disease is included in, or available for, routine donor screening
The negative HIV-1 RNA NAT result should have detected the prion agent, since NAT is described as an ultrasensitive nucleic-acid detection method
The negative syphilis testing result indicates the donor was also free of prion disease, since both agents are cleared using the same nontreponemal/treponemal algorithm
Had the anti-HTLV-I/II result been repeatedly reactive, the resulting supplemental testing algorithm would also have screened for vCJD
Because regional Babesia NAT was negative, this also rules out prion disease transmission from this donor

questions.explanation_correct: The mandatory allogeneic testing panel described in the sources (HBsAg, anti-HBc, HBV DNA, anti-HCV, HCV RNA, anti-HIV-1/2, HIV-1 RNA, anti-HTLV-I/II, WNV RNA, syphilis) plus regional Babesia NAT does not include any assay for vCJD, and the sources state no FDA-approved test for vCJD exists. Therefore a completely negative panel provides no information about vCJD status; donor history/questioning is the only screening tool for this agent.

option_explanations.A: Correct — none of the listed mandatory or regional tests screen for vCJD, and no such test exists per the sources, so a negative panel cannot exclude it.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — NAT is defined as detecting viral RNA or DNA of the specific agents it is validated for (e.g., HIV, HCV, HBV); the sources do not describe a vCJD NAT assay.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — supplemental testing pathways (e.g., for HTLV) are agent-specific confirmatory algorithms; the sources do not describe them as screening for unrelated agents like vCJD.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — the syphilis RPR/treponemal algorithm is specific to syphilis serology and is not described as applicable to prion disease.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — Babesia NAT is a regionally required, agent-specific nucleic acid test and has no described role in prion disease detection.

questions.teaching_point: A negative result on the full mandatory/regional infectious disease testing panel says nothing about vCJD status, because no laboratory assay for vCJD is part of that panel or exists at all — donor history remains the only screening tool.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach; Laboratory Findings; Questioning Only
source_references.supports: Lists the complete mandatory allogeneic testing panel, defines regional Babesia NAT, supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot for HIV), and the syphilis RPR/treponemal 'save' algorithm; separately states vCJD relies solely on donor history because no FDA-approved test exists.

review.notes: The listed mandatory panel (HBsAg, anti-HBc, HBV DNA, anti-HCV, HCV RNA, anti-HIV-1/2, HIV-1 RNA, anti-HTLV-I/II, WNV RNA, syphilis) exactly matches SRC-013's 'Mandatory Testing (Allogeneic)' list, and Regional Babesia NAT matches 'Regional Testing.' Since no vCJD test exists per both sources, key A is well supported. Distractor rationales (NAT is agent/nucleic-acid specific, supplemental testing is agent-specific, syphilis algorithm is syphilis-specific, Babesia NAT is agent-specific) are all consistent with source definitions. Flagging low-severity duplicate-coverage: this item retests the s

questions.stem: Several weeks after donating a unit of whole blood that was subsequently transfused to a patient, a donor contacts the blood center to disclose a history that, if elicited at the time of donation, would have led to deferral based on vCJD risk. No FDA-approved laboratory test exists to test the donor's archived sample or to test the transfused component retrospectively for vCJD. Which of the following best describes the blood center's regulatory obligation regarding notification of the transfusion recipient?

options.text: Because vCJD is classified as a Relevant Transfusion-Transmitted Infection (RTTI) along with HIV, HBV, and HCV, mandatory look-back procedures identical to those infections must be followed

Federal regulation (21 CFR 610.46/47) requires the blood center to retrieve and quarantine remaining components and notify the recipient within 3 calendar days, exactly as for a reactive HIV or HCV test
Recipient notification (look-back) for vCJD is not mandated by federal regulation as it is for HIV and HCV; the decision to notify is instead governed by professional/institutional guidance
The blood center should apply the FDA-defined donor reentry algorithm used for HCV-deferred donors (including its defined waiting period) to determine whether recipient notification is required
No action of any kind is required or appropriate, because donor history obtained after donation has no role in blood safety

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that mandatory recipient notification is legally required only for HIV and HCV (per 21 CFR 610.46/47); for other agents, including prion disease, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance rather than federally mandated. This is explicitly flagged as a common board trap distinguishing mandatory versus recommended look-back.

option_explanations.C: Correct — matches the sources' explicit statement that only HIV and HCV look-backs are legally mandated, with other agents governed by professional guidance.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — the 3-calendar-day retrieval/quarantine requirement is described only for reactive HIV, HCV, WNV, ZIKV, or T. cruzi tests; vCJD is not included, and there is no reactive vCJD test to trigger this rule.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — the sources' RTTI list includes HIV, HBV, HCV, HTLV, syphilis, WNV, T. cruzi, Babesia, and malaria; vCJD is not listed among these, so the mandatory RTTI look-back framework does not apply.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — the FDA-defined donor reentry algorithm with a waiting period is described specifically for deferred donors such as those deferred for HCV, not for post-donation vCJD risk disclosures.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — this overstates the case; the sources indicate notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance for non-HIV/HCV agents, so some action may still be appropriate even though it is not federally mandated.

questions.teaching_point: Because vCJD has no laboratory test, there is nothing to trigger the reactive-result-driven mandatory processes (3-day retrieval/quarantine, RTTI look-back); recipient notification for vCJD-risk donations is governed by professional guidance, not the federal HIV/HCV look-back mandate.

source_references.locator: Management — Retrieval and Quarantine, Mandatory Recipient Notification, Donor Reentry; Definitions — RTTI; Common Board Traps
source_references.supports: States mandatory recipient notification is legally required only for HIV and HCV, with other agents governed by AABB/FDA guidance; separately lists the RTTI agents (excluding vCJD), the 3-day retrieval/quarantine rule (excluding vCJD), and the HCV-specific donor reentry algorithm; flags 'mandatory vs. recommended look-back' as a board trap.

review.notes: Directly matches SRC-013's Common Board Traps section verbatim in substance: 'Only HIV and HCV look-backs are required by federal law (21 CFR 610.46/47); others are governed by professional guidelines,' and Management section: 'Mandatory Recipient Notification: Legally required for HIV and HCV... For other agents, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance.' The citation '21 CFR 610.46/47' used in option B is taken directly from the source, not invented. Distractors are correctly grounded as false: the 3-day retrieval/quarantine rule is explicitly scoped to reactive HIV/HCV/WNV/ZIKV/T. c

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man donates whole blood and answers "no" to all Donor History Questionnaire items regarding recent illness, fever, or travel; he reports feeling entirely well. Several days later, the blood center's screening laboratory reports that his unit is reactive for West Nile virus (WNV) RNA by nucleic acid testing (NAT), performed during a period of increased regional mosquito activity. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism by which this donor was able to transmit WNV despite a negative symptom history?

options.text: WNV can only be transmitted when the donor is manifesting overt neuroinvasive disease such as meningitis or encephalitis at the time of donation
Like Treponema pallidum, WNV is inactivated by routine 4°C refrigerated storage of red cells, so transmission occurred only because the unit had been stored at room temperature
WNV establishes a chronic, persistent carrier state in blood donors analogous to hepatitis B, allowing transmission indefinitely after the initial infection has resolved
Donor questioning alone is sufficient to identify all WNV-infectious donors, so the reactive NAT result most likely represents a laboratory false positive
Most WNV infections are asymptomatic, and the donor was viremic during this stage, so his blood contained detectable viral RNA even though he had no symptoms to report on donor screening

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe WNV infection as typically asymptomatic, with fever, headache, or rare neuroinvasive disease occurring only in a minority of cases. Because donors are viremic (RNA-detectable) during this largely asymptomatic phase, their blood can be infectious even though donor history/questioning reveals nothing concerning — which is precisely why mandatory WNV RNA NAT testing of every donation exists as an independent safety layer beyond donor questioning.

option_explanations.E: Correct: matches the source description of WNV as typically asymptomatic with viremia present regardless of symptoms, explaining why NAT (not history) is required to detect infectious donors.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: this reverses the source's contrast — syphilis transmission is rare specifically because refrigerated (4°C) storage inactivates the treponeme; WNV is an RNA virus screened for by RNA NAT precisely because it is not eliminated by routine cold storage.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: no chronic/persistent WNV carrier state is described in the sources; this fabricates an analogy to hepatitis B that is not supported.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: contradicts the source statement that WNV is "typically asymptomatic," with neuroinvasive disease being a rare presentation, not a prerequisite for infectivity or transmission.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources indicate WNV requires mandatory RNA testing on every donation (unlike malaria/vCJD, which rely solely on donor questioning because no FDA-approved test exists), showing that questioning alone is explicitly considered insufficient for WNV.

questions.teaching_point: WNV transfusion-transmission occurs because most infections are asymptomatic during the viremic phase, so donor history cannot reliably exclude infectious donors — mandatory RNA-based NAT, not symptom screening, is the key safety layer.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations - WNV bullet
source_references.supports: WNV is typically asymptomatic but can present with fever, headache, and rarely neuroinvasive disease (meningitis/encephalitis).

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach - Mandatory Testing (Allogeneic)
source_references.supports: Every allogeneic donation must be tested for WNV RNA as part of mandatory infectious disease testing.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations - Syphilis bullet
source_references.supports: Syphilis transmission is rare specifically because of inactivation by 4°C refrigerated storage, used as contrast for distractor B.

source_references.locator: Domain VI High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor questioning because no FDA-approved screening test exists, implying agents like WNV that do have an approved NAT require more than questioning alone.

review.notes: Core facts are grounded: SRC-013 explicitly states WNV is 'typically asymptomatic, but can present with fever, headache, and rarely neuroinvasive disease' and that 'every donation must be tested for... WNV RNA' (mandatory NAT). Distractor B's syphilis/4°C contrast is directly grounded ('transmission is extremely rare due to 4°C storage'). Distractor C (chronic carrier state) and D (transmission only during neuroinvasive disease) are correctly identified as unsupported/contradicted. Minor gap: the stem's explicit causal chain 'the donor was viremic during this [asymptomatic] stage' is a reasona

questions.stem: A regional blood center normally screens all donations for West Nile virus (WNV) using minipool NAT (MP-NAT), in which 6 to 16 individual donor samples are combined and tested as a single pool. When local mosquito surveillance data indicate a seasonal increase in WNV activity, the blood center switches its algorithm to individual-donation NAT (ID-NAT) testing for WNV. What is the laboratory rationale for this change in testing strategy?

options.text: ID-NAT for WNV is a year-round federal requirement that is unrelated to regional mosquito activity or seasonal viremia rates
Transcription-mediated amplification (TMA) cannot detect WNV RNA in a pooled specimen, so individual testing is the only technology capable of identifying WNV at all
ID-NAT is more sensitive for detecting low viral loads because it eliminates the dilutional effect of pooling, allowing detection of donors with early or low-level viremia that MP-NAT could miss
ID-NAT is performed because a reactive WNV result requires confirmatory Western blot testing, which can only be run on an individual donor sample
Switching to ID-NAT reduces testing costs by allowing the laboratory to combine WNV RNA testing with syphilis serology into a single larger pool

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that MP-NAT balances cost and sensitivity by pooling 6-16 samples, but that ID-NAT is more sensitive for low viral loads and is specifically triggered for WNV during peak activity (mosquito) seasons. Pooling dilutes a low-titer positive sample among many negatives, which can push a weakly reactive result below the assay's detection threshold; testing each donation individually removes this dilution effect and preserves sensitivity when regional transmission risk is highest.

option_explanations.C: Correct: directly matches the source statement that ID-NAT is more sensitive for low viral loads and is triggered for WNV during peak activity seasons, versus MP-NAT which pools samples to balance cost and sensitivity.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: TMA is described as a method that detects viral RNA including WNV; MP-NAT itself is a NAT-based (RNA-detecting) method applied to pooled specimens, so pooling does not make WNV RNA undetectable, only less sensitive at low titers.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources describe supplemental confirmatory testing (e.g., Western blot) in the context of HIV, not WNV; no WNV-specific confirmatory Western blot requirement is described.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: fabricated rationale not supported by the sources; ID-NAT means testing individually (unpooled), which is the opposite of combining tests into a larger pool.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: contradicted directly by the source statement that ID-NAT is triggered for WNV specifically during periods of peak/active mosquito seasons, i.e., it is seasonally and regionally variable, not a constant year-round mandate.

questions.teaching_point: MP-NAT balances cost and sensitivity via pooling, but pooling dilutes low viral loads; ID-NAT is used for WNV during peak transmission seasons specifically to preserve sensitivity for donors with low-level viremia.

source_references.locator: Definitions - Minipool vs Individual Donation NAT; High-Yield Comparison Points - ID-NAT vs MP-NAT
source_references.supports: MP-NAT pools 6-16 samples to balance cost and sensitivity; ID-NAT is more sensitive for low viral loads and is triggered for WNV during peak activity seasons.

source_references.locator: TMA definition
