source_references.supports: TMA is an isothermal amplification method using RNA polymerase and reverse transcriptase that detects viral RNA, including WNV.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings - Supplemental Testing
source_references.supports: Supplemental confirmatory testing (e.g., Western blot) is described for HIV specifically, grounding why this is not a WNV requirement.

review.notes: Directly grounded by SRC-013: 'ID-NAT is more sensitive for low viral loads and is triggered for WNV during peak activity seasons' and 'MP-NAT tests pools of 6 to 16 samples to balance cost and sensitivity.' Distractor B is correctly refuted since MP-NAT itself is a pooled NAT/TMA-based method (SRC-004 confirms TMA detects WNV RNA), so pooling does not make WNV undetectable. Distractor C (WNV-specific Western blot) is unsupported/fabricated -- source only ties Western blot to HIV supplemental testing. D is fabricated and E is directly contradicted by the 'triggered... during peak activity seas

questions.stem: A donor's plasma and RBC unit are collected on the same day. Three days after donation, the blood center receives a WNV RNA-reactive NAT result on this donation. The RBC unit has already been transfused, but the co-collected plasma unit remains in the blood center's inventory, unshipped. According to the FDA/AABB infectious-disease safety standards described in these sources, what is the most appropriate combination of actions?

options.text: Quarantine and retrieve the remaining in-date plasma unit within 3 calendar days of the reactive result; notify the RBC recipient's physician, understanding that this recipient notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance rather than a federal legal mandate for WNV
No retrieval or quarantine action is required for WNV-reactive donations, because federal retrieval/quarantine requirements apply only to HIV- and HCV-reactive donations
Quarantine of the remaining plasma unit must be completed within 24 hours of the reactive result, and any recipient notification is entirely optional with no guidance recommending it
Because WNV is transmitted only through plasma-derived products, the already-transfused RBC recipient requires no further follow-up regardless of guidance
Recipient notification for the transfused RBC unit is legally mandated for WNV under federal law, identical in force to the mandatory notification requirements for HIV and HCV

questions.explanation_correct: The sources specify that retrieval and quarantine of remaining in-date components must be initiated within 3 calendar days of a reactive HIV, HCV, WNV, ZIKV, or T. cruzi test — so the unshipped plasma unit must be quarantined/retrieved on this timeline. Separately, mandatory recipient notification by federal law applies only to HIV and HCV; for other RTTIs such as WNV, notifying the recipient (or their physician) is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance rather than legally required, so option A correctly reflects both obligations.

option_explanations.A: Correct: combines the 3-calendar-day retrieval/quarantine requirement (which explicitly includes WNV) with the correct distinction that WNV recipient notification is guidance-recommended, not federally mandated.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the retrieval and quarantine requirement is explicitly stated to apply to reactive HIV, HCV, WNV, ZIKV, and T. cruzi results, not solely HIV/HCV.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the sources state that mandatory recipient notification is legally required only for HIV and HCV; other agents, including WNV, fall under recommended (not mandated) guidance-based notification.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: misstates the timeline (the source specifies 3 calendar days, not 24 hours) and mischaracterizes notification as having no guidance at all, when AABB/FDA guidance does recommend it.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: fabricated restriction; the sources describe mandatory WNV RNA testing applied to all allogeneic donations generally, with no statement limiting WNV transmission risk to plasma-derived products only.

questions.teaching_point: For WNV-reactive donations, retrieval/quarantine of remaining in-date components must begin within 3 calendar days, but formal recipient notification — though recommended by AABB/FDA guidance — is legally mandated only for HIV and HCV, not WNV.

source_references.locator: Management - Retrieval and Quarantine
source_references.supports: Retrieval and quarantine of remaining components must be initiated within 3 calendar days of a reactive HIV, HCV, WNV, ZIKV, or T. cruzi test.

source_references.locator: Management - Mandatory Recipient Notification; Common Board Traps - Mandatory vs Recommended Look-back
source_references.supports: Mandatory recipient notification is legally required only for HIV and HCV; for other agents, including WNV, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance rather than mandated.

source_references.locator: Definitions - RTTI
source_references.supports: WNV is classified among the FDA-recognized Relevant Transfusion-Transmitted Infections (RTTIs) requiring donor screening.

review.notes: Well-grounded on two explicit SRC-013 statements: 'Retrieval and Quarantine: Must be initiated within 3 calendar days of a reactive HIV, HCV, WNV, ZIKV, or T. cruzi test' and 'Mandatory Recipient Notification: Legally required for HIV and HCV. For other agents, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance,' reinforced by the Common Board Traps bullet citing 21 CFR 610.46/47 limiting mandatory look-back to HIV/HCV. B is directly contradicted (WNV is explicitly listed among agents requiring the 3-day retrieval/quarantine rule). C is directly contradicted (notification legally mandated only f

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man receives a unit of packed RBCs that had been stored continuously at 1-6°C for 28 days. About 45 minutes into the transfusion he develops rigors, a temperature rise from 37.0°C to 39.6°C, tachycardia, and hypotension. The transfusion is stopped; Gram stain of the residual unit shows gram-negative rods, and cultures of both the patient's blood and the unit grow Yersinia enterocolitica. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiologic basis for this organism's classic association with RBC units rather than platelet units?

options.text: The extended 42-day shelf life achieved with additive solutions is the dominant risk factor for gram-negative sepsis, regardless of an organism's temperature tolerance

Yersinia enterocolitica is a psychrophilic (cold-growing) gram-negative organism capable of proliferating during refrigerated (1-6°C) RBC storage, unlike the typical skin-flora organisms seen with platelets
Universal leukoreduction performed on RBC units selectively removes gram-positive organisms while allowing gram-negative organisms to persist
RBC (whole blood) collections omit the initial 30-40 mL volume-diversion step that is used to reduce skin-origin contamination in platelet donations
Yersinia enterocolitica is a gram-positive skin commensal that proliferates at room temperature, similar to organisms implicated in platelet contamination

questions.explanation_correct: RBC components are refrigerated for weeks, an environment that gram-positive skin flora tolerate poorly but that psychrophilic (cold-growing) gram-negative organisms such as Yersinia enterocolitica can exploit to proliferate. This is why the sources contrast RBC units (cold-growing gram-negatives) with platelets, which are stored at room temperature and are instead typically contaminated by gram-positive skin organisms such as Staphylococcus aureus.

option_explanations.E: Incorrect - this reverses the pattern described in the sources: gram-positive skin organisms are the classic contaminant of room-temperature-stored platelets, not refrigerated RBCs, and Yersinia is gram-negative, not gram-positive.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - the sources describe leukoreduction as reducing transmission of leukocyte-associated viruses (CMV, HTLV, EBV); it is not described as a mechanism that selectively filters bacteria by Gram stain type.
option_explanations.B: Correct - the sources specifically attribute RBC contamination to cold-growing gram-negative organisms like Yersinia enterocolitica, distinguishing it from the gram-positive skin flora typical of room-temperature platelet contamination.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect - the sources describe initial volume diversion of the first 30-40 mL as a general blood-collection practice to reduce skin-origin contamination; they do not describe this step as being omitted for whole blood/RBC collection.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - while additive solutions do extend RBC shelf life to 42 days, the sources tie the RBC/gram-negative association specifically to the organism's cold-growth capability rather than to storage duration alone.

questions.teaching_point: Bacterial contaminant type tracks storage temperature: refrigerated RBCs select for psychrophilic gram-negative organisms (e.g., Yersinia enterocolitica), while room-temperature platelets favor gram-positive skin flora (e.g., S. aureus).

source_references.locator: High-Yield Comparison Points: RBC vs. Platelet Contaminants
source_references.supports: States that RBCs are typically contaminated by cold-growing gram-negative organisms such as Yersinia enterocolitica, while platelets are typically contaminated by gram-positive skin organisms such as S. aureus.

source_references.locator: Prevention: Volume Diversion
source_references.supports: Describes initial volume diversion (30-40 mL) as a general prevention measure against skin-origin bacterial contamination, used to refute the distractor claiming RBC collections omit this step.

source_references.locator: Domain VI High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Establishes that bacterial contamination, especially of room-temperature-stored platelets, is the most significant infectious risk, providing background context for the RBC/platelet contrast.

review.notes: Core fact is squarely and explicitly grounded: SRC-015 'High-Yield Comparison Points: RBC vs. Platelet Contaminants' states 'RBCs are often contaminated by cold-growing Gram-negatives (e.g., Yersinia enterocolitica); Platelets are usually contaminated by Gram-positives from donor skin (e.g., S. aureus)' — this directly supports key C and refutes A. Distractor B is correctly refuted (sources tie leukoreduction to CMV/HTLV/EBV, not bacterial Gram-type filtering). Distractor D is correctly refuted (SRC-013 'Prevention: Volume Diversion' describes 30-40mL diversion as a general collection practice

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman receiving pooled platelets develops fever (39.6°C from a baseline of 37.4°C), rigors, and hypotension 20 minutes into the transfusion. Nursing staff stops the infusion and notifies the blood bank of a suspected transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection. In addition to drawing blood cultures from the patient, which specimen should the blood bank obtain from the implicated unit to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: A pilot tube retained from the time of the donor's original phlebotomy
The integrally attached tubing segments from the unit, since they remain sterile and reflect donor-side contamination
A newly collected unit from the same donor's next scheduled donation
The residual platelet concentrate remaining in the primary bag itself
The platelet additive solution used during component processing

questions.explanation_correct: When a transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection is suspected, the transfusion is stopped and the correct confirmatory workup is to culture both the patient's blood and the residual component still in the primary bag. The attached segments are specifically excluded as an appropriate source because they do not reliably represent the contents of the bag at the time of the reaction.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect - the sources explicitly exclude the tubing segments as the specimen for confirming a suspected transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection.
option_explanations.D: Correct - the sources direct culturing of the remaining component in the bag itself (not the segments) alongside the patient's blood to confirm the diagnosis.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - a pilot tube reflects the donor's blood at the time of phlebotomy, not the bacterial status of the stored, implicated component at the time of the reaction, and is not the specimen identified in the sources.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - the additive solution alone, apart from the residual product remaining in the bag, is not the specimen source specified by the sources for confirmatory culture.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - a subsequent donation from the same donor does not reflect the bacterial status of the unit that caused the reaction and is not part of the described TTBI workup.

questions.teaching_point: Confirmatory culture for suspected transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection requires the residual product in the primary bag plus the patient's blood - never the attached segments, which do not reliably reflect bag contents.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach: Suspected TTBI Workup
source_references.supports: States that when a transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection is suspected, the transfusion should be stopped and both the patient's blood and the residual component in the bag (not the segments) should be cultured.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings: Bacterial Testing
source_references.supports: Confirms that mandatory bacterial testing for platelets is performed via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue methods, supporting the emphasis on culturing the actual component.

review.notes: Directly and explicitly grounded: SRC-015 'Diagnostic Approach: Suspected TTBI Workup' states 'Stop transfusion; culture both the patient's blood and the remaining component in the bag (not the segments)' — this is a verbatim match for key B and explicitly excludes option A (segments), which is the classic board trap. Options C, D, and E (pilot tube, additive solution, next donation) are not mentioned anywhere in the sources as appropriate specimens and are correctly refuted as unsupported. Locators verified accurate. Minor flag: this question and question_index 2 both hinge substantially on t

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia is receiving a pooled platelet transfusion when, 15 minutes in, he develops rigors, a temperature rise of 2.3°C above baseline, tachycardia, and hypotension. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Obtain a direct antiglobulin test and haptoglobin level while continuing the transfusion, prioritizing evaluation for an acute hemolytic reaction
Slow the infusion rate, administer acetaminophen, and continue the transfusion if the fever resolves within 30 minutes
Repeat ABO/Rh typing on the patient and obtain a urinalysis for hemoglobinuria before taking any further action
Stop the transfusion immediately, provide supportive care with broad-spectrum antibiotics, and send cultures of the patient's blood and the residual unit
Administer diphenhydramine and continue the transfusion at the original rate

questions.explanation_correct: This presentation (high fever with a rise of at least 2°C, rigors, tachycardia, and hypotension) is the classic picture of transfusion-associated sepsis/transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection, most often seen with room-temperature-stored platelets. Management requires immediately stopping the transfusion, giving the recipient supportive care and broad-spectrum antibiotics, and sending cultures of the patient's blood and the residual unit (not the segments) to confirm the diagnosis.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect - the sources direct that the transfusion be stopped when this reaction pattern occurs; slowing the rate and continuing based on symptomatic response is not the described management.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - antihistamines address allergic symptoms, not septic shock, and the sources do not support continuing the transfusion once this presentation occurs; the transfusion must be stopped.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - continuing the transfusion while pursuing a hemolysis-focused workup is inconsistent with the source-directed requirement to stop the transfusion immediately once a severe reaction is suspected.
option_explanations.D: Correct - the sources specify stopping the transfusion, giving immediate supportive care with broad-spectrum antibiotics for the recipient, and culturing the patient's blood plus the residual unit to confirm transfusion-associated sepsis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - delaying cessation of the transfusion to pursue additional hemolysis-directed testing does not match the source-directed sequence of stopping the transfusion first and initiating supportive/antibiotic therapy.

questions.teaching_point: A high fever (≥2°C rise) with rigors, tachycardia, and hypotension during a platelet transfusion should be treated as transfusion-associated sepsis until proven otherwise: stop the transfusion, give supportive care and broad-spectrum antibiotics, and culture the patient and residual unit.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations: Transfusion-Associated Sepsis (TTBI); Management: Sepsis
source_references.supports: Describes transfusion-associated sepsis as high fever (≥2°C rise), rigors, tachycardia, and profound hypotension, most common with room-temperature platelets, and specifies immediate supportive care and broad-spectrum antibiotics as management.

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls: Bacterial Contamination
source_references.supports: Reinforces that bacterial contamination is the most frequent infectious cause of transfusion-related death, particularly with platelets, underscoring the urgency of stopping the transfusion.

review.notes: Well grounded by combining two explicit source statements in SRC-015: 'Clinical Presentations: Transfusion-Associated Sepsis (TTBI)' ('high fever [>=2C rise], rigors, tachycardia, and profound hypotension; most common with room-temperature platelets') plus 'Management: Sepsis' ('Immediate supportive care and broad-spectrum antibiotics for the recipient') and 'Diagnostic Approach: Suspected TTBI Workup' ('Stop transfusion; culture both the patient's blood and the remaining component in the bag'). The stem's 2.3C rise with rigors/tachycardia/hypotension matches the sourced TTBI definition precis

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man receiving an apheresis platelet transfusion develops shaking chills, a temperature rise from 37.0°C to 39.6°C, and a systolic blood pressure drop from 130 to 80 mmHg within 20 minutes of the start of the infusion. The transfusion is stopped, and cultures of both the patient's blood and the residual platelet unit grow Staphylococcus epidermidis with matching antibiograms. Which of the following best explains how this organism most likely entered the blood component?

options.text: Gamma irradiation of the component failed to sterilize the bacterial contaminant prior to release
The donor had asymptomatic bacteremia at the time of donation, seeding the component directly from the bloodstream
Continuous agitation of the platelet unit during storage generated the organism de novo within the concentrate
A plug of donor skin containing commensal bacterial flora was introduced into the collection bag during venipuncture
A cold-tolerant, Gram-negative organism proliferated in the unit during storage at 1-6°C

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that the majority of platelet bacterial contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora, such as Staphylococcus epidermidis, introduced as a skin plug at the venipuncture site during phlebotomy. This matches both the organism identified and the classic mechanism described for platelet contamination.

option_explanations.D: Correct: sources identify introduction of skin-plug commensal flora during phlebotomy as the predominant source of platelet bacterial contamination, consistent with a Gram-positive skin organism like S. epidermidis.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: cold-tolerant Gram-negative organisms proliferating during refrigerated (1-6°C) storage is the classic mechanism described for RBC contamination (e.g., Yersinia enterocolitica), explicitly contrasted with platelets, which are stored at 20-24°C, not refrigerated.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources attribute the great majority of platelet contamination to skin flora introduced at phlebotomy rather than to unrecognized donor bacteremia; the organism identity and clinical setting here point to the phlebotomy mechanism.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: room-temperature storage with continuous agitation promotes proliferation of an organism already introduced into the unit; it does not generate bacteria de novo.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: gamma irradiation of cellular components is performed to inactivate donor T-lymphocytes and prevent TA-GVHD, not to sterilize bacterial contaminants; it is unrelated to bacterial mitigation strategies.

questions.teaching_point: Platelet bacterial contamination is overwhelmingly due to Gram-positive skin flora introduced as a skin plug during phlebotomy, in contrast to the cold-growing Gram-negative organisms (e.g., Yersinia enterocolitica) implicated in refrigerated RBC contamination.

source_references.locator: Node 15 - Bacterial Mitigation in Platelets, Core Concepts/Contaminant Sources
source_references.supports: States most platelet bacterial contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora (e.g., S. epidermidis) introduced during phlebotomy, and that platelets are stored at 20-24°C with agitation, which promotes proliferation.

source_references.locator: Node 15, Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: Board trap contrasting RBC contamination by cold-growing Gram-negative organisms (e.g., Yersinia enterocolitica) at 4°C with platelet Gram-positive skin-flora contamination.

source_references.locator: Domain V - Hazards of Transfusion, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, establishing irradiation's purpose is unrelated to bacterial sterilization.

review.notes: SRC-061 Node 15 explicitly states most platelet bacterial contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora (e.g., S. epidermidis) introduced during phlebotomy, contrasts this with cold-growing Gram-negative organisms (e.g., Yersinia) implicated in RBC contamination, states agitation at 20-24C 'promotes bacterial proliferation' (not de novo generation, refuting D), and SRC-010 confirms gamma irradiation targets donor T-lymphocytes for TA-GVHD prevention, not bacterial sterilization (refuting E). All stem facts, the key, and each distractor rationale are grounded. Independently the vignette (skin organ

questions.stem: A blood center's quality department reviews a case in which an apheresis platelet unit was released after a primary bacterial culture, obtained 4 hours after collection, returned negative. Eight days later, a recipient of that unit develops transfusion-associated sepsis, and Staphylococcus epidermidis matching the donor's skin flora is isolated from the residual unit. Which of the following best explains this false-negative culture result?

options.text: Staphylococcus epidermidis is a Gram-negative organism and therefore evades detection on standard aerobic culture media
Point-of-issue rapid testing, rather than primary culture-based testing, was used, and this method only detects high bacterial titers late in storage
The sample was collected before the small bacterial inoculum from the skin plug had time to replicate to a detectable level
Pathogen inactivation treatment of the component suppressed bacterial replication at the time of culture without eliminating viable organisms

questions.explanation_correct: The sources note that sampling too early after collection (e.g., less than 24 hours) can produce a false-negative bacterial culture because the bacterial load introduced at phlebotomy has not yet proliferated to a detectable threshold. A sample obtained only 4 hours after collection falls within this false-negative window, and the organism recovered later matches the expected skin-flora source.

option_explanations.C: Correct: matches the sources' stated board trap that culturing too early (<24 hours post-collection) yields false negatives because bacterial load is still below the detection threshold.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: S. epidermidis is a Gram-positive organism, consistent with the typical skin-flora contaminants described for platelets; the false negative here is a timing issue, not a Gram-stain/detection-method issue.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the stem specifies a primary culture-based test was performed, not point-of-issue rapid testing, which is a distinct method used just before transfusion to detect high-titer bacteria in older units; substituting this explanation misidentifies the actual early-sampling mechanism.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: pathogen inactivation is a distinct preventive technology (e.g., amotosalen/UVA crosslinking of nucleic acids) that the vignette does not describe as being used; the sources link false-negative cultures specifically to early sampling, not to PI interference with culture results.

questions.teaching_point: Bacterial culture screening of platelets can yield false-negative results if sampling occurs too early after collection, before the bacterial inoculum introduced at phlebotomy has proliferated to a detectable level.

source_references.locator: Node 15, Common Board Traps and Distractors - Culture Sampling Time
source_references.supports: States that sampling too early (<24 hours post-collection) may cause false-negative bacterial cultures because bacterial load is too low to detect.

source_references.locator: Node 15, Definitions - Primary Testing / Point-of-Issue Testing
source_references.supports: Describes primary testing (LVDS/culture-based) versus point-of-issue rapid testing as distinct screening strategies with different detection windows.

source_references.locator: Node 15, Prevention - Pathogen Inactivation
source_references.supports: Describes pathogen inactivation (amotosalen and UVA light) as crosslinking nucleic acids to prevent bacterial and viral replication, a prevention rather than a culture-detection mechanism.

review.notes: SRC-061 Node 15 'Common Board Traps' states sampling too early (e.g., <24 hours post-collection) may cause false-negative cultures because bacterial load is too low to detect -- this directly supports A, and the stem's 4-hour sample time is comfortably within that window (arguably a stronger/cleaner example than the source's own <24h boundary). Definitions section supports the Primary Testing vs Point-of-Issue Testing distinction used to refute C, and the Prevention section supports the amotosalen/UVA PI description used to refute D. B is correctly refuted since the source explicitly labels S.

questions.stem: A patient receiving an apheresis platelet transfusion develops rigors, a temperature increase of 2.5°C, and a systolic blood pressure drop to 78 mmHg within 15 minutes of starting the infusion. Transfusion-associated sepsis from bacterial contamination is suspected. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Continue the transfusion at the current rate while administering an antihistamine and notifying the physician
Send only a post-reaction patient blood sample for culture; the residual unit can be discarded since transfusion has already occurred
Administer epinephrine and steroids, and discontinue the transfusion permanently without further workup
Stop the transfusion immediately, notify the blood center, and send both the patient's blood and the residual unit (including the bag) for culture
Slow the infusion rate, give acetaminophen, and resume the transfusion once the fever resolves

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that when transfusion-associated sepsis (TAS) is suspected, the immediate action is to stop the transfusion, notify the blood center, and culture both the patient and the remaining component, including the bag itself. A high fever increase (>2°C) with hypotension is explicitly flagged as requiring a bacterial workup rather than being managed as a simple febrile non-hemolytic reaction.

option_explanations.D: Correct: directly matches the sources' stated immediate management for suspected TAS - stop transfusion, notify blood center, culture patient and residual unit including the bag.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this describes management of a mild, uncomplicated FNHTR; the sources specifically warn that a high fever (>2°C) plus hypotension is a bacterial-contamination distractor that mandates a bacterial workup, not simple antipyretic therapy with resumption of the unit.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: epinephrine and steroids with permanent discontinuation and no culture workup describes management of anaphylaxis, not suspected bacterial sepsis; the sources call for culturing the patient and residual unit when TAS is suspected.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: continuing the transfusion during a suspected septic reaction is unsafe; the sources indicate immediate cessation is required for suspected reactions other than isolated urticaria.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources specify that the remaining component, including the bag itself, must also be cultured, not discarded.

questions.teaching_point: Suspected transfusion-associated sepsis mandates immediate cessation of the transfusion, blood-center notification, and culture of both the patient and the implicated unit (including the bag) - a distinct pathway from benign FNHTR or anaphylaxis management.

source_references.locator: Node 15, Management - Immediate Action
source_references.supports: States that if TAS is suspected, the immediate action is to stop the transfusion, notify the blood center, and culture the patient and remaining component including the bag itself.

source_references.locator: Node 32 - Non-Hemolytic Acute Reactions, Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: Notes that FNHTR often mimics early sepsis, and that a high fever (>2°C) or hypotension requires a bacterial workup (Gram stain/culture of the bag).

source_references.locator: Node 31/32, Diagnostic Approach and Management
source_references.supports: States immediate cessation of transfusion is mandatory for all suspected reactions except simple urticaria, and describes anaphylaxis management (epinephrine, steroids, permanent discontinuation) as a distinct pathway.

review.notes: SRC-061 Node 15 'Immediate Action' states verbatim that suspected TAS requires stopping the transfusion, notifying the blood center, and culturing the patient and remaining component including the bag -- exact match for A and for the refutation of E (bag must be cultured, not discarded). SRC-058 Node 32 confirms FNHTR management (antipyretics, no automatic resumption implied) differs from this pathway (refuting B), Node 32 confirms anaphylaxis management is epinephrine/steroids/permanent discontinuation without the culture workup (refuting C), and Node 31 confirms immediate cessation is mandat

questions.stem:
A blood collection facility uses an apheresis/whole-blood collection set with an integrated diversion pouch. The first 30–40 mL of blood drawn at venipuncture is shunted into this side pouch and is not used for the therapeutic component; only blood collected after this initial volume enters the main collection bag or apheresis circuit.

Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which this diversion step reduces the risk of transfusion-transmitted bacterial sepsis?

options.text: It lowers the pH of the collected unit to a level unfavorable for bacterial growth
It removes residual donor leukocytes that could harbor and later release intracellular bacteria during storage
It strips plasma proteins that would otherwise serve as a nutrient source for contaminating organisms
It dilutes the citrate anticoagulant concentration in the main collection bag, which otherwise favors bacterial replication

It preferentially captures the skin plug and associated skin-flora bacteria dislodged by the needle at venipuncture, removing much of the potential bacterial inoculum before it reaches the main collection bag

questions.explanation_correct: The approved source explicitly links initial-volume diversion to reducing skin-origin bacterial contamination: the needle passing through the donor's skin can dislodge a core (plug) of skin tissue carrying resident skin flora, and this contaminated first aliquot is preferentially captured in the diversion pouch rather than the main bag, lowering the bacterial inoculum that ultimately enters the component.

option_explanations.D: Incorrect — the source describes diversion as reducing skin-origin bacterial contamination, not as an anticoagulant-dilution mechanism; no source content links diversion to citrate concentration.
option_explanations.E: Correct — diversion of the first 30–40 mL specifically targets skin-flora bacteria introduced by the venipuncture needle, per the source's stated rationale for this prevention strategy.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — this describes a rationale closer to leukoreduction (which the source ties to CMV/HTLV/EBV risk reduction), not to volume diversion, and conflates two distinct prevention strategies.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — no source content supports a pH-based mechanism for diversion; this is a plausible-sounding but unsupported distractor.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — no source content supports a plasma-protein-depletion mechanism for diversion; this is an unsupported distractor.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8, Prevention section, 'Volume Diversion' bullet
source_references.supports: States that volume diversion (collecting the initial 30-40 mL of blood in a side bag) is a prevention strategy specifically to reduce skin-origin bacterial contamination.

review.notes: Core claim is supported: SRC-013 L61 states 'Volume Diversion: Collecting the initial 30-40 mL of blood in a side bag to reduce skin-origin bacterial contamination.' However, the specific mechanistic detail asserted in option B and the explanation - that the needle 'dislodge[s] a core (plug) of skin tissue' which is 'explicitly' linked by the source - is NOT present in any of the 4 approved sources (SRC-003, SRC-010, SRC-011, SRC-013). That exact 'skin plug' terminology only appears in a different, non-approved source file (transfusion-medicine-waiha-serology-and-platelet-bacterial-mitigation.

questions.stem:
A hospital transfusion service is preparing to issue a single-donor apheresis platelet unit. Shortly before release, the unit undergoes a rapid point-of-issue immunoassay for bacterial contamination and the result is negative. Earlier in its storage period, the same unit had also been screened by automated culture, which was also negative.

Which of the following is the most accurate interpretation of these two negative bacterial screening results?

options.text: They substantially reduce, but do not eliminate, the risk of transfusion-transmitted bacterial sepsis, since bacterial contamination remains the most frequent infectious cause of transfusion-related death despite mandatory platelet testing
They confirm the unit is sterile, equivalent to a unit that has undergone pathogen inactivation
They indicate the donor no longer requires health history screening on future donations
They should prompt reflex testing of the unit for Treponema pallidum by nucleic acid amplification before release
They eliminate the need for any further clinical monitoring of the recipient for signs of a septic transfusion reaction

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that bacterial testing (by automated culture or rapid point-of-issue test) is mandatory for platelets, yet separately identify bacterial contamination as currently the most frequent infectious cause of transfusion-related death, particularly with platelets. Read together, a negative screening result lowers but does not abolish risk — consistent with the source's general concept of residual risk persisting despite layered safety measures.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect — the source treats bacterial detection testing (culture/rapid assay) and pathogen inactivation (which damages nucleic acids to reduce infectivity) as distinct strategies; a negative screening test does not equate to inactivation and does not establish sterility.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — because residual risk persists despite mandatory testing, ongoing clinical vigilance for a septic transfusion reaction remains appropriate; this option overstates what a negative result guarantees.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — unit-level bacterial testing and the donor health history questionnaire are separate, independently required components of the safety system; a negative unit test result does not exempt the donor from future screening.
option_explanations.A: Correct — this reflects the source's residual-risk framing and its explicit statement that bacterial contamination remains the leading infectious cause of transfusion-related death even with mandatory platelet testing in place.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — this conflates bacterial contamination screening with syphilis (Treponema pallidum) testing, which the source describes as a separate mandated test for a different transfusion-transmitted infection, not a bacterial-contamination confirmatory step.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8: Core Concepts ('Residual Risk'), Diagnostic Approach ('Bacterial Testing'), and Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls ('Bacterial Contamination')
source_references.supports: States bacterial testing is mandatory for platelets via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests, defines residual risk as persisting despite all safety layers, and separately states bacterial contamination is the most frequent infectious cause of transfusion-related death, especially with platelets.

source_references.locator: Domain VI: Infectious Hazards of Transfusion, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States bacterial contamination is currently considered the most significant infectious risk, especially in platelets stored at room temperature.

review.notes: Both underlying facts are directly stated in the sources: SRC-013 'Bacterial Testing: Mandatory for platelets; detected via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests' and SRC-013 'Bacterial Contamination: Currently considered the most frequent infectious cause of transfusion-related death, particularly with platelets' (echoed in SRC-010 L89: 'most significant infectious risk, especially in platelets stored at room temperature'). Combining these into 'testing reduces but does not eliminate risk' is a reasonable, defensible synthesis. Minor stretch: the source's formal 'Residual Risk' defi

questions.stem:
A hospital transfusion medicine physician is asked by administration to identify the strategy that is mandated specifically to reduce the risk of bacterial sepsis in recipients of platelet transfusions (as opposed to other transfusion hazards such as CMV transmission or transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease).

Which of the following is the best answer?

options.text: Gamma irradiation of all platelet units
Pathogen inactivation technology applied to all platelet units
Culture-based testing (automated culture or a rapid point-of-issue test) performed on the platelet unit to detect bacterial contamination
Universal leukoreduction of all platelet units
Refrigerated storage of platelets at 4°C, similar to red blood cell storage

questions.explanation_correct: The source explicitly states that bacterial testing, by automated culture or rapid point-of-issue test, is mandatory for platelets — making it the current, required strategy directed specifically at bacterial contamination risk, distinct from other interventions in the sources that target different hazards.

option_explanations.D: Incorrect — the source ties universal leukoreduction to reducing CMV, HTLV, and EBV transmission risk, not bacterial contamination; a classic board trap that conflates two separate prevention strategies.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — the source ties gamma irradiation to preventing TA-GVHD by inactivating donor T lymphocytes, not to bacterial contamination.
option_explanations.C: Correct — the source explicitly describes bacterial testing (culture or rapid point-of-issue assay) as mandatory for platelets, making it the strategy directly and specifically targeted at bacterial contamination risk.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect (in this context) — the source describes pathogen inactivation as 'currently approved' for platelets and plasma, which is a weaker designation than the 'mandatory' bacterial testing requirement; it is a real but not universally mandated strategy per the source language.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — the source states platelets are currently stored at room temperature (the reason bacterial contamination is a significant risk for this component); refrigerated 4°C storage of platelets is not described as an accepted strategy in the sources.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8: Diagnostic Approach ('Bacterial Testing') and Prevention section ('Pathogen Inactivation', 'Universal Leukoreduction')
source_references.supports: States bacterial testing is mandatory for platelets via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests; also lists pathogen inactivation as 'currently approved' (not stated as mandatory) for platelets and plasma, and universal leukoreduction as reducing CMV/HTLV/EBV risk.

source_references.locator: Domain VI: Infectious Hazards of Transfusion, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States bacterial contamination is the most significant infectious risk especially in platelets stored at room temperature, supporting that platelets are not standard-stored at refrigerator temperature.

source_references.locator: Domain V: Hazards of Transfusion, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes, grounding this as a distractor tied to a different hazard.

review.notes: Well grounded in the approved sources. SRC-013: 'Bacterial Testing: Mandatory for platelets; detected via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests' directly supports C as the mandated bacterial-specific strategy. SRC-010 L75: 'TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes' correctly grounds B as a distractor tied to a different hazard. SRC-013 L63: 'Universal Leukoreduction: Reduces the risk of CMV, HTLV, and EBV transmission' correctly grounds A as tied to different hazards. SRC-013 L59: Pathogen Inactivation is described as 'current

questions.stem: As part of routine apheresis platelet collection, the blood center diverts the first 30 to 40 mL of collected blood into a separate satellite pouch that is never used to prepare the final platelet component. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which this step lowers the risk of bacterial contamination of the platelet product?

options.text: The diverted aliquot is treated with amotosalen and UVA light, which crosslinks bacterial nucleic acids before the aliquot is discarded
The diverted aliquot undergoes an automated culture in real time, and phlebotomy is only allowed to continue once that culture is confirmed negative

The diverted aliquot preferentially captures the plug of epidermis/dermis and adherent resident skin flora dislodged by the phlebotomy needle, separating most of this initial bacterial inoculum from the blood that enters the final component
The diverted aliquot is immediately refrigerated to 1-6°C, suppressing growth of the cold-tolerant Gram-negative organisms most likely to contaminate the unit
The diverted aliquot is used to remove donor leukocytes, preventing intracellular bacteria carried within leukocytes from reaching the final component

questions.explanation_correct: Most bacterial contaminants recovered from platelets are Gram-positive skin flora (e.g., Staphylococcus epidermidis) introduced at the moment of phlebotomy. Diverting the initial 30-40 mL into a side pouch physically removes the blood most likely to carry the needle-cored skin plug and associated flora, reducing the inoculum that reaches the collection bag before the final component is made.

option_explanations.C: Correct: this is the stated mechanism - diversion targets the skin-plug/flora carried in with the needle, and platelet contaminants are predominantly Gram-positive skin organisms introduced during phlebotomy.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: amotosalen/UVA crosslinking is pathogen inactivation (PI) technology applied to the finished platelet or plasma component, not a treatment applied to the diverted aliquot.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: leukocyte removal is accomplished by leukoreduction, a distinct process aimed at reducing CMV/HTLV/EBV transmission, not bacterial contamination, and it is not the function of the diversion pouch.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes the rationale for refrigerated RBC storage (where cold-growing Gram-negatives such as Yersinia enterocolitica can proliferate at 4°C); platelets are stored at 20-24°C and diversion involves no refrigeration step.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: primary bacterial testing (culture or NAT) is performed on the processed component after collection as a separate layer of protection, not as a real-time gate on the diverted aliquot during phlebotomy.

questions.teaching_point: Initial volume diversion works by physically excluding the needle-cored skin plug and its resident flora from the main collection, targeting the dominant Gram-positive skin-flora source of platelet TTBI - it is not a testing, cooling, or pathogen-inactivation step.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 15, Core Concepts/Definitions
source_references.supports: Describes initial volume diversion of the first 30-40 mL into a side pouch to reduce skin-plug contamination, and that most platelet contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora introduced during phlebotomy.

source_references.locator: Domain X, Prevention
source_references.supports: Lists initial volume diversion (30-40 mL) alongside skin disinfection and bacterial culture/NAT as platelet bacterial-mitigation strategies, and separately describes pathogen inactivation (amotosalen/UVA) as a distinct technology.

source_references.locator: Domain VI, High-Yield Comparison Points
source_references.supports: Confirms RBCs are more often contaminated by cold-growing Gram-negatives (e.g., Yersinia enterocolitica) versus platelets by Gram-positive skin organisms (e.g., S. aureus), supporting why the refrigeration distractor is wrong for platelet diversion.

review.notes: Core claim matches sources directly: SRC-061 defines Initial Volume Diversion as diverting first 30-40mL 'to reduce skin-plug contamination' and states most platelet contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora (e.g., S. epidermidis); SRC-013/SRC-015 corroborate 30-40mL diversion for skin-origin contamination and RBC-vs-platelet Gram-negative/Gram-positive contrast. Option A's phrasing ('plug of epidermis/dermis... dislodged by the phlebotomy needle') is a reasonable elaboration of the source's terse 'skin-plug contamination' language, not an invented fact. Distractors B (PI applied to aliquot),

questions.stem: A blood center performs donor skin disinfection and initial volume diversion for every collection, and platelet units subsequently undergo primary bacterial culture, with the sample drawn within the first 24 hours after collection. One apheresis platelet unit is cultured under this protocol and reported negative, then issued and transfused on day 5 of its permitted storage. Shortly after the transfusion starts, the recipient develops a temperature rise of more than 2°C and profound hypotension. The transfusion is stopped, and culture of the returned bag (not the segment) grows Staphylococcus epidermidis. Which of the following best explains why the original primary culture failed to detect this contamination despite skin disinfection and volume diversion having been performed?

options.text: A point-of-issue rapid test, rather than a primary culture, should have been performed at the time of collection in order to detect this contamination
Because skin disinfection and volume diversion were both performed, the organism must have been introduced into the bag after the unit reached the transfusion service rather than during collection
Staphylococcus epidermidis is a Gram-negative organism that requires anaerobic culture conditions not used in standard primary bacterial testing

Volume diversion completely eliminates Gram-positive skin flora from the collected unit, so the growth on the returned bag culture most likely reflects contamination of the culture bottle itself rather than the unit

The primary culture sample was likely drawn too early after collection, when the residual bacterial load was below the limit of detection; the organism then proliferated to a pathogenic titer over the ensuing days of room-temperature, agitated platelet storage

questions.explanation_correct: Sampling for bacterial culture too early after collection (e.g., under 24 hours) can produce a false-negative result because the initial bacterial load, even after skin disinfection and diversion have reduced it, may still be below the detection threshold. Platelets are stored at 20-24°C with continuous agitation, conditions that favor bacterial proliferation, so a low initial inoculum can grow to a clinically significant, sepsis-causing titer by day 5 of storage - consistent with the recipient's fever/rigor/hypotension picture and the organism (S. epidermidis, a typical Gram-positive skin contaminant) recovered from the bag.

option_explanations.E: Correct: early sampling (<24 hours post-collection) is a recognized cause of false-negative primary bacterial cultures because bacterial load is too low to detect at that time, and platelet storage conditions permit subsequent proliferation.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: Staphylococcus epidermidis is explicitly described as a Gram-positive skin organism, and is the typical contaminant recovered from platelets - not a Gram-negative organism requiring anaerobic culture.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: point-of-issue testing is a rapid test performed just before transfusion of older units to catch high-titer bacteria; it is a complementary, later-stage strategy, not a substitute for primary culture at the time of collection, and its absence does not explain why the primary culture itself was falsely negative.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: skin disinfection and diversion reduce, but do not eliminate, the risk of skin-flora contamination at phlebotomy; the clinical and microbiologic pattern described (Gram-positive skin organism, sepsis on day 5) is classic for contamination introduced at collection, not after receipt by the transfusion service.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: diversion reduces but does not completely eliminate skin-flora contamination, and the recipient's fever, rigors, and hypotension indicate a true septic transfusion reaction, not a false-positive bag culture.

questions.teaching_point: Volume diversion and skin disinfection reduce the bacterial inoculum but do not guarantee a sterile unit; if primary culture is drawn too early, a low-level contaminant can escape detection and proliferate during platelet storage to cause a true septic transfusion reaction later in shelf life.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 15, Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States that sampling too early (<24 hours post-collection) can cause false-negative bacterial cultures due to low bacterial load, and that most platelet contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora such as S. epidermidis.

source_references.locator: Domain VI, Clinical Presentations/Diagnostic Approach
source_references.supports: Describes TAS clinical presentation as high fever (>2°C rise), rigors, and profound hypotension, and describes the correct workup of culturing the remaining component in the bag itself, not the segment.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8, Diagnostic Approach
source_references.supports: Confirms bacterial testing for platelets is performed via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests, and that platelet storage is at conditions promoting proliferation, framing culture timing as relevant to detection sensitivity.

review.notes: Core answer well grounded: SRC-061's 'Common Board Traps' section states sampling <24h post-collection can cause false-negative cultures due to low bacterial load, and its 'Core Concepts' states platelet storage (20-24C, continuous agitation) promotes bacterial proliferation -- together these fully support option A's reasoning. SRC-015 correctly supports the TAS clinical picture (>2C fever, rigors, hypotension) and the bag-not-segment culture workup. Distractors B (S. epidermidis mislabeled Gram-negative), C (point-of-issue testing conflated with primary culture), D/E (diversion/disinfection c

questions.stem: A hospital transfusion service's blood supplier performs donor skin disinfection and initial 30-40 mL volume diversion for every apheresis platelet collection. The transfusion service's medical director is asked whether these two measures alone are sufficient to safely release platelet components without any additional bacterial risk-reduction step. What is the best response?

options.text: No - skin disinfection and volume diversion reduce, but do not eliminate, the risk of bacterial contamination; current practice also requires mandatory bacterial culture (or NAT) or use of an approved pathogen inactivation technology before or at release of the platelet component
No - pathogen inactivation is mandatory for every platelet unit regardless of culture status, since it is the only accepted bacterial risk-reduction strategy
Yes - because platelet components are gamma irradiated before release, and irradiation destroys any residual bacterial contamination introduced during collection
No - point-of-issue rapid testing must always replace volume diversion, since it is the only strategy capable of detecting bacterial contamination
Yes - skin disinfection and volume diversion alone satisfy all current bacterial risk-reduction requirements for platelet components

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources describe platelet bacterial mitigation as a layered strategy: skin disinfection and initial volume diversion (30-40 mL) reduce the bacterial inoculum introduced at phlebotomy, but mandatory bacterial culture or NAT (or, alternatively, pathogen inactivation, which may replace the need for certain bacterial cultures) is still required before/at release. Diversion and disinfection alone are described as risk-reduction steps upstream of, not a replacement for, this testing requirement.

option_explanations.A: Correct: sources list initial volume diversion and skin disinfection together with mandatory bacterial culture or NAT as the components of platelet bacterial mitigation - diversion/disinfection are not described as sufficient on their own.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this contradicts the explicit listing of mandatory bacterial culture or NAT as an additional required element of platelet bacterial mitigation alongside diversion and disinfection.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: irradiation of blood components is performed to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease by inactivating viable donor T lymphocytes; it is not described as a bacterial risk-reduction measure.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: point-of-issue rapid testing is a distinct strategy used just before transfusion, particularly for older units, to detect high-titer bacteria; it is described as a complement to, not a mandatory replacement for, volume diversion.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: pathogen inactivation is described as a technology that may replace the need for certain bacterial cultures (or irradiation), not as a universally mandated step required in addition to culture for every unit.

questions.teaching_point: Volume diversion and skin disinfection are pre-storage risk-reduction steps that lower, but do not eliminate, bacterial contamination risk in platelets; mandatory bacterial culture/NAT or pathogen inactivation remains a required complementary layer before release, and irradiation and point-of-issue testing serve different, non-interchangeable purposes.

source_references.locator: Domain X, Prevention/Common Board Traps
source_references.supports: States that platelet bacterial mitigation includes initial volume diversion (30-40 mL), skin disinfection, and mandatory bacterial culture or NAT, and separately notes irradiation is required for granulocytes to prevent TA-GVHD, unrelated to bacterial reduction.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 15, Definitions/Prevention
source_references.supports: Describes pathogen inactivation as potentially replacing the need for certain bacterial cultures or irradiation, and defines point-of-issue testing as a rapid test performed just before transfusion to detect high-titer bacteria in older units, distinct from primary testing at collection.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8, Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: Confirms bacterial testing is mandatory for platelets via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests as part of the overall infectious-safety framework.

review.notes: Fully grounded: SRC-031 explicitly lists 'initial volume diversion (30-40 mL), skin disinfection, and mandatory bacterial culture or NAT' together as the platelet bacterial-mitigation strategy, directly supporting that diversion/disinfection alone are insufficient. SRC-061 supports PI 'may replace the need for certain bacterial cultures or irradiation' (i.e., not mandatory for every unit regardless of culture status, refuting E) and defines point-of-issue testing as a distinct pre-transfusion strategy for older units (refuting D). Irradiation is tied only to TA-GVHD prevention via granulocyte

questions.stem: A transfusion medicine service is examining why septic transfusion reactions occur less frequently per therapeutic platelet dose with single-donor apheresis platelets than with pooled whole-blood-derived (WBD) platelet concentrates. Both product types are stored under identical conditions (20-24C with continuous gentle agitation) and both are subject to mandatory pre-release bacterial detection testing. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism responsible for this difference in bacterial contamination risk?

options.text: Apheresis collection inherently includes pathogen-inactivation treatment, a step that is not applied to WBD platelet concentrates

A single apheresis dose is collected from one donor, whereas an equivalent therapeutic WBD dose is pooled from about 4 to 6 individual whole blood donations, multiplying the number of independent venipuncture sites at which skin flora can be introduced into the final product
Bacterial culture or nucleic acid testing is performed only on apheresis platelet units and is not required for WBD platelet concentrates
Apheresis platelets are stored at a colder temperature than WBD platelets, which slows bacterial replication during the storage period
Apheresis platelet concentrates contain a lower total platelet mass than WBD units, leaving less nutrient substrate available to support bacterial growth

questions.explanation_correct: Platelets are stored at room temperature with agitation, which supports bacterial proliferation if any contaminant is introduced at collection, making the number of donor exposures per therapeutic dose the key determinant of contamination risk. An apheresis platelet dose comes from a single donor and single venipuncture, while an equivalent WBD dose requires pooling roughly 4-6 individual whole blood-derived concentrates, each collected from a separate donor at a separate venipuncture. Because skin flora introduction at phlebotomy is the principal source of platelet bacterial contamination, pooling multiplies the number of independent opportunities for a contaminating organism to enter the final product that a single patient receives, even though storage conditions and bacterial screening requirements are otherwise the same for both product types.

option_explanations.B: Correct. The source explicitly states apheresis reduces donor exposure (1 donor) compared to pooled WBD platelets, and that one apheresis unit is equivalent to a pool of 4 to 6 WBD concentrates, each representing an added independent contamination opportunity for the same recipient dose.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect distractor. Both apheresis and WBD platelet components are stored under the same conditions, 20-24C with continuous gentle agitation; storage temperature is not a differentiating factor between the two collection methods.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect distractor and factually reversed. Per the component requirements, an apheresis unit must contain at least 3.0x10^11 platelets, which is far greater than the minimum 5.5x10^10 platelets required for a single WBD unit, so apheresis products do not have a lower platelet mass, and nutrient substrate is not the described mechanism for differential contamination risk.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect distractor. Mandatory bacterial culture or NAT is described as a general prevention strategy for platelet components because platelets are high-risk for contamination; the source does not restrict this testing requirement to apheresis units only.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect distractor. Pathogen inactivation technologies such as amotosalen/UVA are described as treating plasma and platelets generally to reduce transfusion-transmitted disease risk; this is not framed as an apheresis-exclusive step versus WBD platelets.

source_references.locator: High-Yield Comparison Points; Laboratory Findings; Prevention sections
source_references.supports: States apheresis platelets reduce donor exposure (1 donor) versus pooled platelets, and that one apheresis unit equals a pool of 4-6 WBD concentrates; also states bacterial culture/NAT and pathogen inactivation are general platelet-level prevention strategies, not apheresis-exclusive.

source_references.locator: Platelet Component Requirements section
source_references.supports: Gives the platelet component minimum count requirements (WBD >=5.5x10^10 vs apheresis >=3.0x10^11) and states both product types share the same storage condition of 20-24C with continuous gentle agitation.

review.notes: Core comparative facts are explicitly grounded: SRC-031 line 39 states 'A single apheresis unit is equivalent to a pool of 4 to 6 whole-blood-derived platelet concentrates' and line 71 states 'Apheresis reduces donor exposure (1 vs. 5 donors)'; SRC-032 lines 151/153 give the WBD (>=5.5x10^10) vs apheresis (>=3.0x10^11) platelet minimums, and line 155 confirms both are 'Maintained at 20C-24C with continuous gentle agitation' under the shared Platelet Component Requirements heading. SRC-031 line 53 states bacterial culture/NAT is a generic platelet-level requirement (not apheresis-restricted, co

questions.stem: A 50-kg (110-lb) first-time volunteer meets every standard eligibility requirement for whole blood donation. The collection SOP specifies that no more than 10.5 mL of whole blood per kilogram of donor weight may be collected, inclusive of pre-donation testing samples, and that for any donor weighing less than 110 lb, both the collected blood volume and the anticoagulant volume must be proportionately reduced. What is the physiologic rationale for scaling the maximum permissible phlebotomy volume to donor body weight in this way?

options.text: The fixed anticoagulant volume in a standard collection bag is calibrated for a 110-lb donor, so larger donors require proportionally more anticoagulant to avoid under-anticoagulating the unit
Hemoglobin concentration is proportional to body weight, so donors under 110 lb cannot mount sufficient reticulocytosis to replace the withdrawn red cell mass
Citrate anticoagulant returned to the donor's circulation during collection is dose-dependent on body weight, and undersized donors are at greater risk for citrate-induced hypocalcemia

Total circulating blood volume scales with body size, so capping collection at a fixed volume per kilogram limits the fraction of a donor's blood volume removed, protecting smaller donors from a disproportionate acute reduction in blood volume
Smaller donors have a proportionally larger plasma volume relative to red cell mass, which increases their risk of post-donation hyperkalemia

questions.explanation_correct: Because total blood volume is roughly proportional to body weight, a fixed mL/kg ceiling (10.5 mL/kg, inclusive of samples) keeps the percentage of a donor's blood volume removed roughly constant regardless of size. Smaller donors have a smaller absolute blood volume, so without a weight-based cap and proportionate reduction of both blood and anticoagulant volume, they would lose a larger fraction of their circulating volume than a standard-sized donor, raising the risk of hypovolemic donor reactions. This is exactly why the source ties the maximum collection volume to donor weight and requires proportionate reduction of blood and anticoagulant volumes for donors under 110 lb.

option_explanations.D: Correct: weight-based volume capping preserves a safe percentage of estimated blood volume removed, which is the donor-safety principle underlying the 10.5 mL/kg rule and the proportional reduction requirement for donors under 110 lb.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: citrate returning to the donor's circulation and causing hypocalcemia/paresthesia is a described adverse effect of apheresis procedures, not of a standard whole blood collection, where anticoagulant is added to the collection bag rather than substantially infused into the donor.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source ties eligibility hemoglobin/hematocrit thresholds (sex-specific) to a separate criterion from body weight; no stated mechanism links body weight to reticulocyte response or red cell mass replacement.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: hyperkalemia risk in the source material is associated with storage-related potassium leak in older irradiated RBC units (particularly relevant to neonatal transfusion), not with donor body size or plasma-to-red-cell ratio.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this reverses the actual rule — it is donors who weigh less than 110 lb who require proportionately reduced blood and anticoagulant volumes, not larger donors requiring more anticoagulant.

questions.teaching_point: The maximum permissible whole blood collection volume is scaled to donor body weight (10.5 mL/kg, inclusive of samples) to keep the fraction of blood volume removed safe across donor sizes; donors under 110 lb require proportionate reduction of both blood and anticoagulant volume.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 30 – Donor Selection, Deferrals, and Physicals; Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States the 10.5 mL/kg maximum whole blood collection volume (inclusive of samples) and that blood/anticoagulant volume must be proportionately reduced for donors under 110 lb.

source_references.locator: Domain V, Advanced Technologies & Specialized Testing — Apheresis Adverse Effects
source_references.supports: Identifies citrate toxicity/hypocalcemia as an apheresis-specific adverse effect, used to construct the incorrect distractor about anticoagulant returning to donor circulation.

review.notes: The stem's factual claims (10.5 mL/kg max collection inclusive of samples; proportional reduction of blood and anticoagulant volume for donors <110 lb) are directly and verbatim confirmed in SRC-003 Node 30 ('Whole Blood Volume'). However, the keyed answer (A) asserts a specific physiologic mechanism -- 'total circulating blood volume scales with body size... limits the fraction of a donor's blood volume removed' -- that is never stated in SRC-003 or any of the four sources; the source gives the rule but not this explanatory rationale. It is standard, medically uncontroversial physiology and i

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with no cardiac history presents for a preoperative autologous whole blood donation prior to elective orthopedic surgery. She feels well and meets all other screening criteria. Her pre-donation capillary hemoglobin is 11.2 g/dL. How should this hemoglobin result be interpreted for donation eligibility?

options.text: She is ineligible to donate, because autologous units must meet the standard hematocrit cutoff of 38%, which is equivalent to the allogeneic female hemoglobin threshold of 12.5 g/dL
She is eligible to donate, but only with a physician co-signature, since the lower autologous hemoglobin threshold applies solely to donors under a defined age limit
She is ineligible to donate, because her hemoglobin is below the allogeneic female threshold of 12.5 g/dL and autologous donors must meet the same hemoglobin criteria as allogeneic donors
She is eligible to donate autologously, because the autologous minimum hemoglobin standard (≥11.0 g/dL) is less stringent than the allogeneic female threshold (≥12.5 g/dL)
She is ineligible to donate, because her hemoglobin is below the male allogeneic threshold of 13.0 g/dL, which applies regardless of donation type

questions.explanation_correct: Autologous donation standards are deliberately less stringent than allogeneic standards because the unit is intended only for the donor's own later use and the recipient-safety rationale for high allogeneic thresholds does not apply. The autologous hemoglobin minimum is ≥11.0 g/dL, versus ≥12.5 g/dL for allogeneic female donors and ≥13.0 g/dL for allogeneic male donors. At 11.2 g/dL, this donor clears the autologous threshold even though she would not qualify to donate allogeneically.

option_explanations.D: Correct: the source explicitly states autologous requirements are less stringent, citing a hemoglobin threshold of ≥11.0 g/dL, which this donor's 11.2 g/dL result satisfies.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this is the classic board trap of applying the allogeneic female threshold (12.5 g/dL) to an autologous donor; the source specifically distinguishes autologous requirements as less stringent.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: applies the wrong sex-specific allogeneic threshold (male, 13.0 g/dL) to a female autologous donor, and autologous donors are not held to allogeneic thresholds at all.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source notes autologous donation has no age limit, not a physician co-signature requirement tied to age; this option fabricates an eligibility mechanism not present in the source.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: again substitutes the allogeneic female cutoff (38% Hct / 12.5 g/dL Hb) for the correct, less stringent autologous standard.

questions.teaching_point: Autologous donation hemoglobin/hematocrit standards are lower than allogeneic standards (≥11.0 g/dL vs. ≥12.5/13.0 g/dL by sex); do not apply allogeneic thresholds to autologous donors.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 30 – Physical Examination Requirements; Common Board Traps and Distractors (Autologous vs. Allogeneic)
source_references.supports: Gives sex-specific allogeneic hemoglobin/hematocrit thresholds (male ≥13.0 g/dL, female ≥12.5 g/dL) and states autologous requirements are less stringent (Hb ≥11.0 g/dL, no age limit).

review.notes: Fully grounded in SRC-003 Node 30: allogeneic female Hb >=12.5 g/dL (Hct >=38%), allogeneic male Hb >=13.0 g/dL (Hct >=39%), and autologous Hb >=11.0 g/dL with no age limit, explicitly stated as 'less stringent' than allogeneic. The donor's 11.2 g/dL clears the 11.0 g/dL autologous floor. Every distractor rationale maps cleanly onto a real source fact (wrong threshold substituted, or a fabricated eligibility mechanism correctly identified as fabricated). No invented or contradicted facts; single best answer is unambiguous.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a remote history of severe psoriasis presents to donate whole blood. He reports having taken etretinate (Tegison) for his psoriasis, but he stopped the medication over a decade ago and is otherwise healthy, meeting all other donor eligibility criteria. What is the most appropriate donor management decision?

options.text: Accept him for donation now, since more than 3 years have elapsed since his last dose
Defer him for 1 month from his last dose, the deferral period that applies to isotretinoin/finasteride-class medications (Accutane, Proscar, Propecia)
Defer him for 6 months from his last dose, the deferral period that applies to dutasteride (Avodart)
Defer him for 3 years from his last dose, the deferral period that applies to acitretin (Soriatane)
Permanently defer him from allogeneic donation, because prior use of Tegison (etretinate) carries a permanent deferral regardless of the interval since last use

questions.explanation_correct: Among the teratogenic-medication deferrals, Tegison (etretinate) is the outlier requiring permanent deferral, distinguishing it from the related retinoid acitretin (Soriatane), which carries only a 3-year deferral. Because this donor has a documented history of Tegison use, he must be permanently deferred from allogeneic donation irrespective of how long ago he last took the drug.

option_explanations.E: Correct: the source lists Tegison as a permanent deferral, distinct from the time-limited deferrals for other retinoid/5-alpha-reductase medications.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: 3 years is the deferral period for Soriatane (acitretin), a related but distinct drug from Tegison; this is the classic board trap of conflating the two retinoids.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: 6 months is the deferral period for Avodart (dutasteride), an unrelated medication with a different deferral rationale and duration.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: 1 month is the deferral period for Proscar/Propecia/Accutane, not Tegison; applying this shorter interval would inappropriately permit donation.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: because Tegison carries a permanent deferral, no elapsed time interval, however long, restores eligibility.

questions.teaching_point: Among teratogenic acne/psoriasis medications, deferral duration varies by drug: Accutane/Proscar/Propecia = 1 month, Avodart = 6 months, Soriatane = 3 years, but Tegison (etretinate) = permanent deferral — a frequently tested distinction.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 30 – High-Yield Specific Deferrals: Medications
source_references.supports: Lists specific medication deferral periods: Proscar/Propecia/Accutane 1 month, Avodart 6 months, Soriatane 3 years, Tegison permanent.

review.notes: The core deferral-duration facts are verbatim from SRC-003 Node 30 ('High-Yield Specific Deferrals: Medications' -- Proscar/Propecia/Accutane 1 month, Avodart 6 months, Soriatane 3 years, Tegison Permanent), and the key (permanent deferral for Tegison regardless of elapsed time) is unambiguously correct and clearly the single best answer; all four distractors are explicitly and correctly tied to the other listed drugs' real deferral periods. The only ungrounded addition is the stem/rationale pairing brand names with generic names (etretinate=Tegison, and by extension acitretin=Soriatane, dutas

questions.stem: A blood center medical director is asked why prospective donors are screened for malaria and variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (vCJD) using only travel/exposure history questions on the donor history questionnaire, whereas HIV and hepatitis C virus (HCV) are screened with both a questionnaire and laboratory testing (serology/NAT). Which of the following best explains this difference in donor screening strategy?

options.text: Malaria and vCJD screening is restricted to donors in specific endemic or at-risk states, similar to regional Babesia NAT requirements
Pathogen inactivation technology approved for platelets and plasma neutralizes malaria parasites and prions, making donor testing unnecessary
No FDA-approved laboratory screening test exists for either malaria or vCJD, so donor history questioning is the only available deferral tool for these agents
Antibody testing for malaria has such a long window period that questioning alone is considered more sensitive than serologic screening
Malaria and vCJD are excluded from the FDA's list of Relevant Transfusion-Transmitted Infections (RTTIs), so laboratory testing is not required

questions.explanation_correct: Per the source material, malaria and vCJD are two of the agents for which donor eligibility relies solely on donor history/questioning because no FDA-approved laboratory screening test exists for either agent. This contrasts with RTTIs like HIV, HBV, and HCV, which have both mandatory serologic/NAT testing and history-based questions layered together as part of the FDA's blood safety framework.

option_explanations.C: Correct: the source explicitly states malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor history because no FDA-approved screening test exists for donors.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: regional, endemic-area-restricted laboratory testing is described in the source for Babesia NAT, not for malaria or vCJD, which have no lab test at all.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: malaria is explicitly listed among the FDA's Relevant Transfusion-Transmitted Infections (RTTI) in the source, alongside HIV, HBV, HCV, HTLV, Syphilis, WNV, T. cruzi, and Babesia; RTTI status does not require a lab test to exist.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this option implies a malaria antibody test exists and is simply less sensitive than questioning; the source instead states no FDA-approved test exists at all, so there is no window-period comparison to make.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: pathogen inactivation is described in the source as an approved technology for platelets and plasma to reduce infectivity, but nothing in the source suggests it substitutes for donor history screening of malaria or prion disease.

questions.teaching_point: Deferral for malaria and vCJD is history-based rather than laboratory-based because no FDA-approved screening assay exists for either agent, unlike RTTIs such as HIV, HBV, and HCV which have mandated serologic/NAT testing.
