review.notes: Well supported across all three cited sources: SRC-032 Node 17 (preoperative iron/EPO before elective surgery to reduce transfusion risk; restrictive 7-8 g/dL vs liberal 10 g/dL definitions; 'treat the patient not the value'), SRC-033 (PBM three pillars incl. optimization of erythropoiesis; Choosing Wisely restrictive-trigger campaign), SRC-043 (Methods of PBM: preoperative iron/EPO, restrictive triggers; Hb<7 g/dL trigger in stable patients). No invented facts; the minor 7 vs 7-8 g/dL variance across sources is a source-internal inconsistency, not an error introduced by the item, and does not

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with iron-deficiency anemia (hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL) is scheduled for elective colon resection in 5 weeks. He is referred for preoperative iron therapy as part of the institution's patient blood management (PBM) program before surgery. Which of the following best explains the physiologic rationale for giving iron in this setting?

options.text: It reduces the iatrogenic blood loss caused by repeated preoperative diagnostic phlebotomy
It shifts the hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curve to the right, improving tissue oxygen delivery independent of hemoglobin concentration
It minimizes intraoperative blood loss by enhancing platelet aggregation and coagulation factor activity
It permits the surgical team to adopt a more liberal transfusion trigger, since a higher starting hemoglobin creates additional physiologic reserve
It optimizes erythropoiesis, raising red cell mass and hemoglobin before surgery so the patient is less likely to require allogeneic RBC transfusion

questions.explanation_correct: PBM is organized around three pillars: (1) optimization of erythropoiesis, (2) minimization of blood loss/bleeding, and (3) harnessing/optimizing the physiologic reserve of anemia. Preoperative treatment of anemia with iron (or EPO) before elective surgery falls under Pillar 1 - it corrects the deficiency state and raises hemoglobin/red cell mass ahead of the planned blood loss of surgery, which lowers the chance the patient's hemoglobin will fall to a level that triggers allogeneic transfusion.

option_explanations.E: Correct - this is the definition of Pillar 1 (optimization of erythropoiesis) applied to preoperative anemia management with iron/EPO.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - enhancing coagulation/minimizing blood loss is Pillar 2, accomplished with tools like tranexamic acid, cell salvage, and discontinuing antiplatelet agents, not iron repletion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect - PBM philosophy favors restrictive, not liberal, transfusion thresholds; Pillar 3 is about tolerating anemia physiologically, not licensing a higher transfusion trigger.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect - a rightward oxygen dissociation curve shift is driven by increased 2,3-DPG, CO2, temperature, or decreased pH as a compensatory adaptation to anemia; it is not the mechanism by which iron therapy works.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - minimizing diagnostic phlebotomy is a separate PBM strategy to prevent iatrogenic anemia; it is not what preoperative iron supplementation does.

questions.teaching_point: Preoperative iron therapy works through Pillar 1 of PBM (optimization of erythropoiesis) - it treats the correctable deficiency state so the patient enters surgery with a higher hemoglobin/red cell mass, distinct from the blood-loss-minimization tools of Pillar 2 and the anemia-tolerance strategies of Pillar 3.

source_references.locator: essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md, PBM Subdomain Node 17, Core Concepts/Management and Strategies (approx. lines 47-67)
source_references.supports: Defines the Three Pillars of PBM (optimization of erythropoiesis; minimization of blood loss; harnessing physiologic reserve of anemia) and states preoperative anemia is evaluated and treated with iron/EPO before elective surgery to reduce transfusion risk.

source_references.locator: advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md, Subdomain Node 46, Mechanics of the Shift (approx. lines 25-29)
source_references.supports: Describes the mechanism of rightward/leftward shifts of the oxygen dissociation curve (2,3-DPG, CO2, temperature, pH), used to construct the distractor about oxygen delivery.

source_references.locator: essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md, PBM Common Board Traps (approx. line 81)
source_references.supports: States that iatrogenic phlebotomy loss is a separate contributor to anemia that PBM addresses by minimizing blood draws, used to construct the distractor.

review.notes: Confirmed in SRC-032 (Node 17): Three Pillars of PBM (optimization of erythropoiesis; minimization of blood loss; harnessing physiologic reserve), preoperative anemia treated with iron/EPO to reduce transfusion risk, restrictive-strategy definitions, and iatrogenic phlebotomy loss as a separate PBM concern. Distractor D's oxygen-dissociation-curve mechanism is independently confirmed in SRC-002 Node 46 (2,3-DPG/CO2/temp/pH drive the shift, not iron). All option rationales are grounded and correctly reasoned; no invented facts found. Locators check out against actual line content.

questions.stem: As part of preoperative anemia evaluation for a patient scheduled for elective total knee arthroplasty, iron studies are obtained and the ferritin level returns low. Within a patient blood management framework for preoperative anemia optimization, how should this ferritin result be used?

options.text: It is diagnostic of an ongoing hemolytic process and should prompt a direct antiglobulin test rather than iron therapy
It identifies iron deficiency as a treatable cause of the patient's anemia and should prompt iron supplementation before surgery
It confirms an iron overload state that requires chelation therapy before proceeding to surgery
It means iron stores cannot be corrected before an elective procedure, so allogeneic RBC transfusion should be planned instead
It should be disregarded in a surgical patient, because ferritin testing is used only to screen frequent blood donors for iron store depletion

questions.explanation_correct: Ferritin is used as a marker of iron stores; a low level identifies iron deficiency and is the basis for recommending iron supplementation, as described for iron store mitigation. Applied to the preoperative anemia setting, a low ferritin found on evaluation of a patient before elective surgery identifies a correctable cause of anemia, prompting iron therapy (Pillar 1 of PBM: optimization of erythropoiesis) rather than proceeding directly to transfusion.

option_explanations.B: Correct - low ferritin identifies iron deficiency as the treatable etiology of the anemia, directing management toward iron supplementation as part of preoperative optimization.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - while the sources discuss ferritin testing in the context of frequent blood donors, the same underlying principle (ferritin as a marker of iron stores) is the basis for the iron evaluation explicitly listed as part of preoperative anemia workup and treatment before elective surgery.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect - this inverts the interpretation; a low ferritin indicates depleted iron stores (deficiency), not overload, and would not call for chelation.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - ferritin is an iron-store marker, not a hemolysis marker; hemolysis workup is a distinct clinical pathway unrelated to this test result.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect - this contradicts the purpose of preoperative anemia evaluation, which is to identify correctable causes like iron deficiency early enough to treat before elective surgery and thereby reduce transfusion need.

questions.teaching_point: A low ferritin identifies correctable iron deficiency; in the preoperative setting this finding should trigger iron repletion as part of PBM's erythropoiesis-optimization pillar rather than being dismissed or treated as a signal to transfuse.

source_references.locator: advanced-blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-review-nodes.md, Subdomain Node 50, Core Concepts (approx. line 83)
source_references.supports: States that ferritin testing identifies low iron stores (nonanemic iron deficiency), with the response being iron supplementation - the underlying ferritin-interpretation principle used in this question.

source_references.locator: essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md, PBM Management and Strategies (approx. line 63)
source_references.supports: States that preoperative anemia is evaluated and treated (e.g., with iron) before elective surgery to reduce transfusion risk, establishing that iron-status evaluation is part of the preoperative pathway.

review.notes: Answer A is clinically correct and the only defensible choice (B/C/D/E are implausible on their face), but source grounding is thinner than the question implies. The only explicit ferritin discussion across all four sources is SRC-002 Subdomain Node 50, which frames ferritin testing exclusively in the context of screening frequent BLOOD DONORS for nonanemic iron deficiency -- it says nothing about using ferritin to work up a preoperative surgical patient's anemia. The link to the surgical scenario relies on inference from a separate, generic line in SRC-032 ('Preoperative Anemia: Evaluation an

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman is found to have iron-deficiency anemia (hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL) on routine labs obtained 6 weeks before a scheduled elective hysterectomy. She is asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable. According to patient blood management principles, which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of RBCs preoperatively to ensure an adequate hemoglobin reserve going into surgery
Discontinue her antiplatelet medications and give tranexamic acid instead of treating the underlying anemia
Begin iron supplementation now to optimize erythropoiesis before surgery, reserving allogeneic RBC transfusion for symptomatic anemia or a hemoglobin below the institutional restrictive threshold
Proceed to surgery as scheduled without intervening on the anemia, since intraoperative cell salvage will address any operative blood loss
Defer any action until the day of surgery and rely on point-of-care viscoelastic testing to guide intraoperative transfusion instead of correcting the anemia beforehand

questions.explanation_correct: With adequate lead time before an elective procedure, a correctable anemia (iron deficiency) should be treated preoperatively with iron to optimize erythropoiesis (Pillar 1 of PBM), which reduces the likelihood the patient will need allogeneic transfusion. RBC transfusion is reserved for patients who are symptomatic or fall below a restrictive hemoglobin threshold, consistent with PBM's restrictive-strategy and single-unit ('why give 2 when 1 will do') philosophy rather than prophylactic transfusion of a stable, asymptomatic, correctable anemia.

option_explanations.C: Correct - treating the correctable iron deficiency preoperatively is the core preoperative-anemia strategy of PBM Pillar 1, with transfusion reserved for restrictive-threshold or symptomatic indications.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - this is the two-unit blanket-transfusion pattern PBM programs specifically aim to eliminate ('why give 2 when 1 will do'), and is not indicated for an asymptomatic, stable, correctable anemia when there is time to treat it definitively.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect - cell salvage is an intraoperative tool for minimizing blood loss (Pillar 2); it does not correct a preexisting, correctable anemia and does not substitute for preoperative optimization of erythropoiesis (Pillar 1).
option_explanations.E: Incorrect - viscoelastic testing guides component therapy for coagulopathy/bleeding; it is not a substitute for correcting a known preoperative anemia when time allows.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect - discontinuing antiplatelet agents and using tranexamic acid are Pillar 2 (minimizing blood loss/optimizing coagulation) interventions; they do not address her iron-deficiency anemia.

questions.teaching_point: For a stable, asymptomatic patient with correctable preoperative anemia and adequate lead time, PBM directs treatment of the anemia itself (iron, Pillar 1) rather than prophylactic transfusion; allogeneic RBCs are reserved for restrictive-threshold or symptomatic scenarios.

source_references.locator: essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md, PBM Subdomain Node 17 (approx. lines 47-67)
source_references.supports: Establishes the Three Pillars framework, preoperative anemia treatment with iron/EPO, optimizing coagulation by discontinuing antiplatelet agents, and technological tools (cell salvage, TXA, viscoelastic testing) as distinct Pillar 2 interventions.

source_references.locator: essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md, PBM Definitions (approx. lines 55-59)
source_references.supports: Describes the restrictive transfusion strategy (typically 7-8 g/dL) and the 'why give 2 when 1 will do' single-unit transfusion campaign, grounding the rejection of prophylactic 2-unit transfusion.

review.notes: Well supported: SRC-032 Node 17 confirms preoperative iron treatment for correctable anemia (Pillar 1), restrictive strategy/threshold definitions, 'why give 2 when 1 will do' single-unit campaign, and Pillar-2 tools (cell salvage, TXA, antiplatelet discontinuation, viscoelastic testing) as distinct from anemia correction. SRC-043 independently corroborates 'Preoperative iron/EPO, cell salvage, and restrictive triggers' and the PBM push against '2-unit blanket transfusions.' All distractor rationales are accurate and correctly separate Pillar 1 vs Pillar 2 interventions. Minor concern: this qu

questions.stem: A 22-year-old first-time platelet apheresis donor develops circumoral tingling and shivering approximately 20 minutes into the collection. Vital signs remain stable throughout, and the symptoms fully resolve after oral calcium carbonate is given. For this donor adverse event to be accurately captured and trended by the institution's hemovigilance program, it should be attributed to which of the following underlying mechanisms?

options.text: Hydrostatic volume overload from rapid return of processed blood components
IgA-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to infused plasma proteins
Chelation of ionized calcium by the citrate anticoagulant used during apheresis
Direct mechanical trauma to a peripheral nerve during needle placement
Autonomic-mediated hypotension and bradycardia triggered by venipuncture

questions.explanation_correct: The presentation—perioral/circumoral tingling and shivering that resolves with oral calcium—is the classic picture of citrate toxicity, a reaction specific to apheresis donors that results from citrate anticoagulant chelating ionized calcium. Correctly attributing this donor adverse event to its true mechanism (rather than defaulting to the more common vasovagal category) is necessary for an effective hemovigilance program to accurately trend and analyze donor reaction data.

option_explanations.C: Correct: citrate toxicity in apheresis donors results from calcium chelation, producing tingling/shivering that resolves with oral calcium administration.
option_explanations.E: Vasovagal reactions are the most common donor adverse event overall and present with hypotension, bradycardia, and sometimes syncope, not the calcium-responsive tingling/shivering described here—this is a board trap of reflexively picking the 'most common' reaction.
option_explanations.D: Nerve irritation results from direct contact with a nerve during phlebotomy and produces localized pain/paresthesia at the needle site, not circumoral symptoms relieved by calcium.
option_explanations.B: IgA-mediated hypersensitivity describes an allergic-type reaction mechanism discussed in the context of recipient transfusion reactions, not this donor's calcium-responsive symptom complex.
option_explanations.A: Hydrostatic volume overload describes the mechanism of TACO, a recipient-side transfusion reaction from fluid overload, not a donor collection-related event.

questions.teaching_point: An effective hemovigilance program depends on correctly attributing each donor adverse event to its specific underlying mechanism (e.g., citrate toxicity vs. vasovagal vs. nerve irritation) rather than defaulting to the most statistically common category, since accurate classification drives valid trending and analysis.

source_references.supports: Describes citrate toxicity as specific to apheresis donors, caused by calcium chelation, presenting with tingling/shivering and resolving with oral calcium.
Identifies vasovagal reactions as the most common donor adverse event, characterized by hypotension, bradycardia, and syncope.
Describes nerve irritation as direct nerve contact during phlebotomy.
Notes allergic reaction pathophysiology (IgA deficiency) versus febrile reaction pathophysiology (cytokines) in the transfusion reaction context.
Distinguishes TACO (hydrostatic, volume overload) from TRALI as recipient transfusion reaction mechanisms.

review.notes: SRC-059 directly supports citrate toxicity as apheresis-donor-specific, caused by calcium chelation, presenting with 'tingling or shivering,' resolved with oral calcium — this is the classic reaction described and the key (A) is correct and unambiguous. Vasovagal (B, hypotension/bradycardia/syncope), nerve irritation (C, direct nerve contact during phlebotomy), IgA-mediated allergic reaction (D) and TACO (E, hydrostatic volume overload) are all accurately sourced from SRC-059/SRC-043 as distinct, non-matching mechanisms. Minor issue: the stem's specific descriptor 'circumoral' tingling is an e

questions.stem: A platelet apheresis donor becomes acutely syncopal near the end of donation and falls, sustaining no fracture. She is admitted to the hospital for observation and IV fluids for 30 hours before being discharged at her baseline, with no anticipated long-term sequelae. Applying the standardized ISBT/AABB donor adverse event severity grading tool that the hemovigilance program uses to classify events, this reaction should be graded as:

options.text: Grade 2
Grade 3
Grade 4
Grade 5
Grade 1

questions.explanation_correct: Grade 3 is defined as requiring hospitalization for more than 24 hours, OR surgery, OR symptoms/disability lasting more than 6 months. This donor's 30-hour hospital admission for observation meets the hospitalization duration threshold for Grade 3, regardless of the benign eventual outcome.

option_explanations.E: Grade 1 requires resolution with minimal or no intervention and no limitation on activities of daily living; a 30-hour hospitalization exceeds this category.
option_explanations.A: Grade 2 requires outside medical care without hospitalization; because this donor was formally hospitalized (not just seen and released), the event exceeds Grade 2 criteria—a common trap since the ultimate outcome was benign.
option_explanations.B: Correct: Grade 3 specifically includes hospitalization exceeding 24 hours, which this 30-hour admission satisfies, independent of surgery or long-term disability.
option_explanations.C: Grade 4 requires immediate intervention to prevent death, such as anaphylaxis requiring intubation; nothing in the vignette indicates a life-threatening event requiring emergent intervention.
option_explanations.D: Grade 5 is death; the donor recovered fully and was discharged, so this grade does not apply.

questions.teaching_point: A requirement of an effective hemovigilance program is consistent application of a standardized severity grading tool (ISBT/AABB) so that donor adverse events are classified uniformly across facilities; the hospitalization duration threshold (>24 hours), not just the eventual clinical outcome, determines Grade 3 status.

source_references.supports: Provides the ISBT/AABB five-grade donor adverse event severity scale, including the >24-hour hospitalization threshold defining Grade 3.

review.notes: SRC-059 explicitly defines the 5-grade ISBT/AABB scale: Grade 1 (minimal/no intervention, no ADL limitation), Grade 2 (OMC, no hospitalization, ADL impact <=2 weeks), Grade 3 (hospitalization >24h OR surgery OR symptoms >6 months), Grade 4 (immediate intervention to prevent death), Grade 5 (death). The 30-hour hospitalization for observation/IV fluids unambiguously satisfies the >24-hour Grade 3 threshold regardless of the benign final outcome, which is exactly the teaching point and is well supported. No ambiguity in the correct answer.

questions.stem: During a routine internal audit, a blood center discovers that an apheresis instrument used to collect a unit of fresh frozen plasma experienced an undetected deviation from its validated processing parameters that could compromise the product's potency. The affected unit had already been shipped to and transfused at another facility; the recipient experienced no adverse effects. As part of the biovigilance/quality program's external reporting requirements, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Report the event to the FDA as a Biological Product Deviation using Form FDA 3486
Report the event as a donor fatality using the 7-day written follow-up pathway
Report the event to the FDA only if the recipient later develops a delayed transfusion reaction
Quarantine and recall the unit in-house; no external reporting is required since the deviation occurred internally
Classify the event as a near-miss and document it only in the internal quality record

questions.explanation_correct: This event meets all five criteria for a reportable Biological Product Deviation: it is associated with manufacturing (processing), represents a deviation from validated specifications/cGMP, may affect the product's potency, occurred while the product was under the facility's control, and involved a product that had already been distributed. All donor centers, blood banks, and transfusion services must report such events to FDA using Form FDA 3486, regardless of whether the recipient had a clinical reaction.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the event satisfies all five BPD reporting criteria (manufacturing-associated, cGMP deviation, potential effect on potency, occurred under facility control, product distributed), requiring Form FDA 3486 submission.
option_explanations.E: A near-miss event is an occurrence that did not adversely affect the outcome and did not reach the point of actual harm potential; because this product was already distributed and transfused, it does not meet the near-miss definition and cannot be handled with internal documentation alone.
option_explanations.C: BPD reporting obligations are triggered by the deviation itself meeting the defined criteria, not by whether the recipient subsequently develops a reaction—this is a board trap that conflates outcome-based and criteria-based reporting logic.
option_explanations.D: Quarantine and recall address in-house control of a nonconforming product, but once a unit meeting BPD criteria has been distributed, external FDA reporting is still required in addition to any recall action.
option_explanations.B: The FDA fatality reporting pathway (ASAP notification with a 7-day written follow-up) applies specifically to deaths associated with collection or transfusion; no fatality occurred in this scenario, so this pathway does not apply.

questions.teaching_point: An effective biovigilance program requires distinguishing between reporting pathways: Biological Product Deviation reporting is triggered by the deviation meeting defined criteria (manufacturing-associated, cGMP departure, potential impact on safety/purity/potency, occurred under facility control, product distributed) regardless of clinical outcome, whereas near-miss classification and fatality reporting follow different, non-overlapping triggers.

source_references.supports: Lists the five criteria that trigger mandatory BPD reporting to FDA: manufacturing-associated, cGMP deviation, potential effect on safety/purity/potency, occurred under facility control, and product distributed.
States that BPDs are reported via Form FDA 3486 when a cGMP deviation occurring within the facility's control involves a distributed product.
Defines a near-miss event as one that did not adversely affect the outcome but could have resulted in a serious adverse event.
Describes the separate fatality reporting pathway: ASAP notification to FDA/CBER with a written report within 7 days.

review.notes: The core stem and key are well supported: SRC-027 lists the five BPD reporting criteria verbatim (manufacturing-associated, cGMP/spec deviation, potential effect on safety/purity/potency, occurred under facility control, product distributed) and this scenario meets all five; SRC-053/SRC-043 both confirm reporting occurs via Form FDA 3486. Distractors C (outcome-contingent reporting), D (internal-only, no external reporting needed), and E (fatality pathway) are all correctly refuted by source content. However, option B's rationale ('because this product was already distributed and transfused, i

questions.stem: A transfusion service investigates a fatal reaction in which a patient received an ABO-incompatible unit of RBCs. As part of the hospital's biovigilance program, a root-cause analysis (Five Whys) is performed to identify the management-controllable cause so that recurrence can be prevented. Historically, which underlying mechanism most commonly produces this specific type of reaction, and is therefore the primary target of biovigilance surveillance and corrective action for this event category?

options.text: An anamnestic (secondary) alloantibody response occurring several days after transfusion
Clerical/patient-identification error resulting in administration of an ABO-incompatible unit
Hydrostatic pulmonary overload from excessive infusion rate or volume
IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to donor plasma proteins in an IgA-deficient recipient
Cytokine accumulation from residual leukocytes/plasma in the stored component

questions.explanation_correct: The source material explicitly identifies clerical error as the number-one cause of acute hemolytic transfusion reactions (AHTR). Because biovigilance root-cause analysis (Five Whys) is designed to move past 'human error' labels and drill down to management-controllable, systemic causes, the mechanism most relevant to AHTR surveillance and corrective action is a clerical/identification failure (e.g., bedside or specimen mislabeling) that leads to ABO-incompatible transfusion, rather than a cytokine-, IgE-, volume-, or antibody-mediated mechanism.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the source states clerical error is the #1 cause of AHTR, making it the mechanism biovigilance RCA is structured to detect and correct.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: cytokine accumulation from leukocytes/plasma is the mechanism underlying febrile nonhemolytic reactions, not AHTR.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: IgA deficiency/IgE-mediated hypersensitivity is the mechanism cited for allergic reactions, a separate reaction category from AHTR.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: hydrostatic overload is the mechanism described for TACO, a noninfectious pulmonary complication distinct from AHTR.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: an anamnestic antibody response describes delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions, which present days later, not the acute reaction described.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 section: Adverse Reaction Recognition / Acute Hemolytic Reactions, TRALI vs TACO, Allergic & Febrile Reactions, Delayed Reactions
source_references.supports: States clerical error is the #1 cause of AHTR and visual hemolysis is its hallmark; lists distinguishing mechanisms for febrile, allergic, TACO, and delayed hemolytic reactions.

source_references.locator: Section 5: Technical Logic for Nonconforming Events (NCE) / Root Cause Analysis
source_references.supports: Describes root-cause analysis (Five Whys) as a technique for identifying management-controllable causes rather than stopping at 'human error.'

review.notes: Core fact is directly grounded: SRC-043 states 'Acute Hemolytic Reactions (AHTR): Clerical error is the #1 cause; hallmark is visual hemolysis,' and SRC-052 supports Five Whys/RCA logic of drilling past 'human error' to management-controllable causes. Distractor mechanisms for B (cytokine/FNHTR), D (hydrostatic/TACO), and E (anamnestic/DHTR) are directly grounded in SRC-043's parallel bullet list. However: (1) option C's rationale asserts 'IgE-mediated hypersensitivity' as the allergic-reaction mechanism, but the source only says 'Pathophysiology of IgA deficiency (Allergic)' - it never specif

questions.stem: A patient develops signs of a reaction shortly after an RBC transfusion is started. As part of the hemovigilance workup, the transfusion service examines a post-transfusion plasma specimen. Which finding is described as the hallmark that should prompt the reaction to be classified and reported as an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR), as opposed to another reportable reaction category?

options.text: Urticaria and pruritus without fever
Visible hemolysis (pink/red discoloration) of the post-transfusion plasma
A delayed fall in hemoglobin with a newly positive DAT several days after transfusion
Bilateral pulmonary infiltrates with a noncardiogenic (inflammatory) picture
Isolated fever without other associated findings

questions.explanation_correct: The source explicitly names visual hemolysis as the hallmark finding of acute hemolytic transfusion reactions, distinguishing AHTR from the other adverse-reaction categories captured by the hemovigilance system (febrile, allergic, TRALI, and delayed hemolytic reactions), each of which has its own characteristic presentation rather than visible hemolysis of the plasma specimen.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the source identifies visual hemolysis as the hallmark of AHTR.
option_explanations.E: Isolated fever without other findings is the pattern associated with febrile nonhemolytic reactions (cytokine-mediated), not AHTR.
option_explanations.A: Urticaria/pruritus without fever describes an allergic reaction (IgA-deficiency-related mechanism), a distinct reportable category.
option_explanations.D: Bilateral infiltrates with an inflammatory, noncardiogenic picture describes TRALI, which is reported separately from AHTR.
option_explanations.C: A delayed hemoglobin drop with a new positive DAT days later describes a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (anamnestic), reported as a distinct, non-acute category.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 section: Adverse Reaction Recognition subsections (AHTR, TRALI vs TACO, Allergic & Febrile, Delayed Reactions)
source_references.supports: States that visual hemolysis is the hallmark of AHTR and distinguishes febrile, allergic, TRALI/TACO, and delayed hemolytic reactions by their characteristic features.

review.notes: Correct answer is strongly and directly grounded: SRC-043 explicitly states the 'hallmark' of AHTR 'is visual hemolysis.' However, several distractor rationales add specific clinical details not present in the terse source bullets: 'isolated fever without other findings' (B), 'urticaria and pruritus without fever' (C), 'bilateral pulmonary infiltrates' (D), and 'newly positive DAT' (E) are all standard external clinical knowledge, not phrases found in SRC-043's brief category labels (e.g., source only says 'TRALI (inflammatory, noncardiogenic)' with no mention of bilateral infiltrates, and 'De

questions.stem: A patient dies during a transfusion suspected to be due to an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. As the physician responsible for the hospital's biovigilance/hemovigilance reporting obligations, what is the most appropriate immediate reporting action?

options.text: Report only if an autopsy subsequently confirms hemolysis as the cause of death
Document the event internally as a near-miss, since the causative unit was traceable
Wait until the root cause analysis is complete before notifying any external regulatory body
Submit only a Biological Product Deviation report (Form FDA 3486) within 45 days, since this is the mechanism used for reporting transfusion fatalities
Report the fatality to FDA/CBER as soon as possible (orally or electronically), followed by a written report within 7 days

questions.explanation_correct: The source states that fatalities related to collection or transfusion must be reported as soon as possible (oral or electronic) to CBER, with a written follow-up within 7 days. This ASAP/7-day fatality-reporting requirement is distinct from and faster than the 45-day Biological Product Deviation reporting timeline that applies to distributed products with manufacturing-related deviations, so a suspected transfusion fatality triggers the more urgent fatality-reporting pathway regardless of whether RCA or autopsy findings are yet available.

option_explanations.E: Correct: matches the source's FDA fatality reporting requirement (ASAP oral/electronic notification plus written follow-up within 7 days).
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the 45-day BPD/Form 3486 pathway is a separate reporting mechanism for distributed-product manufacturing deviations; it does not replace or extend the ASAP/7-day fatality reporting requirement, making this a classic timeline-confusion trap.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the reporting standard is 'as soon as possible' and does not permit delaying notification until an RCA is finished.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: by definition a near-miss event has the potential for harm but does not result in an adverse outcome; a fatality is an adverse outcome and is therefore not a near-miss.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the fatality-reporting obligation is time-based ('as soon as possible') and is not contingent on autopsy confirmation of the mechanism.

source_references.locator: Domain 3 section: FDA Fatality Reporting; Biological Product Deviations (BPDs)
source_references.supports: States FDA fatality reporting requires notifying CBER as soon as possible (oral/electronic) with written follow-up within 7 days, and separately describes BPD reporting via Form FDA 3486.

source_references.locator: Section 5: Biological Product Deviation (BPD) Reporting
source_references.supports: Specifies the 45-day BPD reporting timeline for distributed blood products with manufacturing-related deviations, distinct from fatality reporting.

source_references.locator: Appendices: Glossary of Board-Critical Terms / Near-miss Event
source_references.supports: Defines a near-miss event as an occurrence with potential for harm that did not result in an adverse outcome, supporting exclusion of a fatality from that category.

review.notes: Very well grounded. SRC-043 explicitly states: 'FDA Fatality Reporting: Fatalities related to collection or transfusion must be reported as soon as possible (oral or electronic) to CBER, with a written follow-up within 7 days' - matching option A verbatim in substance. The 45-day BPD/Form FDA 3486 trap in option B is independently corroborated by both SRC-043 ('Must be reported using Form FDA 3486') and SRC-052 ('Report within 45 days if the event involves manufacturing... and the product was distributed'), correctly distinguishing it as a different, slower reporting pathway. Option D's exclus

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is transfused 1 unit of RBCs on a general medicine ward. Within 10 minutes he develops fever, chills, flank pain, and dark (hemoglobin-tinged) urine, followed rapidly by hypotension and cardiovascular collapse; resuscitation is unsuccessful and he is pronounced dead. Chart review shows the unit administered had been crossmatched and labeled for a different patient with a similar surname, and the bedside two-person identification check at the time of administration was not documented. Because this is a suspected transfusion-related death, the transfusion service must notify the FDA (CBER) as soon as possible by phone or electronic means, followed by a written report within 7 days. Which of the following best describes the underlying mechanism of this fatal reaction?

options.text: Hydrostatic pulmonary edema from a transfusion volume exceeding cardiac reserve (TACO)
Complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis from an ABO-incompatible transfusion caused by patient/sample misidentification
Antibody-mediated neutrophil activation in the pulmonary microvasculature causing acute lung injury (TRALI)
IgG-mediated extravascular hemolysis from an anamnestic (delayed) antibody response to a non-ABO antigen

questions.explanation_correct: The abrupt onset (minutes) of fever, flank pain, and hemoglobinuria progressing to shock immediately after transfusion, together with a documented crossmatch/identification mismatch, is the classic picture of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction caused by ABO incompatibility. The approved sources identify clerical error (patient/sample misidentification) as the primary, largely preventable cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions, and note that hemolytic reactions, along with TRALI and TACO, are the reaction categories most frequently reported to the FDA as transfusion-related fatalities, which is why the ASAP-plus-7-day-written-report requirement applies here.

option_explanations.B: Correct: clerical/identification error leading to ABO-incompatible transfusion produces complement fixation on donor RBCs and acute intravascular hemolysis, matching the immediate hemoglobinuria, flank pain, and shock described, and the sources name clerical error as the primary preventable cause of this reaction type.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: TRALI is one of the three leading FDA-reported fatality categories named in the sources, but it presents as acute respiratory distress/hypoxemia from pulmonary capillary injury, not hemoglobinuria and flank pain, and is not linked to a labeling/identification mismatch.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: TACO is also a leading reported fatality category in the sources, but its mechanism is volume overload producing pulmonary edema, not hemolysis, and the vignette shows no evidence of fluid overload or a labeling error causing it.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: extravascular hemolysis from an anamnestic response is a delayed process (days), not an event beginning within 10 minutes of transfusion, and the vignette's clerical mismatch points to acute ABO incompatibility rather than a recall (secondary) antibody response.

questions.teaching_point: Suspected transfusion-related fatalities require ASAP notification to FDA CBER plus a written report within 7 days. Clerical/identification error causing ABO-incompatible transfusion is the leading preventable mechanism of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions, one of the three reaction categories (with TRALI and TACO) most often reported as fatal.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 18 - Hemovigilance & Adverse Event Monitoring: 'Leading Fatalities' and 'Common Board Traps and Distractors' (Clerical Errors)
source_references.supports: States that clerical errors remain the primary, often preventable, cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions, and lists TRALI, TACO, and hemolytic reactions as the most frequent transfusion-related fatalities reported to the FDA.

source_references.locator: Section 7, 'Fatality Reporting'
source_references.supports: Confirms that fatalities related to blood collection or transfusion must be reported to CBER as soon as possible via telephone/email, with a mandatory written follow-up report within 7 days.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 18: 'Common Board Traps and Distractors' (Reporting Timelines)
source_references.supports: Corroborates the same FDA CBER reporting timeline (oral/electronic ASAP notification, written report within 7 days) for suspected transfusion-related deaths.

review.notes: Core regulatory facts are grounded: SRC-032 Node 18 states 'Clerical Errors...remain the primary cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions' and lists TRALI/TACO/Hemolytic reactions as the 'Leading Fatalities' reported to FDA; SRC-028 Section 7 'Fatality Reporting' confirms the ASAP + 7-day-written-report CBER timeline. However, several asserted facts used to justify the keyed answer and eliminate distractors are NOT found anywhere in the four approved sources: (1) the specific mechanism 'complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis' is never stated (sources only mention complement in the

questions.stem: A transfusion service is investigating a suspected acute hemolytic transfusion reaction so that the case can be correctly classified for hemovigilance surveillance reporting (using NHSN imputability categories such as Definitive, Probable, Possible, or Doubtful/Ruled Out). As part of the workup, the technologist performs a direct antiglobulin test (DAT) on the post-transfusion specimen using polyspecific antihuman globulin (AHG) reagent, and no agglutination is observed. Before this negative DAT can be relied upon to help rule out an immune-mediated hemolytic reaction, which additional step must be performed to validate the result?

options.text: Switch to a PEG-enhanced technique and proceed directly to the wash phase, omitting the 37°C reading
Overcentrifuge the tube to enhance detection of any weak agglutination that may have been missed
Repeat the test using monospecific anti-C3d reagent in place of the polyspecific AHG reagent
Add IgG-sensitized check cells to the negative tube and confirm agglutination occurs, verifying that AHG reagent was added and is reactive

questions.explanation_correct: The approved source specifies that IgG-coated reagent red cells (check cells) must be added to every negative AHG test to confirm that AHG reagent was actually added and remains functional; without this step, a false-negative DAT (for example, from inadequate washing that neutralizes the AHG) could be mistaken for a truly negative result and improperly used to rule out an immune-mediated hemolytic reaction when assigning the NHSN imputability designation for hemovigilance reporting.

option_explanations.D: Correct: the source explicitly requires check cells to be added to all negative AHG tests to confirm the AHG reagent was added and is functional, which is the validity check needed before a negative DAT can support ruling out the reaction.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: monospecific anti-C3d versus polyspecific AHG reagent distinguishes IgG-only from complement-only sensitization; it does not confirm that AHG reagent was actually added and functioning in the original negative test.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source identifies overcentrifugation as a cause of false-positive results, not a method for validating a negative AHG test; using it here would not address the concern about a missed false negative.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the source notes that with PEG enhancement, the 37°C agglutination reading is omitted and testing proceeds directly to the wash phase, but this describes normal PEG technique, not a step that confirms AHG reagent reactivity in a negative test.

questions.teaching_point: A negative AHG-based test (DAT or IAT) is only interpretable after confirming reagent reactivity with IgG-coated check cells; an unconfirmed negative result (e.g., from inadequate washing) risks a false-negative that could lead to misclassification of a reportable hemolytic reaction under NHSN imputability criteria, contributing to underrecognition/underreporting.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20 - Antiglobulin Testing & Enhancement Techniques: 'Common Board Traps and Distractors' (The 'Check Cell' Necessity)
source_references.supports: States that IgG-coated check cells must be added to all negative AHG tests to confirm the AHG reagent was added and is functional.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20: 'Definitions', 'Enhancement Media (Potentiators)', and 'Laboratory Findings (Sources of Error)'
source_references.supports: Distinguishes polyspecific AHG (anti-IgG and anti-C3d) from monospecific AHG reagents, lists overcentrifugation as a cause of false-positive AHG results, and notes that PEG technique omits the 37°C reading before the wash phase.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 18: 'NHSN Imputability Designations'
source_references.supports: Describes the NHSN imputability framework (Definitive/Probable/Possible/Doubtful-Ruled Out) used to classify reactions for hemovigilance reporting, which depends on accurate serologic case-definition data such as a valid DAT.

review.notes: Very well grounded in SRC-032 Node 20: 'IgG-coated reagent RBCs (Check Cells) must be added to all negative AHG tests to confirm the AHG was added and is functional' directly supports the keyed answer and stem. 'False Negatives: Inadequate washing (leading to neutralization of AHG)' supports the explanation's false-negative rationale. Distractor rationales are each grounded: monospecific vs. polyspecific AHG definitions, overcentrifugation as a cause of false positives (not a validity check) is sourced verbatim, and the PEG/37C-omission detail is sourced verbatim. questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with end-stage renal disease maintained on hemodialysis is evaluated before a scheduled minor procedure because of easy bruising, gum bleeding, and recurrent epistaxis. He has no personal or family history of a bleeding disorder and has not had recent cardiac surgery. His platelet count is 210 x 10^9/L, and PT and aPTT are both within normal limits. Which of the following best explains this patient's bleeding tendency?

options.text: Acquired platelet dysfunction due to recent cardiopulmonary bypass
Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) from autoantibodies against platelet antigens
Bernard-Soulier syndrome (congenital glycoprotein Ib deficiency)
Glanzmann thrombasthenia (congenital glycoprotein IIb/IIIa deficiency)
Acquired qualitative platelet dysfunction due to uremia

questions.explanation_correct: The source explicitly lists uremia as a recognized cause of acquired (as opposed to congenital) platelet dysfunction. This patient's normal platelet count and normal PT/aPTT with a mucocutaneous bleeding pattern in the setting of end-stage renal disease is consistent with a qualitative (functional) platelet defect from uremia, distinguishing it from congenital structural defects and from quantitative immune destruction.

option_explanations.E: Correct: the source names uremia directly as a cause of acquired platelet dysfunction, fitting this patient's renal failure and normal platelet count/coagulation screen.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: Bernard-Soulier syndrome is listed in the source as a congenital platelet disorder; it would be expected to present with a lifelong bleeding history, not new-onset bleeding in the context of adult renal failure.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is likewise listed in the source as congenital; the absence of a lifelong bleeding history argues against a congenital glycoprotein defect.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source defines ITP as thrombocytopenia caused by antiplatelet autoantibodies; this patient's platelet count is normal, which is inconsistent with a quantitative destructive process like ITP.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: cardiopulmonary bypass is also listed in the source as a cause of acquired platelet dysfunction, but there is no history of recent cardiac surgery in this vignette, so it does not fit the clinical context even though it is the correct general category.

questions.teaching_point: Acquired qualitative platelet dysfunction (uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, drugs) presents with mucocutaneous bleeding, a normal platelet count, and normal PT/aPTT, distinguishing it from congenital structural defects (Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann) and from quantitative immune destruction (ITP).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping; 'Platelet Function' bullet
source_references.supports: States that platelet function disorders can be congenital (Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann's) or acquired, with uremia and cardiopulmonary bypass named as acquired causes.

source_references.locator: Domain I – Clinical Practice, Definitions, 'Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP)'
source_references.supports: Defines ITP as thrombocytopenia due to autoantibodies against platelet antigens, used to build the ITP distractor as a quantitative rather than qualitative process.

review.notes: Core fact is directly sourced: SRC-006 Node 62 states 'Platelet Function: Disorders can be congenital (e.g., Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann's) or acquired, such as those caused by uremia, cardiopulmonary bypass, or drugs.' This cleanly supports keying A over congenital B/C and over the CPB distractor E (ruled out by 'no recent cardiac surgery' in the stem). ITP is correctly distinguished via SRC-019's definition ('Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP): Thrombocytopenia caused by autoantibodies directed against platelet antigens') combined with the normal platelet count in the stem. However, the explanatio

questions.stem: A woman develops abrupt, severe thrombocytopenia several days after receiving a red blood cell transfusion, and post-transfusion purpura (PTP) is suspected clinically. Testing for a platelet-reactive alloantibody in her plasma yields weak, equivocal serologic reactivity, and HPA-typed reagent platelets are not available in the laboratory to confirm antibody specificity or to identify antigen-negative platelet donors. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to identify compatible platelet donors for this patient?

options.text: Human Platelet Antigen (HPA) genotyping of potential donors
Direct antiglobulin test using monospecific anti-C3d reagent
Corrected Count Increment (CCI) calculation after a trial platelet transfusion
High-titer cold agglutinin titration for anti-I specificity
Donath-Landsteiner test

questions.explanation_correct: The source states that molecular (HPA genotyping) testing is essential for finding compatible donors precisely when an antibody is weak or when HPA serologic reagents are unavailable, and that HPA investigation is central to working up post-transfusion purpura. This directly matches the scenario described.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the source states genotyping is essential for finding compatible donors when an antibody is weak or HPA reagents are unavailable, and that HPA is critical in investigating PTP.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the Donath-Landsteiner test identifies a biphasic hemolysin with anti-P specificity used to diagnose paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria, an RBC-directed process unrelated to platelet antigen investigation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: DAT with monospecific anti-C3d is used to classify RBC-directed immune hemolysis (e.g., cold agglutinin syndrome), not to identify HPA-compatible platelet donors.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: high-titer cold agglutinin testing with anti-I specificity is used in the work-up of cold hemagglutinin disease, an RBC hemolytic process, and has no role in platelet antigen donor matching.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the CCI evaluates the adequacy of a platelet transfusion increment (accounting for body surface area and platelets transfused) rather than identifying antibody specificity or antigen-negative donors before transfusion.

questions.teaching_point: When platelet-antibody serology is weak or reagent HPA-typed platelets are unavailable, molecular HPA genotyping is the tool specified for locating antigen-compatible donors, distinct from RBC-directed serologic tests (DAT, Donath-Landsteiner, cold agglutinin titer) and distinct from CCI, which measures transfusion response rather than donor compatibility.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 62 – Platelet Immunity, HPA, and Genotyping
source_references.supports: States platelets carry HPA; genotyping is essential for finding compatible donors when an antibody is weak or HPA reagents unavailable; HPA is critical in investigating PTP and FNAIT.

source_references.supports: Describes DAT/anti-C3d for AIHA classification, the Donath-Landsteiner test for PCH, and high-titer cold agglutinins for CAS, used to construct distractors unrelated to platelet antigen serology.
