questions.subdomain: Rh(D) and Other Fetal-Maternal RBC Incompatibilities
questions.stem: A woman who is group O, Rh(D) negative was sensitized to the D antigen during a prior pregnancy with an Rh(D) positive fetus. In her current pregnancy, the fetus is again Rh(D) positive, and ultrasound now shows signs of fetal anemia with extramedullary hematopoiesis. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this fetal anemia?

options.text: Naturally occurring maternal anti-D, present since birth, reacts with paternally inherited D antigen on fetal RBCs even without any prior sensitizing pregnancy
Maternal IgM anti-D crosses the placenta and activates complement directly on fetal RBCs, producing intravascular hemolysis
Maternal anti-D binds D antigen on fetal erythroid precursors in the bone marrow, suppressing erythropoiesis rather than destroying mature circulating RBCs
Maternal anti-D activates the classical complement cascade on fetal RBCs, causing direct intravascular hemolysis and hemoglobinuria

Maternal IgG (IgG1/IgG3) anti-D crosses the placenta and binds D antigen on fetal RBCs, leading to extravascular destruction of the RBCs by the fetal reticuloendothelial system, with compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis

questions.explanation_correct: HDFN is caused by maternal IgG (specifically IgG1 and IgG3), the only immunoglobulin classes actively transported across the placenta. Maternal anti-D binds paternally inherited D antigen on fetal RBCs, and these antibody-coated cells are cleared extravascularly (by the fetal reticuloendothelial/splenic macrophage system) rather than lysed within the vessel. The resulting fetal anemia drives a compensatory increase in extramedullary (hepatic/splenic) hematopoiesis, i.e., erythroblastosis fetalis.

option_explanations.E: Correct: matches the source-described pathogenesis — IgG1/IgG3 placental transfer, extravascular RBC destruction, fetal anemia, and compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: IgM does not cross the placenta (only IgG subclasses IgG1/IgG3 are actively transported), and the mechanism described in Rh HDFN is extravascular destruction, not complement-mediated intravascular lysis.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: unlike anti-A/anti-B, which are naturally occurring and can cause ABO HDFN even in a first pregnancy, anti-D is not naturally occurring — it requires prior maternal sensitization, consistent with this patient's sensitizing prior pregnancy.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this precursor-suppression mechanism describes anti-K (Kell) antibodies, which bind Kell antigens expressed on RBC precursors and suppress erythropoiesis — it is specifically contrasted with the peripheral hemolytic mechanism of anti-D.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the described mechanism of Rh HDFN is extravascular destruction via the reticuloendothelial system, not classical-pathway complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis.

questions.teaching_point: Rh(D) HDFN results from IgG-mediated extravascular destruction of fetal RBCs following maternal sensitization; this differs mechanistically from ABO HDFN (naturally occurring antibody, can affect first pregnancy) and anti-K HDFN (precursor suppression of erythropoiesis rather than pure hemolysis).

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain VII, Core Concepts (Maternofetal Transfer; Pathogenesis of HDFN)
source_references.supports: Only IgG (IgG1/IgG3) crosses the placenta; maternal alloantibody targets paternal fetal RBC antigens causing extravascular destruction, fetal anemia, and compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis.

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain VII, Laboratory Findings (Anti-K Specificity)
source_references.supports: Anti-K is distinguished from other HDFN antibodies because Kell antigens are on RBC precursors, causing erythropoiesis suppression rather than pure peripheral hemolysis — used to build the precursor-suppression distractor.

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain VII, Common Board Traps (ABO HDFN Timing)
source_references.supports: ABO HDFN can occur in a first pregnancy because anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring, implying anti-D (unlike ABO antibodies) requires sensitization and is not naturally occurring.

review.notes: Core teaching (IgG1/IgG3 placental transfer, extravascular RBC destruction by fetal RES, compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis) is directly and explicitly supported by SRC-017 'Core Concepts: Maternofetal Transfer' and 'Pathogenesis of HDFN'. Distractor D (anti-K precursor suppression) is directly supported by SRC-017 'Anti-K Specificity'. Distractor B (IgM crossing placenta) is correctly refuted by the same Maternofetal Transfer statement (only IgG1/IgG3 cross). Distractor E (classical complement intravascular hemolysis) is correctly refuted by the extravascular-destruction statement. Dis

questions.subdomain: Rh(D) and Other Fetal-Maternal RBC Incompatibilities
questions.stem: A woman known to be Rh(D) negative was sensitized to the D antigen in a prior pregnancy. In her current pregnancy, serial anti-D titers remain persistently well above a titer of 4, a level considered consistent with active (rather than passively administered) alloimmunization. She delivers a live infant. Cord blood testing shows: forward Rh(D) type = negative; direct antiglobulin test (DAT) = 3+ positive with anti-IgG. Which of the following best explains this typing result and identifies the correct next step?

options.text: Because only IgM anti-D can produce a blocking phenomenon on fetal RBCs, and the antibody here is IgG (per the anti-IgG DAT reagent), a blocked D reaction can be excluded and the infant should simply be typed as Rh(D) negative

This is a blocked D (blocked Rh) phenomenon: actively produced maternal IgG anti-D has coated essentially all D antigen sites on the fetal RBCs, producing a false-negative forward Rh(D) type; an eluate should be performed to remove the bound antibody so the true D antigen status can be determined

The persistently elevated anti-D titer (>4) is most consistent with passively administered RhIG, which typically does not exceed a titer of 4; therefore this maternal antibody is unrelated to the cord result, and the infant should be considered truly Rh(D) negative without further workup
The DAT positivity is an unexpected finding in this setting and should prompt investigation for a cause unrelated to Rh HDFN, since a positive DAT is not typically seen in Rh HDFN
The peripheral smear would be expected to show numerous spherocytes, which would confirm this is a case of Rh HDFN rather than a serologic artifact

questions.explanation_correct: A persistently elevated maternal anti-D titer (well above 4) indicates active alloimmunization rather than passive RhIG antibody. Heavy transplacental IgG anti-D can coat essentially all D antigen sites on the newborn's RBCs, blocking reagent anti-D from binding and producing a falsely negative forward Rh(D) type despite a strongly positive DAT — the classic 'blocked Rh' phenomenon. The bound antibody must be released from the cells (via an eluate) before accurate D antigen typing can be performed.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the source-defined blocked Rh phenomenon (false-negative newborn D type from heavy maternal anti-D coating), resolved specifically by performing an eluate.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the stem specifies the titer is persistently well above 4, which the source states almost always indicates active alloimmunization, not passive RhIG (which rarely exceeds a titer of 4) — this option inverts that logic and wrongly dismisses further workup.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: spherocytes are characteristic of ABO HDFN; in Rh HDFN the peripheral smear more typically shows erythroblasts (nucleated RBCs) rather than spherocytosis, so this finding would not be expected here.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: a positive DAT (with anti-IgG) is an expected component of the newborn HDFN workup, not an unusual finding requiring an unrelated explanation.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: only IgG crosses the placenta and mediates HDFN (IgM does not cross at all), so the blocked D phenomenon is an IgG-mediated process — this option's premise misstates the immunology and would lead to a missed true D-positive result.

questions.teaching_point: A falsely negative newborn Rh(D) type in the setting of a positive DAT and a maternal anti-D titer indicating active alloimmunization should raise suspicion for the 'blocked Rh' phenomenon; an eluate is required to remove bound maternal antibody before true D antigen status can be established.

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain VII, Common Board Traps ('Blocked Rh')
source_references.supports: Defines 'blocked Rh' as a false-negative newborn Rh type from heavy maternal anti-D coating D-antigen sites, resolved by performing an eluate.

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain VII, Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls (Passive vs Active Anti-D)
source_references.supports: States passive RhIG-derived anti-D rarely achieves a titer above 4, while titers above 4 in a mother almost always indicate active alloimmunization.

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain VII, Laboratory Findings (Newborn Workup; Morphology)
source_references.supports: Lists positive DAT (anti-IgG) as an expected newborn HDFN workup finding, and notes spherocytes are common in ABO HDFN but rare in Rh HDFN (erythroblasts predominate).

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain VII, Core Concepts (Maternofetal Transfer)
source_references.supports: States only IgG antibodies are actively transported across the placenta, underpinning why the blocked D phenomenon is IgG-mediated.

review.notes: Fully grounded in SRC-017. 'Blocked Rh' is defined verbatim in 'Common Board Traps and Distractors' as a false-negative newborn Rh type from heavy maternal anti-D coating D-antigen sites, resolved by eluate — matching option A and the stem's cord findings (negative forward D, 3+ DAT with anti-IgG). The stem's premise that a titer persistently 'well above 4' indicates active (not passive) alloimmunization is directly supported by the 'Passive vs. Active Anti-D' teaching pearl (RhIG rarely exceeds titer 4; titers >4 almost always indicate active alloimmunization), which correctly refutes distrac

questions.subdomain: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn
questions.stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 26 weeks' gestation has anti-K (anti-Kell) identified on her antibody screen, with a maternal titer of only 1:4. Fetal middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV) is 1.7 MoM, consistent with moderate-to-severe fetal anemia, despite the low maternal titer. Which of the following best explains the mechanism responsible for the fetal anemia in this case?

options.text: Like anti-D, anti-K severity correlates directly with maternal titer because Kell antigens are confined to mature circulating RBCs
Anti-K, like anti-A and anti-B, is a naturally occurring antibody, so it produces HDFN independent of prior maternal sensitization

Kell antigens are expressed on erythroid progenitor cells in fetal marrow, so anti-K directly suppresses erythropoiesis in addition to causing peripheral extravascular hemolysis, which makes maternal titer a poor predictor of severity
Anti-K activates the classical complement cascade to produce predominantly intravascular hemolysis, unlike other clinically significant RBC alloantibodies
Anti-K is an IgM antibody that is actively transported across the placenta and directly agglutinates fetal RBCs intravascularly

questions.explanation_correct: Kell blood group antigens are expressed on erythroid precursors in the fetal bone marrow, not just mature RBCs. Anti-K therefore suppresses erythropoiesis directly (in addition to peripheral hemolysis of mature cells), which is why anti-K titer correlates poorly with fetal anemia severity compared with anti-D, where titer is more predictive.

option_explanations.C: Correct: the source explicitly attributes anti-K's poor titer-severity correlation to its action on RBC precursors, suppressing erythropoiesis rather than causing hemolysis alone.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: only IgG (subclasses IgG1 and IgG3) is actively transported across the placenta; IgM does not cross and does not cause HDFN by this mechanism.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources describe HDFN pathogenesis as maternal IgG-mediated extravascular destruction of fetal RBCs, not a complement-driven intravascular process specific to anti-K.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this reverses the source's key teaching point — anti-K titer is specifically noted as LESS predictive of severity than anti-D, precisely because Kell antigens are present on precursors, not confined to mature RBCs.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: naturally occurring antibodies unrelated to prior sensitization (permitting first-pregnancy HDFN) describes anti-A/anti-B in ABO HDFN, not anti-K, which requires immune sensitization from prior transfusion or pregnancy.

questions.teaching_point: Kell antigen expression on erythroid precursors makes anti-K uniquely capable of suppressing erythropoiesis, so titer is an unreliable severity marker compared with anti-D.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings > Anti-K Specificity
source_references.supports: Anti-K titer is less predictive of HDFN severity because Kell antigens are expressed on RBC precursors, causing suppression of erythropoiesis rather than hemolysis alone.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts > Maternofetal Transfer
source_references.supports: Only IgG (subclasses IgG1/IgG3) is actively transported across the placenta, making it the cause of HDFN; this underlies why an IgM-transport distractor is wrong.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors > ABO HDFN Timing
source_references.supports: ABO HDFN, unlike Rh/Kell HDFN, can occur in a first pregnancy because anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring — used to build the naturally-occurring-antibody distractor.

review.notes: SRC-017 'Anti-K Specificity' states almost verbatim: 'Anti-K titer is less predictive of severity because Kell antigens are expressed on RBC precursors, causing suppression of erythropoiesis rather than just hemolysis' — this directly supports keyed answer A. Distractor B (IgM transport) is refuted by SRC-017 core concept (only IgG1/IgG3 cross placenta). Distractor D correctly reverses the source's key point. Distractor C (complement-driven intravascular hemolysis specific to anti-K) is not asserted anywhere in sources but is reasonably rejected since sources describe extravascular IgG-mediate

questions.subdomain: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn
questions.stem: A woman with known Rh alloimmunization (anti-D) delivers a term infant. Cord blood testing shows: forward Rh(D) typing negative, but the direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is strongly positive (4+) with anti-IgG. The infant's peripheral smear shows no spherocytes but does show circulating erythroblasts. What is the most appropriate next step, and what phenomenon does this pattern most likely represent?

options.text: Conclude the DAT positivity and erythroblastosis are due to ABO incompatibility rather than an Rh-mediated process, since the D typing was negative
Repeat the Rh typing using the same immediate-spin monoclonal IgM reagent only, since this type of discrepancy is typically due to reagent error at that phase
Report the infant as Rh-negative and conclude that no further workup or maternal postpartum RhIG consideration is needed, since forward typing is definitive
Perform an eluate on the infant's RBCs to identify the coating antibody's specificity; this pattern is consistent with a "blocked" Rh type from heavy maternal anti-D saturating fetal D-antigen sites
Assume the 4+ DAT reflects passively transferred anti-D from antenatal RhIG prophylaxis, since passively acquired anti-D rarely produces strong DAT reactivity

questions.explanation_correct: A D-negative forward type with a strongly positive DAT in an infant of a mother with known anti-D is the classic "blocked Rh" phenomenon: maternal anti-D so heavily coats fetal D-antigen sites that routine reagents cannot detect them, producing a false-negative D type. An eluate dissociates the bound antibody so its specificity (anti-D) can be confirmed, correctly identifying the infant as D-positive with HDFN — which also has implications for maternal RhIG management.

option_explanations.D: Correct: the sources specifically describe performing an eluate to resolve a D-negative type with a strongly positive DAT (blocked Rh), confirming true D-positive status.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this is the classic board trap — accepting a false-negative Rh type at face value would miss both a true HDFN diagnosis and the antigen status needed for maternal management.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: repeating with the same IgM-based reagent will not resolve blocked Rh, since the D sites are already saturated with maternal antibody; an eluate, not reagent repetition, is the correct step.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the morphology described (erythroblasts predominating, spherocytes rare) is characteristic of Rh HDFN, not ABO HDFN, which more typically shows spherocytes.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the mother has known active alloimmunization (anti-D from sensitization), not merely passive RhIG-derived anti-D, and in any case passive anti-D rarely reaches a strength associated with 4+ DAT reactivity; blocked Rh from active maternal anti-D is the better explanation here.

questions.teaching_point: A D-negative typing paired with a strongly positive DAT in the setting of known maternal anti-D should trigger an eluate to rule out blocked Rh before accepting the D-negative result.

source_references.locator: Definitions > Blocked Rh; Common Board Traps and Distractors > Blocked Rh Distractor
source_references.supports: Blocked Rh is a false-negative D type from heavy maternal anti-D coating fetal D sites, resolved by performing an eluate.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors > "Blocked Rh"
source_references.supports: Blocked Rh false-negative typing in a newborn from heavy maternal anti-D coating, resolved by eluate.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings > Morphology
source_references.supports: Spherocytes are common in ABO HDFN but rare in Rh HDFN, where erythroblasts predominate.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors > Passive vs Active Anti-D
source_references.supports: Passively acquired anti-D from RhIG rarely achieves a titer >4; a titer >4 in a mother usually indicates active alloimmunization.

review.notes: Near verbatim match to SRC-020 'Blocked Rh Distractor': 'If an infant's cells type as D-negative but have a 4+ DAT, perform an eluate to identify the blocked D antigen' and SRC-017's identical teaching point — strongly supports keyed answer A. Distractor D is supported by SRC-017 Morphology note (spherocytes common in ABO HDFN, rare in Rh HDFN where erythroblasts predominate — matches stem's erythroblasts-without-spherocytes finding). Distractor C (repeat IgM reagent) is a reasonable inference, not explicitly refuted in sources but logically sound given blocked-Rh mechanism. Issue: distractor

questions.subdomain: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn
questions.stem: A fetus at 28 weeks' gestation with confirmed maternal Rh alloimmunization undergoes cordocentesis after MCA-PSV Doppler showed a value of 1.8 MoM; fetal hemoglobin returns at 6 g/dL, consistent with severe anemia. The maternal-fetal medicine team proceeds with intrauterine transfusion (IUT). Which unit of red blood cells is most appropriate for this procedure?

options.text: Group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, CMV-safe, HbS-negative RBCs less than 7 days old
Group AB, Rh-negative, irradiated RBCs up to 21 days old
Crossmatched maternal whole blood, non-irradiated, to minimize alloantigen exposure to the fetus
Group O, Rh-positive, non-irradiated RBCs less than 7 days old, since D-positive cells will simply be cleared by the maternal/fetal anti-D response
Group O, Rh-negative, irradiated RBCs up to 21 days old, without CMV testing, since maternal CMV status is unknown

questions.explanation_correct: Intrauterine transfusion for severe fetal anemia (<10 g/dL) requires units that are group O, Rh-negative (usually), less than 7 days old, irradiated, CMV-safe, and HbS-negative. Irradiation is mandatory for IUT to prevent TA-GVHD, and the fresh, CMV-safe, HbS-negative, group O/Rh-negative specifications minimize additional risk to the fetus.

option_explanations.A: Correct: this precisely matches the stated IUT unit requirements — group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, CMV-safe, HbS-negative, and less than 7 days old.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: transfusing Rh-positive cells into a fetus with maternal anti-D would expose the cells to ongoing antibody-mediated destruction, worsening rather than correcting the anemia; irradiation is also mandatory and is omitted here.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the required ABO group for IUT units is group O, not AB, and the required unit age is less than 7 days, not up to 21 days.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: maternal blood is not the specified source for IUT units, and omitting irradiation risks TA-GVHD, which irradiation is specifically mandated to prevent.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: IUT units must be CMV-safe and less than 7 days old; using units up to 21 days old and skipping CMV testing does not meet the stated requirements.

questions.teaching_point: IUT units must simultaneously satisfy multiple safety specifications — group O, Rh-negative, fresh (<7 days), irradiated, CMV-safe, and HbS-negative — all of which serve distinct risk-reduction purposes for the fetus.

source_references.locator: Management > Intrauterine Transfusion (IUT)
source_references.supports: IUT is indicated for severe fetal anemia (<10 g/dL Hgb) and requires units that are group O, Rh-negative, <7 days old, irradiated, CMV-safe, and HbS-negative.

source_references.locator: Management > Intrauterine Transfusion (IUT)
source_references.supports: IUT typically requires Group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, HbS-negative RBCs less than 7 days old.

source_references.locator: Prevention > Irradiation
source_references.supports: Irradiation is mandatory for IUT, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates to prevent TA-GVHD.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach > Fetal Monitoring
source_references.supports: MCA-PSV Doppler >1.5 MoM indicates moderate-to-severe fetal anemia, providing the clinical trigger for cordocentesis/IUT in this vignette.

review.notes: SRC-017 states IUT requires units 'group O, Rh-negative (usually), <7 days old, irradiated, CMV-safe, and HbS-negative' for 'severe fetal anemia (<10 g/dL Hgb)' — matches keyed answer A and the stem's 6 g/dL fetal Hgb value exactly. SRC-020 corroborates group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, HbS-negative, <7 days (CMV-safe detail is sourced from SRC-017 only, but is consistent and not contradicted). MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM trigger for moderate-to-severe anemia matches SRC-017 Diagnostic Approach. Irradiation-mandatory-for-IUT claim matches SRC-017 Prevention section verbatim. All distractors (B non-irradi

questions.stem: A term newborn delivered to a mother with known anti-D alloimmunization has cord blood sent to the blood bank. The infant's RBCs do not spontaneously agglutinate when suspended in saline, but a direct antiglobulin test (DAT) performed with polyspecific antihuman globulin (AHG) reagent is strongly positive.

options.text: The maternal IgG anti-D bound to the infant's RBCs in vivo is an "incomplete" antibody too small to bridge the gap between adjacent sensitized cells; AHG reagent bridges this gap, cross-linking the cells into a visible agglutination lattice
Polyspecific AHG reagent cannot bind IgG-only sensitized cells, so monospecific anti-IgG reagent must always be substituted to detect this reaction
The AHG reagent works by stripping complement components off the RBC surface, which allows the residual IgG molecules on adjacent cells to self-aggregate
Bovine albumin must first be added to the cord blood sample to reduce the RBCs' zeta potential before AHG reagent can cross-link the sensitized cells
Overcentrifugation of the cord blood specimen produces the same cell-to-cell bridging effect that AHG reagent normally provides

questions.explanation_correct: The source distinguishes sensitization (in vivo antibody attachment to antigen) from agglutination (visible cross-linking of cells). IgG antibodies such as anti-D are described as "incomplete" because they are too small to bridge the gap between RBCs in saline; AHG reagent is required to bridge this gap and cross-link the sensitized cells into a visible lattice, which is exactly what occurs when maternal IgG has coated the infant's RBCs in HDFN.

option_explanations.A: Correct: matches the source's explicit sensitization-versus-agglutination and "incomplete antibody" concepts describing why AHG bridging is required for IgG-coated cells.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the source describes AHG reagent as binding to (not removing) human globulins already attached to RBC antigens; it does not strip complement from the cell surface.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source defines polyspecific AHG as reagent containing antibodies against both IgG and C3d, so it does detect IgG-only sensitization; monospecific reagent is an alternative for distinguishing components, not a requirement.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the source describes albumin as an enhancement medium that speeds antibody uptake during incubation of in-vitro test systems, not a required step to visualize AHG-mediated agglutination of already in-vivo-sensitized DAT cells.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the source lists overcentrifugation as a cause of false-positive agglutination (an artifact), not as an alternative mechanism for the genuine antibody-bridging action of AHG reagent.

questions.teaching_point: A positive DAT in HDFN reflects in vivo sensitization of neonatal RBCs by maternal IgG; because IgG is an "incomplete" antibody, AHG reagent is required to bridge sensitized cells into visible agglutination.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20 - Antiglobulin Testing & Enhancement Techniques, Core Concepts (lines 177-181)
source_references.supports: AHG test principle; sensitization vs. agglutination; incomplete IgG antibodies requiring AHG to bridge the gap between sensitized RBCs

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20, Enhancement Media and Laboratory Findings sections (lines 189-205)
source_references.supports: Polyspecific AHG reagent contains antibodies against both IgG and C3d; enhancement media (albumin) mechanism; false-positive causes including overcentrifugation

review.notes: The core tested mechanism (sensitization vs. agglutination; IgG as an 'incomplete' antibody requiring AHG to bridge sensitized cells) is verbatim from SRC-032 Node 20, Core Concepts (lines 177-181), and all four distractor rationales (B: AHG doesn't strip complement; C: polyspecific AHG does detect IgG per line 189; D: albumin is an in-vitro enhancement medium per line 195, not a DAT-visualization requirement; E: overcentrifugation is a listed false-positive cause per line 205, not a bridging mechanism) are accurately refuted using source text. Option A is the clear single best answer. However

questions.stem: A pregnant patient with anti-D alloimmunization undergoes serial antibody titers to help guide the timing of fetal surveillance. Her titer at 18 weeks, performed by the tube method at her local hospital laboratory, was 8. At 22 weeks, her titer is repeated at a regional reference laboratory using the gel method and results in 32.

options.text: The higher gel-method result should be disregarded because gel testing requires monospecific anti-C3d reagent, which is not appropriate for anti-D detection
The higher result indicates that the maternal antibody class switched from IgM to IgG between the two testing dates

The apparent rise may not represent a true increase in antibody strength, because gel-method titers are inherently more sensitive and typically yield higher results than tube-method titers; serial titers should be performed by the same method, and ideally the same laboratory, for valid comparison
This is an unequivocal, clinically significant rise that by itself confirms severe fetal anemia requiring immediate delivery
Because titer methodology should always produce identical results regardless of technique, this discrepancy indicates a laboratory testing error at one of the two sites

questions.explanation_correct: The source explicitly notes as a board trap that gel-method titers are more sensitive and typically yield higher levels than tube-method titers. A change in method between two time points (tube at 18 weeks, gel at 22 weeks) can therefore produce an apparent rise that does not reflect a true change in maternal antibody strength, so consistent methodology is needed to validly trend serial titers.

option_explanations.C: Correct: directly reflects the source's teaching that gel titers run higher than tube titers, making cross-method comparison unreliable.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: no numeric critical titer value or delivery threshold is provided in the source; this overstates what a single titer result establishes.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: contradicts the source, which states that gel and tube methods are expected to differ (gel yields higher levels), so an identical-result assumption is false.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the source does not address immunoglobulin class switching as an explanation for titer differences between methods.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: misapplies the source's polyspecific-versus-monospecific AHG reagent distinction; reagent specificity for IgG versus complement is a separate concept from why gel and tube titer values differ.

questions.teaching_point: Serial antibody titers must be performed by a consistent method (ideally the same laboratory) because gel and tube techniques are not numerically interchangeable, and an apparent titer rise across methods can be a testing artifact rather than a true increase.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20, Common Board Traps and Distractors - Gel vs. Tube Titers (line 211)
source_references.supports: Gel-method titers are more sensitive and yield higher results than tube-method titers, a stated board trap

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20, Definitions (lines 189-191)
source_references.supports: Definitions of polyspecific vs. monospecific AHG reagent used to construct a misapplied distractor

review.notes: The keyed fact — gel-method titers run more sensitive/higher than tube-method titers, making cross-method serial comparison invalid — is a direct, verbatim board trap from SRC-032 Node 20 (line 211). Option A is correctly the single best answer; C is directly contradicted by that same line, and B overstates what a single titer value establishes (no critical-titer threshold appears anywhere in the sources). However, as with Q0, the entire obstetric premise (serial anti-D titers in pregnancy 'to help guide the timing of fetal surveillance') is asserted with no support in any of the three sources

questions.stem: A 28-year-old G3P2 woman who is group O, Rh-negative is found on her first-trimester antibody screen to have anti-D. Her prior pregnancy was complicated by mild HDFN that did not require intervention. Her obstetrician requests a laboratory surveillance plan to trend her antibody strength across this pregnancy so that escalation to fetal monitoring can be triggered appropriately.

options.text: Perform serial direct antiglobulin tests on maternal peripheral blood samples throughout the pregnancy to detect and quantify her circulating alloantibody

Perform serial antibody titers on the maternal serum by antiglobulin testing, using the same method and, whenever possible, the same laboratory for each successive titer so sequential results can be validly compared and trended
Alternate between the tube and gel methods for each successive titer to maximize the sensitivity of alloantibody detection across the pregnancy
Use monospecific anti-C3d AHG reagent exclusively for all titer testing, since anti-D is presumed to be a complement-fixing antibody
Omit check cells on any titer result that is negative, since her antibody screen was already confirmed positive earlier in the pregnancy

questions.explanation_correct: The indirect antiglobulin test (IAT) is the technique used to detect in-vitro reactions between reagent RBCs and unknown serum/plasma, which is the basis of antibody screening and titration on maternal serum. Because gel-method titers are more sensitive and run higher than tube-method titers, using a single consistent method (and ideally the same laboratory) across serial testing is necessary for sequential results to be validly compared and trended over the pregnancy.

option_explanations.B: Correct: combines the source's IAT definition (testing serum/plasma against reagent cells) with its teaching that consistent titer methodology is required for valid trending.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the DAT detects in-vivo coating of a patient's own RBCs, not free circulating antibody in maternal serum, so it is the wrong test for monitoring maternal antibody strength.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: directly contradicts the source's teaching that switching titer methods invalidates trend comparisons because gel titers run higher than tube titers.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: misapplies the monospecific (anti-IgG-only or anti-C3d-only) versus polyspecific reagent distinction; the source does not tie titer reagent selection to an assumption about complement fixation.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the source states check cells must be added to all negative AHG tests to confirm the reagent was added and is functional, with no stated exception for a previously positive screen.

questions.teaching_point: Maternal alloantibody surveillance during pregnancy relies on the indirect antiglobulin test performed on serum with a consistent titer method and laboratory, not the DAT (which tests the patient's own RBCs for in-vivo sensitization).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20, Definitions (line 187)
source_references.supports: IAT definition: detects in-vitro reactions between reagent RBCs and unknown serum/plasma, e.g., antibody screen/titer

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 20, Common Board Traps and Distractors (lines 211-215)
source_references.supports: Gel vs. tube titer methodology must remain consistent for valid comparison; check cells required on all negative AHG tests

review.notes: Option A correctly combines two sourced facts: the IAT tests serum/plasma against reagent cells (SRC-032 line 187) and titer methodology/lab must stay consistent for valid trending (line 211). Distractors are well refuted: B conflates DAT (in-vivo RBC coating) with IAT/titer (free serum antibody) — a real and correctly-flagged error; C directly contradicts line 211; E contradicts the explicit, exception-free check-cell requirement at line 215. Single best answer is well supported. As with Q0/Q1, however, the disease-specific framing — that this exact protocol constitutes 'maternal alloantibody

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman who is blood group A, Rh(D) negative and alloimmunized to the D antigen from a prior pregnancy (anti-D identified on antibody screen) is now pregnant with an Rh(D)-positive fetus. Obstetric ultrasound at 24 weeks shows findings consistent with fetal anemia. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which the maternal antibody produces this fetal effect?

options.text: Anti-D activates complement within the maternal circulation, and activated complement proteins then cross the placenta to lyse fetal red cells directly
Maternal IgG anti-D crosses the placenta and binds D-positive fetal red cells, leading to their destruction (hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn)
Maternal anti-D antibody crosses the placenta and suppresses fetal bone marrow erythropoiesis directly, without binding fetal red cells
Maternal IgM anti-D crosses the placenta and directly lyses fetal red cells intravascularly via complement
Anti-D antibody binds fetal red cells only at the moment of delivery, when maternal and fetal blood mix, causing acute intravascular hemolysis of the neonate

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that HDFN pathophysiology involves IgG crossing the placenta, and separately distinguish IgG from IgM by their clinical kinetics. Because the D antigen is IgG-immunogenic and only IgG antibody is described as crossing the placenta in HDFN, maternal IgG anti-D transits to the fetal circulation and coats fetal D-positive red cells, leading to their destruction — the source-defined mechanism of hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the source statement that HDFN pathophysiology is driven by IgG crossing the placenta and binding fetal (D-positive) red cells, producing hemolysis.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources distinguish IgG from IgM by clinical kinetics, and only IgG is described as the placenta-crossing antibody responsible for HDFN; IgM is not the mediator in this scenario.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the source-described mechanism is antibody (IgG) crossing the placenta and binding fetal cells, not maternal complement proteins crossing the placenta.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect on timing: the stem shows fetal anemia detected by ultrasound at 24 weeks, i.e., an antenatal effect, which is inconsistent with a mechanism restricted to blood mixing only at delivery.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the source describes antibody-mediated destruction (hemolysis) of red cells as the HDFN mechanism, not direct suppression of erythropoiesis.

questions.teaching_point: Only IgG antibodies cross the placenta; this is the mechanistic basis for HDFN, distinguishing it from IgM-mediated processes that remain confined to the maternal circulation.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 section, 'HDFN Pathophysiology' bullet; Domain 1 section, 'Basic Immunology' bullet
source_references.supports: States HDFN pathophysiology is due to IgG crossing the placenta, and separately lists IgG vs. IgM clinical kinetics as a core immunology concept.

source_references.locator: Domain VII, Major Concepts
source_references.supports: Confirms HDFN as an antibody-mediated disease with 'pathogenesis of ABO and Rh HDFN' listed as a major concept.

review.notes: Core fact is directly supported: SRC-043 line 89 states 'HDFN Pathophysiology: IgG crossing the placenta; MCA-PSV for fetal anemia monitoring.' This grounds option A's central claim (IgG crosses placenta -> HDFN). However, the elaborated mechanism in the stem/explanation ('binds D-positive fetal red cells, leading to their destruction') and the IgG-vs-IgM contrast used to reject option B are extrapolations from a single 15-word bullet, not verbatim source content; SRC-043 line 15 only lists 'IgG vs. IgM clinical kinetics' as a topic heading without detailing that IgM cannot cross the placenta

questions.stem: A 29-year-old Rh(D)-negative woman, previously alloimmunized to the D antigen, has antibody identification testing performed at 26 weeks of her current pregnancy with an Rh(D)-positive fetus; anti-D is confirmed. Her obstetrician wants to assess the fetus for anemia without performing an invasive procedure. Which of the following is the most appropriate study to obtain?

options.text: Direct antiglobulin test (DAT) performed on umbilical cord blood
Middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV) Doppler ultrasound
Amniocentesis for spectrophotometric analysis of amniotic fluid
Serial maternal anti-D antibody titers alone, with no further fetal testing
Maternal complete blood count and reticulocyte count

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources specifically pair HDFN pathophysiology with MCA-PSV as the tool used to monitor for fetal anemia. Because it is a noninvasive Doppler ultrasound measurement, it is the appropriate study to assess this alloimmunized pregnancy for evolving fetal anemia without the risks of an invasive procedure.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the sources explicitly name MCA-PSV as the modality used for fetal anemia monitoring in the setting of HDFN.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: amniocentesis is an invasive procedure; the source specifically designates MCA-PSV, not amniotic fluid analysis, as the fetal anemia monitoring tool, and the stem asks for a noninvasive option.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: DAT is described in the sources as a postnatal diagnostic test associated with immune-mediated hemolysis (positive DAT); it requires a sample obtained at/after delivery and does not assess an in-utero fetus prenatally.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: antibody identification/titer is part of maternal serologic screening, but the source specifically identifies MCA-PSV — not repeat maternal titers — as the means of monitoring the fetus itself for anemia.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this evaluates maternal, not fetal, hematologic status and does not address the question of fetal anemia.

questions.teaching_point: MCA-PSV Doppler ultrasound is the noninvasive, source-designated method for monitoring fetal anemia risk in alloimmunized pregnancies.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 section, 'HDFN Pathophysiology' bullet
source_references.supports: States that MCA-PSV is used for fetal anemia monitoring in the HDFN pathophysiology bullet.

source_references.locator: Section 6, 'Immune-Mediated Hemolysis and AIHA (Ch 14)'
source_references.supports: Describes DAT as the diagnostic hallmark used in immune-mediated hemolysis (Ch 14), supporting its characterization as a hemolysis-diagnosis test rather than a prenatal fetal-anemia monitoring tool.

source_references.locator: Domain VII, Major Concepts
source_references.supports: Lists pathogenesis of ABO/Rh HDFN and RhIg prophylaxis as major concepts, framing the clinical context of alloimmunized pregnancy.

review.notes: The keyed answer is well supported: SRC-043 explicitly pairs HDFN with 'MCA-PSV for fetal anemia monitoring,' directly matching option A as the noninvasive fetal-anemia study. However, option C's rationale asserts specific facts not present in any source reviewed: that DAT 'requires a sample obtained at/after delivery' and functions as a cord-blood postnatal test. The only DAT reference found (SRC-027 line 167) is 'Immune-Mediated Hemolysis and AIHA (Ch 14): Focuses on the positive DAT and managing autoimmune hemolytic anemia' -- this is about adult AIHA, not neonatal cord-blood DAT testing in

questions.stem: A 24-year-old primigravida, blood group B, Rh(D) negative, presents for her first prenatal visit. Her antibody screen is negative, and her partner is Rh(D) positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate management to reduce this patient's risk of maternal alloimmunization to the D antigen during this pregnancy?

options.text: Transfuse Rh(D)-positive red blood cells to the mother
Perform therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE)
Administer intravenous immune globulin (IVIG)
Administer Rh immune globulin (RhIG)
Withhold any prophylaxis and repeat the antibody screen only if she becomes symptomatic later in pregnancy

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that RhIG is indicated for Rh-negative mothers specifically to prevent alloimmunization to the D antigen. This patient is Rh(D) negative with a currently negative antibody screen and an Rh(D)-positive partner, placing her at risk for D sensitization during this pregnancy; RhIG prophylaxis is the management directed at preventing that alloimmunization.

option_explanations.D: Correct: the sources identify RhIG as indicated for Rh-negative mothers to prevent D alloimmunization, matching this patient's risk profile.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: RhIG is a preventive (prophylactic) measure given while the antibody screen is still negative; waiting until sensitization might already be reflected in a positive screen defeats the preventive purpose described in the source.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: exposing the mother to D-positive red cells is the sensitizing event that causes alloimmunization, the opposite of the intended preventive goal.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources identify TPE as a primary treatment for TTP, not as a method for preventing RBC alloantibody formation.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: IVIG is a distinct product; the sources specifically identify RhIG, not generic IVIG, as indicated to prevent D alloimmunization in Rh-negative mothers.

questions.teaching_point: RhIG prophylaxis is the source-defined intervention to prevent D alloimmunization in Rh-negative pregnant patients; it must be given before sensitization occurs, not reactively after a positive antibody screen.

source_references.locator: Domain VII, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States that RhIg is indicated for Rh-negative mothers to prevent alloimmunization to the D antigen.

source_references.locator: Section, apheresis high-yield facts
source_references.supports: States that TPE is a primary treatment for TTP, supporting its characterization as unrelated to alloimmunization prevention.

review.notes: The keyed answer is strongly and directly supported: SRC-010 line 101 states verbatim, 'RhIg is indicated for Rh-negative mothers to prevent alloimmunization to the D antigen.' However, the source_reference for option D's TPE/TTP rationale cites SRC-028 ('Section, apheresis high-yield facts') -- this is a misattribution. SRC-028 (domain-4-advanced-technologies-specialized-testing-study-guide.md) contains no mention of TPE, TTP, ADAMTS13, thrombotic, or plasma exchange anywhere in the file (verified by full-text grep); the actual 'TPE is a primary treatment for TTP (Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic

questions.stem: A Rh-negative multigravida with a remote history of red cell transfusion is now found to have anti-K alloimmunization and is confirmed to be carrying a K-positive fetus. Her anti-K titer remains low and stable throughout the pregnancy, yet serial middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV) Doppler measurements progressively rise above 1.5 multiples of the median (MoM), indicating worsening fetal anemia that eventually requires intrauterine transfusion. Which mechanism best explains why the severity of fetal anemia in anti-K alloimmunization correlates poorly with the maternal antibody titer?

options.text: The fetal anemia in this case reflects maternal/fetal ABO incompatibility enhancing clearance of the anti-K antibody before it can cross the placenta
Like maternal anti-D, anti-K causes extravascular destruction of mature circulating fetal RBCs, so its severity should track the maternal titer in the same fashion as Rh-mediated hemolytic disease

Anti-K IgG binds Kell antigens expressed on early erythroid precursors in the fetal bone marrow, suppressing erythropoiesis rather than acting predominantly through hemolysis of mature circulating RBCs
Anti-K crosses the placenta as IgM independent of maternal serum antibody concentration, producing fetal disease that is unrelated to the measured maternal titer

questions.explanation_correct: Kell blood group antigens are expressed on early RBC precursors in the fetal marrow, not just mature circulating cells. Anti-K therefore suppresses erythropoiesis at the precursor stage rather than acting mainly through hemolysis of mature RBCs, which is why maternal titer (a measure of circulating antibody, not marrow effect) is a poor predictor of fetal anemia severity in anti-K disease, unlike anti-D.

option_explanations.C: Correct: the source explicitly attributes anti-K's disproportionate severity to suppression of erythropoiesis via targeting of RBC precursors, distinct from titer-driven hemolysis.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: this describes the Rh/anti-D mechanism (extravascular hemolysis of mature IgG-coated cells), which is precisely why Rh disease correlates with titer while anti-K does not — the two mechanisms are contrasted, not equivalent.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: only IgG (subclasses IgG1 and IgG3) is actively transported across the placenta; IgM is not transplacentally transferred, so this proposed mechanism is not supported.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this repurposes a different concept — maternal/fetal ABO incompatibility is described as protective against RhD sensitization of the mother, not as a mechanism affecting anti-K antibody clearance or fetal disease severity.

questions.teaching_point: Anti-K severity is driven by suppression of fetal erythropoiesis at the RBC precursor level, not solely by titer-dependent hemolysis as in Rh disease — so anti-K pregnancies are followed by MCA-PSV Doppler regardless of titer trend.

source_references.locator: Domain VII – Laboratory Findings – Anti-K Specificity
source_references.supports: Anti-K titer is less predictive of severity because Kell antigens are expressed on RBC precursors, causing marrow suppression rather than hemolysis alone.

source_references.locator: Domain VII – Core Concepts – Maternofetal Transfer
source_references.supports: Only IgG (IgG1/IgG3) is actively transported across the placenta, underlying HDFN pathogenesis.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 2 – Clinical Presentations – Transfusion Reactions
source_references.supports: Rh IgG antibodies typically cause extravascular hemolysis, contrasted with the Kell precursor-suppression mechanism.

source_references.locator: Domain VII – Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls – Protective Effect
source_references.supports: Maternal/fetal ABO incompatibility partially protects the mother from RhD sensitization (concept misapplied to Kell in distractor D).

review.notes: SRC-017 'Laboratory Findings – Anti-K Specificity' explicitly states anti-K titer is less predictive of severity because Kell antigens are expressed on RBC precursors, causing suppression of erythropoiesis rather than hemolysis — directly grounds the keyed answer. SRC-017 'Core Concepts – Maternofetal Transfer' confirms only IgG (IgG1/IgG3) crosses the placenta, correctly refuting distractor C. SRC-020 confirms Rh IgG antibodies typically cause extravascular hemolysis, correctly contrasting with distractor B. SRC-017 'Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls – Protective Effect' confirms the ABO-incomp

questions.stem: A neonate born at 33 weeks' gestation had received three intrauterine transfusions in utero with group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, leukoreduced, HbS-negative donor red cells for severe RhD hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn. At delivery, cord blood forward Rh typing with anti-D reagent shows a mixed-field pattern — a distinct population of clearly agglutinated cells alongside a distinct population of clearly unagglutinated cells — rather than a single uniformly weak reaction. Which of the following best explains this typing pattern?

options.text: Weak D phenotype, in which a reduced number of D antigen sites on a single RBC population produces uniformly diminished reactivity detectable only at the antiglobulin phase
Blocked Rh phenomenon, in which heavy coating of the neonate's D-positive cells by maternal anti-D produces a uniformly false-negative direct typing result
Artificial chimerism, in which two distinct RBC populations are circulating together — the neonate's own RBCs and residual transfused group O, Rh-negative donor RBCs
Partial D phenotype, in which qualitative loss of specific D epitopes on a single RBC population causes discordant reactivity between different anti-D reagent clones

questions.explanation_correct: Intrauterine transfusion introduces donor RBCs (group O, Rh-negative) directly into the fetal circulation, creating artificial chimerism — two genetically distinct RBC populations coexisting in one individual. This is serologically recognized by a mixed-field agglutination pattern (some cells agglutinate, some do not), which is different from a uniform weak reaction (weak D), a uniform false-negative reaction (blocked Rh), or discordant single-population reactivity across reagent clones (partial D).

option_explanations.C: Correct: artificial chimerism is most commonly induced by transfusion, and its characteristic serologic signature is mixed-field agglutination during ABO/Rh typing — exactly the pattern described, consistent with a mixture of the neonate's own cells and the transfused group O, Rh-negative donor cells.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: weak D reflects a quantitatively reduced number of D sites on a single RBC population, producing uniform weak reactivity typically only detected at the antiglobulin (IAT) phase — not a bimodal mixed-field pattern of two separate populations.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: blocked Rh describes a uniformly false-negative D typing result caused by heavy antibody saturation of all D sites, resolved by an eluate — it does not produce a mixed-field pattern of agglutinated versus unagglutinated cells.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: partial D is a qualitative loss of specific D epitopes within a single RBC population, which can cause discordant results between different anti-D reagent clones, but it does not represent two distinct circulating cell populations as seen in mixed-field agglutination.

questions.teaching_point: A mixed-field (bimodal) agglutination pattern in a neonate with an IUT history should prompt recognition of artificial chimerism from residual donor RBCs, not weak D, partial D, or blocked Rh — each of which produces a different, non-bimodal serologic pattern.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 51 – Chimerism – Diagnostic Logic
source_references.supports: Artificial chimerism is most commonly induced by blood transfusion and presents serologically as mixed-field agglutination on ABO/Rh typing or phenotyping.

source_references.locator: Domain VII – Management – Intrauterine Transfusion (IUT)
source_references.supports: IUT uses group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, HbS-negative, CMV-safe donor RBCs, establishing the donor cell population responsible for chimerism after IUT.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 2 – Definitions and Common Board Traps
source_references.supports: Definitions of weak D, partial D, and blocked Rh distinguishing their serologic patterns from mixed-field chimerism.

review.notes: SRC-012 'Subdomain Node 51 – Chimerism – Diagnostic Logic' explicitly states artificial chimerism (most commonly transfusion-induced) presents as mixed-field agglutination on ABO/Rh typing — directly grounds the keyed answer, and the IUT donor-unit description matches SRC-017's IUT specs. SRC-020 correctly grounds Weak D (uniform reactivity, AHG-phase detection) and Blocked Rh (uniform false-negative from maternal anti-D coating, resolved by eluate) as contrasted patterns. However, distractor D's specific claim that partial D 'causes discordant reactivity between different anti-D reagent clone

questions.stem: Cordocentesis performed on a 27-week fetus with severe RhD alloimmune hemolytic disease reveals a fetal hemoglobin of 6.2 g/dL. The maternal-fetal medicine team proceeds immediately with intrauterine transfusion. Which of the following donor RBC units is most appropriate for this transfusion?

options.text: Group O, Rh-positive, irradiated, leukoreduced, HbS-negative RBCs collected 5 days earlier
Group AB, Rh-negative, irradiated, leukoreduced, HbS-negative RBCs collected 5 days earlier
Group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, leukoreduced, HbS-negative RBCs collected 5 days earlier
Group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, leukoreduced, HbS-positive RBCs collected 5 days earlier
Group O, Rh-negative, non-irradiated, leukoreduced, HbS-negative RBCs collected 5 days earlier

questions.explanation_correct: Intrauterine transfusion requires units that are group O, Rh-negative, irradiated (to prevent TA-GVHD), leukoreduced (CMV-safe), HbS-negative, and recently collected (fresh, well within the required age window) to maximize 2,3-DPG and minimize potassium. Option A is the only unit meeting all of these required characteristics simultaneously.

option_explanations.C: Correct: this unit satisfies every required characteristic for an IUT product — group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, leukoreduced (CMV-safe), HbS-negative, and freshly collected.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: IUT typically requires Rh-negative units; an Rh-positive unit does not meet the standard product requirement, even though the other characteristics are otherwise appropriate.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: irradiation is mandatory for IUT units to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD); a non-irradiated unit is unsafe for this indication regardless of its other qualifying features.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: group O is the required ABO group for IUT (and for neonatal transfusion generally) to avoid incompatibility; a group AB unit does not meet this requirement.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: HbS-negative status is a specified requirement for IUT units; an HbS-positive unit fails to meet this product specification.

questions.teaching_point: IUT RBC units must simultaneously satisfy all required specifications — group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, CMV-safe (leukoreduced), HbS-negative, and fresh — since deviation from any single characteristic makes the unit inappropriate for fetal transfusion.

source_references.locator: Domain VII – Management – Intrauterine Transfusion (IUT)
source_references.supports: IUT requires units that are group O, Rh-negative (usually), fresh (<7 days), irradiated, CMV-safe, and HbS-negative.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 2 – Management – Intrauterine Transfusion (IUT)
source_references.supports: IUT typically requires Group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, HbS-negative RBCs less than 7 days old.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36 – RBC Requirements / Safety Standards / ABO-Rh Logic
source_references.supports: Units for large-volume/IUT should be fresh to maximize 2,3-DPG and minimize potassium; cellular components must be irradiated to prevent TA-GVHD and leukoreduced for CMV-reduced risk; group O, Rh-negative is standard.

review.notes: SRC-017 and SRC-020 both explicitly specify IUT units as group O, Rh-negative, irradiated, HbS-negative, <7 days old; SRC-051 (Subdomain Node 36) corroborates freshness (<7-10 days to preserve 2,3-DPG/minimize K+), mandatory irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention), leukoreduction (CMV-safe), and group O/Rh-negative standard. The stem's fetal Hgb of 6.2 g/dL correctly falls under the source's <10 g/dL severe-anemia IUT threshold. Each distractor (Rh-positive, non-irradiated, group AB, HbS-positive) is cleanly and individually refuted by the same source requirements. No unsupported or invented facts id

questions.subdomain: Fetal and Neonatal Transfusions, Phototherapy, IVIG, etc
questions.stem: A fetus at 26 weeks' gestation undergoes intrauterine transfusion for severe hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn due to anti-D. The unit selected is group O, D-negative, crossmatched against maternal serum, and leukoreduced, but is transfused without irradiation. Ten days after birth, the neonate develops fever, a diffuse maculopapular rash, watery diarrhea, and pancytopenia. Skin biopsy shows a dense lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermal-epidermal junction. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism of this complication?

options.text: Donor plasma proteins cross-link recipient IgE on mast cells, producing an anaphylactic reaction
Passively transferred maternal IgG anti-D continues to coat and hemolyze the neonate's own RBCs after delivery
Viable donor T-lymphocytes in the transfused unit engraft in the immunologically immature host and proliferate, attacking host skin, gut, and marrow
Recipient antibodies directed against donor leukocyte antigens trigger cytokine release, producing a febrile nonhemolytic reaction
Complement-fixing antibody in the donor unit causes rapid intravascular hemolysis of the neonate's own red cells

questions.explanation_correct: The delayed onset (10 days), fever, rash, diarrhea, pancytopenia, and lymphocytic dermal infiltrate describe transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD). The sources identify TA-GVHD as a delayed immune reaction mediated by donor T-cells, prevented specifically by gamma irradiation (25 Gy), which halts T-cell proliferation. Neonates are explicitly named among the at-risk populations (along with BMT recipients and directed donations from relatives) for whom cellular components must be irradiated, reflecting their inability to reject engrafting allogeneic donor lymphocytes when a nonirradiated unit is given.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect: this describes the mechanism of HDFN hemolysis itself (IgG-mediated destruction of antigen-positive RBCs), not the rash/diarrhea/pancytopenia/lymphocytic skin infiltrate described, which reflects a distinct donor-cell-mediated process.
option_explanations.C: Correct: TA-GVHD results from engraftment and proliferation of viable donor T-lymphocytes in a host unable to reject them; irradiation is the specific preventive measure because it stops T-cell proliferation.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction pathophysiology (antibodies to donor leukocytes or preformed cytokines), which presents as isolated fever, not the multisystem, delayed picture described.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this describes anaphylaxis, which is immediate-onset with respiratory distress/hypotension, not a 10-day delayed rash and pancytopenia.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this describes acute hemolytic transfusion reaction physiology (complement-fixing antibody causing intravascular hemolysis), which does not produce rash, diarrhea, or the described skin biopsy findings.

questions.teaching_point: Fetal and neonatal transfusions (including intrauterine transfusion for HDFN) must be irradiated because these hosts are immunologically immature and cannot reject viable donor T-lymphocytes; unirradiated units risk TA-GVHD, a delayed, often fatal donor-T-cell-mediated reaction distinct from hemolytic, febrile, or allergic reactions.

source_references.locator: Domain IV.1 Blood Component Modification - Irradiation
source_references.supports: 25 Gy irradiation to center of unit prevents T-cell proliferation and thus TA-GVHD

source_references.locator: Domain II Transfusion Reactions table - Delayed Immune reactions
source_references.supports: TA-GVHD is listed as a delayed immune reaction mediated by donor T-cells

source_references.locator: Prevention section - TA-GVHD
source_references.supports: Gamma irradiation of cellular components is required for at-risk patients including neonates <1200g, BMT recipients, and directed donations from relatives

source_references.locator: Node 31 AHTR pathophysiology; Node 32 Non-Hemolytic Acute Reactions
source_references.supports: Distractor mechanisms for AHTR (complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis), FNHTR (antibodies to donor leukocytes/cytokines), and anaphylaxis (IgE-mediated, associated with IgA deficiency)

review.notes: The core teaching point (irradiation prevents TA-GVHD via donor T-cell proliferation; TA-GVHD is listed as a delayed immune reaction mediated by donor T-cells; neonates are an explicitly named at-risk group) is directly supported by SRC-044 (lines 137, 185) and SRC-019 (line 67). However, none of the four approved sources describe the actual clinical presentation of TA-GVHD used to build the vignette (fever, diffuse maculopapular rash, watery diarrhea, pancytopenia, or the skin-biopsy finding of a dense lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermal-epidermal junction), nor do they state that IUT units

questions.subdomain: Fetal and Neonatal Transfusions, Phototherapy, IVIG, etc
questions.stem: A term neonate born to a group O, D-positive mother becomes visibly jaundiced within the first 24 hours of life. The infant's blood type is A, D-positive, and the direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive. To identify the antibody specificity eluted from the neonate's RBCs and support the diagnosis of ABO hemolytic disease of the newborn, which elution technique is the laboratory standard for this indication?

options.text: Chloroquine diphosphate treatment
Cold-acid elution
Lui freeze-thaw elution
Donath-Landsteiner test
Acid glycine/EDTA elution

questions.explanation_correct: The approved curriculum map explicitly pairs elution techniques with their standard clinical indications: Lui freeze-thaw elution is designated as the standard method for investigating ABO HDFN in neonates, distinguished from cold-acid elution, which is used to recover warm-reactive auto/alloantibodies (e.g., in WAIHA workups) rather than neonatal ABO incompatibility.

option_explanations.C: Correct: explicitly listed as the standard technique for investigating ABO HDFN in neonates.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: cold-acid elution is used to recover warm-reactive auto- or alloantibodies from sensitized RBCs, the source explicitly contrasts this with Lui freeze-thaw for ABO HDFN.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: chloroquine diphosphate is used to dissociate IgG from RBCs to permit phenotyping of DAT-positive cells, not to identify eluted antibody specificity for HDFN diagnosis.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: acid glycine/EDTA is used to remove antibodies from RBCs (and destroys Kell system antigens in the process), a different application than confirming ABO HDFN antibody specificity.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the Donath-Landsteiner test diagnoses paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria via a biphasic hemolysin, an entity unrelated to ABO HDFN.

questions.teaching_point: Lui freeze-thaw elution is the standard technique for confirming ABO HDFN in neonates; cold-acid elution, chloroquine diphosphate, acid glycine/EDTA, and the Donath-Landsteiner test each serve different, specific serologic purposes and are common board distractors when the vignette involves a jaundiced newborn.

source_references.locator: Core Immunohematology Hierarchy - Antibody Identification: Elutions
source_references.supports: States Lui freeze-thaw is used for ABO HDFN while cold-acid elution is used for warm autoantibodies

source_references.locator: Essential Serological Methods table - Methods 4-4, 4-1, 2-20, 2-21, 4-11

Independently the answer is clearly A, matching the key, and no other opti

questions.subdomain: Fetal and Neonatal Transfusions, Phototherapy, IVIG, etc
questions.stem: A neonate born at 27 weeks' gestation (birth weight 980 g) has hemolytic disease of the newborn due to anti-D and requires a simple RBC transfusion for symptomatic anemia. The blood bank has an ABO/D-compatible, prestorage-leukoreduced RBC unit ready to issue. Which additional processing step must be performed before this unit is transfused, and what complication does it specifically address?

options.text: A repeat crossmatch only, since ABO/D compatibility has already been confirmed
Pathogen inactivation with riboflavin/UV light to prevent transfusion-transmitted infection
No further processing is needed; prestorage leukoreduction already protects against donor lymphocyte-mediated disease in this patient
Additional leukoreduction to further reduce the risk of HLA alloimmunization
Gamma irradiation (25 Gy) to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease

questions.explanation_correct: This neonate's birth weight (980 g) falls below 1200 g, placing the infant in the explicitly named at-risk group (along with BMT recipients and recipients of directed donations from relatives) for whom cellular blood components must be gamma irradiated (25 Gy) to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease. Leukoreduction, by contrast, is credited only with preventing febrile nonhemolytic reactions, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission — it does not substitute for irradiation.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect: leukoreduction's documented benefits are prevention of FNHTR, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission — TA-GVHD prevention is not among them, so leukoreduction alone is insufficient for this at-risk neonate.
option_explanations.E: Correct: irradiation to 25 Gy specifically prevents donor T-cell proliferation (TA-GVHD), and neonates under 1200 g are an explicitly named at-risk group requiring this step.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: pathogen inactivation via riboflavin/psoralen and UV light is applied to platelets and plasma to disrupt pathogen nucleic acids; it is not the indicated processing step for this RBC unit or this specific risk.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: leukoreduction (already performed prestorage) is credited with reducing HLA sensitization, FNHTR, and CMV transmission, but repeating it does not address the TA-GVHD risk relevant to this low-birth-weight neonate.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: confirming ABO/D compatibility does not address the cellular (donor T-cell) risk that irradiation specifically mitigates in this at-risk patient.

questions.teaching_point: For fetal/neonatal transfusion support in HDFN, irradiation and leukoreduction serve distinct, non-interchangeable purposes: irradiation (25 Gy) is mandatory for at-risk neonates (<1200 g) to prevent TA-GVHD, while leukoreduction addresses FNHTR, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission — a classic board trap is assuming leukoreduction alone suffices.

source_references.locator: Prevention section - TA-GVHD
source_references.supports: Gamma irradiation required for at-risk patients, explicitly including neonates under 1200 g

source_references.locator: Domain IV.1 Blood Component Modification - Irradiation, Leukoreduction, Pathogen Inactivation
source_references.supports: Distinguishes irradiation (prevents TA-GVHD via T-cell proliferation block) from leukoreduction (prevents FNHTR, HLA sensitization, CMV transmission) and pathogen inactivation (platelets/plasma pathogen nucleic acid disruption)

review.notes: Fully grounded and well-mapped to the blueprint node. SRC-019 line 67 explicitly names 'neonates <1200g' as an at-risk group requiring gamma irradiation of cellular components to prevent TA-GVHD, and this neonate's 980g birth weight falls squarely under that threshold. SRC-044 lines 185/187/189 cleanly distinguish irradiation (prevents TA-GVHD via T-cell proliferation block), leukoreduction (prevents FNHTR, HLA sensitization, CMV transmission), and pathogen inactivation (riboflavin/UV, applied to platelets/plasma, not RBCs) -- exactly matching the stem and every distractor rationale (A, C, D,

questions.subdomain: Congenital Red Cell Membrane and Enzyme Defects
questions.stem: A term newborn is noted to have mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Over the following years the child has a persistent, compensated hemolytic anemia with reticulocytosis. A peripheral blood smear obtained at a well-child visit shows numerous stomatocytes. Comprehensive pretransfusion RBC antigen typing shows no detectable reactivity with anti-D, anti-C, anti-E, anti-c, or anti-e reagents, even though the same reagents react normally against known positive control cells. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of this child's chronic hemolytic anemia?

options.text: An acquired somatic mutation in PIGA causes loss of glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) membrane anchors, leading to chronic intravascular hemolysis
A hybrid RHD/RHCE gene produces qualitatively altered D epitopes (partial D) while the other Rh antigens remain normally expressed

A congenital defect in RhAG (Rh-associated glycoprotein) prevents normal membrane insertion of the Rh protein complex, producing the Rh-deficiency (Rhnull) syndrome with stomatocytosis and chronic hemolysis
A loss-of-function mutation in FUT1 impairs formation of H antigen on the RBC membrane
Absence of the RHD gene eliminates D-antigen expression via the RHD locus, a mechanism distinct from the RHCE locus that governs C, E, c, and e

questions.explanation_correct: The source material identifies the RhAG system as essential for Rh protein expression on the RBC membrane; a congenital defect in RhAG produces the 'Rh-deficiency' (Rhnull) syndrome, whose clinical consequences include stomatocytosis and chronic hemolytic anemia. Because RhAG is required for membrane insertion of the entire Rh protein complex, its absence abolishes reactivity with all five principal Rh antigens (D, C, E, c, e) simultaneously, which is exactly the pattern in this child and distinguishes this congenital membrane defect from isolated D-antigen abnormalities or acquired membrane disorders.

option_explanations.C: Correct: RhAG is described as essential for Rh protein expression; its congenital absence produces Rh-deficiency (Rhnull) syndrome with stomatocytosis and chronic hemolytic anemia, matching this child's total loss of all five Rh antigens.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: PIGA mutation with GPI-anchor loss describes paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria, an acquired clonal disorder rather than a congenital one, and it does not eliminate Rh antigen expression.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: RHD governs D-antigen inheritance specifically; its absence produces isolated D-negativity, not the simultaneous loss of C, E, c, and e seen in this child.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: a hybrid RHD/RHCE gene alters D epitopes qualitatively (partial D) but does not abolish expression of the other Rh antigens or cause stomatocytosis.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: FUT1 governs formation of H antigen (the erythroid H gene) in the ABO/H pathway, which is unrelated to Rh antigen expression or this child's membrane/hemolytic findings.

questions.teaching_point: Rhnull (Rh-deficiency) syndrome is a congenital red cell membrane disorder caused by loss of RhAG, the protein required for membrane insertion of the entire Rh complex; this produces simultaneous loss of all Rh antigens plus a structurally abnormal membrane (stomatocytosis) and chronic hemolysis, distinguishing it from isolated D-antigen variants and from acquired GPI-anchor disorders such as PNH.

source_references.locator: master-board-review-guide-transfusion-medicine.md, line 33
source_references.supports: RhAG is essential for Rh protein expression; its defect underlies Rh-deficiency (Rhnull) syndrome with stomatocytosis and chronic hemolytic anemia

source_references.locator: advanced-immunohematology-and-transfusion-medicine-review.md, lines 45-47
source_references.supports: PNH results from an acquired PIGA mutation causing GPI-anchor loss, used to build the contrasting distractor

source_references.locator: master-board-review-guide-transfusion-medicine.md, line 29
source_references.supports: RHD and RHCE are distinct genomic loci; RHD governs inheritance of the D antigen

source_references.locator: master-board-review-guide-transfusion-medicine.md, line 31
source_references.supports: Weak D (quantitative) vs Partial D (qualitative) distinction, used to build the partial D distractor

source_references.locator: blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-board-curriculum.md, line 43
source_references.supports: FUT1 encodes the enzyme forming H antigen on RBCs (erythroid H gene), used for the FUT1 distractor

review.notes: Checked all four approved sources directly. Across SRC-043, SRC-051, SRC-010, and SRC-004, the ENTIRE factual basis for Rhnull/RhAG is a single terse bullet in SRC-043 line 33: 'Rhnull Syndrome: Clinical consequences of the "Rh-deficiency" syndrome, including stomatocytosis and chronic hemolytic anemia. Includes the RhAG system (030), which is essential for Rh protein expression.' It never explicitly states that RhAG loss abolishes reactivity with all five antigens (D, C, E, c, e) simultaneously -- the question's central diagnostic mechanism is inferred/extrapolated rather than directly source

questions.subdomain: Congenital Red Cell Membrane and Enzyme Defects
questions.stem: A 6-year-old with a lifelong history of mild jaundice and compensated hemolytic anemia is being worked up before an elective transfusion. Peripheral smear shows stomatocytes. RBC antigen typing shows no reactivity with anti-D, anti-C, anti-E, anti-c, or anti-e, while positive and negative control cells react as expected. Flow cytometry demonstrates normal expression of CD55 and CD59 on the patient's red cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH)
Reagent or technical typing error
Weak D phenotype
Partial D phenotype
Rhnull (Rh-deficiency) syndrome

questions.explanation_correct: The combination of stomatocytosis, chronic compensated hemolysis, and complete nonreactivity with all five principal Rh antisera (D, C, E, c, e) despite valid assay controls is the classic serologic picture of Rhnull (Rh-deficiency) syndrome, caused by a defect in RhAG that prevents membrane expression of the entire Rh protein complex. Normal CD55/CD59 expression argues against PNH, since PNH is diagnosed by flow cytometry demonstrating a population of cells deficient in these GPI-anchored proteins.

option_explanations.E: Correct: total loss of D, C, E, c, and e reactivity with valid controls, plus stomatocytosis, chronic hemolysis, and normal CD55/CD59, fits the RhAG-deficient Rhnull phenotype rather than a GPI-anchor disorder.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: PNH is diagnosed by flow cytometry showing a population of cells deficient in the GPI-anchored proteins CD55 and CD59; this patient's CD55/CD59 expression is normal, arguing against PNH, and PNH does not eliminate Rh antigen expression.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: weak D reflects a quantitative reduction in D-antigen sites, not complete nonreactivity with D, and it does not affect C, E, c, or e expression.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: partial D reflects a qualitative alteration of D epitopes from a hybrid RHD/RHCE gene, not total loss of D plus C, E, c, and e.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: a technical or reagent failure is excluded because the assay controls reacted appropriately, confirming valid reagent performance and technique.

questions.teaching_point: When RBC antigen typing shows nonreactivity with all five principal Rh antisera in a patient with congenital hemolytic anemia and stomatocytosis, and controls confirm valid technique, the pattern should prompt consideration of Rhnull syndrome; normal CD55/CD59 expression on flow cytometry helps exclude PNH, whose hemolysis instead reflects acquired loss of GPI-anchored complement regulators.

source_references.locator: master-board-review-guide-transfusion-medicine.md, line 33
source_references.supports: Rhnull/Rh-deficiency syndrome clinical picture (stomatocytosis, chronic hemolytic anemia) and RhAG's role in Rh protein expression
