source_references.supports: PNH flow cytometry diagnostic logic (quantifying cells lacking CD55/CD59), used to build the exclusionary PNH distractor

source_references.locator: master-board-review-guide-transfusion-medicine.md, line 31
source_references.supports: Weak D (quantitative) vs Partial D (qualitative) distinction, used to build distractors C and D

review.notes: Same single-line source (SRC-043 line 33) is the sole basis for the Rhnull/RhAG/stomatocytosis/chronic-hemolysis facts; it does not explicitly enumerate that all five Rh antigens (D,C,E,c,e) are lost together, though this is a reasonable extrapolation from 'RhAG...essential for Rh protein expression' and is standard, uncontroversial teaching. PNH-exclusion logic (normal CD55/CD59 flow cytometry) is well supported by SRC-004 lines 45-47 and 57. Weak D vs. partial D distinction (options C/D) is supported by SRC-043 line 31, and unlike question 0, this item does not add an unsourced mechanistic c

questions.subdomain: Neonatal Blood Banking Practices (Dedicated Units, WBC-Reduction, Irradiation, etc.)
questions.stem: A 2-day-old infant born at 31 weeks' gestation requires a small-volume packed red blood cell transfusion for anemia of prematurity. Per NICU protocol, the blood bank issues a leukocyte-reduced, CMV-safe, group O RBC unit that has additionally been gamma-irradiated before issue. Which of the following best explains how irradiation of this unit protects this patient?

options.text: Irradiation cross-links donor plasma IgA, preventing an allergic/anaphylactic reaction in an IgA-deficient recipient
Irradiation physically removes residual donor leukocytes from the unit, lowering the risk of febrile nonhemolytic reactions and CMV transmission
Irradiation inactivates complement proteins in the donor plasma, preventing an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
Irradiation sterilizes the unit, eliminating the risk of transfusion-transmitted bacterial contamination
Irradiation inactivates donor T-lymphocytes, preventing their proliferation and a graft-versus-host response in the recipient (i.e., prevents TA-GVHD)

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources state that TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components, which acts by inactivating donor T lymphocytes so they cannot proliferate and mount a graft-versus-host response; irradiation is listed as a required neonatal blood banking practice specifically for this purpose, and TA-GVHD is noted as carrying approximately 90% fatality, underscoring why this mechanism is clinically critical.

option_explanations.E: Correct: irradiation's mechanism is inactivation of donor T-lymphocytes, which prevents their proliferation/engraftment and thus TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.B: This describes leukoreduction, not irradiation; the sources separately attribute FNHTR and CMV-transmission prevention to leukoreduction, distinct from irradiation's TA-GVHD-prevention role.
option_explanations.C: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are attributed in the sources to clerical/ABO identification error, not to a complement-inactivating effect of irradiation.
option_explanations.D: The sources identify bacterial contamination as chiefly a room-temperature platelet storage risk; irradiation is not described as a sterilization step against bacteria.
option_explanations.A: Allergic reactions are attributed in the sources to recipient IgA deficiency reacting to donor plasma proteins, a mechanism unrelated to irradiation.

questions.teaching_point: Irradiation and leukoreduction are complementary but mechanistically distinct neonatal blood banking safeguards: irradiation inactivates donor T-lymphocytes to prevent TA-GVHD, while leukoreduction removes/reduces leukocytes to lower FNHTR and CMV-transmission risk. Neither substitutes for the other.

source_references.locator: Domain V (Hazards of Transfusion) and Domain VII (Obstetric and Pediatric Patients) sections
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components, which works by inactivating donor T lymphocytes; also states neonates require dedicated units or CMV-reduced components as specific blood banking practices.

source_references.locator: Domain 2: Clinical Practice & Patient Management section (Pretransfusion Processing; Delayed Reactions; Allergic & Febrile Reactions; Acute Hemolytic Reactions lines)
source_references.supports: Distinguishes pretransfusion processing roles: irradiation for TA-GVHD prevention vs. leukoreduction for FNHTR/CMV prevention; notes TA-GVHD carries ~90% fatality and that acute hemolytic reactions are chiefly caused by clerical error, and allergic reactions relate to IgA deficiency.

review.notes: Core claim is a near-verbatim match to SRC-043 ('Pretransfusion Processing: Irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention) and leukoreduction (FNHTR/CMV prevention)') and SRC-010 ('TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes'). Distractor rationales are each traceable: B to the leukoreduction/FNHTR-CMV line (SRC-043); C to 'Clerical error is the #1 cause' of AHTR (SRC-043); D to bacterial contamination being framed as a room-temperature-platelet risk, not an irradiation function (SRC-010); E to allergic reactions being attributed to IgA deficiency, not

questions.subdomain: Neonatal Blood Banking Practices (Dedicated Units, WBC-Reduction, Irradiation, etc.)
questions.stem: A term neonate undergoes a two-volume exchange transfusion using an aliquot from a leukocyte-reduced, CMV-safe, group O RBC unit that had been gamma-irradiated several days earlier (an "older" irradiated unit). Shortly after the procedure the infant develops bradycardia with peaked T waves on ECG; a basic metabolic panel drawn at that time shows a markedly elevated serum potassium and a normal ionized calcium. Which of the following best accounts for these laboratory findings?

options.text: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload from the volume infused during the exchange
Citrate toxicity from the anticoagulant-preservative solution in the transfused unit
Potassium leakage from the transfused red cells, a risk that increases with the interval between irradiation and transfusion of the unit
Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease from inadequately irradiated donor lymphocytes
An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from ABO incompatibility due to a clerical identification error

questions.explanation_correct: The sources identify hyperkalemia—especially with older irradiated units—as a recognized metabolic risk of neonatal transfusion, alongside the separate risk of citrate toxicity. The lab pattern here (markedly elevated potassium with a normal ionized calcium) is consistent with hyperkalemia from an older irradiated unit rather than citrate toxicity, which would be expected to lower ionized calcium instead.

option_explanations.C: Correct: the sources explicitly flag hyperkalemia risk with older irradiated units as part of neonatal transfusion metabolic risk, matching the elevated potassium seen here.
option_explanations.B: Citrate toxicity is a listed neonatal transfusion risk, but it causes hypocalcemia; the normal ionized calcium in this vignette argues against it as the explanation for these findings.
option_explanations.E: AHTR from clerical/ABO error is described as the leading cause of acute hemolytic reactions, but the vignette shows an isolated electrolyte disturbance without the hemolysis findings that characterize AHTR.
option_explanations.A: TACO is described as a hydrostatic, volume/infusion-rate-related complication, not an electrolyte disturbance, so it does not explain the elevated potassium.
option_explanations.D: TA-GVHD is described as a delayed reaction, not an acute post-procedure electrolyte/ECG abnormality, so its timing and presentation do not fit this scenario.

questions.teaching_point: In neonatal large-volume transfusion (e.g., exchange transfusion), an acute rise in serum potassium with a normal ionized calcium points toward hyperkalemia from an older irradiated RBC unit rather than citrate toxicity (which causes hypocalcemia) or other acute reactions with different lab/clinical signatures.

source_references.locator: Domain 2: Clinical Practice & Patient Management section (Neonatal Transfusion Logic; Acute Hemolytic Reactions; TRALI vs. TACO lines)
source_references.supports: Neonatal Transfusion Logic lists metabolic risks of hyperkalemia (especially with older irradiated units) and citrate toxicity as distinct entities; also describes AHTR as caused chiefly by clerical error with a hemolysis hallmark, and TACO as a hydrostatic/volume-overload reaction.

source_references.locator: Domain V (Hazards of Transfusion) section
source_references.supports: Notes TA-GVHD is a delayed reaction prevented by irradiation, supporting exclusion of TA-GVHD as an acute post-procedure finding.

review.notes: The core testable concept is well grounded: SRC-043's 'Neonatal Transfusion Logic: Metabolic: Risk of hyperkalemia (especially with older irradiated units) and citrate toxicity' is an almost verbatim source for both the keyed answer and the key distinction from option B. However, the vignette's clinical findings used to justify picking A over B/C/D (bradycardia, peaked T waves as hyperkalemia's ECG signature, and 'normal ionized calcium' as evidence against citrate toxicity) are not stated anywhere in the four approved sources -- they rely on outside physiology knowledge to interpret the sourc

questions.subdomain: Neonatal/Infant Plasma, Cryoprecipitate, and Neutrophil Transfusions
questions.stem: A full-term neonate is well-appearing on day 3 of life, but a routine CBC obtained for jaundice screening shows an absolute neutrophil count of 200/µL; hemoglobin and platelet counts are normal, and there are no signs of infection. The mother's pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated, and the infant has not received any blood products. Serologic workup identifies a maternal IgG antibody directed against a human neutrophil antigen (HNA-1a) that is present on the infant's neutrophils (inherited from the father) but absent from the mother's own neutrophils. Which of the following best explains the mechanism responsible for this infant's neutropenia?

options.text: Non-irradiated donor T-lymphocytes from a prior intrauterine transfusion engraft and proliferate, producing transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease
The neonate's own immature immune system independently generated an alloantibody against the paternal neutrophil antigen while in utero
Maternal antibody against HNA-3a on the neutrophil choline transporter triggers neutrophil-mediated pulmonary endothelial injury
Maternal IgG antibody against the paternally inherited neutrophil antigen crosses the placenta and mediates destruction of the infant's neutrophils
Maternal IgM antibody against the infant's neutrophils crosses the placenta and fixes complement to lyse circulating neutrophils

questions.explanation_correct: This is neonatal alloimmune neutropenia (NAN). The source material states that maternal IgG antibodies directed against a paternally inherited human neutrophil antigen (HNA) cross the placenta and destroy fetal/neonatal neutrophils, and that HNA-1 (located on the FcγRIIIb receptor) is the system whose antibodies (anti-HNA-1a and anti-HNA-1b) are the most common cause of NAN. The condition is typically self-limited over weeks but carries a high risk of neonatal infection while neutropenia persists — consistent with this well-appearing but severely neutropenic infant.

option_explanations.D: Correct: matches the described mechanism of NAN — maternal IgG anti-HNA-1a crosses the placenta and destroys the infant's paternally derived neutrophils, exactly as described for this antigen system.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect trap: IgM does not cross the placenta (the sources emphasize IgG vs. IgM clinical kinetics as a key distinguishing concept and specifically describe placental transfer of IgG, not IgM, in alloimmune neonatal cytopenias).
option_explanations.A: Incorrect trap: this describes TA-GVHD, a distinct delayed complication (90% fatality) prevented by irradiating cellular components, mediated by engrafting donor T-lymphocytes rather than by maternal antibody — a different mechanism and clinical entity, and no transfusion history is given here.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect trap: the sources state neonates generally do not produce their own alloantibodies; detected antibodies are passively acquired from the mother, not independently generated by the neonate's immature immune system.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect trap: antibodies against HNA-3a (on the neutrophil choline transporter CTL2) are specifically associated with severe, often fatal TRALI in a transfusion recipient — a different clinical syndrome and antigen target than the HNA-1a-mediated NAN described in this stem.

questions.teaching_point: Neonatal alloimmune neutropenia is mechanistically analogous to HDFN but targets neutrophils: maternal IgG against a paternally inherited HNA (most often HNA-1a/1b on FcγRIIIb) crosses the placenta and destroys fetal/neonatal neutrophils, producing usually self-limited neutropenia with infection risk — distinct from TA-GVHD (donor T-cell engraftment) and from HNA-3a-mediated TRALI.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 43 – HNA & Clinical Syndromes
source_references.supports: Describes NAN mechanism: maternal IgG antibodies to paternal HNA cross the placenta and destroy fetal neutrophils; states HNA-1 is on FcγRIIIb and anti-HNA-1a/1b are the most common cause of NAN; also describes anti-HNA-3a as the antibody associated with severe/fatal TRALI.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 36 – Pediatric & Neonatal Transfusion Specifics
source_references.supports: States neonates generally do not produce their own alloantibodies and that detected antibodies are passively acquired from the mother; states cellular components must be irradiated to prevent TA-GVHD.

source_references.locator: Domain 1 Basic Immunology; Domain 2 Delayed Reactions
source_references.supports: Lists IgG vs. IgM clinical kinetics as a core immunology concept and identifies TA-GVHD (90% fatality) as a delayed transfusion reaction distinct from antibody-mediated destruction.

review.notes: Core mechanism is explicitly and strongly grounded in SRC-004 Node 43: 'NAN (Neonatal Alloimmune Neutropenia): Maternal IgG antibodies to paternal HNA cross the placenta and destroy fetal neutrophils. Usually self-limiting within weeks but carries a high risk of neonatal infection'; and 'HNA-1: Located on the Fcγ receptor IIIb; antibodies to HNA-1a and HNA-1b are the most common cause of NAN'; and 'TRALI: Antibodies to HNA-3a (located on the choline transporter CTL2) are associated with the most severe and fatal TRALI reactions' — this fully supports the stem, keyed answer A, and option E's ra

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with multiple myeloma is scheduled for autologous HPC transplantation. To collect sufficient CD34+ cells by apheresis, he receives daily subcutaneous G-CSF (filgrastim) for several days prior to collection, with plerixafor added on the evening before apheresis because his peripheral CD34+ count remains low. Which of the following best explains the physiologic mechanism by which this regimen increases the number of circulating CD34+ progenitor cells available for apheresis collection?

options.text: G-CSF and plerixafor volume-reduce the marrow compartment, mechanically pushing progenitor cells into peripheral blood
G-CSF and plerixafor irradiate residual tumor cells in the marrow, forcing progenitor cells into the blood
G-CSF and plerixafor increase red cell mass, which secondarily raises the peripheral CD34+ percentage on flow cytometry
G-CSF and plerixafor act together to mobilize hematopoietic progenitor cells from the marrow niche into the peripheral blood so they can be collected by apheresis
G-CSF and plerixafor cause donor T cells to attack marrow stroma, releasing progenitor cells into circulation

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources define mobilization as the use of growth factors (G-CSF/filgrastim) and/or plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) specifically to move HPCs from the marrow to the peripheral blood so they can be collected by apheresis. This is the mechanistic basis for HPC(A) collection and is listed under both 'Management' (mobilization) sections of the HPC transplantation sources.

option_explanations.D: Correct: this is precisely how the sources define mobilization — G-CSF and/or plerixafor move HPCs from marrow to peripheral blood for apheresis collection.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect and not supported; the sources describe mobilization as movement of progenitor cells into blood, not a red-cell-mass or percentage artifact.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect; irradiation is discussed only in the context of the recipient's transfusion support and as something the HPC graft itself must never receive — mobilization is not an irradiation-based mechanism.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect; T-cell attack on stroma describes GVHD pathophysiology after allogeneic engraftment, not the pharmacologic mobilization mechanism used before apheresis collection.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect; 'volume reduction' in the sources refers to graft processing after collection (e.g., for ABO-incompatible grafts), not a mechanism of mobilization.

source_references.locator: Management section
source_references.supports: Defines mobilization: G-CSF/Filgrastim and/or Plerixafor (CXCR4 antagonist) used to move HPCs from marrow to peripheral blood for apheresis collection.

source_references.locator: Management section, Node 24
source_references.supports: Reiterates mobilization definition under HPC Processing & Infusion Management section using the same G-CSF/plerixafor mechanism.

review.notes: Core fact matches SRC-038 and SRC-040 verbatim: 'Mobilization: Use of growth factors (G-CSF/Filgrastim) and/or Plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) to move HPCs from the marrow to peripheral blood for collection.' Distractor rationales (B red-cell mass, C irradiation, D T-cell attack on stroma, E volume reduction) are all correctly identified as unsupported/misapplied per the sources. Only minor concern: distractors C ('irradiate residual tumor cells... forcing progenitor cells into blood') and D ('donor T cells attack marrow stroma') are physiologically implausible on their face and could be elimi

questions.stem: A blood bank apheresis collection facility processes a peripheral blood HPC product from a mobilized autologous donor. Per standard practice for board purposes, which laboratory test is used as the rapid surrogate marker to quantify progenitor cell content of the collected product, and what is the recognized 'gold standard' method for confirming that these cells are functionally viable progenitors?

options.text: HLA typing is the rapid surrogate; chimerism analysis by STR is the gold standard for potency
CD34+ enumeration by flow cytometry is the rapid surrogate; the colony-forming unit (CFU) assay is the in-vitro gold standard for potency, though it requires about two weeks of incubation
Total nucleated cell (TNC) count is the rapid surrogate; CD34+ enumeration is the gold standard for potency
Trypan blue exclusion is the rapid surrogate; 7-AAD flow cytometry is the gold standard for potency
The colony-forming unit (CFU) assay is the rapid surrogate; CD34+ enumeration by flow cytometry is the gold standard for potency

questions.explanation_correct: Both HPC-focused sources state that CD34 enumeration by flow cytometry is the standard, rapid marker used to quantify HPCs, while the colony-forming unit (CFU) assay is explicitly called the in-vitro 'gold standard' for measuring progenitor potency, with the caveat that it is limited by a roughly two-week incubation period.

option_explanations.B: Correct: matches the sources exactly — CD34 flow cytometry as the rapid surrogate, CFU assay as the gold-standard potency test limited by a ~2-week culture period.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect; trypan blue and 7-AAD are both listed only as viability assays, not as the surrogate quantification marker or the potency gold standard.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect; TNC count is used as a cord blood dosing threshold, not described as the rapid surrogate for potency, and CD34 is not described as a 'gold standard' — that designation belongs to the CFU assay.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect; this reverses the actual roles described in the sources — CFU is the slow gold standard, not the rapid surrogate, and CD34 flow cytometry is the rapid test, not the gold standard.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect; HLA typing and chimerism monitoring are used for donor matching and engraftment/relapse surveillance, not for quantifying progenitor cell potency in the collected product.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings / Diagnostic Approach
source_references.supports: States CD34 enumeration via flow cytometry quantifies HPCs, and CFU assay is the in-vitro gold standard for potency, limited by a 2-week incubation.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach, Node 24
source_references.supports: Restates CD34 enumeration as a rapid surrogate for potency and CFU assay as the gold standard limited by a 2-week culture period; viability assessed separately by 7-AAD or trypan blue.

review.notes: Exact match to both sources: SRC-038 'CD34 Enumeration: ...quantify HPCs via flow cytometry' + 'Potency Testing: The colony-forming unit (CFU) assay is the in-vitro gold standard for measuring progenitor potency, though limited by a 2-week incubation period'; SRC-040 Node 24 restates the identical CD34-surrogate/CFU-gold-standard relationship and separately lists viability dyes (7-AAD, trypan blue) as distinct from potency testing, supporting elimination of option B. TNC (option C) is tied only to the cord blood dosing threshold, not framed as a potency surrogate, supporting elimination of C.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with severe aplastic anemia has no HLA-matched sibling or unrelated adult donor identified within her required timeframe. The transplant team is deciding among the three HPC sources described for allogeneic transplantation. Which statement best reflects the source-grounded tradeoffs that should guide selection among HPC(M), HPC(A), and HPC(CB) for this patient?

options.text: HPC(CB) should be excluded from consideration because, unlike marrow and apheresis products, cord blood units require ABO identity between donor and recipient

HPC(CB) carries no risk to the donor and a lower risk of acute GVHD, but typically has a smaller cell dose (often requiring two units in adults) and a longer time to engraftment compared with marrow or apheresis-derived grafts
HPC(M) is preferred over HPC(A) because marrow grafts engraft neutrophils and platelets faster than peripheral blood apheresis grafts
HPC(A) should be avoided in this setting because peripheral blood apheresis grafts have a higher risk of acute GVHD than either marrow or cord blood grafts
HPC(A) is the only source that requires screening the biological mother's blood sample for infectious disease markers

questions.explanation_correct: The sources explicitly list cord blood advantages as no risk to the donor, lower risk of acute GVHD, and greater availability through registries, while its disadvantages are a small cell dose (often requiring two units for adults) and a longer time to engraftment — making option A the accurate summary of the clinically relevant tradeoff for a patient without a matched donor.

option_explanations.B: Correct: directly matches the sources' stated Cord Blood Advantages (no donor risk, lower acute GVHD, availability) and Disadvantages (small cell dose often needing two units, longer engraftment time).
option_explanations.D: Incorrect; the sources state HPC(A) has a higher risk of chronic GVHD compared with HPC(M), not acute GVHD, and do not compare HPC(A) acute GVHD risk to cord blood in this way.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect; this reverses the source comparison — HPC(A) engrafts neutrophils and platelets faster than HPC(M), not the other way around.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect; the sources state HSCT does not require ABO identity for any source, since HPCs do not express ABO antigens — this applies uniformly, not selectively excluding cord blood.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect; the maternal-sample infectious disease testing requirement applies specifically to HPC(CB), not HPC(A), because no approved direct infectious disease tests exist for umbilical cord blood.

source_references.locator: High-Yield Comparison Points; Common Board Traps
source_references.supports: Lists Cord Blood Advantages (no donor risk, lower acute GVHD risk, registry availability) and Disadvantages (small cell dose often needing two units, longer engraftment); also states HPC(A) has higher chronic GVHD risk than HPC(M) and ABO identity is not required.

source_references.locator: High-Yield Comparison Points; Teaching Pearls, Node 24
source_references.supports: Confirms HPC(A) engrafts neutrophils/platelets fastest and HPC(CB) has lowest acute GVHD risk but longest engraftment time; notes maternal sample used for cord blood infectious disease testing.

review.notes: Matches sources closely: SRC-038 'Cord Blood Advantages: No risk to the donor, lower risk of acute GVHD, and more readily available through registries' and 'Cord Blood Disadvantages: Small cell dose (often requiring two units for adults) and longer time until engraftment'; SRC-040 'HPC(A) engrafts neutrophils and platelets fastest; HPC(CB) has the lowest risk of acute GVHD but takes the longest to engraft.' Distractors are strong, source-grounded 'Common Board Traps': B correctly caught by the fact that sources attribute HIGHER CHRONIC (not acute) GVHD risk to HPC(A) vs HPC(M); C correctly cau

questions.subdomain: HPC Apheresis – Characteristics and Adverse Effects
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with multiple myeloma is undergoing large-volume leukapheresis for autologous HPC(A) collection after G-CSF mobilization. About 90 minutes into the procedure he develops circumoral tingling and numbness in his fingertips, with otherwise stable vital signs. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism of his symptoms?

options.text: Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) in the collection bag is causing direct neurotoxicity
The citrate anticoagulant used in the apheresis circuit binds ionized calcium, producing acute hypocalcemia
Rapid extracorporeal fluid shifts are producing a vasovagal reaction
He is having an IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to the citrate anticoagulant
Circulating G-CSF-mobilized granulocytes are releasing inflammatory cytokines that trigger paresthesias

questions.explanation_correct: The apheresis domain source identifies citrate toxicity presenting as hypocalcemia as a common adverse effect of apheresis procedures. The citrate anticoagulant used to prevent clotting in the extracorporeal circuit binds ionized calcium, producing the perioral and digital paresthesias classically seen during citrate-induced hypocalcemia in apheresis, including HPC(A) collection.

option_explanations.B: Correct: citrate toxicity/hypocalcemia is the source-identified apheresis adverse effect, and citrate's calcium-binding property is its physiologic basis.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: DMSO toxicity is described as a complication of infusing cryopreserved HPC product after thawing, not of the collection procedure, and presents with coughing, flushing, rash, nausea/vomiting, and cardiovascular instability rather than perioral paresthesias — a classic phase-of-care mix-up trap.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: no cytokine-release mechanism is described in the sources for apheresis-associated paresthesias.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: vasovagal reactions produce hypotension/lightheadedness, not the localized perioral and digital paresthesias of citrate-induced hypocalcemia.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the described adverse effect is a metabolic/chelation phenomenon, not an IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction.

questions.teaching_point: Citrate anticoagulant used during any apheresis procedure, including HPC(A) collection, can chelate ionized calcium and cause hypocalcemic paresthesias; do not confuse this collection-phase adverse effect with DMSO toxicity, which occurs during infusion of the thawed cryopreserved product.

source_references.locator: Domain IV: Apheresis — High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Common apheresis adverse effects include citrate toxicity presenting as hypocalcemia.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 24 — Clinical Presentations — DMSO Toxicity
source_references.supports: DMSO toxicity is tied to infusion of cryopreserved product with distinct symptoms, distinguishing it from collection-phase citrate toxicity.

review.notes: Core fact is grounded: SRC-010 (Domain IV: Apheresis, High-Yield Facts) states verbatim 'Common adverse effects include citrate toxicity, presenting as hypocalcemia.' However, this statement is made at the level of general therapeutic/donor apheresis, not specifically tied in the source text to HPC(A) leukapheresis — the question extrapolates a general-apheresis fact onto the HPC(A) collection scenario, which is a reasonable but unverified extension given the blueprint node is specifically 'HPC Apheresis.' Additionally, the specific symptom set in the stem (circumoral tingling, fingertip numbn

questions.subdomain: HPC Apheresis – Characteristics and Adverse Effects
questions.stem: A transplant program is performing HPC(A) collection by apheresis from a mobilized autologous patient. To decide, on the day of collection, whether the product obtained so far is adequate or whether an additional apheresis session is needed to reach the CD34+ dose goal, the laboratory reports a same-day CD34+ cell count by flow cytometry rather than waiting for a colony-forming-unit (CFU) assay result. Which of the following best explains the rationale for using the flow cytometric CD34 count for this real-time decision?

options.text: Flow cytometric CD34 enumeration is rapid and available the same day, whereas the CFU assay, although the in-vitro gold standard for progenitor potency, requires roughly two weeks of culture
Flow cytometric CD34 enumeration also directly establishes cell viability, eliminating the need for a separate viability assay
The CFU assay cannot be performed on peripheral blood apheresis products and is restricted to bone marrow specimens
Flow cytometric CD34 enumeration is a more accurate measure of true progenitor potency than the CFU assay
The CD34+ count cannot be expressed as a cells/kg dose, so it is used only as a screening test pending CFU confirmation

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe CD34 enumeration by flow cytometry as the standard, rapid surrogate for potency used to guide same-day HPC(A) collection decisions, while the CFU assay remains the in-vitro gold standard for progenitor potency but is limited by its roughly two-week culture period, making it impractical for real-time apheresis decisions.

option_explanations.A: Correct: directly grounded in the sources' description of CD34 flow cytometry as a rapid surrogate and the CFU assay as the slower (2-week) gold standard for potency.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: no source statement restricts the CFU assay to marrow specimens only; this restriction is not supported.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources explicitly call the CFU assay, not CD34 enumeration, the 'gold standard' for potency; CD34 count is described only as a standard surrogate.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources list viability testing (7-AAD by flow cytometry or trypan blue by microscopy) as a separate assessment from CD34 enumeration, not something the CD34 count itself establishes.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the collection goal is explicitly expressed in CD34+ cells/kg of recipient weight, showing the CD34 count is used directly for dosing decisions, not merely as a screen awaiting CFU results.

questions.teaching_point: Flow cytometric CD34 enumeration is the practical, same-day surrogate used to guide real-time apheresis collection decisions; the CFU assay, though the true potency gold standard, is too slow (about 2 weeks) to inform whether to continue collecting.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 24 — Diagnostic Approach / Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: CD34 flow cytometry is a rapid potency surrogate; CFU assay is the gold standard but limited by a 2-week culture period; collection goal is stated in CD34+ cells/kg.

source_references.locator: Diagnostic Approach — Potency Testing / CD34 Enumeration / Dosing Goal
source_references.supports: Confirms CFU assay as gold standard limited by 2-week incubation, CD34 flow cytometry as the enumeration method, and a CD34+ cells/kg dosing goal.

review.notes: Fully grounded in both SRC-040 and SRC-038, which are near-identical on this point: CFU assay is explicitly termed the in-vitro 'gold standard' for potency but limited by a ~2-week culture period, while CD34 enumeration by flow cytometry is the rapid standard surrogate. Collection dosing goal is explicitly expressed in CD34+ cells/kg (2-6 x10^6/kg range), supporting elimination of distractor E. Distractor B (CFU restricted to marrow only) is unsupported by any source statement and correctly flagged as invented. Distractor C inverts the sourced 'gold standard' designation. Distractor D conflate

questions.subdomain: HPC Apheresis – Characteristics and Adverse Effects
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with relapsed lymphoma is mobilized with G-CSF alone before a planned autologous HPC(A) collection. On the scheduled morning of apheresis, his peripheral blood CD34+ count is unexpectedly low, predicting a poor progenitor cell yield. Which of the following is the best next step to improve mobilization before proceeding with apheresis collection?

options.text: Collect the apheresis product as planned and irradiate it afterward to boost progenitor recovery
Delay any further mobilization decision until colony-forming-unit (CFU) assay results are available
Add plerixafor, a CXCR4 antagonist, to the mobilization regimen
Proceed directly to a bone marrow harvest procedure instead of apheresis
Collect the apheresis product as planned and leukoreduce it before release to improve purity

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe mobilization for HPC(A) collection as the use of G-CSF and/or plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) to move progenitor cells from the marrow into peripheral blood. Adding plerixafor is the grounded pharmacologic option to enhance mobilization when a G-CSF-only regimen produces an inadequate peripheral CD34+ count.

option_explanations.C: Correct: plerixafor is explicitly named alongside G-CSF as a mobilization agent (CXCR4 antagonist) that moves HPCs into peripheral blood for apheresis collection.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: switching to marrow harvest changes the HPC source rather than applying the described pharmacologic strategy for improving peripheral mobilization, and is not supported as the preferred next step.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: classic board trap — the sources explicitly state the HPC graft must never be irradiated, as this destroys the graft's ability to engraft.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: classic board trap — the sources explicitly state the HPC graft should never be leukocyte reduced.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the CFU assay requires roughly two weeks to result, making it impractical for a same-day mobilization or collection decision.

questions.teaching_point: When G-CSF alone yields inadequate mobilization, plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) can be added to improve peripheral CD34+ yield before apheresis; remember the graft itself must never be irradiated or leukoreduced, regardless of collection difficulties.

source_references.locator: Management — Mobilization
source_references.supports: Mobilization uses G-CSF (Filgrastim) and/or plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) to move HPCs from marrow to peripheral blood for apheresis collection.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors — Irradiation/Leukoreduction
source_references.supports: The HPC graft must never be irradiated or leukocyte reduced, unlike standard cellular blood components given to the recipient.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 24 — Diagnostic Approach — Potency Testing
source_references.supports: The CFU assay is limited by a roughly 2-week culture period, making it unsuitable for real-time mobilization decisions.

review.notes: The core pharmacology fact is well sourced: both SRC-040 and SRC-038 name G-CSF (Filgrastim) 'and/or' Plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) as the agents used for mobilization. However, neither source explicitly describes the clinical rescue scenario in the stem — i.e., that plerixafor is specifically added when a G-CSF-only regimen yields an unexpectedly low peripheral CD34+ count on the morning of apheresis. This is standard, well-established real-world transplant practice (plerixafor as a 'just-in-time' rescue agent) but is a synthesis beyond what the source text states verbatim ('and/or' does no

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with relapsed lymphoma is undergoing G-CSF (Filgrastim) mobilization prior to autologous HPC(A) collection. Because his peripheral blood CD34+ count remains low on day 4, plerixafor is added to his regimen the evening before apheresis to boost the circulating CD34+ progenitor count. Which mechanism best explains how plerixafor increases the number of hematopoietic progenitor cells circulating in peripheral blood?

options.text: It produces sublethal marrow stromal injury analogous to irradiation, physically expelling progenitor cells from the marrow space
It functions as a hematopoietic growth factor that stimulates progenitor proliferation in essentially the same manner as G-CSF
It cleaves CD34 from the progenitor cell surface, generating a CD34-negative population that circulates freely in peripheral blood
It antagonizes CXCR4 on hematopoietic progenitor cells, interrupting the interaction between CD34+ cells and bone marrow stromal cells that normally retains progenitors within the marrow niche

questions.explanation_correct: The sources classify plerixafor as a CXCR4 antagonist used, with or in place of G-CSF, to move stem cells from marrow into peripheral blood for apheresis collection. Because CD34+ progenitors normally traffic and home back to marrow through interactions between CD34+ cells and bone marrow stroma, an agent that antagonizes CXCR4 disrupts this same retention/homing interaction, releasing progenitors into the circulation for collection.

option_explanations.D: Correct: plerixafor is explicitly described as a CXCR4 antagonist, and the CD34+/bone marrow stromal interaction is the mechanism by which progenitors are retained in and home to the marrow, making its disruption the basis for mobilization.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources list G-CSF/Filgrastim (a growth factor) and plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) as distinct classes of mobilizing agents, not interchangeable mechanisms.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: CD34 remains expressed on mobilized progenitors and is in fact the standard marker used for their enumeration and enrichment by flow cytometry after mobilization; it is not cleaved off.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: irradiation is never applied to an HPC graft or marrow because it destroys the ability of progenitor cells to engraft; it plays no role in the biology of pharmacologic mobilization.

questions.teaching_point: Plerixafor mobilizes progenitors by CXCR4 antagonism, disrupting the same CD34+/marrow-stromal interaction that normally retains and homes hematopoietic progenitor cells within the marrow niche; this is mechanistically distinct from growth-factor-based mobilization with G-CSF.

source_references.locator: HPC Processing & Infusion — Core Concepts (Infusion Kinetics) and Management (Mobilization)
source_references.supports: States mobilization uses G-CSF (Filgrastim) or plerixafor (CXCR4 antagonist) to move stem cells into peripheral blood, and that infused HPCs home to marrow via CD34+ cell/bone marrow stromal interactions.

source_references.locator: Domain VIII — Management (Mobilization) and Laboratory Findings (CD34 Enumeration)
source_references.supports: Confirms mobilization uses growth factors (G-CSF/Filgrastim) and/or plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) to move HPCs from marrow to peripheral blood, and that CD34 remains the marker used to quantify progenitors via flow cytometry.

source_references.locator: HPC Processing & Infusion — Prevention (Processing Rules)
source_references.supports: States HPC products should never be irradiated, grounding rejection of the irradiation-based distractor.

review.notes: Key answer A is the only defensible choice (plerixafor = CXCR4 antagonist is explicitly stated in SRC-040 line 111 and SRC-038 line 47; B/C/D are all clearly contradicted by the sources: mobilization agents are listed as distinct classes, CD34 is explicitly the marker used for enumeration/enrichment (never described as cleaved), and HPC grafts/marrow are never irradiated). However, the specific causal mechanism asserted in option A and the explanation -- that CXCR4 antagonism disrupts the same 'CD34+ cell / bone marrow stromal cell' interaction described in the source -- is a synthesis, not an

questions.stem: During peripheral blood stem cell mobilization with G-CSF, the transplant team orders a peripheral blood CD34+ cell count by flow cytometry each morning to decide whether to proceed with apheresis that day. Which of the following best explains the rationale for using this specific assay, rather than the colony-forming-unit (CFU) assay, to guide the timing of collection?

options.text: CD34 enumeration is required to confirm ABO compatibility between the mobilized product and the recipient before apheresis can proceed
The CFU assay is the standard method for assessing cell viability with 7-AAD staining, while CD34 flow cytometry provides no viability information
CD34 enumeration is performed to verify that the mobilized product has not been inadvertently irradiated before collection

CD34 enumeration by flow cytometry gives a same-day result, whereas the CFU assay requires roughly two weeks of culture, making CFU impractical for real-time collection decisions even though it remains the in-vitro gold standard for progenitor potency

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe the CFU assay as the in-vitro gold standard for progenitor potency but note it is limited by a roughly two-week culture period. CD34 enumeration by flow cytometry is described as a rapid surrogate for potency, which is why it, rather than CFU, is used to make same-day decisions about whether peripheral blood CD34+ levels are adequate to begin apheresis.

option_explanations.D: Correct: the sources explicitly contrast the two-week CFU culture time with rapid flow cytometric CD34 enumeration, explaining why CD34 counts are used operationally to time collection.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: viability is assessed with dyes such as 7-AAD (by flow cytometry) or trypan blue (by microscopy), not by the CFU assay, which measures potency rather than viability.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: HSCT does not require ABO identity because HPCs do not express ABO antigens, so ABO compatibility is not the purpose of CD34 enumeration.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: HPC products must never be irradiated; CD34 enumeration has no role in confirming irradiation status, since irradiating the product would itself be an error that destroys engraftment capacity.

questions.teaching_point: CD34 flow cytometric enumeration is used as a rapid, real-time surrogate to guide the timing of apheresis collection, while the CFU assay remains the true potency gold standard but is too slow (about two weeks) for same-day clinical decisions.

source_references.locator: Domain VIII — Laboratory Findings (CD34 Enumeration, Potency Testing)
source_references.supports: States the CFU assay is the gold standard for potency but limited by a two-week incubation, while CD34 is the marker quantified by flow cytometry.

source_references.locator: HPC Processing & Infusion — Diagnostic Approach (Potency Testing, CD34 Enumeration, Viability)
source_references.supports: States CD34 enumeration is a standard, rapid surrogate for potency performed by flow cytometry, contrasted with the two-week CFU culture gold standard, and that viability is assessed via 7-AAD or trypan blue.

source_references.locator: Domain VIII — Common Board Traps and Distractors (ABO Antibodies)
source_references.supports: States HSCT does not require ABO identity because HPCs lack ABO antigens, grounding rejection of the ABO-compatibility distractor.

review.notes: Well grounded: SRC-038 line 41 and SRC-040 lines 97/99 both explicitly contrast the CFU assay's ~2-week culture period (in-vitro potency gold standard) with CD34 flow cytometric enumeration as the rapid surrogate used operationally -- this directly supports the stem's rationale for using CD34 counts rather than CFU to time apheresis. Distractor B is correctly rejected because 7-AAD/trypan blue (not CFU) are the sources' stated viability methods (SRC-040 line 101); C is correctly rejected because ABO identity is explicitly not required for HSCT (SRC-038 line 73); D is correctly rejected because

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with multiple myeloma is mobilizing for autologous HPC(A) collection. After several days of G-CSF (Filgrastim) alone, his peripheral blood CD34+ count remains too low to expect an adequate apheresis yield. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to improve his mobilization before proceeding to apheresis?

options.text: Add plerixafor, a CXCR4 antagonist, to the G-CSF regimen to further increase the number of CD34+ progenitor cells circulating in peripheral blood
Substitute an allogeneic umbilical cord blood unit, HPC(CB), for the planned autologous collection because of inadequate mobilization
Proceed directly to a bone marrow harvest, HPC(M), instead of apheresis, since marrow collection does not depend on peripheral blood mobilization
Irradiate the patient's marrow to force additional progenitor cells into the peripheral circulation before repeating the CD34+ count

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe mobilization as accomplished with growth factors (G-CSF/Filgrastim) and/or plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) used together to move HPCs from marrow into peripheral blood for apheresis collection. When peripheral CD34+ counts are inadequate on a growth factor alone, adding the CXCR4 antagonist to the regimen is the mobilization strategy supported by the sources, rather than changing the graft source or irradiating the patient.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the sources describe G-CSF and plerixafor as agents used together ("and/or") to move stem cells into peripheral blood for collection, supporting combination use to enhance an inadequate response.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sources define HPC(M), HPC(A), and HPC(CB) as distinct source categories rather than describing marrow harvest as a routine salvage step for inadequate peripheral mobilization in an apheresis-planned patient.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: irradiation of an HPC graft or marrow is never part of mobilization; the sources state irradiation would destroy progenitor cells' ability to engraft, not assist their release.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: cord blood is an allogeneic product with its own disadvantages, including a small cell dose and longer time to engraftment; it is not described as a substitute for an autologous patient's own inadequately mobilized graft.

questions.teaching_point: When peripheral CD34+ mobilization on G-CSF alone is inadequate, the sources support adding plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) to the growth factor regimen to enhance progenitor egress into peripheral blood, rather than switching graft source or irradiating the marrow.

source_references.locator: Domain VIII — Management (Mobilization)
source_references.supports: States mobilization uses growth factors (G-CSF/Filgrastim) and/or plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist) together to move HPCs from marrow to peripheral blood for collection.

source_references.locator: HPC Processing & Infusion — Management (Mobilization)
source_references.supports: Corroborates that mobilization uses G-CSF or plerixafor to move stem cells into peripheral blood for collection.

source_references.locator: Domain VIII — Definitions (HPC Sources) and High-Yield Comparison Points (Cord Blood Disadvantages)
source_references.supports: Describes HPC(M), HPC(A), and HPC(CB) as distinct sources and lists cord blood's disadvantages (small cell dose, longer engraftment), grounding rejection of the marrow-harvest and cord-blood distractors.

source_references.locator: HPC Processing & Infusion — Prevention (Processing Rules)
source_references.supports: States HPC products should never be irradiated, grounding rejection of the irradiation distractor.

review.notes: Key answer A is best supported: SRC-038 line 47 explicitly states mobilization uses 'growth factors (G-CSF/Filgrastim) and/or Plerixafor (a CXCR4 antagonist)' -- the 'and/or' phrasing is the textual basis for combining the two agents. Distractors are well rejected: B conflates HPC(M)/HPC(A)/HPC(CB) as interchangeable when sources treat them as distinct source categories with no salvage-substitution logic described; C is refuted by the explicit 'graft must never be irradiated' rule; D is refuted by the sources' stated cord-blood disadvantages (small cell dose, longer engraftment), which argue a

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia, blood group A, undergoes myeloablative allogeneic peripheral blood HPC transplantation from her HLA-identical sibling, blood group O (a minor ABO-incompatible pairing). Approximately 10 days after infusion she develops a falling hemoglobin, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, and a positive direct antiglobulin test. Which of the following mechanisms best explains this reaction?

options.text: Direct expression of ABO blood group antigens on the transplanted CD34+ progenitor cells, triggering complement-mediated lysis
An anamnestic recipient antibody response causing rapid destruction of a recently transfused Kidd-antigen-positive red cell unit
Donor-derived passenger lymphocytes engrafting and producing antibody directed against the recipient's own native red cell antigens
Preformed recipient isoagglutinins reacting against residual donor red cells left in the unprocessed graft
Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease from infusion of non-irradiated graft lymphocytes

questions.explanation_correct: This is a minor ABO-incompatible graft (group O donor into a group A recipient), meaning the donor's immune system, not the recipient's, carries antibody against the host's red cell antigens. As donor-origin ("passenger") lymphocytes in the graft engraft, they can produce anti-A antibody directed at the recipient's own red cells, producing hemolysis, a positive DAT, and hyperbilirubinemia in the characteristic 5-16 day post-infusion window — Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome.

option_explanations.D: Incorrect — recipient antibody hemolyzing donor red cells describes the mechanism of MAJOR (not minor) ABO incompatibility, and it would manifest acutely during/soon after infusion of the graft, not roughly 10 days later.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — HPCs themselves do not express ABO blood group antigens, unlike solid organ transplants, so there is no antigen on the stem cells for a recipient antibody to attack.
option_explanations.C: Correct — donor lymphocytes carried in a minor-mismatched graft can engraft and secrete antibody against the recipient's native red cell antigens, producing hemolysis typically 5-16 days post-infusion (Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome).
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — TA-GVHD is prevented by irradiating cellular blood products transfused to the recipient (the HPC graft itself must never be irradiated); it is a donor-T-cell attack on host tissue broadly, not the described isolated hemolytic picture.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — nothing in the vignette indicates a recent RBC transfusion or Kidd sensitization; this option is unrelated to the ABO-mismatched transplant physiology being tested.

questions.teaching_point: Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome arises from donor-derived lymphocytes in a minor ABO-mismatched graft producing antibody against the recipient's own red cells (~5-16 days post-infusion) — mechanistically distinct from the recipient-antibody-mediated hemolysis of major ABO incompatibility.

source_references.locator: ABO Incompatibility Management / Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls: Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome
source_references.supports: Minor ABO mismatch = donor-origin lymphocytes producing antibody against recipient RBC antigens (Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome), occurring about 5-16 days after infusion.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors: ABO Antibodies
source_references.supports: HPCs do not express ABO antigens, distinguishing HSCT from solid organ transplant (used to eliminate distractor B).

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps: IRRADIATION ERROR; Prevention: TA-GVHD
source_references.supports: The HPC graft must never be irradiated; irradiation applies to cellular blood products given to the recipient to prevent TA-GVHD (used to eliminate distractor D).
