Distractor B is directly refuted by SRC-038's 'HSCT does not require ABO identity, as HPCs do not express ABO antigens.' Distractor D is directly refuted by SRC-038's irradiation-error pearl (graft must never be irradi

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with AML, blood group O, undergoes myeloablative allogeneic HPC(A) transplantation from his HLA-identical sibling, blood group A (a major ABO-incompatible graft). His absolute neutrophil count and platelet count have both recovered, confirming myeloid engraftment. However, several weeks later he remains dependent on RBC transfusions, with a reticulocyte count under 1% and a bone marrow biopsy showing normal myeloid maturation but near-absence of erythroid precursors. Which of the following best explains these findings?

options.text: Pure red cell aplasia caused by persistent recipient anti-donor isoagglutinins suppressing donor-derived erythropoiesis
Passenger lymphocyte syndrome from donor anti-recipient isoagglutinins destroying the recipient's residual native red cells
Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease from prior infusion of non-irradiated cellular blood products
A delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction from an anamnestic alloantibody response
Acute rejection of the graft with loss of donor hematopoiesis

questions.explanation_correct: Pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) is a recognized complication of major ABO-mismatched HPC transplantation, reported in 3%-29% of such transplants. It is caused by persistence of the recipient's own anti-donor isoagglutinins, which continue to suppress erythropoiesis from the donor-derived (group A) marrow even after myeloid and platelet lineages have engrafted normally. The laboratory picture — reticulocyte count under 1% with a bone marrow showing absent erythroid precursors despite intact myeloid maturation — is the source-defined signature of PRCA.

option_explanations.A: Correct — this laboratory and marrow picture (reticulocytes <1%, absent erythroid precursors, preserved myeloid engraftment) is the defining presentation of PRCA in major ABO-mismatched HPC transplantation, driven by persistent recipient anti-donor isoagglutinins.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — passenger lymphocyte syndrome is a MINOR-mismatch phenomenon (this case is major-mismatched) and it presents early, in the 5-16 day window, as hemolysis of the recipient's red cells, not as a late isolated erythroid aplasia.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — myeloid and platelet engraftment are intact, arguing against overall graft rejection, which would be expected to affect all hematopoietic lineages rather than erythropoiesis alone.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect — TA-GVHD typically involves skin, gastrointestinal, and hepatic manifestations with broader marrow suppression, not an isolated erythroid defect, and nothing in the vignette points to this process.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction is a hemolytic (not aplastic) process driven by an anamnestic response to a foreign red cell antigen; it does not explain absent marrow erythroid precursors.

questions.teaching_point: Pure red cell aplasia after major ABO-mismatched HPC transplantation is diagnosed by reticulocytopenia (<1%) with marrow erythroid precursor absence despite normal myeloid engraftment, reflecting ongoing recipient anti-donor isoagglutinin activity rather than graft failure.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings: Pure Red Cell Aplasia (PRCA)
source_references.supports: PRCA occurs in 3%-29% of major ABO-mismatched transplants and is defined by reticulocytes under 1% with absent erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment.

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls: Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome
source_references.supports: Passenger lymphocyte syndrome is a minor-mismatch complication occurring roughly 5-16 days post-infusion (used to eliminate distractor B).

source_references.locator: Definitions: Engraftment
source_references.supports: Engraftment is defined by ANC recovery, framing why intact myeloid/platelet recovery argues against overall graft rejection (used to eliminate distractor C).

review.notes: The epidemiology and lab picture are strongly grounded: SRC-038 states PRCA 'Occurs in 3%-29% of major ABO-mismatched transplants; characterized by reticulocytes <1% and absence of erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment' — an exact match to the vignette and to the major-mismatch classification (recipient O, donor A) per the SRC-044 table. However, the explanation's specific causal mechanism — 'persistent recipient anti-donor isoagglutinins suppressing donor-derived erythropoiesis' — is not stated verbatim anywhere in the four approved sources; only the epidemiologic association and d

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with blood group O undergoes myeloablative allogeneic HPC transplantation from an HLA-matched donor with blood group A (a major ABO-incompatible pairing). Before his red cell population has fully converted to donor type, which combination of blood components is most appropriate for his ongoing RBC and plasma/platelet transfusion support?

options.text: Donor (group A)-type red cells; group AB plasma and platelet products
Group AB red cells as the universal-donor red cell choice; recipient-type plasma and platelet products
Group O red cells (washed if the recipient's anti-A isoagglutinin titer is high); recipient (group O)-type plasma and platelet products
Group O red cells; group AB plasma and platelet products
Donor (group A)-type red cells; recipient (group O)-type plasma and platelet products

questions.explanation_correct: For a MAJOR ABO incompatibility (the recipient holds antibody against the donor's red cell antigens), the recommended support strategy is group O red cells — washed if the anti-A/anti-B titer is high — to avoid exposing lingering recipient isoagglutinins to donor-type antigen, paired with recipient-type plasma and platelet products. This differs from the strategies used for minor or bidirectional incompatibility.

option_explanations.C: Correct — matches the source's major-incompatibility selection: group O red cells (washed if titer is high) with recipient-type plasma/platelets.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect — donor-type red cells with group AB plasma/platelets is the strategy for MINOR incompatibility, not major; giving donor-type (A) red cells here risks reaction with the recipient's still-present anti-A.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect — group O red cells with group AB plasma/platelets is the strategy reserved for BIDIRECTIONAL incompatibility, not an isolated major mismatch.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect — this combination scrambles the selection logic, pairing donor-type red cells (appropriate only for minor mismatch) with recipient-type plasma (appropriate for major mismatch); it does not correspond to any of the source-defined categories.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect — group O, not group AB, is the universal red cell donor type; group AB is used as a universal plasma source, not for red cells, so this option reflects a basic ABO selection error.

questions.teaching_point: Blood component selection after ABO-incompatible HPC transplantation follows a structured, category-specific logic: major incompatibility uses group O RBCs (washed if high titer) with recipient-type plasma/platelets; minor uses donor-type RBCs with group AB (or recipient-type) plasma/platelets; bidirectional uses group O RBCs with group AB plasma/platelets.

source_references.locator: Section 3: Hematopoietic Stem Cell (HSCT) Support: ABO Logic table
source_references.supports: HSCT ABO support table: major incompatibility = group O RBCs (wash if high titer) with recipient-type plasma/platelets; minor = donor-type RBCs with group AB (or recipient-type) plasma/platelets; bidirectional = group O RBCs with group AB plasma/platelets.

source_references.locator: Management: Group O Universalism
source_references.supports: Group O is the universal fallback red cell type and group AB the universal plasma type when ABO compatibility is uncertain (used to eliminate distractor E).

source_references.locator: Domain VIII: HPC Transplantation — Major Concepts
source_references.supports: General topic framing that HPC transplantation requires distinct major/minor ABO incompatibility management.

review.notes: The keyed answer is an almost verbatim match to the SRC-044 table: 'Major (Recip has Ab vs Donor) | Group O (Wash if high titer) | Recipient Type.' SRC-041's 'Group O Universalism: Always fall back to Group O RBCs and/or Group AB plasma' correctly supports eliminating option E. However, option D's rationale is factually wrong relative to the cited source: it claims the combination 'donor-type red cells; recipient-type plasma and platelet products' scrambles the logic and 'does not correspond to any of the source-defined categories.' But the same SRC-044 table's minor-mismatch row reads 'Donor

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with AML (blood group O, Rh-positive) undergoes allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation from an HLA-matched sibling donor who is blood group A (major ABO incompatibility). Within the first hour of marrow product infusion, he develops fever, hypotension, hemoglobinemia, and hemoglobinuria. Which of the following best explains the mechanism responsible for this reaction?

options.text: A recipient alloantibody that was below the detection limit at pretransplant testing is reappearing anamnestically
Non-irradiated donor T lymphocytes are engrafting and attacking recipient tissue antigens
The recipient's preformed anti-A antibody binds A antigen on donor red cells within the graft and fixes complement, producing intravascular hemolysis
Cytokines that accumulated in the stored graft during processing are triggering fever without red cell destruction
Donor-derived lymphocytes within the graft produce antibody directed against the recipient's own red cell antigens

questions.explanation_correct: Major ABO incompatibility means the recipient possesses an antibody (isohemagglutinin) against a red cell antigen present on the donor graft — here, group O recipient anti-A meeting group A donor red cells. As with a classic acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, when this IgM/IgG antibody encounters its target antigen it fixes complement, producing intravascular hemolysis, which accounts for the fever, hypotension, hemoglobinemia, and hemoglobinuria occurring within the first hour of infusion.

option_explanations.C: Correct: recipient antibody against donor graft antigen fixing complement is the immediate, intravascular hemolysis mechanism.
option_explanations.E: This is the mirror-image mechanism of minor ABO incompatibility (donor graft antibody-producing cells vs. recipient red cells), which characteristically produces delayed, not immediate, hemolysis.
option_explanations.A: This describes the anamnestic mechanism of a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction, which evolves over days, not within the first hour of infusion.
option_explanations.B: This describes the T-cell-mediated tissue-attack mechanism of transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease, a distinct entity that does not cause red cell hemolysis.
option_explanations.D: This describes a febrile non-hemolytic mechanism, which does not produce hemoglobinemia or hemoglobinuria.

questions.teaching_point: Major ABO incompatibility = recipient antibody vs. donor graft RBC antigen -> immediate, complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis, mechanistically analogous to AHTR; minor incompatibility = donor graft-derived antibody vs. recipient RBC antigen -> typically delayed hemolysis.

source_references.locator: Domain V, 'HSCT Support' line
source_references.supports: Names major vs. minor ABO-incompatible transplant protocols as a distinct HSCT support topic, anchoring the clinical scenario.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 31 (AHTR pathophysiology), Node 33 (DHTR), Node 32 (FNHTR); master guide TA-GVHD line
source_references.supports: Provides the IgM/IgG complement-fixation mechanism of immediate intravascular hemolysis (AHTR), the anamnestic DHTR contrast, and the TA-GVHD/FNHTR mechanisms used as distractors.

review.notes: Across all 4 sources, HSCT-specific ABO incompatibility appears in exactly one unelaborated line (SRC-043: 'HSCT Support: Major vs. Minor ABO-incompatible transplant protocols'). No source states that major ABO incompatibility in HPC transplantation causes immediate intravascular hemolysis via recipient-antibody-vs-donor-graft-RBC complement fixation; this is inferred by analogy to the general AHTR mechanism in SRC-058 Node 31 (IgM/IgG complement fixation -> intravascular hemolysis), which is textbook-accurate but not stated for the transplant/marrow-infusion context specifically. The keyed an

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with blood group A undergoes allogeneic HPC transplantation from a blood group O donor (minor ABO incompatibility). Approximately two weeks after transplant, as donor engraftment proceeds, she develops a gradual, mild drop in hemoglobin with mild jaundice; she remains hemodynamically stable throughout, and workup confirms an antibody-mediated hemolytic process directed against her own residual A-positive red cells. Which of the following represents the most appropriate management of this patient?

options.text: Immediate administration of vasopressors for hypotension
First-line treatment with high-dose intravenous immune globulin (IVIG)
Aggressive intravenous normal saline plus diuretics to maintain urine output above 1 mL/kg/hr, with close monitoring for disseminated intravascular coagulation
First-line treatment with corticosteroids
Supportive monitoring of hemoglobin, since this process is typically self-limiting, with antigen-negative red cells provided for future transfusion needs if required

questions.explanation_correct: This gradual, donor-antibody-mediated hemolytic process in a hemodynamically stable patient parallels a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction, which is described as usually self-limiting and managed with supportive monitoring of hemoglobin, reserving antigen-negative red cells for future transfusion needs. Aggressive renal-protective fluid/diuretic therapy, pressors, IVIG, and corticosteroids each correspond to management of other, distinct entities and are not indicated for this stable, self-limited picture.

option_explanations.E: Correct: mirrors the described DHTR management approach — usually self-limiting, monitor hemoglobin, antigen-negative blood for future needs.
option_explanations.C: This aggressive fluid/diuretic/renal-protection and DIC-monitoring regimen is the described management for an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction with intravascular hemolysis and hypotension risk, not for this stable, gradual, delayed process.
option_explanations.B: IVIG is the described treatment of choice for post-transfusion purpura, a distinct entity causing profound thrombocytopenia, not hemolytic anemia.
option_explanations.D: Corticosteroids are the described first-line therapy for warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, a distinct autoantibody-mediated process; this patient's hemolysis is donor(allo)antibody-mediated in the transplant setting, not autoimmune.
option_explanations.A: Vasopressors are reserved for intractable hypotension during acute hemolytic reactions; this patient is explicitly hemodynamically stable.

questions.teaching_point: Delayed, donor-antibody-mediated hemolysis in minor-ABO-mismatched HPC transplantation is managed like a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (supportive, self-limiting) rather than with the aggressive renal-protective measures of acute hemolysis or the disease-specific therapies (IVIG for PTP, corticosteroids for WAIHA) used for other immune-hematologic entities.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 33 (DHTR management), Node 31 (AHTR management)
source_references.supports: Provides DHTR management (usually self-limiting, monitor hemoglobin, antigen-negative blood for future needs), AHTR management (aggressive fluids/diuretics/renal protection, pressors), and PTP management (IVIG).

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 14, WAIHA Management
source_references.supports: Establishes corticosteroids as first-line WAIHA management, used as a distractor for a non-autoimmune hemolytic process.

source_references.locator: Domain V, 'HSCT Support' line
source_references.supports: Names major vs. minor ABO-incompatible transplant protocols, anchoring the clinical context for this management decision.

review.notes: The four distractor managements are well grounded: aggressive fluids/diuretics/DIC monitoring for AHTR (SRC-058 Node 31), IVIG for PTP (SRC-058 Node 33), corticosteroids for WAIHA (SRC-061 Node 14), and vasopressors for intractable hypotension (SRC-058 Node 31). The keyed answer (supportive monitoring, self-limiting, antigen-negative blood for future needs) closely mirrors SRC-058 Node 33's DHTR management line verbatim ('Usually self-limiting; monitor hemoglobin and provide antigen-negative blood for future needs'), but Node 33 describes management of a classic recipient-anamnestic alloantibo

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with blood group A undergoes myeloablative allogeneic HPC(A) transplantation from his HLA-identical sibling donor, who is blood group O. Neutrophil engraftment has already occurred. On post-transplant day 11, he develops jaundice, dark urine, and a hemoglobin drop from 9.8 to 7.1 g/dL. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism of this patient's hemolysis?

options.text: Donor T-cells broadly infiltrate and attack recipient skin, gut, and liver tissue
Recipient cytotoxic T-cells attack donor hematopoietic precursors, producing rejection of the graft
Persistent recipient antibody lyses developing donor erythroid precursors in the marrow, halting erythropoiesis
Donor-derived lymphocytes transferred within the graft produce antibody directed against the recipient's own RBC antigens
Residual recipient isoagglutinins in plasma react against donor-derived RBCs contained in the graft

questions.explanation_correct: This is a minor ABO mismatch (donor O plasma contains anti-A/anti-B; recipient RBCs carry A antigen). Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome occurs when donor-origin lymphocytes carried within the graft engraft and begin producing antibody against the recipient's own remaining RBC antigens, causing hemolysis typically 5-16 days after infusion, consistent with this patient's day-11 presentation.

option_explanations.D: Correct: this is the defined mechanism of PLS — donor-origin lymphocytes in the graft produce antibody against the recipient's remaining RBC antigens, causing hemolysis.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect direction: recipient antibody attacking donor RBCs describes the mechanism of acute hemolysis in a major ABO mismatch, not PLS, and in this case the donor (group O) has no A/B antigen for recipient antibody to target.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: ABO incompatibility causes RBC hemolysis but does not cause rejection of the HPC graft itself; this option describes a rejection mechanism that is not how PLS operates.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this describes the mechanism of pure red cell aplasia (PRCA), in which recipient antibody lyses developing donor erythroid precursors — a complication of major, not minor, ABO mismatch.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this describes GVHD, a multi-system syndrome from donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues broadly, distinct from the antibody-mediated, RBC-restricted hemolysis of PLS.

questions.teaching_point: PLS is an antibody-mediated (donor lymphocyte-derived isoagglutinin), minor-mismatch phenomenon targeting recipient RBC antigens — mechanistically distinct from major-mismatch acute hemolysis, PRCA, and GVHD.

source_references.locator: Definitions; Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Defines PLS as donor-origin lymphocytes producing antibody against recipient's remaining RBC antigens, causing hemolysis, and specifies the 5-16 day post-transplant timing

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls
source_references.supports: Confirms PLS occurs in minor ABO mismatches with donor-origin lymphocytes producing antibody against recipient RBC antigens, typically 5-16 days post-infusion

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps; Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Confirms ABO incompatibility causes hemolysis but not graft rejection (distractor C), and describes GVHD as multi-system donor T-cell attack on tissues (distractor E)

review.notes: Recipient A / Donor O is exactly the worked example SRC-035 gives for minor ABO incompatibility. PLS mechanism (donor-origin lymphocytes producing antibody against recipient RBC antigens) and 5-16 day onset window are stated verbatim in SRC-035 ('Passenger Lymphocyte Syndrome... donor-origin lymphocytes in the graft produce antibodies against the recipient's remaining RBC antigens') and SRC-038 ('typically 5-16 days post-infusion'); day 11 fits. Distractor B correctly maps to the major-mismatch mechanism (and is doubly wrong since donor is group O and has no A/B antigen). Distractor C matches

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with blood group B receives a minor ABO-mismatched allogeneic HPC transplant from a blood-group-O unrelated donor. On post-transplant day 9 she develops fever and a falling hemoglobin, and passenger lymphocyte syndrome (PLS) is suspected. Which of the following laboratory findings would most specifically support the diagnosis of PLS, as distinguished from other posttransplant hematologic complications?

options.text: Reticulocyte count persistently less than 1% with absent erythroid precursors on marrow biopsy despite myeloid engraftment
Mixed-field agglutination on forward ABO typing in the absence of a positive DAT
Positive direct antiglobulin test (DAT) with an eluate demonstrating anti-B specificity directed at the recipient's own native RBC antigen
Chimerism studies by short tandem repeat (STR) analysis showing a rising recipient-derived cell fraction
Biopsy-confirmed donor T-cell infiltration of skin and liver with multi-system organ dysfunction

questions.explanation_correct: PLS serology is characterized by a positive DAT and a reactive eluate that shows specificity for the recipient's original RBC antigen (here, anti-B, since the recipient is group B) — reflecting antibody produced by donor-origin passenger lymphocytes against the recipient's own remaining red cells.

option_explanations.C: Correct: PLS serology is specifically defined by a positive DAT and reactive eluate with specificity for the recipient's original antigen (e.g., anti-A or anti-B).
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: mixed-field agglutination on forward typing is characteristic of the normal transition from recipient- to donor-origin cell populations, not a specific marker of PLS, and would not occur without a positive DAT in this hemolytic presentation.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this reticulocytopenia/absent-erythroid-precursor pattern with intact myeloid engraftment describes pure red cell aplasia (PRCA), a complication of major (not minor) ABO mismatch, not PLS.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: a rising recipient-derived fraction on chimerism analysis would suggest graft failure or relapse, not the antibody-mediated hemolysis of PLS.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: multi-system donor T-cell infiltration with organ dysfunction describes GVHD, a distinct donor T-cell-mediated tissue process, not the isolated hemolysis of PLS.

questions.teaching_point: The diagnostic serologic signature of PLS is a positive DAT with an eluate showing anti-A/anti-B specificity against the recipient's own antigen — distinct from the mixed-field pattern of normal engraftment, the aplastic marrow picture of PRCA, or chimerism/GVHD findings.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: States PLS serology shows positive DAT and reactive eluates with specificity for recipient antigens such as anti-A or anti-B

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: Describes mixed-field agglutination as characteristic of the recipient-to-donor transition period on forward ABO typing

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: Defines PRCA as reticulocytes <1% with absence of erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment, occurring in major ABO-mismatched transplants

review.notes: SRC-035 states verbatim: 'PLS Serology: Positive Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT) and reactive eluates showing specificity for recipient antigens (e.g., anti-A or anti-B)' -- exact match for keyed option A (recipient is group B, eluate anti-B). Option B's mixed-field agglutination is explicitly described in SRC-035 as characteristic of the normal recipient-to-donor transition, not PLS-specific. Option C matches the PRCA lab picture from SRC-038 ('reticulocytes <1%... absence of erythroid precursors despite myeloid engraftment'), correctly attributed to major (not minor) mismatch. Option D's link

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man with blood group AB undergoes a minor ABO-mismatched allogeneic HPC(A) transplant from an HLA-matched unrelated donor with blood group A. On post-transplant day 10 he becomes acutely symptomatic with fatigue, jaundice, and a hemoglobin of 6.9 g/dL. Workup confirms a positive DAT and an eluate with anti-B specificity, consistent with passenger lymphocyte syndrome. He requires RBC transfusion support. Which of the following is the most appropriate RBC product to select?

options.text: Rituximab and therapeutic plasma exchange alone, without RBC transfusion
Group AB RBCs, matching the patient's original pretransplant blood group
AB plasma component support only, deferring RBC transfusion until the DAT becomes negative
Emergency RBC depletion of a newly collected graft product before further transfusion, as required for major ABO-mismatched grafts
Group A (donor-type) or group O RBCs

questions.explanation_correct: Per ABO-incompatible HSCT component-selection principles, RBCs given in a minor mismatch should be donor-type or group O — cells that lack the B antigen targeted by the donor lymphocyte-derived anti-B isoagglutinin causing this patient's hemolysis. Group A (donor-type) or group O RBCs are compatible with both the recipient's circulating anti-B-provoking antibody and avoid exposing transfused cells to ongoing lysis.

option_explanations.E: Correct: for minor ABO mismatches, RBC selection should be donor-type or group O, which lack the B antigen the PLS-associated anti-B antibody is targeting.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: group AB RBCs still express the B antigen, the exact target of the donor-derived isoagglutinin identified in the eluate, and would be subject to the same ongoing hemolysis.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: RBC depletion is a preventive graft-manipulation step performed on the graft before infusion for major (not minor) ABO mismatches to prevent acute hemolysis from excess donor RBCs; it is not a treatment applicable to an already-infused minor-mismatch graft or to established PLS.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: AB plasma is the universal-compatibility fallback described for plasma/platelet component selection, not for RBC selection, and withholding RBC support does not address this patient's symptomatic hemolytic anemia.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: rituximab, plasma exchange, and donor lymphocyte infusion are described as therapies for pure red cell aplasia (PRCA), a different major-mismatch complication, not as RBC-selection guidance or first-line therapy for symptomatic PLS-related anemia.

questions.teaching_point: Because PLS reflects a minor ABO mismatch, symptomatic patients should receive RBCs that lack the antigen targeted by the donor-derived isoagglutinin — donor-type or group O — while major-mismatch maneuvers (RBC depletion) and PRCA-specific therapies (rituximab, plasma exchange, DLI) are misapplied in this context.

source_references.locator: Management — Component Selection Protocols
source_references.supports: States that in minor ABO mismatches, RBC component selection should use donor-type or group O red cells, compatible with both donor and recipient

source_references.locator: Management — Graft Manipulation; Component Selection Protocols
source_references.supports: Describes RBC depletion as a graft manipulation used for major mismatches and AB plasma as the universal fallback for plasma/platelet products, not RBC selection

source_references.locator: Management — PRCA Management
source_references.supports: Identifies rituximab, plasma exchange, and DLI as PRCA management therapies, a distinct major-mismatch complication

review.notes: Recipient AB / Donor A is correctly worked out as a pure minor mismatch (donor A plasma contains anti-B, targeting the B antigen the AB recipient still carries; no major-mismatch component since AB recipients make neither anti-A nor anti-B). SRC-035 states directly: 'in minor mismatches, use donor-type or Group O' for RBC component selection -- matches keyed option A exactly. Option B (AB RBCs) is correctly rejected because AB units still carry the B antigen targeted by the eluate-confirmed anti-B. Option C (RBC depletion) is correctly identified as a major-mismatch graft-manipulation step fro

questions.stem: A group A Rh-positive patient underwent allogeneic HPC transplantation from a group O Rh-positive unrelated donor (minor ABO mismatch). Two weeks after infusion she has an acute hemoglobin drop and a DAT positive with anti-IgG and anti-C3d. To identify the specificity of the antibody coating her red cells, the blood bank performs a technique that dissociates the bound antibody from the red cell membrane into a testable fluid. Which technique was performed, and what antibody specificity is most likely identified?

options.text: Neutralization; anti-Lewis
Lui freeze-thaw technique; anti-B
Adsorption; anti-A
Donath-Landsteiner testing; anti-P
Elution; anti-A

questions.explanation_correct: Dissociating antibody already bound to the red cell membrane into a fluid (eluate) for specificity testing is, by definition, elution. In this O-donor-into-A-recipient minor mismatch, the passenger donor lymphocytes produce anti-A, which coats the recipient's own A-positive red cells; eluting and testing this antibody would identify it as anti-A.

option_explanations.E: Correct: elution dissociates bound antibody from the red cell into an eluate to determine specificity, and anti-A is the expected antibody given the O-into-A minor mismatch.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect technique: adsorption uses antigen-positive cells to remove antibody from serum; it does not dissociate antibody already bound to the patient's own red cells.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect technique and specificity: neutralization inactivates antibody by adding its corresponding soluble antigen (e.g., Lewis substance) and is not used to recover antibody bound to red cells; Lewis specificity is unrelated to this ABO-mismatch scenario.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect technique and specificity: the Donath-Landsteiner test identifies a biphasic IgG anti-P antibody in paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria, an unrelated clinical entity.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect application and specificity: the Lui freeze-thaw technique is used to detect IgG ABO antibody in neonatal HDFN eluates, not adult post-transplant hemolysis, and the antibody targeting this group A recipient's cells would be anti-A, not anti-B.

questions.teaching_point: Elution recovers antibody bound to red cells for specificity testing; in a minor ABO-mismatched transplant, an eluate from the recipient's DAT-positive cells is expected to reveal the donor-derived isohemagglutinin (anti-A in an O-into-A mismatch).

source_references.locator: Definitions section, Elution
source_references.supports: Defines elution as dissociating bound antibody from red cells into an eluate to determine specificity, critical for HDFN and HTR workups.

source_references.locator: Definitions section, Adsorption
source_references.supports: Defines adsorption as removing antibody from serum using antigen-positive cells, used to construct the adsorption distractor.

source_references.locator: Definitions section, Neutralization
source_references.supports: Defines neutralization as inactivating antibody with soluble antigen (e.g., Lewis substance), used to construct the neutralization distractor.

source_references.locator: Domain I, Clinical Practice, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States the Donath-Landsteiner test identifies a biphasic IgG anti-P antibody in PCH, used to construct that distractor.

source_references.locator: Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls section
source_references.supports: States the Lui freeze-thaw technique is used to detect IgG ABO antibody in neonatal HDFN eluates, used to construct that distractor.

source_references.locator: Domain VIII, HPC Transplantation, Major Concepts
source_references.supports: Lists 'major/minor ABO incompatibility in HSCT' as a major concept, supporting the O-into-A mismatch framework of the stem.

review.notes: The technique-identification content is well grounded: elution (SRC-001 line 17, 'dissociating bound antibody from red cells into a solution... to determine its specificity'), adsorption (line 15), neutralization/Lewis (line 19), Lui freeze-thaw for neonatal HDFN eluates (line 91), and Donath-Landsteiner/anti-P for PCH (SRC-010 line 13) are all accurately used to build distinct, correct distractors. However, the clinical conclusion that the eluted antibody 'is most likely' anti-A depends entirely on the same unsupported PLS mechanism flagged in Q0 (O-into-A minor mismatch producing donor anti-

questions.subdomain: Indications, Methods, Results, and Adverse Effects
questions.stem: A hospital cellular therapy laboratory manufactures a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell product—an immune effector cell (IEC) therapy—for a patient with relapsed acute lymphoblastic leukemia. During manufacturing, the patient's own antigen-directed T cells are engineered by introducing new genetic material to redirect their antigen specificity before infusion. Which of the following best characterizes the mechanistic classification of this genetic modification?

options.text: Germline gene therapy, because the engineered T cells are capable of expansion and long-term persistence, making the modification heritable
A form of allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell transplantation, since the manufactured product is intended to replace the patient's own marrow stromal cell population
Pathogen inactivation technology, since ultraviolet light and a photosensitizing compound are used to cross-link the T-cell genetic material
Somatic gene therapy, because the genetic material is introduced into non-reproductive cells and the resulting changes are not transmitted to the patient's offspring

questions.explanation_correct: The sources define somatic gene therapy as the introduction of new genetic material into somatic (non-reproductive) cells to treat or cure a disease, with the resulting changes not transmitted to future generations—unlike germline gene therapy. CAR-T/IEC manufacturing, which involves engineering a patient's antigen-directed T cells, fits this definition exactly.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The sources contrast somatic therapy with germline therapy, which produces heritable changes transmitted to offspring; engineering a patient's own T cells does not alter reproductive cells or affect future generations.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. This conflates CAR-T/IEC processing of antigen-directed T-cells with hematopoietic progenitor cell/marrow transplantation; the source describes CAR-T processing as involving antigen-directed T-cells (and marrow stromal cells during preparation), not replacement of the marrow stromal compartment.
option_explanations.D: Correct. This matches the source's explicit definition of somatic gene therapy and its description of CAR-T (IEC) manufacturing as engineering of antigen-directed T-cells.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Pathogen inactivation technologies use UV light with or without a photosensitizer (e.g., amotosalen, riboflavin) to cross-link pathogen nucleic acids in platelets/plasma, preventing pathogen replication—an unrelated process from gene-therapy engineering of a patient's own cells.

questions.teaching_point: CAR-T/immune effector cell manufacturing is a form of somatic gene therapy: modification is confined to non-reproductive cells and is not heritable, distinguishing it from germline gene therapy, from HPC/marrow transplantation, and from unrelated technologies such as pathogen inactivation.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 69 – Immune Effector Cells (IECs) & Specialized Cell Therapies
source_references.supports: Defines somatic vs. germline gene therapy and describes CAR-T/IEC manufacturing as processing of antigen-directed T-cells and marrow stromal cells

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 48 – Pathogen Inactivation (PI) Technologies
source_references.supports: Describes the pathogen inactivation mechanism (UV light with/without photosensitizer cross-linking nucleic acids), used to construct distractor D

review.notes: SRC-011 Node 69 explicitly defines somatic gene therapy ('introduction of new genetic material into somatic cells... not transmitted to future generations') and separately states CAR-T cells are IECs and that CAR-T processing involves engineering antigen-directed T-cells. However, the source never explicitly states 'CAR-T/IEC engineering IS an instance of somatic gene therapy' — the two facts sit as adjacent bullets under the same node and the link is inferred, not asserted verbatim. The inference is standard and reasonable (matches real-world FDA classification), so this is a minor gap rather

questions.subdomain: Indications, Methods, Results, and Adverse Effects
questions.stem: An HLA-matched sibling is being evaluated as an allogeneic donor of a hematopoietic cellular product for a recipient whose malignancy makes transplantation immediately life-threatening if delayed. Mandatory infectious disease screening of the donor returns a repeatedly reactive result on the initial serologic assay; confirmatory (supplemental) testing has not yet resulted, and standard donor eligibility criteria are not currently met. Which of the following best reflects the correct interpretation and regulatory handling of this situation?

options.text: The donor must complete a standard 6-month reentry waiting period with repeat negative testing before this product can be used, regardless of the recipient's clinical urgency
Because the reactive result was obtained on the initial screen, mandatory recipient look-back procedures must be initiated immediately to notify prior recipients before this product can be considered

The repeatedly reactive screening result alone does not establish true infection; supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot for HIV or neutralization for HBsAg) is required to differentiate a true positive from a false positive, and the product may still be permitted for use under an 'urgent medical need' exception if specific FDA criteria are met
Infectious disease screening requirements apply only to volunteer allogeneic blood donors, not to donors of cellular therapy products, so the reactive result requires no further evaluation
A single repeatedly reactive screening test is diagnostic of true infection, so the donor and product must be excluded from any use, regardless of the recipient's clinical urgency

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that supplemental testing is required for repeatedly reactive serologic screens to differentiate true infection from false positives, and separately state that the FDA mandates infectious disease screening of allogeneic donors of these advanced cellular products but that donors may still donate under an 'urgent medical need' exception if specific criteria are met. Combining these two source-stated principles yields the correct handling of this scenario.

option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Look-back, per the sources, is the retrospective process of identifying and notifying recipients of prior components/products from a donor later found to have a transmissible infection; it does not apply prospectively to determine whether this donor's current product can be released.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The sources state supplemental testing is required for repeatedly reactive screens specifically to differentiate true infection from false positives; a single reactive screen is not described as diagnostic on its own.
option_explanations.C: Correct. This combines the source-stated need for supplemental (confirmatory) testing to resolve a repeatedly reactive screen with the source-stated FDA 'urgent medical need' exception permitting donation of these advanced cellular products despite not meeting standard eligibility, if specific criteria are met.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The sources explicitly state that FDA mandates screening and testing of allogeneic donors of these advanced cellular products for relevant infectious diseases.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The 6-month reentry waiting-period algorithm described in the sources applies to previously deferred donors seeking to requalify to donate again later; it is a distinct regulatory pathway from the urgent medical need exception described for current release of a needed product.

questions.teaching_point: A repeatedly reactive donor infectious-disease screen requires supplemental testing before true infection is confirmed, and the FDA's urgent medical need exception can allow use of an otherwise-ineligible allogeneic cellular product—concepts that must not be conflated with look-back (a retrospective notification process) or the routine donor reentry waiting-period algorithm.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8 – Diagnostic Approach / Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: States supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot for HIV, neutralization for HBsAg) is required to resolve repeatedly reactive screening results

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 69 – Safety Requirements
source_references.supports: States FDA mandates infectious disease screening of allogeneic donors of IEC/advanced cellular products but allows an 'urgent medical need' exception if specific criteria are met

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8 – Definitions / Management
source_references.supports: Defines look-back as the retrospective process of notifying prior recipients of components from a donor later found infected

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 50 – Infectious Reentry Logic
source_references.supports: Describes the 6-month HBV NAT donor reentry waiting period with repeat negative testing as an example of the standard reentry algorithm, used to construct distractor E

review.notes: Both components of the key answer are explicitly stated: SRC-013 states supplemental testing (Western blot for HIV, neutralization for HBsAg) is required to resolve repeatedly reactive screens and differentiate true infection from false positives; SRC-011 Node 69 states FDA mandates infectious-disease screening of allogeneic donors of advanced cellular products but allows an 'urgent medical need' exception if specific criteria are met. The synthesis into a single correct option is well grounded. Distractors are each cleanly refuted by source: look-back (A) is explicitly defined in SRC-013 as r

questions.subdomain: Indications, Methods, Results, and Adverse Effects
questions.stem: A hospital-based transplant program is establishing a new service line to collect, process, and administer chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell products—an immune effector cell (IEC) therapy—for patients with relapsed hematologic malignancies. To ensure appropriate programmatic oversight of the collection, processing, and administration of these advanced cellular products, which of the following should the program pursue, according to the sources?

options.text: No additional accreditation is required beyond routine donor infectious disease screening, since IEC/CAR-T products are processed identically to standard blood components
Implementation of a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) program in place of institutional accreditation, since FMEA proactively identifies possible process failures
Reliance on AABB accreditation alone, since AABB guidance governs general blood banking and transfusion medicine practice
Accreditation from the Foundation for the Accreditation of Cellular Therapy (FACT), the body specifically identified as sought by facilities handling these advanced cellular products

questions.explanation_correct: The sources state that facilities handling advanced cellular products such as CAR-T/IEC therapies often seek accreditation from the Foundation for the Accreditation of Cellular Therapy (FACT), distinguishing this oversight pathway from general blood banking accreditation and from quality-improvement tools.

option_explanations.C: Incorrect. AABB is referenced in the sources as a general source of blood banking/transfusion medicine guidance (e.g., recipient notification recommendations), but the sources specifically identify FACT—not AABB—as the accreditation sought by facilities handling IEC/CAR-T products.
option_explanations.D: Correct. The source states that facilities handling these advanced products often seek accreditation from FACT.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. FMEA is described in the sources as a proactive, step-by-step quality tool for identifying possible failure modes within a process; it is not an accrediting body and does not substitute for institutional accreditation.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The sources treat IEC/CAR-T products as 'advanced products' with their own accreditation pathway (FACT) and their own FDA-mandated donor screening framework, distinct from routine blood component processing.

questions.teaching_point: Facilities that collect, process, and administer immune effector cell products such as CAR-T are described in the sources as seeking FACT accreditation, a distinct oversight pathway from general blood banking accreditation and from quality-improvement tools such as FMEA.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 69 – Logistics and Oversight
source_references.supports: States facilities handling advanced cellular products (IEC/CAR-T) often seek FACT accreditation

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 8 – Management
source_references.supports: References AABB as a source of general transfusion medicine guidance, used to construct distractor A

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 68 – RCA Tools & Methodology
source_references.supports: Describes FMEA as a proactive quality-improvement tool for identifying process failures, used to construct distractor C

review.notes: SRC-011 Node 69 states directly: 'Facilities handling these advanced products often seek accreditation from the Foundation for the Accreditation of Cellular Therapy (FACT)' — a near-verbatim match to option B. FMEA (option C) is correctly and separately described in Node 68 as a proactive quality tool, not an accrediting body. AABB (option A) appears elsewhere in the sources only as a general TM guidance source (e.g., recipient notification recommendations in SRC-013), never as the accreditor named for IEC/CAR-T facilities, supporting A as a distractor. Option D is refuted by the source treati

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia, blood group O, undergoes allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation from his HLA-matched sibling donor, blood group A (major ABO incompatibility). Neutrophil and platelet engraftment occur on schedule, and chimerism studies confirm full donor myeloid chimerism. However, at post-transplant day 65 the patient remains red cell transfusion-dependent, with a reticulocyte count persistently below 1%. Which of the following best explains this patient's ongoing red cell aplasia?

options.text: Donor-origin passenger lymphocytes are producing anti-A antibody that is hemolyzing the patient's residual red cells
Persisting recipient anti-A isoagglutinins are lysing donor-derived erythroid precursors in the marrow
The recipient has rejected the HPC graft because of the underlying ABO incompatibility
The HPC graft contained an inadequate CD34+ cell dose to support erythroid lineage recovery
Donor T lymphocytes in the graft are attacking the recipient's bone marrow stroma as part of graft-versus-host disease

questions.explanation_correct: Pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) is a recognized complication of major ABO-incompatible HSCT: persisting recipient isoagglutinins (here anti-A) target and lyse developing donor-derived erythroid precursors in the marrow, producing myeloid (and platelet) engraftment without erythroid recovery, with reticulocytes remaining below 1% for more than 60 days post-transplant, exactly as described in this vignette.

option_explanations.B: Correct — matches the source-defined mechanism of PRCA: recipient antibody lyses donor erythroid progenitors while sparing myeloid and platelet lineages.
option_explanations.E: GVHD is a multisystem syndrome (rash, fever, diarrhea, liver dysfunction) from donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues broadly; it is not described as a cause of isolated erythroid aplasia with otherwise normal myeloid/platelet engraftment.
option_explanations.D: CD34+ dose relates to overall graft potency and engraftment, not a selective erythroid-only failure; normal myeloid and platelet engraftment argue against a global graft-dose problem.
option_explanations.A: This describes passenger lymphocyte syndrome, which arises from minor (not major) ABO incompatibility and classically causes hemolysis presenting 5-16 days post-transplant, not isolated erythroid aplasia persisting past day 60.
option_explanations.C: ABO incompatibility causes hemolysis, not rejection of the HPC graft itself; full donor myeloid chimerism confirms the graft engrafted successfully.

questions.teaching_point: PRCA after major ABO-mismatched HSCT results from recipient isoagglutinins lysing donor-derived erythroid precursors in the marrow, producing selective erythroid failure (reticulocytes <1% beyond day 60) despite normal myeloid/platelet donor engraftment — distinct from GVHD, PLS, or graft rejection.

source_references.locator: Definitions / Clinical Presentations / Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls
source_references.supports: Defines PRCA as a major ABO-mismatch complication: myeloid engraftment without erythroid recovery, reticulocytes <1% for >60 days, caused by recipient antibody lysing donor erythroid precursors ('Targeting the Progenitor').

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Describes GVHD as a multi-system syndrome from donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues, distinguishing it from isolated erythroid aplasia.

source_references.locator: Definitions / Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Defines passenger lymphocyte syndrome and its 5-16 day post-transplant timing, contrasting with the day-65 PRCA picture.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States ABO incompatibility causes hemolysis but does not cause rejection of the HPC graft (explicit board trap).

source_references.locator: Domain VIII High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Notes CD34 is the primary antigen used to quantify HPCs in a graft, relevant to the CD34-dose distractor.

review.notes: SRC-035 defines major ABO incompatibility exactly as 'Recipient O, Donor A' and defines PRCA verbatim as recipient isoagglutinins lysing developing donor erythroid precursors in the marrow, with myeloid engraftment but reticulocytes <1% for >60 days (Teaching Pearl 'Targeting the Progenitor' repeats the same mechanism). Day 65 satisfies the >60-day criterion. Option B (GVHD as multi-system syndrome) and option E ('ABO Rejection Distractor: causes hemolysis but not rejection') are both verbatim-matched board traps in SRC-035. Option D is correctly excluded because PLS is a minor-mismatch phenom

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with blood group A undergoes allogeneic HPC transplantation from an HLA-matched unrelated donor with blood group O (minor ABO incompatibility). On post-transplant day 9 she develops fever and a decreasing hemoglobin. The direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive, and the eluate demonstrates anti-A reactivity. No RBC transfusion has been given since transplant. Which of the following best accounts for these findings?

options.text: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from an ABO-incompatible red cell unit
Passenger lymphocyte syndrome
Acute hemolysis from residual donor red cells infused with the graft
Pure red cell aplasia
Graft rejection due to ABO incompatibility

questions.explanation_correct: In minor ABO incompatibility (donor O, recipient A), donor-origin lymphocytes transferred within the graft can produce antibody directed against the recipient's own red cell antigens. This is passenger lymphocyte syndrome, classically presenting 5-16 days post-transplant with a positive DAT and an eluate reactive with specificity for the recipient's antigen (anti-A here), matching this patient's day-9 presentation in the absence of any intervening RBC transfusion.

option_explanations.B: Correct — the timing (day 9, within the 5-16 day window), the minor-mismatch setting, and the DAT/eluate pattern (anti-A reactivity against the recipient's own antigen) are the defining features of passenger lymphocyte syndrome.
option_explanations.D: PRCA is a complication of major, not minor, ABO mismatch, and presents as isolated erythroid marrow failure with reticulocytes <1% beyond 60 days — not an acute day-9 DAT-positive hemolytic event.
option_explanations.A: No RBC transfusion was given, so an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction to an incompatible unit cannot explain these findings.
option_explanations.E: ABO incompatibility causes hemolysis, not rejection of the HPC graft; nothing in the vignette indicates graft failure.
option_explanations.C: Acute hemolysis from residual donor red cells occurs at the time of graft infusion in major mismatches with a large donor RBC volume, not nine days later in a minor-mismatch setting.

questions.teaching_point: A positive DAT with an eluate specific for the recipient's own ABO antigen, appearing 5-16 days after a minor-mismatch HSCT, is the serologic signature of passenger lymphocyte syndrome — donor graft lymphocytes producing antibody against the recipient's red cells.

source_references.locator: Definitions
source_references.supports: Defines minor ABO incompatibility (donor isoagglutinins vs. recipient RBC antigens, e.g., recipient A/donor O) and passenger lymphocyte syndrome as donor lymphocytes producing antibody against recipient RBC antigens causing hemolysis.

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations / Laboratory Findings
source_references.supports: States delayed hemolysis (PLS) typically presents 5-16 days post-transplant with positive DAT and reactive eluate specific for recipient antigens (anti-A or anti-B).

source_references.locator: Clinical Presentations
source_references.supports: Associates PRCA with major (not minor) mismatch and reticulocytes <1% for >60 days, distinguishing it from this acute day-9 presentation.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: States ABO incompatibility causes hemolysis, not rejection, of the HPC graft.

review.notes: SRC-035 defines minor ABO incompatibility exactly as 'Recipient A, Donor O' and states PLS ('Delayed Hemolysis') 'Typically presents 5 to 16 days post-transplant' with 'Positive DAT and reactive eluates showing specificity for recipient antigens (e.g., anti-A or anti-B)' — this vignette (day 9, DAT+, eluate anti-A against a group-A recipient) is a near-verbatim match. Option B (PRCA) is correctly excluded on both mismatch type (major vs minor) and timing (>60 days vs day 9), per source. Option D matches the 'ABO Rejection Distractor' language. Option C and E are excluded by internal vignette f

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with blood group O is scheduled to receive an allogeneic peripheral blood HPC graft from her HLA-identical sibling, blood group A (major ABO incompatibility). The cellular therapy laboratory is preparing the apheresis product before infusion. Which processing step is most appropriate to reduce the risk of acute hemolysis at the time of graft infusion?

options.text: Gamma irradiation of the graft
Infuse the graft without modification
RBC depletion of the graft
Bedside leukoreduction filtration of the graft at infusion
Plasma reduction of the graft

questions.explanation_correct: Acute hemolysis at graft infusion is a recognized risk of major ABO incompatibility when the apheresis product contains a substantial volume of donor red cells incompatible with the recipient's isoagglutinins. RBC depletion of the graft is the manipulation used for major mismatches to remove this incompatible red cell volume and prevent acute hemolysis; plasma reduction (removing isoagglutinin-containing plasma) is instead reserved for minor mismatches.

option_explanations.C: Correct — RBC depletion is the graft manipulation specified for major ABO mismatches to prevent acute hemolysis on infusion.
option_explanations.A: The HPC graft itself must never be irradiated, since this destroys the stem cells' proliferative capacity; irradiation is reserved for blood components transfused to the patient, not the graft.
option_explanations.E: Plasma reduction lowers isoagglutinin load and is the manipulation used for minor, not major, ABO mismatches; it does not remove the excess incompatible donor RBC volume that drives acute hemolysis in a major mismatch.
option_explanations.D: Routine bedside leukoreduction of the graft is not performed, similar to irradiation, and would not address red-cell-mediated hemolysis risk.
option_explanations.B: Unmodified infusion of a graft with substantial incompatible donor RBC content in a major mismatch carries a recognized acute hemolysis risk that graft manipulation is specifically intended to prevent.

questions.teaching_point: For major ABO-mismatched HSCT, RBC depletion of the graft prevents acute infusion hemolysis; for minor mismatches, plasma reduction lowers isoagglutinin load — but the graft itself is never irradiated or routinely leukoreduced, unlike supportive blood components given to the patient.

source_references.locator: Management / Component Selection Protocols
source_references.supports: States RBC depletion is used for major mismatches to prevent acute hemolysis, while plasma reduction is used for minor mismatches to reduce isoagglutinin load.

source_references.locator: Common Board Traps and Distractors
source_references.supports: Explicit board trap that the HPC graft must never be irradiated or routinely leukoreduced, as this would impair stem cell function.

source_references.locator: Section 3, Specialized Manipulations
source_references.supports: Lists volume reduction via plasma/RBC removal as a specialized HPC processing manipulation, corroborating RBC depletion as a recognized graft-processing step.

review.notes: SRC-035 states verbatim under Management: 'RBC depletion is used for major mismatches (>10-30 mL RBCs) to prevent acute hemolysis; plasma reduction is used for minor mismatches to reduce the isoagglutinin load' — this recipient O / donor A pairing is the source's own textbook example of major mismatch, so RBC depletion is unambiguously correct. Option B is directly supported by the 'NEVER Irradiate the Graft' board trap (destroys stem cell proliferative capacity). Option D is directly supported by the 'Leukoreduction of Graft' board trap (no routine bedside leukoreduction of the graft). Option

questions.stem: A cellular therapy processing laboratory is finalizing a peripheral blood HPC(A) apheresis product for long-term storage prior to an upcoming autologous transplant. Per standard operating procedure, the product is mixed with 5%–10% dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and then subjected to controlled-rate cooling before being placed into a liquid nitrogen freezer. Which of the following best explains the underlying purpose of this cryoprotectant plus controlled-rate cooling step?

options.text: To remove residual donor red cells and prevent an acute hemolytic reaction in an ABO-major-mismatched recipient
To deplete donor T lymphocytes and lower the risk of graft-versus-host disease
To prevent cellular damage to the hematopoietic progenitor cells as they transition to ultra-low storage temperatures
To enumerate CD34+ cells by flow cytometry and confirm the graft meets the potency threshold for engraftment
To reduce the recipient's risk of transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD)

questions.explanation_correct: HPC processing standards describe cryoprotectants such as DMSO, combined with controlled-rate cooling, as being used specifically to prevent cellular damage to the progenitor cells during the transition to ultra-low storage temperatures. This preserves stem cell viability and potency so the graft can still support engraftment after thaw. This is mechanistically distinct from CD34+ enumeration (a separate potency/QC step), irradiation-based TA-GVHD prevention (which is applied to the recipient's other blood components, never to the graft), red cell reduction (a separate manipulation for major ABO-mismatched grafts), and T-cell depletion (a separate manipulation aimed at GVHD risk).

option_explanations.C: Correct. Cryoprotectant plus controlled-rate cooling is described as functioning specifically to prevent cellular damage to HPCs during the transition to ultra-low temperatures, preserving viability for engraftment.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. CD34+ enumeration by flow cytometry is a distinct quality-control step used to confirm cell dose/potency; it is not accomplished by, or the purpose of, adding DMSO or controlled-rate cooling.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. TA-GVHD prevention is achieved by irradiating the recipient's other cellular blood components (RBCs, platelets); the HPC graft itself must never be irradiated, as this would destroy the stem cells' ability to proliferate.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Preventing acute hemolysis from residual donor red cells is the purpose of red cell reduction, a separate specialized manipulation performed for major ABO-mismatched grafts, not a function of cryopreservation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Reducing GVHD risk by removing donor T cells is the purpose of T-cell depletion, a distinct specialized manipulation; GVHD itself results from donor T-cells attacking host tissue, not from inadequate cryoprotection.

questions.teaching_point: Cryopreservation (cryoprotectant + controlled-rate cooling) protects HPC viability during transition to ultra-low temperature storage; it is a mechanistically distinct processing step from CD34+ enumeration (potency QC), red cell/plasma reduction (ABO-incompatibility management), T-cell depletion (GVHD-risk reduction), and irradiation, which must never be applied to the HPC graft itself.

source_references.locator: Section 3, 'Stages of Specialized Processing and Infusion' (Specialized Manipulations; Cryopreservation; Quality Control)
source_references.supports: Describes cryoprotectant (DMSO) plus controlled-rate cooling as preventing cellular damage during transition to ultra-low temperatures, and lists CD34+ enumeration (QC), T-cell depletion, and volume/RBC reduction as separate specialized manipulations distinct from cryopreservation.

source_references.locator: Management ('Cryopreservation', 'ABO Incompatibility Management'); Common Board Traps ('IRRADIATION ERROR'); Prevention ('TA-GVHD', 'GVHD')
source_references.supports: States HPCs are typically frozen in 5%–10% DMSO; states the HPC graft must never be irradiated because recipients (not the graft) receive irradiated components to prevent TA-GVHD; describes red cell reduction for major ABO mismatch to prevent acute hemolysis; describes GVHD as donor T-cells attacking host tissue.

review.notes: All facts verified directly against sources. SRC-038 line 49: 'Cryopreservation: HPCs are typically frozen in 5%–10% DMSO and stored in liquid nitrogen freezers.' SRC-028 line 59: 'Cryopreservation: Utilization of cryoprotectants (e.g., DMSO) and controlled-rate cooling to prevent cellular damage during the transition to ultra-low temperatures' — matches stem and correct answer A verbatim in substance. SRC-010 line 115 corroborates 'DMSO is the standard cryoprotectant used for freezing HPC products.' Distractor B (CD34+ enumeration) supported as a separate QC/potency step (SRC-038 line 39, 57;

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem: A hospitalized, alert, competent adult is ordered to receive a unit of RBCs. No discussion of the transfusion ever takes place with the patient at any point — the order is simply carried out. Several days later the patient develops a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction caused by an antibody that could not have been detected by routine pretransfusion testing. Setting the reaction itself aside, which legal mechanism best characterizes the hospital's liability exposure specifically for the fact that no consent of any kind was obtained before the transfusion was given?

options.text: Strict liability — holding the hospital liable for a defective product regardless of fault
No liability, because the causative antibody was not detectable by pretransfusion testing
Battery — an intentional tort arising from performing the transfusion without obtaining any consent
Ordinary negligence, since harm occurred and can be measured as compensable damages
Negligence — because the standard of care required disclosure of reaction risk regardless of whether consent was ever discussed

questions.explanation_correct: The approved sources classify performing a transfusion without obtaining any consent as the intentional tort of battery, distinct from negligence. Negligence instead requires proof of five separate elements — a duty owed, breach of that duty, harm to the patient, the breach being the direct/predictable cause of that harm, and measurable damages. Because no consent process occurred at all here, the consent failure itself is battery, not a negligence claim.

option_explanations.C: Correct: the sources explicitly define battery as transfusion performed without obtaining any consent, an intentional tort separate from negligence analysis.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this frames the issue as a standard-of-care/disclosure failure (negligence), but the fact pattern describes total absence of any consent discussion, which the sources classify as battery rather than a duty-breach analysis.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: strict liability is a doctrine the sources note is rarely applied to blood in most states, and it addresses defective products, not absence of consent.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this distractor invokes the negligence framework (harm plus damages) even though the underlying problem — no consent obtained at all — is specifically what the sources label battery rather than negligence.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: whether the antibody was detectable may be relevant to a negligence defense regarding the reaction itself, but it does not resolve or excuse the separate consent failure, which remains a battery issue.

questions.teaching_point: Complete absence of any consent before a transfusion is battery (an intentional tort); negligence instead requires proving all five elements — duty, breach, harm, causation, and damages.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 45 – Medicolegal & Ethical Aspects
source_references.supports: States that battery is performing a transfusion without obtaining any consent, while negligence is failure to follow standards of care that leads to injury; also notes strict liability is rarely applied to blood.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 63 – Advanced Medicolegal Scenarios
source_references.supports: Confirms battery as the intentional tort for transfusion without any informed consent, lists the five elements of negligence (duty, breach, harm, causation, damages), and reiterates strict liability is rarely applied to blood.

review.notes: Verified against SRC-004 line 95 ('Battery: Performing a transfusion without obtaining any consent') and SRC-006 line 43 ('Performing a transfusion without obtaining any informed consent is legally classified as an intentional tort of battery'). Both locators (Node 45 'Medicolegal & Ethical Aspects' and Node 63 'Advanced Medicolegal Scenarios') match exactly. The five negligence elements listed in SRC-006 (duty, breach, harm, causation, damages) exactly match the explanation. Strict liability rarely applied to blood is confirmed in both sources. The DHTR/undetectable-antibody framing is unsour

questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem: An unconscious trauma patient arrives in hemorrhagic shock and requires immediate RBC transfusion before pretransfusion testing can be completed and before any surrogate decision-maker is available to discuss consent. Which of the following best reflects the correct handling of blood release and documentation in this situation?

options.text: Transfusion must be withheld until written informed consent is obtained from the patient or a surrogate, regardless of clinical urgency
The unit should be labeled as fully crossmatched to avoid delaying transfusion, since retrospective testing will confirm compatibility
Uncrossmatched RBCs can be released with no physician documentation at all, since the emergency itself constitutes sufficient authorization

The transfusion service may issue uncrossmatched group O RBCs under emergency release, provided a signed physician's statement is obtained and the unit is clearly labeled to indicate that testing is incomplete
The transfusion service may proceed only once strict liability has been formally waived by the requesting physician

questions.explanation_correct: The sources describe emergency release as the accepted standard for urgently issuing uncrossmatched group O RBCs when full pretransfusion testing (and by extension, a full consent discussion) cannot be completed in time. This standard specifically requires a signed physician's statement and labeling that clearly indicates the testing is incomplete, allowing life-saving transfusion to proceed while preserving documentation and transparency.

option_explanations.D: Correct: matches the emergency release standard exactly — signed physician statement plus clear labeling that testing is incomplete.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this rigid interpretation ignores the emergency release mechanism the sources describe specifically for situations where obtaining consent/testing before transfusion is not feasible.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: strict liability is a separate liability doctrine the sources note is rarely applied to blood; it is not something 'waived' to authorize emergency release.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the emergency release standard still requires a signed physician's statement; documentation is streamlined, not eliminated.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect and unsafe: the standard requires labeling that clearly indicates testing is incomplete, not a false label of full crossmatch.

questions.teaching_point: When true emergency prevents a normal consent/testing process, emergency release of uncrossmatched group O RBCs requires a signed physician's statement and unit labeling that clearly discloses testing is incomplete — documentation is streamlined, not skipped.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 55 – Emergency Management & Continuity
source_references.supports: Defines emergency release as the standard for urgent issuance of uncrossmatched group O RBCs, requiring a signed physician's statement and clear labeling indicating testing is incomplete.

Locator 'Subdomain Node 55 – Emergency Management & Continuity' matches exactly. Option A reproduces this definition precisely. Option C ('strict liability formally waived') is a weak/somewhat contrived distractor -- not a real legal mechanism referenced anywhere near emergency release in the sources -- but it is clearly wrong and does not create ambiguity about the best answer. questions.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
questions.stem: A competent adult who is a Jehovah's Witness is scheduled for elective surgery with anticipated moderate blood loss. Preoperatively, she clearly states and documents that she does not want any allogeneic blood products transfused, even if her life is at risk intraoperatively. Which of the following is the most appropriate approach to her perioperative management?

options.text: Substitute hemoglobin-based oxygen carriers for allogeneic blood without further discussion, since they are freely available and do not require her consent
Seek a court order to compel transfusion before surgery, since informed refusal does not apply to elective procedures
Cancel the surgery indefinitely, since a competent patient may not decline a clinically indicated treatment

Honor her informed refusal and pursue a bloodless medicine strategy, such as preoperative erythropoietin and iron, meticulous surgical hemostasis to minimize blood loss, and intraoperative cell salvage
Proceed with allogeneic transfusion intraoperatively if her hemoglobin falls critically low, since preserving life overrides a previously documented refusal

questions.explanation_correct: The sources affirm that competent adults have the legal right to refuse transfusion based on religious or personal beliefs (informed refusal), explicitly citing Jehovah's Witnesses as an example. Management of such patients centers on Patient Blood Management's three pillars — optimizing hemoglobin, minimizing blood loss, and improving anemia tolerance — using bloodless medicine tools including erythropoietin, high-dose iron, intraoperative cell salvage (Cell Saver), and meticulous surgical hemostasis.

option_explanations.D: Correct: directly reflects informed refusal plus the described PBM three pillars and bloodless medicine tools (EPO, iron, cell salvage, meticulous hemostasis).
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: this overrides the patient's documented legal right to informed refusal, which the sources state applies to competent adults regardless of clinical severity.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: nothing in the sources supports compelling transfusion via court order for a competent adult's informed refusal of an elective procedure; this contradicts the stated legal right to refuse.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources state HBOCs are currently available primarily through clinical trials or expanded access/compassionate use, not freely available for routine substitution, and consent principles still apply.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: the sources do not support indefinite cancellation; instead, management proceeds via bloodless medicine strategies while respecting the refusal.

questions.teaching_point: Informed refusal is a legal right of competent adults (e.g., Jehovah's Witnesses); appropriate management shifts to Patient Blood Management's three pillars and bloodless medicine strategies rather than overriding the refusal.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 54 – Management of Transfusion Refusal
source_references.supports: States competent adults have the legal right to refuse transfusion (e.g., Jehovah's Witnesses); describes PBM's three pillars and bloodless medicine tools (EPO, iron, cell saver, hemostasis); notes HBOCs are mainly available via trials/compassionate use.

review.notes: Verified against SRC-012 lines 69-77 (Node 54 'Management of Transfusion Refusal'): informed refusal is confirmed as a legal right for competent adults citing Jehovah's Witnesses explicitly; PBM 'Three Pillars' (optimizing hemoglobin, minimizing blood loss, improving anemia tolerance) and bloodless medicine tools (EPO, high-dose iron, Cell Saver, meticulous surgical hemostasis) match option A verbatim.
